There are some things about this game that the community seems to strongly dislike. The timer, the loot negotiations, and more. And allegedly, the devs got feedback on these things from playtesters before the early access period, but chose to ignore that and release the game without addressing the issues. I don't deny that these things suck.  But despite it all, the game is still very fun. Guns are fun to shoot. Absurd builds can be cobbled from the random perks. The random caves are as varied and fun to explore as the ones from the original DRG. And it does seem like the devs are planning to address the issues after the community backlash. The game is still only in early access, I think it shows promise, and I am excited to see where it goes.

After playing for 85 Hours here's my take; It's exactly what i expected it to be. For early access it still needs some work. Overall it is fun and true to the RDG universe. However I do wish for more variety of choices. I understand persons dislike for the barter system. Once you have a team that makes reasonable choices it can be fun. But at the end of the day it really is first pick first served. People need to learn to not be upset with people choosing what they want. The timer is not as bad as some reviews have made it out to be. If the team uses their head and the work load is split properly a lot can get done. what i would love to see is an additional skill tree level for chips so that harder levels are more feasible. Also for ascencions actually do something to buff the character even a tiny amount. I also would love for a possible stage where we find the missing miners and have to escort them out while waves come at us, like on a bus/doretta/apc type thing till we get to the drop pod. Instead of us mining at each stop the miners run out and mine for us while we protect the vehicle and hope they mine enough for us to get the upgrades. All in all a good game and i look forward to the end result.

Despite all the criticism I have seen online, I actually really enjoy Rogue Core. I think it mixes DRG (which I am a huge fan of and have over 900 hours in) and roguelikes like RoR2 (which I also played for about 300 hours). I think however, that people should stop viewing it as either one: Rogue Core is its own game, and it is really fun.    I think the timer is fine, and simply means you have to plan what you can loot and what you have to skip, the upgrades are unique and interesting, and so are the classes.     The only criticism I have is the slight lack of content, like it needs some more enemy types. I am sure Ghost Ship is planning on adding more stuff, but it's not unplayable.    I have mostly played it with 2 friends and like the upgrade mechanic. I can see how playing with randoms might be annoying though.     All in all amazing game, can't wait to see what more will be added as the game comes out of early access. Thank you Ghost Ship, ROCK AND STONE!!!

Alright. Here is who I am in this review context. - Some mad-lad with over 4,000 hours in Deep Rock Galactic (DRG). - Only plays on a modded difficulty (that I created) centered on spawning 5.1x the amount of bugs you find in hazard 5, (Called ''Certified Hell Hole''). The difficulty aims to make enemies not bullet sponges, the player very tanky, and the difficulty being from the endless bug tide. It was very successful at this. - Is a hardcore gamer but goes under the rule of -- challenging but fun. - I only started hosting public lobbies after unlocking hazard 3/4. - Have not done the gauntlet content. - Has an 8,000 character limit.  Analog conclusion: If you want to play a team-focused hoard shooter that will test your metal, this is a great pick even in early access! GAME IS GOOD!  [b]NOTE:[/b] I will assume you know the shpeal of early access games. I will not be mentioning things that I believe are strictly an "early access moment".  [hr][/hr]  So what is Rogue core? (Abbreviated to RC). I can tell you it's not Deep Rock Galactic (DRG). Something people aren't wrapping their heads around is that what defines a game is it's mechanics, not its universe. I will however say, there could be better ways for RC to make it clear it does not play like DRG but I believe it's equally a social and gameplay issue.   RC is a game that demands you to step up to the game's expectations in your performance. The skill floor (the minimum skill needed) to play this game is very high for modern games, including DRG. A misconception however is that RC is *just hard*. It is not. If you are an experienced gamer, RC will welcome you nicely. The difficulty in RC stems from having a time limit pressure you to be efficient, the inability to make optimized builds pre-mission (forcing you to use junk and make it work), and by the game not being shy to spawn hoards of enemies for you to deal with.  [-] You drop in. Start up the mission and get your standard gear. This gear very likely has the ability to carry you to the end of the mission if you are good enough. No upgrades or anything.  [-] The mission is split into a marathon. Each level is largely empty with only 3 points of interests at minimum, 2 of which are very important, and 5 at maximum. It's all DRG deep dives basically so the appeal of the upgrade system is not really to give you a new experience or make the mission easier but to make mistakes less punishing so you don't bleed out so easily on the marathon. -- The upgrade system is alright. I had HUGE skepticism for it but somehow the devs made it work very well. [list]     [*][POS] Upgrades are largely irrelevant. This is probably because of how worthless most of them are but in theory this should remain true. The main way you will get through missions is not by RnG giving you an amazing build but by you playing well. You kill enemies efficiently, you get hit less, you are quick. That is how you win missions. The upgrade system is simply a bonus to make things somewhat more interesting and less punishing. There do exist upgrades that make the mission a lot easier but they are so few in number you really can see upgrades as irrelevant.     [*][POS] Having a shared pool is not that bad. There are so few upgrades and even fewer good ones that you can very easily have the *entire list* be irrelevant. Everyone just picks what's vaguely good for them or just use the skip upgrade for health. I personally have had many runs where I stack the health upgrade (the skip upgrade) on purpose. There really isn't any fighting over selection. And again, upgrades are largely irrelevant. It's about your skill.     [*][NEG] The upgrade pool is so small and has even fewer good upgrades that the core mechanic of RC can straight up be ignored. Even on haz 4/4. Upgrades are largely worthless so mining is largely a waste of time and the events are pointless. Sure, the upgrades can help but I am very confidant that if everyone decides to focus on playing well and grabbing the workbench and bio-booster (the two important Points of interest), the game would be easier than putting the time to mine and do events. [b]HOWEVER:[/b] People will largely mine anyways and do the events because it's fun and interesting to do and you [i]can[/i] get cool upgrades.     [*][NEG] The game focuses on chipping you away to try and make upgrades relevant. The enemy design is explicitly designed to be able to land cheap hits on you with a busted damage stat. If you don't plan to be perfect, you are going to want upgrades on higher haz levels, especially if you are not solo as super speedy enemies that can kill you in 6 hits spawn *everywhere* in massive numbers. You will take damage. [/list]  - GAMEPLAY - [list]     [*][POS] The gameplay is wonderful. Focusing on being efficient is very rewarding and encouraged. People complain about the timer but in DRG, I noticed in my first 1,000 hours how much everyone (including me) would loiter. Doing absolutely nothing. By 4,000 hours I have gotten rid of this loitering entirely (unless socializing) in my game-play and this optimization is *everything* in RC. If a swarm does not bog you down and you don't loiter, you can do everything in the cave with some time to spare. It can all be done quite easily if everyone brings their A-Game.      [*][POS] The combat and movement is just like DRG but you have added spices from class abilities, RC original equipment (looking at you Anti-Grav Emitter), and boss shenanicans (looking at you Molaktula). It feels great and plays great.     [*][NEG] The sound design is rather lacking on all fronts. They focus too much on being *ooouuuhhhh, ssscciiii-fffiiiii* vs GUN GO [b]BANG[/b] (this is an unpopular opinion). It makes sound ques basically impossible to figure out as everything blends together. ESPECIALLY ENEMY RELATED SOUNDS. Guh...     [*][POS] The teamplay is just like DRG but rather than the classes being defined by loadouts, they are defined by abilities. The teamplay is also just as positive.     [*][NEG] I will say it is really a bummer that we can't make pre-mission builds other than perks. The perks in RC by the way are much better than in DRG. It tends to feel very clunky and cursed working with the very cursed build RnG has blessed you with. None of that pre-mission kit building magic from DRG is here which I argue was the best quality of DRG, even when it meant using the same loadout thousands of times after 4,000 hours.     [*][b][NEG][/b] *Everyone* must be on their A-Game. One screw up can endanger the level very easily and quickly. This is probably why people are really complaining about the timer and difficulty. If one person is a bit new / not a seasoned gamer, they very easily become a devastating liability, which is quite a shame especially with the rapid difficulty curve and how much the game pushes you into harder missions. Everyone on their A-Game? It's a mythical experience and this game fosters such moments so they aren't rare. [/list]  - ENEMIES - [list]     [*][POS] The enemies, to my amazement, don't follow AAA design logic. Rather than making them *always* a threat that *must* be dealt with *or else*, the enemies (and you) are glass cannons. Aim well, everything dies. Screw up, you die. It's all about the high skill ceiling.      [*][NEG] The enemies are black and purple in a dark cave. This makes lighting the cave basically irrelevant as it will not help in perceiving enemies efficiently. Though this isn't catastrophic because they have obvious / glowing weakpoints, which is a great design choice. [/list]  - MISC - [list]     [*] It appears the classes have unique voice lines and voice acting! DRG very much lacked this and it was huge missed potential.      [*] The characters very much have "DRG charm" for lack of a better label. The minigame you do at the ramrod when idle is exercise minigames of which the characters will groan about. The minigames seem to not have unique voice lines. [/list]

NOTE: Obviously, this is early access, so expect things to be kind of rough.  While I've been sold on the concept of a DRG-themed roguelite from the moment it was announced, it didn't quite click with me what Rogue Core's gameplay was actually going for, upon playing it for the first time. But I kept at it, unlocking features and learning the tools I had at my disposal until eventually I had a successful run that was so nail-bitingly down to the wire that my hands were shaking when it was over.  Needless to say, [i]I get it, now.[/i]   [h3]The Point[/h3] The thing about Rogue Core that really gets its hooks into you is its sense of urgency; squeezing everything you can out your limited time window, making risk-and-reward decisions about which optional events and resources you're willing to make a detour for on your way to the elevator while just barely beating the clock is the name of the game, here. How long do you want to hang around and sponge up vital upgrade minerals from an Expenite Detonator's explosion? Do you have time to go off the beaten path to find that workbench toolbox and improve your equipment loadout? Are you crazy enough to trigger the elevator enemy wave at the same time as the next scheduled wave on the timer and fight both at the same time [i]while[/i] juggling all the other things I just mentioned? Just how much are you willing to risk in the name of efficiency with the precious little time you have to strengthen yourself before the boss? This is what Rogue Core is about, and it's not unlike Risk of Rain 2, in this regard; although, I'd actually argue that Rogue Core's timer system is more engaging and provides more immediate feedback for your choices than the macro-level threat meter of RoR2. This is thanks to the timers being per-floor (dropping invincible pursuing worm enemies into your midst when they run out) and enemy waves occurring at predetermined, clearly communicated points on those timers (allowing for more interesting route planning on the players' part). It all comes together to make for a really engaging descent into Hoxxes IV.  This is why I feel that the many player requests to remove the timer from the game entirely are poorly considered; [b]the timer is kind of the whole point[/b]. Not every fan of the original DRG is going to be into this more urgent gameplay style, and that's okay; this is a separate game for a reason, and I applaud the devs for sticking to their guns, on this.  That said, for those looking not to rush [i]quite[/i] as much (or, inversely, to absolutely run like mad), a few timer [i]duration[/i] options with scaling rewards for your runs are confirmed to be arriving with the next update. I feel this is the best way to go; accommodating more preferences and playstyles without sacrificing the heart of the game design.  [h3]Issues[/h3] At the time of writing, the biggest issues I've run into are the somewhat clunky function key chord system for class abilities, a slight lack of variety in world generation and upgrade/equipment pools, some wonky balancing for things like ammunition allotment, a number of bugs (most notably that certain gamepad inputs for class abilities don't function [i]at all[/i] as some classes while working just fine on others; something I noticed while configuring Steam Input so I could play with gyro aiming on my DualSense and had to work around by replacing them with keyboard inputs), and some significant performance drops and stuttering when compared to the original DRG despite its comparable visuals (I suspect this to be due to the change to Unreal Engine 5, which is pretty notorious in gaming circles for this exact reason).  Pressing as they are, all of these issues are to be expected on some level, given the game's early access status; I knew what I was getting into, and I fully expect these wrinkles to be ironed out as time goes on.  [h3]Conclusion[/h3] In spite of its current growing pains, I think the future is really bright for Rogue Core. Once the [i]core[/i] experience (pardon the pun) is more polished and development reaches the point at which the folks at Ghost Ship can really start to go crazy with new content (as they have with the original DRG's seasonal additions), it's liable to become an absolute staple of the roguelite-shooter subgenre, spoken of in the same breath as the aforementioned Risk of Rain 2. It's fun, the iconic DRG weapon, equipment, and terrain systems mesh well with the new format and lend the game an identity of its own, and I can't wait to see how it improves throughout its lifetime.

At present, I am writing about the Pre-1.0 release of this game so consider that if reading in the future. But, for what's here, it feels like a real attempt at learning from the two other shooter games already under their belt while also navigating what made Deep Rock Galactic the success it is today.     DRG is about co-operation, team play and efficient action. Rogue Core bumps that up to overdrive by making a game all about doing these things better than you've ever done them before, or die. The new parts to help make the hoards of core spawn managable, is the addition of the Roguelike bits which make for an experience that's lengthy but enjoyable.    Before, you're abilities are locked in at the loadout menu and the variance comes from what you encounter in the mission. DRG is great but once you land on kit you are comfy with, it's hard not to feel like there's no point jumping to anything else. This works; why change? Rogue Core forces you to get better with a range of kit, though there isn't enough in the game yet to really stretch that out. Rogue Core introduces more weapons, a couple new tools but also kicks out some key kit.     Like the Grappling Hook, which I can only imagine is for balancing reasons. The Scout can cover so much ground in a short amount of time, it would make the hurried nature of a mission facile. Just zip from A to D, skipping B and C, slap the elevator awake and be done in 5 minutes. That said, the Flare Gun is still here except I'm not sure how helpful it is except for spotting particluar threats on the ceiling. The caves don't always get very big and nothing is hard to spot (especially Expenite, which glows bright green, unlike Nitra and other resources) so it's the only tool that feels like an outright dud at present.    The rest of the new kit is pretty neat. Mainly side arms and rifles, as many standard weapons that hit hard have now been designated a 'Heavy Weapon' that must be found. If you loved your Minigun like a child, you'll now need to find them in the caves and make do with either a accurate charging pistol or a plasma rifle that gives the plasma carbine competition. It's good to see the standard kit from DRG in here too, especially old reliable staples like the Deepcore and Zhukovs though it's fun to realise they're no longer class locked. Mixing and planning around what my character can do is consideration that's worth the time.    Indeed, the new characters and their powers are very cool through require a little muscle memory to use. I have it set that I have to toggle the power equipment and while it is an adjustment, it's quickly become like pulling out drills or the grappling hook to access my class' special powers. I can dash with Slicer to pick up things from awkard places, keep enemies under control with Guardian, have Falconer what my back and so on. For something in early access, all of the characters are pretty well designed to cover some aspect of the upgrade pool or handling the hoards of monsters coming after you in different ways. No one is fully support or aggro (except maybe Retcon) but they're all able to fill any roll required.     Most of this is down to the upgrades themselves being very potent once you get more than one under your belt. There's still a clear debate over whether to build and get upgrades early or wait until you get a 'Luck' bonus between missions but I feel I would prefer to have even common or uncommon upgrades ASAP to ensure I can even get to where I am going. Some builds can feel like they get broken or fun to play with really easily but that's necessary because the core spawn aren't messing around.     Honestly, it starts to feel a lot like DRG Themed 'Gunfire Reborn' but DRG builds on that by making you commit to a gun, making building around it much easier, and giving more upgrades more often. Lots of choices often, all for doing the things you do anyway. Every gain, especially big amounts, of Expenite will cost you time that's ticking down to a constant wave and horrid monsters that are looking to drag you away off the map. You need to be efficient and getting busted OP is a good way to beat the tide back. Though. skillful use of what you have been given can pay off too.     Still, each character leans into particular upgrades best. Spotter can gain 100% Critical Hit chance, so will want anything that procs of that more than anyone else. Falconer specialises in electric damage, Guardian can stun reiably, Slicer can lean phenominally well into melee which is surprisingly effective in this game if you get more than one stack of Vampire. Retcon is the odd one because they're effectively a retooled Chaser from Overwatch. Which is fun but has a higher skill floor than everyone else.     In fact, this game is just plain harder. I have many hours in DRG, maining Scout, and it's apparent that you will need every ounce and gram of skill from your time mining to handle reclaiming work. Compared to the glyphids, core spawn are faster, meaner and can hop around ensuring that they'll not fall for all the same tricks. Certainly not convincing a core spawn you're a friend and recruiting it into the team as a 'Steve'. They're here for blood.     Partly it's because you have only one gun and a traversal tool, that's it. But also because these things have to stand up to a roided up Reclaimer with a tricked out pistol that crits for thousands of damage. Even then, the core spawn feel like a threat at all times in ways that the glyphids could only manage at the very highest difficulties.     Even so, to make things easier, missions are less explore-y. Most expects a straight-shot to the finish, but there's still plenty to bounce around finding. All of it will help somehow, it just depends on how long you want to spend. That time limit feels strict. Especially with 4 players.     But the tone and feel of the game seems to reflect the heavier stakes. DRG is kinda jovial, a bit of a piss around and a doss at the bar before you load up and ship out. It's not funny, persay, but it's not taking itself particularly seriously either. You can paint yourself bright blue and dress up as Papa Smurf; DRG is supposed to be fun.    Rogue Core isn't entirely devoid of humour or that similar energy, but it's definately muted and wants to present itself as a more focused, almost-hardcore game incomparison. The Reclaimers are billed as an elite group with better tech than the mining crew we've gotten used to. They don't have Mission Control, they have a slightly egotistic AI called Omega running things with an eye on efficiency. The Ramrod is grayer and, currently, not dressed quite as comforting as the Spacerig. Even the Hall of Heroes and the promotion system gets it's own unique animation, lending pomp and circumstance to the act of making your character icon a little fancier. It all serves to push the idea that you are going to have to give more than you have done before to succeed here.     This reverence makes Rogue Core feel a little more serious, as this is the next part of this world and the fictional company, we're getting to see. It characterises the Reclaimers as being more elite very effectively compared to the more lackadaisical Mining crew of DRG. I even like the detail that while the mining crew is perfectly fine-ish to go to work blind drunk, the Reclaimers have to earn their drinks by working out and staying in shape, turning their calories burnt into a currency. Its a small change but speaks volumes.    At present, I am enjoying this game. I am looking forward to what else they add. I think more ways to move around and alter terrain would be excellent to see before any new guns or dwarves. Though very exciting to think that we might get new (or old) classes in the game.     Also, a fantastic feminist move of not gender locking anything. Ladybeards for all! As it should be!

zone, the zone being so miniscule that you could only fit a bough wasp in between all 4 players, and that one guy who is desperate for mid swarm upgrades to get health back because there isnt any red As someone who started out loving roguelike/lites because of DRG: Survivor, I love the hell out of this game.  Deep Rock Galactic is one of my all-time favorite games, and with 1500+ hours in the original game, this game has a bit of a learning curve that you'll have to adapt to, compared to what you might be used to in the original game. Things are much faster paced, and you don't exactly have a lot of time to dawdle or dilly-dally, so time is of the essence.   One of the first things you may notice as you complete the tutorial is that whenever you fall from a relatively high point, instead of continuing to run forward, your dwarf will stagger for a brief moment, before moving at normal speed again. This can be circumvented with a couple of methods, such as platforms or certain upgrades.  If you get greedy and let the threat timer reach maximum, you're gonna have a rough time, unless you're just [i] that [/i] guy, or have an insanely overpowered loadout.  The biggest thing you have to watch out for if you do end up reaching maximum threat level, are the [spoiler] Reaperworms [/spoiler].  Spoiler: [spoiler] If you let one grab you, it will gradually drain your hp and draw you back into its burrow. If you are in a group and it successfully reaps you, you're gone until the next level. You'll keep any weapons and expenite you may have had on you, but your team will have to release you from an alien cocoon thing and revive you upon exiting the elevator on the next level. If you're playing solo and Cooper does not save you, or, your whole team including yourself get grabbed by Reaperworms, that's it. Your run is dead. [/spoiler]  Speaking as someone very familiar with Deep Rock Galactic, you'll find that the difficulty curve ramps up quite quickly in Rogue Core compared to the original game. Your depth level (1-4) essentially corresponds to what your Hazard level would be. Complexity plays a big role in how difficult a facility (mission) can be, a lot more so in Rogue Core than I consider to be the case in DRG.   Each facility is essentially just its own Deep Dive, your initial weapons and cave layout remain the same upon multiple runs of the same facility, though you usually will not be able to play the same facility more than once, unless if you speedrun it maybe. As opposed to having 3 set levels per Deep Dive, each facility in Rogue Core will have 4-6 levels, and at the very last level, when you reach the very end of the cave system you will be faced with 1 of 3 bosses.    Some of this is likely subject to change, considering the game is in early access at the time of writing.  As of right now, many changes are coming to the game, along with difficulty options for the threat timer.   I do believe this game has a lot of places and spots it needs to improve in, but currently as it is, it's still a very fun game, I can definitely see myself switching between the two games occasionally, and wholeheartedly recommend this game if you like Deep Rock Galactic.

I did not play DRG all that much, it wasn't really my type of game. I should also mention that the above literally doesn't matter, because this isn't DRG, it's just apart of the same world. As of the time of this review, I played one game with a single friend, and a second one solo. Both were wins.  Reading through other reviews, I don't understand why people say this game is too hard. Even lore-wise it makes sense that this game is harder than DRG. You play as a Reclaimer. And these guys are supposed to be the bad-a**es that that clear out as many enemies as they can, while also getting the lost facilities up and running. These are the same areas in DRG that are grayed out, that are apparently overrun with baddies, and so you can't mine there.  The whole point of this game is to run through the deep facilities and do the main objectives (repairing the facilities or decimating a strong baddie, side objectives too if you've got the time. Go at a decent pace, don't be too slow, and you'll have no issue getting done in time. It's meant for some decently serious co-op play too, since the rogue-lite mechanics are shared between everyone.  If you don't have a group of people you can talk to in a vc to play with, I DO NOT recommend this game for you. Playing with randoms on public is not the way to go. You can't communicate well enough or fast enough to pick out what the upgrades you'd like. Not to mention, even if there was an in-game vc (there might be I didn't check) you can't expect everyone to use it.  Overall, if you want mildly difficult, relatively fast-paced gameplay, this is it.

Let me preface this review by saying: I think the foundation of this game is extremely solid, I just think that it needs work. I am also far from an expert on roguelikes/lites, but I do enjoy the genre through Vermintide 2 Chaos Wastes and Risk of Rain 2, and, despite it's countless flaws, Rogue Core.   This is one of those games where I kinda wish there was a middle option in the Steam reviews, because I don't know whether or not I can in good faith set it on either recommended or not recommended. Because I do personally enjoy the game in it's current state, I am setting the review as positive.   On with the yap:  I think by far the thing that requires work is the upgrades. In general. From the Expenite upgrades, to the Bio-boosters (which can be somewhat circumvented by using another classes' deck), to the Enhancements you get from your build that you can play with before a mission, to the Artifact upgrades. I think that all of these need some work, the best way I could put it is the pool is diluted with stuff that you don't really want and that doesn't really justify it's value (seriously? a legendary upgrade that is only as good as an Armor Boost you get from before you even start the mission?). Personally I think this is the worst part of the upgrade system, that there's numerous talents that do the same thing but in different tiers and the percentage is a bit higher. I would make it in a sort of RoR2 way, where the common upgrade should just be the percentage go up increase and then the following tiers should do something interesting (think Lens-Maker Goggles + Harvester's Sight/Predatory Instincts/Laser Scope in RoR2). Say for example you'd have Better Red Sugar in the common tier, still the same effect (or maybe slightly higher to offset the other effects no longer being the same percentage increase) and then in the following tiers you have an upgrade that somehow interacts with the Red Sugar. Maybe you picking it up heals everyone, maybe reviving a teammate drops red sugar/heals both of you. Anything could be better than just "same thing but bigger number and it has a red icon now!".  Both Vermintide and RoR2 upgrades/items/boons (whatever name applies), even the most common ones are able to really help out with your build, especially if you then get a legendary that pairs well. A lot of the builds in RC feel more like I am going through a check list: -Do I have a way to generate health/armor? -Do I have a way of getting more damage? -Do I have a way of CC? -And some class specific ones like CDR and such And sure, there's still combos with weapons, there's still elemental synergies like in DRG, but it doesn't feel like that has as much value. It feels like every class kind of goes for a similar route when making their build, when that couldn't be further from the truth in, say, Chaos Wastes in Vermintide 2.  In Vermintide 2, you for example have quite common bomb talents (I see them pretty much every run) that make the AoE of your thrown bombs cause an AoE that can give extremely high damage, crit or CDR depending on which of these you have. This is of course, extremely situational for many classes that do not have a way of easily sustaining bombs, but you could be playing a career like Ranger Veteran or Outcast Engineer that have ways of doing so (with the former, it being the ult modifier Parting Gift, that gives you a free use of your bomb every time you use the career skill, with the latter, it is the talent Ingenious Ordnance that gives Bardin a new bomb every 80 seconds). Of course, this doesn't make these talents good necessarily, most classes still find no synergy with it, but some do have said synergy.   This sort of feeds into another problem - all of the classes feel really samey to play. I know that this is pretty much a consequence of the classes not having a kit apart from their abilities that's persistent, but if this is a game where there are different classes, there should be more to differentiate them. Pretty much all of the classes have: -An ability that restores resources (think Guardian's "shield" or Spotter's ammo crate) -An ability that deals damage (think Slicer's slice or Guardian's gauntlet blast) -An ability that increases damage dealt (think Retcon's rage or Spotter's darts) And in this all of them feel like they arrive at the same place, just through slightly different means (best way I can put it as a public transport nerd is Spotter takes the bus while Retcon takes the tram), though I must say some feel a lot better than others - Slicer and Spotter, for example, being really fun classes to play, while Retcon and Guardian for me just seem a bit of a nothing burger for the former (as in the abilities not changing her gameplan/build too much) and too clunky to get value of for the latter (the CDs should just be separate and not one cooldown).   Small gripes/acknowledgements: - The new weapons seem rather weak to their DRG counterparts, same with certain abilities (e.g. Guardian's kit compared to Gunner's). The Lark pistol feels like a worse Subata, despite it being much bigger (I would love a Deagle build and kill Corespawn, Max Payne style...), for example - I don't like how the female dwarves have missing voice lines for certain scenarios, I hope that this is just a time constraint/scheduling conflict ordeal and that I will be allowed to cough and groan frustratingly on the Ramrod as Retcon/Falconer. I mention this because I do actually really enjoy the female dwarves, I think the VA actually nailed the voice, so much so I initially thought that it's a VA from Darktide, which I think has some of the best voice work. That is a pretty high compliment that I am happy I am able to give to the new voice.  Overall, I think that Rogue Core is not as fun as it could be, especially with how much it was teased and worked on, what with S5 in DRG being a prelude. That was almost 2 years ago. I don't know much about game development, but a part of why I wrote this review in the first place is that I have faith that things can actually get better.  If you're deciding on whether or not you want to pick the game up, I'm personally not sure, as I've said in the beginning - I would put the game sort of between recommended and not recommended, but I lean towards that I do recommend it, I think that despite the flaws, this is still a fun game to experiment with, and if GSG's recent post is to be believed, let's hope it keeps getting better, fingers crossed.

The best elements of DRG and DRG: Survivor rolled into a great 4 player co-op roguelike first person shooter.     The setting of exploring existing mines means that the levels are far more navigable than in DRG. The tools to navigate terrain no longer being a class focus but a utility choice redirects the focus to the class abilities and passives that make the combat synergies fun to pursue.     The weapon choices and upgrades mean that no run is the same. While that means some runs will be more 'fun' than others, it also keeps you on your toes and incentivizes experimentation. Achieving goals that unlock points to spend means you get to shore up the weaknesses and pain points so that you can play how you want.    The level difficulty seems to be on the higher end compared to DRG. While you still get rewards for a failed run, I would personally appreciate a slightly lower difficulty curve at the start.     You'll want to be in voice chat for all of the reasons you would in DRG, but also to communicate choices offered for weapons/upgrades/etc. This has resulted in better team play for my group of friends, and each dive offering so many levels of variety has the foundations for keeping the game something we'll continue to come back to for a long time to come.

Gem alert!!!!  The combat in this game is fun. The roguelike aspect is pretty good too.  However it isn't without flaws: -Balance is kinda lacking. The enemies drop extremely slowly if you are not hitting weakspots, and on top of that if you aren't hitting weakspots you are hitting their armor which makes you deal even less damage. In regards to bosses I've seen only 2 of them, and Ramok Gatekeeper is really unfair with its charge attack being almost impossible to dodge. -Some weapon upgrades/stats are useless or straight downgrades, for example homing missles on the plassma rifle makes it worse as the projectiles will target the center of whatever enemy they are closest to rather then its weakpoint, so the "upgrade" that supposedly should make it easier to aim makes it actualy harder as you need to shoot in such a way that the projectiles will arc towards the weakpoint. -The time limit even on the second difficulty (complexity?) is too low, it may have something to do with me playing in a 2 man team but the timer usualy was barely enough for getting the main upgrades assuming we didnt ♥♥♥♥ up, and was never enough for optional ones.  But overall the game is still fun and worth playing.

Giving this game the positive review it deserves for being an incredibly creative & entertaining twist on an already fantastic game. The core gameplay is relatively the same as Deep Rock Galactic; you explore caves, mine minerals, kill bugs, and rock & stone! If you like DRG, I have a hard time believing you won't have a good time with this one too. The new mechanics in this game are what I believe makes it stand out from DRG. The biggest difference being that your loadout is built during the mission, similar to Deep Rock Galactic: Survivor. This new mechanic on its own brings a whole new approach to gameplay from the original game that my friends & I all enjoy a lot. It also greatly lowers the barrier of entry for new or infrequent players, with most of your character's abilities being upgradeable within the mission. I think some of the complaints this game has been getting around launch, even while greatly exaggerated, still legitimately shows us players, and the devs, where the biggest holes in the ship are. Luckily, the devs have already started taking action to make this game even better than it already is. With that being said, the timer, negotiation system, & ammo economy are all mostly fine how they are currently implemented. I love seeing the devs taking swift action to address all this negative feedback, but even if they didn't, the way these mechanics work currently still add up to a really fun game with a ton of replay value!

EDIT: Accidentially left my laptop on. Don't mind the playtime total >_>  [h1] Deep Rock Galactic for people without patience [/h1]  DRG: Rogue Core (currently) trades all the formulaic mission map structures of the original [u]Deep Rock Galactic[/u], its wholesome mining trudgery and completion of objectives for... Survival. Indeed; [b]DRG: Rogue Core (DRG: RC)[/b] is instead of mostly wholesome (but still difficult) journeys through atmospheric mines? [i]Wholefully[/i] hellish in underground realms beset by cosmic horrors, wholefully uncompromising in its focus on teamwork, selective action and quite frankly? Acting as a testmanent to human greed.   If you have played other popular titles within the co-op shooter genre, this game would be aptly be described as a mix of [u]Deep Rock Galactic's[/u] focus on assymetric teamwork, the unforgiving difficulty curves of [u]GTFO[/u] (at least, until the gameplay clicks and/or you moreover decide to communicate with the team [i]before[/i] ♥♥♥♥ hits the fan; god forbid you engage in direct social activity in a team-focused game), many of the addictive roguelike elements in [u]Risk of Rain 2[/u] and arguably; being more reminiscent of the quite satisfying movement+gunplay of [u]HELLDIVERS 2[/u].  [h3] Less trudgery, more interaction. ...Wait, what was that about "No Patience" again? [/h3]   What I think might be this game's most controversial, yet genuine and genial simplistic mechanic, is the upgrade [i]"Negotiation"[/i] system. The game has several different roguelike "upgrade" systems where your team's inidividuals becomes stronger as a run progresses, however, the most fundamental *level up* and weapon acquisiton systems asks players to communicate with eachother: Is this particular upgrade/powerup/weapon best for me, my class and my current build; or theirs? Players can mark options they think they work best with (several also massively benefit the team/other teammates when applied right).  When a team clicks, it *clicks*. When it doesn't click, and players just pick the shiniest possible option with haha funny high numbers? There is bound to be some annoyance, yes. However, it's a social game like any other. Adapt or find people you synergize better with.  [h3] Gameplay opinions and insights [/h3]  Very, very fun and rewarding. Gunplay is snappy and precise, enemies are numerous, often agile and can suprisingly often attack in formations that prove difficult for a single dwarf to comfortably handle without a plan or consumable grenades in mind. Every side-objective and lootable is optional, every one of them presents risks and time expenditures that may very well spell your doom early... or slowly but surely be the cause of a VERY quick and sudden death later on. The objectives themselves also welcome teamwork to hasten their completion by forming supply lines, delegation of tasks to ensure the machines/things you are engaging with progress as smoothly as possible etc. etc. Time is very much of the essence. Because?  As mentioned, this game may very well act as a testament to human greed (comically apt, considering its narrative themes). Once you pass the intial 'setup' phase of the game, you will always be on a timer. A timer that not only shows when pre-set hordes of enemies (besides small groups and hordes spawned by doing objectives/events e.g. for loot) will spawn, but once it expires? Yeah. That horde? Is now infinite. The horde itself? Gets worse and worse. Worst of all?  The very earth you walk under? Is infested with invulnerable, instenstine-like worms that can and will take every chance to swallow you whole and ♥♥♥♥ you into next [strike] week [/strike] sector; SHOULD your team be lucky enough to survive and escape the current one with but a single dwarf still standing. And? Of course, on the next level, you or your teammates will have to deal with the weight of the team's failures by fighting large masses of enemies while simply trying to get those earlier defeated/captured dwarves back into the game again. Punishing but also hype as hell at times when your group manages to soldier on and recover the time and resources lost, rushing the sector to finally find stability in the next one.  I have only played 3 out of 5 difficulty settings so far, but the jump between each one is quite noticeable. The maps and hazards themselves also do get worse as you delver deeper into Hoxxes to 'rightfully' reclaim  the mines which your jolly miners of *Deep Rock Galactic* once put their lives and joys into and wish to return to.  The game released barely a week ago, but it has a quite meaty progression system, solid variety in map generation and roguelike stuff. Especialy noticeable if you don't just mostly play a single class like I do; and even then do I personally discover interesting combos from the currently existing options after a solid amount of hours. Can only go up from here, I think.   Ghost Ship Games, the company behind the **DRG** series, appear to be a passionate lot through and through. We shall see what comes next, and they have already adressed some of the issues the community seem to be experiencing.  ...  Human skill issues, in my not-so-humble opinion :^) A more comfortable jump between 1st and 2nd difficulty may perhaps be welcome for those who lack the patience to learn a game proper however, though. Which seems to be part of the plans. Oh well.  Rock and Stone. See you in the ravaged facilities of interstellar anarchy.

Even on the launch patch, it's a lot of fun, though there are a few rougher edges. All of said rougher edges, however, are being addressed in the first patch, which is releasing to the experimental branch three weeks after the game launched. That said, it's not the same game as DRG; the tension is a lot higher, you've got a lot to do and pretty frequently not enough time to do all of it, and as is customary with roguelikes you'll be slapping a build together with whatever you can get your hands on. You will need to get familiar with handling a wide variety of weapons and a wide variety of options, on top of juggling your class-specific set of 3 abilities, hacking into terminals, and throwing yourself headfirst into two simultaneous swarms in a harebrained attempt to grab just one more event before you run out of time and get jumped by invulnerable worms.     In short, if you like DRG as a 'chill time hanging out with friends doing whatever, mopping through hordes of glyphids with your well-oiled machine of a build you've perfected over the last 50 hours' experience, Rogue Core might not be for you.   On the other hand, if you're a maniac like me who played about 20 hours of DRG before deciding 'nah, this is too easy' and promptly installed Mission Content Revise to clear 100 Aquarq Point Extraction on Hazard 5 solo and/or you just think roguelike mechanics are cool and/or you think DRG was nice but a little samey (and maybe a bit heavy on the 'hold E on these objects'), Rogue Core may just be the game for you.

Rogue Core takes some pacing cues from Hazard 5+ in the original game, and wraps that into a roguelike with pressure on multiple layers. This sets a pretty strong foundation for the game to evolve out of Early Access, though it might be a temperature shock if you are used to taking your time in missions to grab everything.    Short-term pressure comes in combat and quick resource extraction. This is very similar to base DRG, but it replaces the shield stat with an armor stat. Armor absorbs half the damage while it's active, and drains slowly rather than being removed all at once. That mitigates bursts of incoming damage, which is extra nice for classes like Retcon that lean on taking hits as part of their kit. The tradeoff is automatic replenishment: you top armor back up from fixed resources on the map or through upgrades. The result pushes you forward to grab more resources rather than hanging back and waiting for a shield recharge.    Medium-term pressure comes in the timer. Time-based waves from DRG are ported over and labeled explicitly on a roughly ten-minute timer, after which the level gets very nasty. This gives you an incentive to push through, weigh optional objectives against upcoming waves, and get out fast. The upcoming update will let you tune this both up and down, which I think is the right call.    Long-term pressure comes in the roguelike elements, such as upgrades, bio boosters, and toolboxes. Choices made here can have a lot of impact on how you clear out areas and how quickly you do so.    The upgrades are surprisingly well-tuned once you've put in some time. A few look completely nonsensical for a resource-constrained roguelike at first glance, such as exploding when you take damage. Play a little more and you realize it works really well on a class like Retcon, which deliberately takes damage during timeshift to build up Berserk. Play even more and you find it can save you from Cave Leeches, which have become an even bigger threat to dwarves rushing through a cavern on a strict schedule.    Weapon-tuning is mostly fine, though there are some clear outliers in either ammo efficiency or boss effectiveness. I'm not a fan of how some of the modifiers (Homing Bullets on Drak-25, Auto Aim on M1000, Return To Sender on Breach Cutter) can easily end up bricking the weapon. Thankfully, you're never required to take a weapon that comes with those, or pick the 'upgrade' when it's offered.     Classes, outside of Guardian, feel really powerful with their abilities. The control scheme for abilities is clunky and takes some getting used to, so I hope future updates give more options in that area. I've looked through a lot of the negative reviews and I have to say I agree with them. The game is too punishing, builds feel somewhat repetitive, synergies are... odd in most places, etc. However, I believe those things aren't what make a game great, especially when it's in early access. Game feel is much harder to get right in development, and I think that's where this game will shine.  Playing things like WoW or Warframe, you find that the game isn't fun because of the grind. Those games have fantastic gameplay and you keep going because the mechanics are satisfying. Or at least for me. If you remember OG DRG, you didn't get overclocks for some time on a fresh account. You get them after playing the game and grinding and leveling, which can feel awful for the material gathering. The pacing makes it hard to ever get a good bank if you're only logging in doing the weekly or specifically mining for mats you need. But the gameplay was solid, and it lead to finding more and more content to enjoy on top of an amazing foundation.   Rogue Core has a great skeleton. At the time of writing this, there's a patch in the works that, in my mind, addresses most of the concerns on the timer, balancing, etc.  Personally I'm addicted to good combat, IRL and in games, so this game scratches that itch.   I didn't grind dungeons in WoW because the dungeon kept offering new and exciting things, nor did the idea of playing something that wasn't fun to get to more exciting content drive me. The fun was in the core mechanics and I just did what I needed to get to mythic raiding. I didn't grind void relics in Warframe because spending 15 minutes to pop a gatcha mechanic was fun, but because playing my frames was satisfying.   I don't believe people are wrong in their criticism, and I don't believe they should change their minds on what they look for in a game. But personally, what I look for in a game exists in Rogue Core: great mechanics, good direction from the devs, and a very high ceiling for what's possible. If this is the worst the game will ever be, I think it's going to be amazing. (Personally haven't seen any glitches, but I also haven't seen two hippos going at it. Doesn't mean it isn't happening.)

This game is great but i see a lot of people complain about its most important mechanics.     The timer: The timer in rogue core is the threat that makes you go deeper into the levels. when the timer runs out an endless swarms spawns, and these cute little wormies come out to pull you down to their nests and lay their eggs in you. If rogue core didn't have the timer there would be no reason to move to the next level unless you have already done everything in the cave. Therefore making the game really easy.    The negotiations system: When dwarves collect enough expenite and deposite into the R.E.P.D they will get a upgrade. all dwarves must be close to the R.E.P.D to get the upgrade. Then you and your team mates must negotiate on what upgrade you should take. each person can only take 1 item per upgrade and everyone takes from the same pool of items. if someone takes an item, nobody else can get that item. The game has a feature that lets you heart a item you want to let the other dwarves know that you want this item. Also everyone can see everyones mouse cursors. personaly i think this is a great system it encourages teamwork and makes you communicate with your team, enhancing the coop experience. a lot of the people saying that this is a bad mechanic and should be removed or reworked often say that they "Imagine" that it would be a horrible experience because you would never get what you want. in my experience i have NEVER ran into a situation with randoms (also most of my playtime has been in public lobbies.) where people fight over what item they want. because people who play this game a usaly really nice. also i forgot to mention but you also take turns to pick items and the order of who goes first cycles so if you picked last on the previous upgrade you now pick first.    now i will say some of my legitamet complaints:    Right now there is not alot of enemy variety so you will be usaly fighting the same guys on most missions.    There is not much content to play through, i basicaly did everything there was to do in 20 hours. (a result of it being in early development.)    All of these problems are activly being worked on by the devs and i am really hoping they don't drop the ball on this game because i can allready see that this could be a amazing game when it is finished.    Final thing is that this game is NOT deep rock galactic 2. If you like deep rock galactic there is no garentee you will like this game. the are very differnet and thats a good thing.

After playing this for some time I like the overall foundation that this game has built up so far and I am quite looking forward to seeing where the devs bring it in the future. In its current state there are many aspects of the game that definitely need improvement but the overall package so far is still very enjoyable. The combat overall feels just as good as the original Deep Rock while being additionally more difficult to fit this games faster pace. The new weapons are mostly very satisfying to use as well and feel just as punchy as the returning weapons. Some of the new ones could use a few buffs and tweaks but that will probably come with time. I also actually do like the timer system and the overall faster pace that this game provides. This system requires on the fly thinking that requires you and your team to determine what objectives in a cave are worth risking and benefiting from or skipping and playing it safe which I find quite engaging. This system does have its problems in public lobbies though as the average player base in my experience hasn't realized that gameplay loop in the original DRG of exploring every inch of a cave simply isn't applicable in this game. However, when with friends which I have spent most of my playtime with, this system overall works very well with proper communication. Overall, Rogue Core definitely has the potential to be just as good as the original DRG and I believe the devs are capable of bringing something truly great to the table.   There are some issues I do have with the game though that I would like to give my feedback on. At this point I'm sure everyone has mentioned this but the upgrades themselves definitely need to be more interesting. A decent chunk of the upgrades I never take because they simply don't seem very impactful. I really hope more interesting upgrades that significantly effect your stats similar to how overclocks impacted a build in the original get added. Another issue I personally have is with selecting missions themselves in the ship. Spinning the globe around to find a mission I want to play in a specific biome is honestly very annoying for me. I genuinely find myself spending more time trying to find the specific biome I want to play in then choosing the mission itself. To fix this, all they need to do is add a button somewhere on the side of the screen which lists each biome and clicking this button rotates the globe automatically to that biome. On the topic of biomes, the cave generation in each biome needs to be improved. The biome you chose in the original DRG significantly impacted the way you would have to tackle a mission with each biomes unique generation, terrain, and hazards. In Rogue Core the biome you choose ultimately doesn't matter from my experience. Regardless of the biome you choose, the layout and size of each cave feels basically the same with a couple minor tweaks that ultimately don't impact anything. On top of this the caves in general just feel kind of empty. None of the random passive enemies from DRG are present, on top of only one mineral type being minable leads to a somewhat repetitive, uninteresting experience during the downtime between swarms when roaming the cave. The last major critique I have has to do with the Ramok Gatekeeper boss as both me and my friend I have been playing with don't like this fight. The extremely disruptive tornado attack while funny at first has gotten very annoying due to the fact it doesn't telegraph it at all. Imagine if the massive slam attacks the dreadnaughts do wasn't telegraphed at all and unfairly did a ton of damage; that's what the tornado feels like. Additionally, its charge attack is frustratingly quick and hard to avoid, as well as being able to one shot you after a revive regardless if you have armor from a resupply or not. This fight is a let down for me as I really like the other two gatekeepers but this one needs adjustments in its current state. Also one last quick thing, make the female dwarves a togglable option on all classes instead of being class specific. This feature being class specific instead of a basic option the player can choose is simply dumb.  My overall final thoughts lead me to giving this game a decently solid 7/10. I am looking forward to this games future and I hope my feedback helps the devs. Rock and Stone!

Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core is a rogue-lite standalone version of the 'Deep Dives' from the original Deep Rock Galactic by Ghost Ship Games. As someone who thoroughly enjoyed 'Deep Dives' from the series' first installment I am absolutely taken by what I have seen so far.   I confess I am not the biggest fan of the high tech vibe of the Reclaimer's overall aesthetic, however I believe it'll grow on me in time and I appreciate the expansion on the wider canon of Deep Rock Galactic.   Critique of some of the mechanics is well deserved and I agree with the sentiment that at this current moment the game is not beginner friendly. Some of the mechanics are fairly unforgiving, much like in 'Deep Dives' and there are times where it truly feels like playing Hazard IV on a brand new save.   While I enjoy it and can confidently recommend it to people who enjoy a challenge, all the more so anyone who doesn't mind dying the first several rounds they're playing, I can understand if you'd like to hold off until at least the upcoming patch on June 17th 2026.   With Ghost Ship Games' past record as a development team, I am confident the game will an all around enjoyable experience for any fan of the series in the near-future and I am happy, despite pining for it to be released, that they delayed it for as long as they did.  At the moment, I believe it's best enjoyed in bursts to keep frustrations low, especially in cases where your runs end like in my last one: my duo partner downed and no way to revive him as Falconer (my preferred class) with my long distance revive ability on account of him still being stuck in a Corespawn cocoon only to get snatched by a cave leech myself that I unfortunately missed due to the wave of enemies about to descend on me.  In short: I recommend it to people who enjoy 'Deep Dives' in the original DRG and those who don't mind trying, over and over again, until it all finally goes as planned.

I'd give a thumbs sideways if I could, but seeing that it's early access, the current flaws are forgivable given that the devs address them. And it looks like they will. There are some pretty good bones to this game already, and I appreciate the change of pace from DRG. Having put in around 300 hours into DRG, I've had my fill of the game, but Rogue Core offers something new that I've been quite enjoying. While DRG for the most part is rather chill, having my builds fine tuned and optimized, RC provides a fun challenge in the form of adapting to what the game gives you. Working from having basically scraps to being a mean corespawn killing machine is satisfying. Depth 1 seems to be around Hazard 3ish, so from the start RC is meant for those with a bit of DRG experience. I still haven't cleared Depth 4, but with some de-rusting of my DRG skills and some good luck, I feel like it's still manageable. A roguelite isn't supposed to be something you clear every time, it's a challenge, which I feel like some disagree with.   Though I can say that meta progression is a bit lacking compared to DRG, most of the upgrades being stat boosts. I'd like to see more things like bio-booster deck swapping, upgrades that shake up how you build yourself during a run, or maybe meta progression that allows you to alter the weapon pool once more weapons are in the game.  Most of my gameplay is solo, so I can't really comment on multiplayer until I rally some friends to play with.  I can't wait to see what is cooked up in the future. Rock and stone, Reclaimers!

40 hours in, and I am absolutely loving DRG: RC. I've played 500 hours of DRG, but this was a change of pace for me. Initially I was frustrated by the difficulty and the sheer confusion of the game, but around 10hrs in I figured out how to play the game.   My tips from a noob for other noobs: In a nutshell, it's a race to the elevator, and if you lose the race to the timer, things get hairy. Ammo management is going to be something to keep in mind while you're racing to the elevator. Most upgrades also stack, so keep that in mind as well. Use your minimap to orient yourself and follow the cable on the ground to find your way to the elevator. Complete as many events and as much mining as you can, but don't let the timer get past 3/4 (9:00 position) or else the elevator charge up will be very stressful. This means you may have to abandon some upgrades or minerals or events, in order to survive to the next level. Work quickly, in any case.   Above all, rock and stone and never leave a dwarf behind (but actually you might have to, you'll just have to revive them on the next level)

This game is great! Been having a ton of fun and if this is the start (of early access) then the future is bright.  I know there are a fair amount of complaints about some aspects of the game, but I disagree with most of them. I think some of this stems from an understandable, but incorrect, expectation of this to be DRG 2.0. I haven't seen any complaints that, even if valid, would make this a non-recommend.   "I don't like having to work with my teammates to pick upgrades." I guess if you're playing with randos then they might take the thing you wanted, but I've yet to come across a time where there weren't good options. Maybe you don't get the BEST option. Oh well. Welcome to roguelite games and their innate RNG aspect. You have to make the most of what you're give, and I have seen that we're always given cool options.  "The vibe is off, needs to be sillier." While it is slightly more serious, I do not understand this at all. There's an angry roomba, various "rock and stone" voice lines, workout station, funny beers, Omega is great, and more. It's a good mix of serious stuff with some goofiness to boot. I felt like DRG had the same mix of vibes as well.   "Not enough exploration" / "Timer is too short." While I can see an argument for making sure the timer is balanced, please, this is a roguelite, not DRG. It's designed to put the pressure on and for you to have the decision space and options to make calculated risks. If you wanted tons more time to explore everything and take it easy, then play DRG. I'm not saying the game needs to be hardcore, but roguelites generally are exciting because of the tempo and the need to make quick choices based on time and what is available to do. All that being said, it never hurts to have some toggles for difficulty, timers, etc., for those who do want the casual experience. I just think that knocking this game for this is odd.  "Gameplay/balance/upgrades/enemies are not great." This is early access. While I'm not saying you can't complain about some of the aforementioned items, I cannot fathom why you wouldn't recommend the game just because they're still working out balance and getting some aspects just right. It's not so off-balance that the game isn't fun or doesn't function. The foundation here is excellent. The gunplay is great, movement feels good, getting lots of upgrades over the course of a mission is neat progression, working together feels meaningful and important, it has charm, the enhancements are nice bumps, very few bugs, the classes are all SUPER unique from one another and feel special to use, and the gameplay loop generates excitement while making each run feel just fresh enough.  All in all, this isn't DRG. It's a roguelite. It has the same vibes as DRG but has a different focus. This game is excellent and has space to grow, but that doesn't mean it isn't fun or recommendable in its current state. Been having a blast with it among friends, which is the ideal way to play. But I don't see why solo or with randos wouldn't also be fun, albeit different. Stop making things "best game ever" or "worst game ever" based on some things we do/don't care for. Everything has imperfections and I'm boggled at the non-recommends. Way more pros than cons, and those cons are being worked on by the devs. Easy recommend. Buy it.

DRG: Rogue Core so far is extremely fun to play both Solo and with Friends. A couple things that I personally would love to see, although I doubt my opinion really matters -   1: Myself, and many others it seems, would love for there to be different minerals to mine and spend on more upgrades/different currency system. Mining one mineral gets boring. While we ARE Reclaimers, we're also still Dwarves. Dwarves never turn down Gold or other precious resources... You could potentially implement a weapons upgrade system using gold and other minerals to guarantee specific weapon appearances, attributes, etc. should you get them from a negotiation. Heck, even throw in different armor buffs. Maybe overshields even.  2: I really wish the upgrade tree and menu for the upgrade selections would be more organized. When you go to equip the newest upgrade you got from the tree, you might spend a good minute or two looking for it in the jumbled mess of tier I, II, III, and IV variants of them.  3: Working out on the RamRod should yield buffs depending on how well you do before each mission. And where are the beer buffs?!  4: Don't know if it's coming or not, but I miss seeing chunks of armor flying off of enemies as they are damaged. Armored Glyphids in the original DRG games would lose their armor, and take increased damage. While enemies can have their armor "Broken", I'd like to see a VISUAL REPRESENTATION of that. Unless I'm blind and missed it entirely. In that case, please ignore this...  All-in-all though, I'd still recommend getting this game. Still a blast to play. It just has pretty big boots to fill following the original DRG.

This is a good game, and here's why:    This is not DRG, and that's a good thing. It isn't a copy of DRG with a few small tweaks, it's a completely different experience. So liking DRG doesn't automatically mean you'll like this. It's a roguelike, and if you hate the genre, this game probably isn't for you. But I love roguelikes, and this is one of the best FPS roguelikes I've played.    Be warned: the game is hard. You have to be okay with failing a lot, especially in the beginning. But what makes it click is the buildcrafting, and a roguelike stands and falls with exactly that. Rough Core absolutely nails it. You craft your entire build during the run, starting with nothing but a few flat stat boosts, not even weapons. Most roguelikes give you only one or two sources from which all your upgrades come, mostly because you already chose your upgrades and weapons before the run. Rough Core, on the other hand, has six different sources, each with its own distinct upgrade pool that affects a different aspect of your character. Every stage you're guaranteed three of them: leveling up, the Bio Booster, and the Workbench. The other three are optional. Between levels, you choose from the Heavy Weapon Crate, the Equipment Crate, and the Artifacts for your next stage. It's a fantastic concept that makes replayability insanely fun, every run feels completely unique, and it will only get better as they add more content.    Speaking of which, they just revealed the first update, and they've addressed a lot of player feedback. Ghost Ship Games listens to its community like no other developer. You can wait for the full release, and that's perfectly fine, but if you jump in now, you can help shape this game into another gem like DRG. I already love it, and I can't wait to see the rest of the content.    Rock and Stone!

I wanted to wait until I had 50+ hours in this game until I gave my opinion on this game and let me tell you I absolutely adore this game. From what I've seen on social media, the game has been getting mixed reviews for various reasons. I agreed with many of the reasons when I started, but as I've kept playing I've realized how the systems worked, gotten better at the game, and appreciated the game and how it works way more. At this point this game has become one of my favorite games right up there with Deep Rock Galactic.  The game's movement feels just as good as the first Deep Rock Galactic. Running, jumping, shooting, all feel just as satisfying as it was in that game. The missions are very fun to run through and the gameplay loop is very addictive. A bunch of your favorite weapons and equipment from DRG return and there are a lot of fun new ones to find and try out. The enemies feel threatening but very fun to fight. But that's all expected for a Deep Rock Galactic game. What's new?  First is the most important part of a roguelike game, the upgrades. At first they came off as a little simple, but I realized they're often times designed in a way to synergize with other upgrades really well and create builds you wouldn't normally try. Certain epic and rare upgrades can change what you focus on entirely. For example, I had a run where I was offered upgrades that synergized with ice elemental damage. I didn't have any ice damage, so I ignored them. Partway through I got an upgrade that automatically froze nearby enemies which was the best option pf the list provided because it's generally useful at dealing with cave leeches and close range hordes. I then got offered more ice themed upgrades and pivoted to an ice themed strategy by the end. The game often encourages you to try out new builds with epic or rare upgrades that make each run feel unique. Some people complain about the upgrade sharing which I do think could be more forgiving, but I also think it does make every run feel different. It also feels very satisfying to see what builds your teammates are creating.  The classes in this game also feel great. Each one has a little bit of that DRG sauce to them; having supportive abilities designed to focus on teamwork and not just on yourself. Each class also has a variety of the upgrades that synergize with their base kit better than others, meaning each character provides a unique experience. Sometimes each character will also surprise you. I've played the Spotter (the most far ranged focused class in the game) as a full melee build before, and I've played Retcon as a full explosions based build. Speaking of the Retcon, she does a phenomenal job at being a close ranged character in a horde shooter. Most of the time close ranged characters feel worse as you get to higher difficulties, but she feels really good and I was so excited when I realized how she worked.  I've also heard some people complain about the timer, which I personally did not have a problem with. It feels well balanced for each difficulty, where most of the time you will have just enough time to get everything you need and get to the end. But eventually as you play more and get better, you'll figure out how to do missions more effectively, and the timer become less of an issue. That's when you know it's a good time to move onto the next difficulty. This is really neat because it ends of providing a broader experience as you conquer difficulties. Once you start mastering higher difficulties, the earlier missions feel a lot more relaxed and like you can mess around more. It's so satisfying to learn and improve your skills in this game and I think the timer does a good job forcing you to learn.  I do think there are some reasons this game might not be for everyone. The game rests in this space in between Deep Rock Galactic and a typical roguelike that I personally think is a sweet spot but can understand why some people might not like it at first. It's much faster paced than DRG, meaning you will constantly have to be on your toes each layer and you can't relax and explore as much. Compared you most roguelikes, working and communicating as a team is far more important.  Another reason I think is valid is that as of right now the missions can get a little repetitive. You have the same goal in each of them, and the only way to unlock new bosses and biomes is to get good and beat harder missions, which means if you're struggling you'll be stuck playing similar missions a lot. What I think this game really needs is just more updates. More enemies, more upgrades, new things to do. Which are all things they're planning on adding in the future. The game is in early access, and if you want to wait until they finish it that's perfectly valid. But I also think the experience of getting to see the game evolve will be extremely rewarding.   I would highly recommend this game to anyone who's enjoys the combat of Deep Rock Galactic or interested in a very fun horde shooter/roguelike to play with friends. I love this game, and I hope people don't pass it up.  Rock and Stone!   Side note: I've played this game both solo and with my friends down through depth 3. I don't know what the experience playing with random players online is like yet.

I like this game way more than the original Deep Rock Galactic.  Why I like it more:  Each run feels different - The gameplay loop for deep rock galactic largely consisted of grinding mission after mission to unlock a new gun or item, then playing with that to grind mission after mission for the next gun. The ability to choose an entirely new weapon regardless of class, combined with the random upgrades that force you to be flexible in your build each run makes me look forward to each new run rather than just thinking of it as another checklist item to complete.  Failing doesn't feel bad - I've played a dozen or so runs so far and only beat the first boss twice. But I never felt like I wasted my time. At the end of each run, I still met objectives, made progress in security clearance, and was left strategizing how I would do things differently the next run. In contrast, nothing feels worse in the original DRG game than sinking an hour + into a mission just to die at the very end and get nothing.  The objectives are more straightforward - In the original DRG, many objectives (and almost all secondary objectives) boiled down to "explore the entire map until you find 90% of some different glowing object." This resulted in a significant part of each mission just searching back and forth through a complex cave for the ~last~ bit of morkite, fester flea, etc. In contrast, the Rogue core objectives are generally more straightforward and the map is a lot easier to understand which makes the gameplay overall feel more meaningful.  All that being said, the game is not perfect. I think the timer is largely fine (I wouldn't mind it pausing during core spawn events) and the ammo balance is closer than what people think (and honestly I think better than the ammo balance in the original DRG), but the upgrade selection system does need to change so players aren't all competing for the one "exciting" upgrade among 3 other boring/useless ones. However the game is still in very early development and I expect these things to be addressed as time goes on.  With it's gameplay similarities, it's impossible not to compare this game to the original DRG, but I think it's important to remember that from a broader perspective it is fundamentally different in its approach to the progression and the gameplay loop. I can see why hardcore fans of the original DRG might be disappointed that it's not DRG 2, but for someone like me who was frustrated by some of the core mechanics in the original DRG, this game feels like a breath of fresh air. Looking forward to seeing what the devs continue to do with the game.

I have to say two things first.   One: the game is early access. Comparing this game to current state DRG is not a fair evaluation of the game.   Two: Some rogue games have timer mechanics and some have shared loot. Just cause you dont like it, doesn't mean its a bad rogue game.    Okay. Now my review. The game is trying to balance traversal, task efficiency, and killing swarms. I think the best comparisons to this "style" of rogue are Ravenswatch and Nighreign. This is time constrained rogue and you have to complete objectives to power up. If you dont like those two games, I do not see you liking this game. It requires being cohesive with your team and being effective. This is not the casual DRG you are used to. Another thing I have to say. It is a rogue you are going to lose sometimes. Its okay.    The guns are cool. Does this have the best builds in a rogue game. No not at all. but there are some interesting builds still if you get creative. Some classes are underwhelming. Some upgrades are broken in both good and bad ways. I have enjoyed playing this game. With that said, playing with randoms can be rough. One person can ruin your run so be careful with that. Overall I would recommend the game to rogue enjoyers who can let an early access game cook in their library.
