DRG: Rogue Core is a very complicated case for me. I would be lying if I said I was not enjoying my time with it, however I can most certainly understand where a lot of negative reviews are coming from. Actually, I would say a lot of what these reviews criticize I would agree with... to an extent anyways.   It should be obvious that this is not directly supposed to mirror Deep Rock's original gameplay loop, it is much more combat-focused and encourages decision making rather than just straight-forward exploration. You are still encouraged to more-or-less explore caves (albeit much more linear ones), though this time in search of means to upgrade your character. You have to sort of weigh your odds and determine if it's really worth it to go for these things.   This is due to a timer mechanic that I personally think gets way too much flack and is honestly probably the least troublesome problem in the game. I do think it needs to scale for lower player counts though or at the very least solo, which I'm told it is very harsh in. It's clear Rogue Core is balanced around this mechanic and I personally think they struck a nice balance. Really, it keeps runs from being even longer than they need to be because boy are runs LONG. It also provides a nice sense of urgency and keeps the pressure up in an engaging way.   I've seen people upset with the negotiation systems when it comes to picking upgrades and gear as well, and to be honest I could take it or leave it at this point. It irked me for the first few runs but eventually grew on me, it sort of encourages cooperation in a unique and engaging way and forces you to consider your teammates' builds. This is rarely a problem for me considering I exclusively play with friends, but I can absolutely see how this would be a nightmare with randoms.  [i]My[/i] main gripe with the game is the extreme lack of variety at the moment. There are, of course, a handful of interesting upgrades to be found here but a large portion of them are either just really dull stat-boosts or passives that only apply in frustratingly specific/niche scenarios. Even some of the more powerful upgrades like the special "Artifacts" are pretty uninspired and it feels like a missed opportunity. Also not a fan of how ability upgrades are tied to the same station that weapon upgrades are. Workbenches are just not that engaging and ultimately leave you choosing between "Do I want to be an all-rounder or a one-trick with this specific weapon or my ability," with no real nuanced choices. Don't understand why you can't upgrade more than just your primary ability either, seems like another missed opportunity. I would like to see some sort of rework to workbenches or at least have ability upgrades sanctioned to their own station in the caves, perhaps there you could be offered upgrades for the rest of your skills?  Other issues for me are more minor, like why do I have to spend merits to unlock the equivalent of daily challenges? Moreover, why do I have to buy all three individually???? Very interesting design choice. Enhancements are kind of just... there. Could be better could be worse and some Bio Booster upgrades could probably be enhancements instead.  Ultimately though I am, again, enjoying my time with the game so I would recommend it with the caveat that it is not quite as varied as a lot of other roguelikes, which I fear is an expectation (and a very valid one) that a lot of people have. It is a pretty cool expansion of Deep Rock's universe that leans more into the horror side of things, introducing a lot of wicked new enemy designs. You get a lot of weapons completely new to the series and they are all honestly very well-designed, feel pretty nice to use, and look sick as hell (shoutout to the Hammer Caster and the Mag Blaster). Even using some of the returning weapons re-contextualized for a more combat-focused game has been quite exciting.   One final note: I do agree with people that the product that has launched here into Early Access is a little lacking given the time spent developing it and pushing DRG to the side. It is a valid concern especially when you consider that apparently a lot of the feedback provided by playtesters was allegedly ignored. Nonetheless I am still hopeful that GSG can make things right and turn this game into something special further down the line

I will admit, the game started out a bit rough. You cannot turn off chromatic abberation in the settings so I had to skip the tutorial as it was straining to look at.  Then comes the upgrade system everyone loves to complain about. Hated it at first but every day I play I notice I lose my distaste for it. Still wish I could just upgrade in the elevator cus using it as an extra heal during the last fight feels really cheap on top of the problems everyone echoes, but hey it's a beta and they said they would work on it right?  Lastly the absence of a cosmetic shop threw me off to the point where I searched the whole main area of the Ramrod two times over to try and find it because in DRG Scrip is a secondary way to get cosmetics on top of money AND a special mineral so it was really easy to get a decent unique look together for cheap in that game. Here things would already be more expensive due to more classes, so why so stingy on when and how I can get the funny hat? Even with the supporter edition my options were too limited at the start of the game.  But credit where it is due, I love most of the new weapons so far, I like the diverse builds and how some classes are better at certain builds, like falconeer and stubby being a match made in heaven as both work with electric damage, without making said gun completely useless on other classes. Music is great, I like the Ramrod ship and am warming up to the gym and bar system more and more.   Not sure why the jukebox is free now though, bit counterproductive to have the harder working dwarves chill out for free while the miners have to pay per song. Same goes for why they get stuck with a shield that unlike that of DRG does not block all incoming damage. These can easily be explained away by some voicelines such as "man I sure wish the shields worked near the core. Those miners must have it so easy up there!" much like similar voicelines that already exist. Until it is explained though, it will bother me. I like this game but it needs some work.  [[[ If you are reading this: This game is not DRG 2, nowhere is it marketed as having the same gameplay loop and tone of DRG, if you buy this and are upset that you got "scammed" you are to blame. It takes a single search online to discover this game is not for you. If you want a slow-paced more relaxed experience like DRG this is not it. ]]]  As someone who has actually played the game unlike the legions of non-owners talking about it in forums, these are the main issues people are having: - The timer is a nonissue. The game gives you plenty of time to do every side objective and event, the only reason you wouldn't be able to complete them all is if your team is slow. Most events are fast to complete besides a few (Rock-Cracker, Ram-Hammer, and Sealed Vault) that take longer, still you can skip them without issue if you are cutting it close. - The ReaperWorms (consequence to timer running out) are avoidable and just need to be shot with weapons to deter, also your teammates can be saved when grabbed if you shoot the worm. Players struggling with the worms and inexperienced and are clearly taking too long per level if they are constantly having problems with them. - The shared loot system in my experience has mostly been a nonissue so far, your experience with the negotiation system will vary greatly depending on the randoms you're paired with. The decision to make it this way is certainly bizarre and very naive of the devs, my interpretation of this is that they figured the friendly / cooperative community fostered by DRG would have no issue with sharing and negotiating upgrades. However the reality (that they should've expected) is that tons of players refuse to cooperate and want to play this like a single player game. I fully expect them to change this to be instanced by the player or at least make all loot pools have upgrades/weapons of the same rarity to ensure players don't get screwed by the picking order. Still I don't see this mechanic being a dealbreaker that ruins the game for people.  - As explained above most complaints boil down to players being inexperienced, refusing to learn the mechanics, and playing this assuming it is the same as DRG. Unironically in this situation they do need to "get gud" and learn the game like everyone else, if your death isn't because of a game bug- it is your fault and you should learn from the experience to get better. All of the other complaints about balancing are legitimate though, the game definitely needs lots of tweaking.  Reminder that this game recently just released and may likely improve over time. I've enjoyed my time with the game so far but tbh I'd rate mixed (leaning more positive of course) if I could. If the issues mentioned above are a dealbreaker for you then I recommend against buying. 'Will update if things change.  Hopefully GSG doesn't take the illegitimate (casuals) criticism seriously and make the game piss easy like they did with DRG

As someone who has currently played 60 hours (30ish additional hours in closed access) of gameplay  right now, I can say right now the game is in a promising state, but clearly needs more meat on its bones. Been playing both solo and co-op with friends and while I can say co-op is pretty fun with a group of friends that will cooperate in this game, solo can be a mixed bag. Either you're winning, or you're barely staying alive.  Weapons in this game are pretty fun to use and it's good to see weapons like stubby always has lightning damage and such, though I will admit some guns suffer from a lack of ammo, mainly the Devastator Pistol, and the Bolt-Tec HAR-50. I always seem to run out of these ammo quickly. I do hope for more weapon variety soon, but also more elemental base weapons (More on upgrades)  Upgrades are what makes or breaks a run and I'll be honest it's fun when you can get your build going, but the times when all your upgrade pools are trash, it becomes unfun quickly especially on solo when you start going down over and over until you fail the mission. Sharing the same loot pool is interesting as with friends you all have to talk with each other to see which one everyone wants, but with strangers is a different story as if you run into a greenbeard who takes without asking, it won't be the greatest time you'll have. Right now I do just wish for a refresh button in case you get terrible loot.  Now should you buy this? If you have friends who'll join you on the journey to the mines, I'd recommend it. If you'll play with randoms, maybe wait for a discount? If it's solo then I say wait awhile till this game gets more content. Right now, the game is in a good state, but needs variety as the same layout over and over will get boring pretty quickly.

Rogue Core is in an interesting state right now. The time that I have played has exclusively been with one friend and just running the missions as a duo which has been a lot of fun. I can absolutely see where the frustration with the shared upgrade system could be a problem with randoms as there's never going to be perfect communication and more people means less choices overall, but with the changes GSG has said they want to impliment such as rerolls I feel like this can become a much better feature really quickly.  The timer honestly isn't something I have too many complaints about, maybe it should be a little bit slower yes, but the stress of the timer has kept me on my toes and is one of the reasons I have been enjoying the game so much. The only real issue I have is the fact that the threat that appears once the timer ends is fully unkillable and increadibly brutal to deal with if you get snagged, but that has only really happened once so far because I learned to manage my time in the caves much better after that first loss to the timer.  The game is in early access which is very apparent. If you are looking for as streamlined experience right now isn't the perfect time to grab the game. There are issues that are being worked on and I can't wait to see where this game is going to go in a year or two, especially when the 1.0 launch happens. I really do love this game, but it isn't for everybody so either wait for a few updates or learn that this isn't DRG 2 and move on.

People need to remember this is not a sequel to DRG, it's a spinoff, so do not expect the same atmosphere, pacing or gameplay.  If you are, then you're just setting yourself up for dissapointment.  This game is also a rougelite, so it will be much harder than DRG, so there should not be the expectation to win every game.  This review is gonna be based on coop only.  With friends and randoms.  This game is a blast.  Overtime I can see myself playing this way more than DRG.  The timer is a nice way to keep people moving.  It also makes people make decisions.  People will need to weigh if it is worth trying for an upgrade, or potentially running out of time, making things way more difficult.  The shared upgrades is a system I've been enjoying overall.  It does need some tweaks, but I think it's still good.  The more people there are the more perks you'll get to see,  As a worst case there is one perk which any amount of people can take, which gives you more hp and full heals your hp.  This also means that if someone takes this perk before your turn to pick, you get another option to choose from.  The only real issue I have with the system is how much time it gives people.  I originally wasn't a fan of how it interupts the games pacing, but people play more, they realize that you can stack upgrades and can do multiple rounds of picking at once.  The picking order also rotates.  The inital order is random, but after that if follows the same order, cycling who goes first.  Unfortuneately it still seems to suffer a couple of the same issues as DRG.  The main two that seem to play the biggest impact are, the enemies can clip through terrain and hit you through floors, and the mantling/vaulting.  At times the mantling and vaulting feel even worse than DRG, which is quite the feat.
Quite fun to do these new dives with friends, i do enhjoy the perk system more than the old one!

This is not DRG2    And that's fine. Even if Rogue Core shares the name with DRG in the title, despite playing differently and having some flaws, I'm having a fun time with it.    Now to comment on major complaints mentioned in the roadmap:    [h1]The timer[/h1]  I think being pressured by threat timer is what makes RC unique from DRG, but it could be a bit tweaked down, especially for solo players - more often than not, whenever I discover an expenite event I either do it or not based on current time. All events are doable solo but some are better designed with team in mind (like digging sites) or would be optimal with right equipment (like Bosco survey drones). For expenite events that trigger corespawn swarms I think slowing down timer would be at least bearable.    Personally I think the timer should either go as it is or slower based on team scale (slower on solo, "normal" at full team). Also I believe during the boss stage, instead of giving invincible reaperworms after the timer runs out, it should summon the boss immediately    [h1]Negotiation system[/h1]  I don't usually play with randoms, but I get this might be frustrating part of the game. Since the only way to communicate is giving a heart to an option you want, it's either they don't pick it or they pick it anyway. I hate to imagine what would happen when one of the upgrades is a legendary.  Didn't had much issues when playing with friends as we could communicate well, but I'm curious of how it'll be revamped.    [h1]Ammo economy[/h1]  Without specific enhancements for the class, ammo management becomes a problem. This often happens when I'm about to leave to the next level while having weapons with low or no bullets remaining, and I have to rely on my class abilities, my pickaxe and skills in outrunning enemies, while waiting for the elevator to arrive, while also preventing corespawns from damaging the drive unit as hard as possible. Even if an ammo supply is near the elevator, they usually spawn in awkward positions. Luckily this is not common during a boss fight.    Some minor complaints from my part:  - "Coordinates downloaded to scanner" voiceline after rebooting the terminal is misleading (seem to be only said by male dwarf classes). The first time I heard it I legit looked up at the map to check if something appeared on the map.  - Molaktula's defenses (the "???" that spawns fiery spikes) are somewhat poorly telegraphed, especially if there's a difference between ground that makes it look like you're being hit by nothing if you're within its proximity. Lost a facility because of that.  - No dwarven popsicle after drinking an Arkenstout :(    Overall I trust Ghost Ship in providing a good game they can give. Since this is just Early Access it's expected some stuff don't work as intended but I'm positive with enough time and patience we'll get something good out of it.    Rock and Stone, to the Reclaimers

No match to original DRG but still a good game.    I haven't played many rogue likes but as a fan of the og drg I thought I had to get this game. At first I felt it was too different to the original style of the game but then I realised that that made sense because this is a spinoff. I like the ideas of upgrades and the perks/ upgrades are really cool. I also like the idea of progressing and upgrading your weapons as you go but the ammo distribution in this game is surprisingly awful (they said they would fix it tho). I like many of the new weapons however I often find myself drifting back into the old weapons I know and love. The new dwarves are cool and don't get me wrong some characters are certainly better than others, they are all quite well rounded. Luckily the dwarves haven't lost their funny dumb but also kinda smart nature. Unfortunately I'm not a big fan of O.M.E.G.A who is not as friendly or funny as Mission Control and is worse in pretty much every way. I don't mind Ellis (the deposit drone) but of course nothing will ever beat Molly. The fact that there is only one mission type and only different bosses at the end is fine but it can make runs a bit repetitive. Unlocking new biomes and missions is very slow unless you prioritise intelligence quests over everything else so progression is slow but worth it.     Of course because this game is in early access the cosmetics are going to be fairly limited and that is certainly true because the small cosmetic tree mainly consists of hair cosmetics and the occasional paintjob. I assume they will add more DLC'S and cosmetics in the future but right now don't expect to be able to create full themed character sets like you can in DRG. The beer and bar system is relatively the same as in DRG except there is no charming Lloyd robot to serve you drinks and instead of paying in credits for beer you have to do a workout to gain 'calorie points' which isn't bad but can be a little annoying sometimes. For enemy wise I think the enemies are cool and obviously a bit more tough than in DRG however I don't like that there isn't any non-hostile creatures in the cave like those little slug things in DRG or the huge silkweaver things or whatever they're called. So yeah when there's not a relentless horde chasing you the caves can seem a little dead and unlively. Because there is only one type of mineral you can mine (exponite) and it's only function is to get 'negotiation' upgrades the caves can feel a little barron but it's not to bad.     The gunplay and gameplay overall is pretty good but obviously it could use a lot of changes and improvements that I hope GSG can do. But anyway, if you're looking to buy this game and you have no prior experience in DRG I wouldn't say it's that nesessary (♥♥♥♥ I can't spell), it just helps. The games pricing is pretty accurate however when there is more content I would say it's perfect. Thanks GSG for everything I hope you get a lot of money off this so that you can keep blessing the community with great updates and content.     FOR ROCK AND STONE!    -Happy mining

I think a lot of the mixed reviews are over-reacting. It's a fun game, but very difficult. I think the issue the game "looks finished" because it uses the already-complete original game as a base, but in reality the devs are still working out the kinks and adding new gameplay features as feedback rolls in.  As for my experience in the game right now, it's pretty good but not worth $30. My group and I are very experienced DRG players (i.e. playing on haz 5 consistently) and find Rogue Core to be a fun challenge. However, the only gameplay variety is trying different classes, weapon/ability modifiers, and fighting different bosses. The actual missions are almost entirely the same each run, which is a problem when the missions often reach towards a whole hour in length.  I would recommend the game in this state to DRG super-fans that want to support the next iteration of the game. It's rough around the edges, but you'll get some enjoyment out of it. Everyone else should wait until a full release - it's too difficult and gets stale quickly. Just go play the original instead!

At the moment, Rogue Core is a hard game to recommend because it definitely isn’t for most people. Plus, the fact that it’s currently in Early Access means — in this case — the game is nowhere near complete.  Ultimately, yes, I recommend Rogue Core for what it currently is — but only for Deep Rock Galactic players who really enjoy the Deep Dive mode and frequently play (if not exclusively) Haz 5 (maybe 4-5). These players will likely appreciate the same level of challenge with a roguelite twist.  For everyone else who doesn’t fit into that narrow group, I’d suggest holding off for now until the game is more fleshed out, with additional content and better balance.

Great game if you are looking for a challenging roguelike FPS. I am having a lot of fun with it in it's current state, my biggest gripe would have to be the performance at the moment. I am in the situation of having an older CPU and with it the game drops to ~40fps during intense moments which feels quite awful. I does not really matter what graphics settings I use, it all performs the same.  Regarding some of the common complaints, I can not really say I had these issues.  First, the shared upgrade system: In my experience playing mostly with randoms in the EU, 99% of players are very cooperative in choosing upgrades. Now this might have to do with me playing on the highest difficulty or maybe i just got lucky in my 70 hours of playtime, but i really like the system the way it is. It feels rewarding to assemble a build not just for yourself, but for your entire team.  Second, the timer: I don't have a strong opinion on the timer. As someone who played a lot of DRG on higher difficulties as well, I did not have much trouble with the play style the timer enforces. Since the timer resets on every stage it feels quite different to the timer of Risk of Rain 2, as you still try to gather as much each stage as possible while staying within the time limit. However it does not feel great to have to skip parts of the loot in a stage because you ran out of time. You are already limited in how long you can stay in a stage because of the limited ammo and I would like to at least try this game with a less strict version of the timer at some point.  Third, ammo economy: As said above the ammo does push on you in a similar way the timer does, but if you just take the +20% ammo enhancement pretty much any build has a chance of working. I really do not have this issue. At worst it forces you to rush for an ammo crate at an inopportune time, but this to me is a quite fun challenge.    Again, if you are looking for a challenging FPS roguelike experience, I would really recommend this game already in it's current state.

game's in bit of a rough spot for balancing and co-op features, but generally it's a very fun game.  pros: 1: raw gameplay feels solid. movement, shooting, enemy hitboxes. it hits all the essentials of a good shooter.  2: new guns introduced look cool and are fun to use (aside from the silverback, that thing kinda blows)  3: has that DRG charm, dialogue, characters' personalities, weapon animations, environment, stuff to do around the rig. it still feels like it kept it's identity  4: i find this game more enjoyable to play solo over DRG, maybe it's less objectives and more action orientated. so if i wanted to play a solo game, i'd pick rogue core.  5: despite some of the other reviews, i find the timer pretty fair. forces you to make choices like "should i skip this event?"  "should i go back to revive my team mate or go to the elevator?"  "oh, i have plenty of time to explore, let's get expenite."  "should i press the elevator before when it's safe or after so we have more time in the cave, but have to deal with the worms?" adds a decent challenge that forces players to make uncomfortable decisions that i feel some people won't like, but the game needs to have urgency to challenge it's players. only real issue i have with the timer is slow team mates, but that's a learning curve that can be ironed out over time.  cons: 1.1: sharing loot pools and the "heart" system. sounds good on paper, but if you're playing with randoms - you're taking a gamble if the person in front will respect your hearted choice. most times i found randoms will respect it, but it can take one bad apple to spoil a game. 1.2: also, someone hearting something you'd like could be fustrating. i always try to respect it (unless i accidentally didn't notice), but say the 4th person in row will heart something within the first second, so the 1st choice player will have to begrudginly choose something else. although, nothing is stopping them from taking the hearted choice, but then that loops back to former issue.  maybe a better design is everyone has their own loot pools (like how the workbench functions) to prevent this tug-of-war over upgrades.  2: slow starts. running to the rift wall, building the 2 devices, waiting for everyone to be in the circle, go to the abandoned facility, wait for the doors to open, wait for ellie to open the weapons cache, then wait for ellie to open the grenade cache, and now the mission can finally start. it becomes tedious over time, especially if i'm past the 100th, 500th, 1,000th hour game time. maybe let us choose our loadouts (gun, grenade, tool) in the drop pod, and make the devices we have to build to open the rift - just open themselves automatically if a player is close in proximity  3: balancing. the game starts off difficult when you have no enhancement perks and slots playing depth 1&2, but once you unlock them all; running tier 4 enhancements and 8 slots per character - it does trivialises depth 4. most runs now feel like god-mode, even if i'm dealt with poor weapons. of course there's difficulty modifiers (like double enemy HP or jumbled upgrades), and they can give more of a challenge, but baseline depth 4 is unfortunetly too easy now.  conclusion: overall, i'd still recommend playing rogue core in it's current state, it is barebones as of now, so i won't blame people sitting out for a while waiting for more content - that's understandable. though i find it very fun still despite.  also, i feel like some people are comparing this game too much to DRG and faulting RC for being something else. i have over 1,900 hours in DRG and that'll always be my main game, but i feel some of the fellow fans need to expect this game to be a different experience. judge RC on the merits of a roguelite game, and not this "DRG 2".

Rogue Core isn't as bad as many of the reviews claim, a lot of them having severe rose-tinted glasses for DRG or just not vibing with roguelikes. I have already racked up half of my total playtime of Risk of Rain 2 in Rogue Core and I enjoy playing Rogue Core co-op with a friend more than I did with RoR2 (that game is barely co-op to begin with and is better played solo). When DRG dropped into early access it was in a much rougher state than Rogue Core, with years of updates and feedback needed to bring it to the state it is in today. Rogue Core is already very polished for a early access release and has a gameplay loop that offers several hours of gameplay. I'm a huge DRG fan that has extracted thousands of hours of enjoyment from DRG and I believe Rogue Core will be the same story. But the game still needs more work, changes and updated to get to that point.  - The timer doesn't take into account complexity. You have as much time on your hands on Complexity 1 as you do on Complexity 3. Having an additional multiplier that gives you a little more time depending on the complexity level could help. The player count time multiplier could do with a 3% increase as well. - There is a severe time-and-effort discrepancy between Expenite Events. Cargo Hauling takes little time and effort to complete, paying out the same amount of Expenite as the Ram Hammer Escort. The Research Subject event can either be a breeze to do, or be the worst slog in the world that can pay out much less than every other event depending on if you have the ability to freeze it or not. There needs to be some balancing to make the slow, tedious and risky events feel worth doing compared to the significantly less hazardous events. - Too many filler upgrades. There are some really good upgrades buried under a trash heap of upgrades that really need QoL and additional minor effects. It feels bad to get melee upgrades when you aren't playing Slicer or have the drills. Giving some upgrades a minor bonus effect that synergize with itself would help make them more interesting. Why not give Combo Strike a +5% increase to swing speed, Nerves Of Steel +10% recoil reduction, Critical Strike gives your pickaxe +4% crit chance. - Not enough elemental upgrades and terrible synergy between the elements. Fire and Ice have temperature shock and are very strong on their own, plus they are the easiest to build into as you very often get the Incendiary grenade and the Cryo grenade at the start. Electricity no longer procs like in DRG, requiring you to build it up like you do with Fire and Ice. Radiation is strong if you get it on a weapon with AoE but it has no elemental synergy to speak of and not a single upgrade to make a build out of it. Poison and Acid are bad, with Poison only having 4 rather bad upgrades and Acid having none. Any element that isn't Fire or Ice have terrible synergies with each other, are too conditional to be useful, and have pretty bad payoffs for even procing them. -There aren't enough unique weapon upgrades and some weapons have so many upgrades that you have a low chance of getting something useful. I had two workbenches where the upgrades were both of the times Rate of Fire and Recoil Control for the Stubby SMG. Some upgrades could honestly be combined with each other to reduce the clutter. Imagine getting the choice between having Shot Spread + Weak-point Damage and Rate of Fire + Damage. Upping the selection from 2 weapon upgrades to 3 could alleviate some of the issues of getting weapon upgrades you don't want. That or a re-roll option. More weapons need unique upgrades and some of existing are underwhelming or outright terrible. I'm looking at you Decker with your Bleed Bullets. - Enemy balance. The Corespawn are much more durable, agile and deadlier than the Glyphids, making Rogue Core much more challenging compared DRG. I'm fine with the enemies being much more challenging than the Glyphids in DRG but making them into bulletsponges doesn't make it a interesting one. What the Corespawn lack is distinct niche they are try to fill and weaknesses to compensate for their strengths. In DRG the enemies followed some kind of archetype and are balanced around it. The Glyphid Guard is more durable and armored compared to the regular Grunt, but move much slower if they get shot at and spawn in lesser numbers. The Corespawn Vanguard on the other hand have double the health of a Creeper, deals more damage, move much faster and is just a complete nuisance with how many the game throws at you. The Bruiser is not only super durable and deal a severe amount of damage but they can also knock you back. None of the enemies have specific damage types that they are weak to, no additional weak-points to exploit or interesting ways to counter them.  Beyond those issues I have only a few minor nitpicks. - The old DRG weapons are much less detailed than the Rogue Core weapons and could do with a re-model or update to bring them up to par. - The Enhancement selection in the loadout menu becomes very cluttered after you have unlocked every tier. A suggestion I have is that when you unlock a new tier, it should hide the previous one and only display a lower tier Enhancement if you have already slotted in the highest version. There is no reason for us to be able to select Tier 1 of Skull Crusher when you already Tier 4 unlocked and ready to be used. - Some of the doors in missions take too long to open and close, plus they don't stay open for long to kick the bucket of Expenite and leave. - The game spawns most of the armor pickups in the starting cave of the next level instead of spreading it throughout the cave. - The sound of the Pickaxe Customization Terminal is becoming grating from passing it by to get to the Ascension hall or going to the Wardrobe Terminal. The game has a solid foundation but needs more work for it to become a truly great co-op roguelike experience where working with your team matters.

In general, I'm enjoying DRG Rogue Core a lot. That's the short answer, i still have a longer answer and some complaints:  DRG Rogue Core is not completely "DRG 2", and that's fine, I am a worm timer defender, and it was already a thing in Base DRG with swarms getting bigger and bigger until a dreadnought spawns, but it never goes noticed unless you spend 3 hours in a cave with other players because it's wayyy more lenient then Rogue Core's worm timer.  HOWEVER, in terms of minute-to-minute gameplay mechanics, i would prefer if more base DRG players would occupy the public population of this game, because I've had too many games with randoms who presumably haven't played base DRG, where people:  1: rush side objectives or stack swarms while their team has no ammo or time. 2: don't listen to their team, level/upgrade dibs, Ctrl+RMB pings, the text chat, OR the voice chat. 3: Don't understand basic DRG gameplay mechanics, like movement tools (drills, plats, ziplines, etc) or how to use their abilities in general.  If you're reading this and you haven't played Base DRG, I BEG you, PLEASE. for the LOVE of GOODNESS GRACIOUS: try the base game, spend at least more than 10 minutes as a class that isn't gunner (preferably driller or scout) and give Rogue Core a different point of view.  it goes on sale very often and is much more polished than Rogue Core currently.  I could complain about the game itself being difficult, or it's conflicting game mechanics, and I will. But if you know anything about Base DRG, you know how annoying it is when someone isn't doing their part, and the randoms/public lobby experience bothers me more than the actual gameplay.  WHINING ASIDE, ACTUAL GAMEPLAY STUFF:  The difficulty curve feels very sudden and steep with the depth mechanic, which isn't the biggest problem, my main gripe is that just like in, you guessed it, Base DRG: the difficulty is increased or decreased not only with more or less enemy spawns but also with more or less special enemies. This bothers me in particular in Base DRG because if i want to bring over friends who aren't used to FPS'es to the game, say on a lower Haz mission, they'll see less different types of enemies and be less prepared or knowledgeable for higher and higher hazard missions on their own. Rogue Core's enemy designs exacerbate this issue by being more powerful or more annoying in general, and even if they didn't: picking between a mission with only 4 to 5 enemy types or a mission that overwhelms you with all of the available enemy types is just not very good design in both games.  there's more i could talk about but i prefer to actually discuss it than post it.

Honestly, I'm not sure why the current complaints are what they are. The game is pretty fun, the timer feels very well-tuned, the ammo economy is just scarce enough to make you consider your decisions, and the perk draft, while potentially a cause for friction, makes perk selection inherently more interesting.  My complaints are the following: - Lack of variety (the game desperately needs more grenade options and enemy types) - Unfair difficulty (sometimes enemies clip through walls, making them impossible to counter) - Boring meta-progression (mostly just flat stat increases with nothing that opens up new build potential)  The game has a long way to go, but the structure it has in place is undeniably fun, particularly with friends. Frankly, I think people are just averse to the difficulty, particularly because the missions become quite challenging as soon as you hit Depth 2, which doesn't take long. A more gradual difficulty curve could make this a lesser issue.

Hey heads up reader - this game is in early access, its a roguelite, and you have to work together as a team. That should address the concerns of 75% of the negative reviews.  So far I love what I'm seeing from this game. The timer on missions really pushes your team to prioritize making it safely to the next level vs. finding more expenite for that next upgrade, with enough variables going on that the decision is not always the same. Sometimes it is worth staying longer, and sometimes its not.   Personally I like the upgrade system, having to get everyone together and then choosing from the same set of upgrades. Hot tip, you don't have to upgrade as soon as it available. You can just keep moving along and not have to worry about getting everyone together for a single upgrade. Save until the elevator and get 4 at once, which gives you more time to find more expenite to get to those 4.   I've done solo runs, duos with a friend, and quads with randoms - and with friends or randoms is where it really shines. Making decisions as a team, letting others have an upgrade that will go better with their build instead of yours, staying on the move, and ammo management is what this game is all about. I'm finding it to be a good challenge where a good team can move through the caves with a few hiccups and still make it out, but a bad team will get wiped.   I'm excited to see the work that will be put into this game. I love the framework that is down now.  Edit: They've made updates to the things I talked about so I will report back.

[b]Прежде всего:[/b] если думали, что это DRG2 - идём мимо, не задерживаемся. Реюз контента и общий сеттинг - это всё, что роднит данный вбоквел с основной игрой. Убрать всё визуально знакомое, дать другое название и никакого родства не останется. Для тех, кого это не смущает - продолжим.    [h1]Беги, Споттер, беги![/h1]    Первый ключевой момент игры и, по совместительству, основная боль ищущих DRG2 - таймер. Этот надоедливый ромбик в левом верхнем игру с утекающей полоской постоянно действует на нервы, не давая обслюнявить каждый сантиметр пещеры. Чем выше сложность и комплексность генерации - тем чаще встаёт выбор между уйти сейчас или рискнуть и сделать ещё один ивент ради потенциально хорошего апгрейда. При этом сам по себе таймер не убивает, но когда время кончается - местные космические макаки начинают валить из разломов без остановки. Но самое главное - черви. Бессмертные черви, хватающие дворфов и утаскивающие их на следующий этаж, если вовремя не шугнуть свинцом.    По началу бесит неимоверно. Потом осваиваешься. Понимаешь, что движение - жизнь. В итоге приходит осознание, что без таймера игра бы просто не работала. Тебе нужен этот стимул, этот уровень принятия решений. Тем более, что остальной дизайн игры с ним вполне сочетается. Здесь есть всего один ресурс для добычи и всего несколько точек интереса на каждом этаже помимо него при том, что основная задача всегда одинаковая - добраться до лифта, уехать ниже, в самом низу завалить большую ебаку. Одну из трёх, пока что.    Да и, честно говоря, полный забег и с таймером-то идёт от 30 до 70 минут, в зависимости от вашей скорости и количества этажей.    [h1]Кто первый встал - того и пушка...[/h1]    Отряд спускается в пещеру без нихрена. По какой в точности причине, кроме описанной чуть позже, у вас нет хотя бы одной фиксированной стартовой пушки - не ясно. Вооружаемся в первой комнате.    И тут видим второй ключевой момент игры и самую спорную его часть - общий пул выбора на всю команду. Как это выглядит: подходим в круг, игра ставится на паузу и вам предлагают несколько вариантов из которых в порядке очереди каждый из отряда выбирает что-то одно и следующий выбранное взять уже не может, выбирая из того, что осталось. Это осознанное дизайнерское решение. Такое же, как необходимость всему отряду собираться в кругах, для активации выбора.    Звучит ужасно. В половине случаев делает игру с рандомами фрустрирующим опытом. В целом же, не так страшно за счёт невозможности нахождения в отряде двух одинаковых классов и невозможности со старта получить одинаковые пушки. Вам не нужны одинаковые апгрейды большую часть времени, даже если очень сильно хочется. Есть, конечно, пачка относительно универсальных штук, которые придутся кстати всегда, но в основном больше бесит не общий пул на команду, а сам ролл выпавшего.    [h1]Все профессии нужны, все профессии - не влезут[/h1]    Дворфов теперь пять, а мест в отряде так же - четыре, при том, что скиллы у ребят отличаются. Очень сильно отличаются. Это означает, что для полной команды есть четыре разные комбинации классов, в каждой из которых чего-то будет не доставать. То патроны резко закончатся, а ящик скинуть некому; то неудачный спавн волны вайпнет всю тиму, а самовоскрешающегося реткона в ней нет; то с апгрейдами на сустейн не повезёт, а маяк с регеном армора остался вместе с гардианом на базе... всякое случается.    При этом у каждого класса кроме скиллов есть свои особые апгрейды - био-бустеры, которые могут значительно изменить ситуацию по ходу забега. Игра даже позволяет менять наборы бустеров между классами, но для этого надо прокачать до упора не только класс кому менять, но и с кого. Это значит, что воспользоваться этой фичей можно минимум после асценда двух разных классов и заменить можно только один набор из двух.    Все пять классов играются хорошо с единственной оговоркой: инпут активации навыков. Часть можно поменять в настройках, но переназначить полностью всю схему игра не даёт.    [h1]Метапрогрессия[/h1]    Мы тут в рогалике, как никак и самое стрёмное, что я могу рассказать по этому поводу - валюта. Она нужна на открытие слотов для перков, на открытие слотов дейликов, на разблокировку тиров перков и на асценд.    Первый способ получения валюты такой же, как в основной игре - за милстоны. Насобирал сколько-то камер в пещере - вот тебе горстка валюты. Убил сколько-то врагов - вот тебе ещё горстка. Милстонов много, но чем дольше ты играешь - тем реже ты получаешь валюту, т.к. трешхолды увеличиваются. Учитывая, что у каждого класса по восемь слотов под перки и сами перки разделены на четыре тира - можно оказаться в ситуации где у тебя полно места под перки, полно чипов для приобретения следующего тира перков, но нихрена нет валюты, чтобы открыть этот тир. Если что, гейт на финальный четвёртый тир - 7500 и это нифига не маленькая сумма. Я же был в обратной ситуации: открыл и загриндил перки четвёртого тира, но мне некуда было их ставить - не хватало валюты для слотов на новых классах. До сих пор не хватает, но шесть слотов вместо двух - уже лучше.    В дальнейшем можно получать валюту с дейликов, а после асценда даётся доступ к испытаниям, где тоже предлагают неплохое количество в награду, если осилить.    Сами перки при этом стоят того, чтобы с этим заморачиваться. Первый тир, ожидаемо, не особо сильный, но добравшись до четвёртого... ух, это весьма значимые цифры и возможности, которые заметно упрощают жизнь в пещере. Чипы на прокачку перков даются просто за опыт в забегах.    При этом у самих пещер тоже есть своя прогрессия. Сейчас она поделена на 8 уровней, в каждом из которых надо выполнять какие-то условия: собирать камеры в указаных биомах, бить боссов, проходить на определённой сложности и т.д. Открываются за это новые биомы, возможность активации новых ивентов, более глубокая сложность, комплексность генерации, больше выбора модификаторов между этажами, глобальные модификаторы забега. В общем, заняться есть чем.    [h1]Разное спорное[/h1]    Мне персонально не нравится механика брони в этой игре. Мало того, что вместо перезаряжающегося энергощита у нас тут конечный ресурс армора, так он ещё и работает как демедж редакшн: пока у тебя есть хотя бы одна единица брони - урон по хп снижен на 50%. Это буквально означает, что иметь какой-то сустейн малого количества армора выгодней, чем разгонять её вместимость.    Часть новых пушек как будто специально сделана так, что ими просто невозможно играть без перков или апгрейдов на снижение отдачи. Там такой бешеный рекоил, что стабильно попадать из этой хреновины - не самая тривиальная задача. Я уж молчу, что попадать надо в уязвимую точку, а не куда-то там. Патроны в дефиците, да и враги порезвее жуков из основной игры.    Френдли фаер весьма брутален в условиях жёсткой нехватки хп и армора при необходимости кучковаться время от времени. Никаких перков, чтобы это нивелировать в игре сейчас нет, хотя вроде как были в альфе, но я сам не видел.    В соло играть легче, чем вдвоём. По тем же причинам, что и в основной игре - Купер, младший братан Боско, слишком хорош. Втроём играть приятнее, чем вчетвером - пул выбора апгрейдов уже как на полную команду, но хаоса и потенциального френдли фаера в замесах происходит не на столько много.    [h1]Так что в итоге?[/h1]    Играбельно. Вполне. Рандом вносит свою перчинку, но тут может быть весело и интересно. Контента маловато и в плане его качества есть куда стремиться, но для раннего доступа - вполне солидно. Не пожалел.    [b]P.S.:[/b] Музыка в игре хороша. Особенно в активных фазах забегов.

I must perform this review with a few questions.     Is this game fun? yes.   Is this DRG 2? No, do not expect that. This is a roguelike; it plays and feels VERY different but has similar accents.   Is this game complete from beginning to end? No, early access at the time, let them cook.  Can I solo this game? Yes but it works best with friends.     This is not what everyone seems to expect it to be but it reminds me of DRG in its earlier days: a well performing game by a smaller studio that is surprisingly well polished and a lot of potential. If you have the money, want to support these devs, and are in for DRG with a different focus and flavor then this is for you. If not, give it time and come back every few months and see what is changing. If you try it and hate it, get a refund and maybe try later.     I have a lot of faith in these guys and gals who made DRG and they have shown that they are dedicated to supporting their games and flushing them out with time and research. This may not be everyone's cup of tea, but it is certainly worth your consideration.

It's Elden Ring: Nightreign but with Deep Rock DNA and it friggin' slaps.    Play fast, watch the timer, work together, and balance the risk-reward of going for that extra vein of XP minerals or cutting out early before all hell breaks loose to deplete your lives and ammo resources.    + The classes are all pretty fun to play, and it's hard to pick a favourite. Just when I think one class is mid, I'll give them another shot and I'll end up figuring out how they fit into a team and what cool builds I can put on them.  + The roguelite elements of making weird builds for each run and adding progression buffs per class make for some improvised creative builds.  + Femme dwarves, and they have sick beards and mohawks.  Hell yeah  + I would've been happy with just the weapons from Deep Rock Galactic and Deep Rock Survivor, but the new additions on top of those are creative at worst, and brilliant at best - from a pistol that shoots projectile blocking shields (great for melee classes and for support) to a slow punchy rifle that shoots giant slugs.    - The UI for equipping buffs for each class/loadout quickly gets cluttered and hard to read, with no way to organize it [as of this writing in Early Access, late May]  - Unlocking cosmetics with your "scrips" you earn is done per class, instead of across the board. This makes things a little clunky, having to dig around to double check which class has which beard etc  - Mission selection is done by selecting "Depth" aka difficulty and circling around a globe to pick a mission. Being able to use arrow keys/D-pad to cycle through them quickly feels like it'd be more intuitive.    = Similiar to Nightreign, you'll notice it takes time after a game comes out for the player base to kinda get the hang of knowing when to leave some events/XP mining stuff be and move on to keep things going. The more linear cave design and the voice-cues from the narrator help this, design wise. Just be patient with people and yourself. Even failed runs produce progression! Rock and Stone!  = Beer that provides buffs would be cool, but I suspect that'll be added once they figure out how to balance it.    TLDR: It's Nightreign but Deep Rock and I mean it. You're going to be motoring through quickly and risking it for the biscuit. Normally I like waiting out Early Access, but this is worth it right now to get in early!

Absolutely fantastic addition to the DRG universe, that adds something different (and harder) to the normal DRG experience, alongside the survivor genre. As with any of the "four players kick ass" genre, it is A MILLION TIMES BETTER WITH FRIENDS.  Reading through a lot of the criticisms has me surprised that people don't seem to realise its a Rogue-like and you are going to fail. You are going to get awful runs, and god-tier runs. Its less "routine" than DRG, which makes it much harder. I don't know what people expect alternatively to this.  The comments about the timer sum this up. It is literally what makes you have to get on with it and puts that pressure on, unlike the "normal" miner experience in DRG. People complaining about having to get together to upgrade - you probably shouldn't be running backwards to do this anyway, but do this at a natural grouping point (i.e. a wave?). If you don't want a timer, go play DRG?  My only issues at the moment are, as others have said;   - The perks are a bit all over the place. It'd be really great to get some kind of way to manipulate them (i.e. Hades-style), with rerolls or relics or something. I'm sure these will grow with time.  - I don't get the RetCon's kit, but that's just me?

I see a lot of people leaving negative reviews, expecting this to be DRG 2 and calling everything that is different about this game trash.  I honestly don't know how they ever got that expectation.  From the day this game got announced, it was pretty clear that it would be a totally different genre of game, but with similar environments and assets. It was never marketed as a direct sequel, and nowhere did I see people talking about it as such. Now the game is out and suddenly there is a significant portion of the playerbase complaining that the roguelike game has roguelike elements they don't like, and it's leaving me and my friends confused. We love roguelikes, and we were excited for this game specifically BECAUSE it was going to be a roguelike take on a game we all love, and we are all really happy with how the game looks and feels right now already, so to see the game have mixed reviews is upsetting.  I hope the developers don't react too hastily to some of these negative reviews from people that don't really like roguelikes in the first place, because I really like the direction the game is taking, and I would hate to see that be ruined.  With all that being said, great game 10/10 would recommend

Don't let negative comments put you off. If you like DRG? You will probably like this, I know I sure do! Now yes this game is a little bare bones at the moment but even the devs said themself it's nowhere near a finished product. But remember that it's older brother title was in exactly the same state when it first came to steam on early access and it turned out to be an incredible game that is still fun to play now! Yes Rouge Core is fairly similar to standard DRG at the moment with a little less to do and the game play being more Rouge Lite-ish, key words being *At the moment*. Rouge Core just needs more time in the oven. I have no doubt Ghost Ship Games will bake a brilliant game here! Good things come to those who wait.     Rouge Core to DRG is what Sister Location is to standard FNAF. It has the same coat of paint with similar structure but different mechanics along with tying into the story of DRGs world very well. This game takes place just after the event's of DRG where Hoxxes IV has began to shift and change. In a way it's now fighting back against the dwarfs that hope to harvest it's resources by gaining a barrier that shuts down most equipment that enters it meaning you must scrouge up older equipment left in the caves by previous dwarf miners. It has also enlisted new guards to help protect it, your main foes will not be the bugs this time but instead the core spawn. Yeah THOSE core spawn that you could rarely encounter in the first game. BUT! it's not all doom and gloom. You are not the Miners this time, You are the Reclaimers! A division of Dwarfs specialised in kicking rear end. The miners weren't built especially for combat yet could kick serious butt, So to play as a group whos main goal is to kill? Yeah you are gonna have some fun. And this time there are five new classes to play as! All doing a specialised job but I won't spoil the specifics ;D.    In summation go in with an open mind. Once again this game isn't finished but what is here shows good promise. Give DRGs sister game a chance to grow! FOR ROCK AND STONE!

I am writing this review as Deep Rock Galactic veteran. While developers stated that this game is not DRG, it's really hard not to compare the games when they are situated in the same universe and on the SAME planet.  In short, I believe there is enough in here to justify the purchase. But, there are a few caveats to this. If you're expecting Deep Rock Galactic 2, look elsewhere.  In it's current state state, the game is almost punishing you for playing solo. And I will list a few reasons why i think so:  [olist]     [*][b]Stage timer[/b] - Generally playing solo takes longer that playing with friends or stranger dwarves. Having imposed 15 minutes timer per stage makes me feel like i cannot do or ever figure out how certain events work. I'll often stumble upon even, take a look at the timer, sigh and continue on because there is simply no time to do the event and extract. This is often worsened when doing missions with higher complexity cave generation (it takes so long  to get or figure out how to get from point a to b). Some events are straight up a waste of time like [spoiler] exponite bulk detonator.. looks fun in practice, but when you take into account that it leaves minable exponite after it's explosion, much like crassus detonator in DRG, I will just skip it because the mining it would take a chunk from my already depleted time window [/spoiler]     [*][b]Mining does not feel great[/b] - Most of the time, you as a dwarf will only get to mine exponite. That's it. It doesn't feel great as i won't get to enjoy the spoils from my mining after missions ends. I am still a dwarf for beards sake. I like shiny things. I only get to enjoy my spoils in the mission which are promptly taken away after it ends. Nothing carries over. NOTHING.      [*][b]Randomness[/b] - I have observed there is some degree to randomness, and by than i mean conditional. If you get a equivalent of a water squirt gun to deal with D-04, the run is sometimes as good and done right then and there. There is not option to reroll or even start the same mission again with different weapon choices. If you abandon the mission, and pick the same one again, you will be presented with the exact same choices, same cave, same events. I have had a first swarm where i felt like i was just spraying water at the corespawn. Yelling insults at them might have been more effective. [/olist]  [h2]General notes:[/h2]  [h3]Positive:[/h3] [list]     [*]All reclaimers offer very unique playstyles     [*]New guns, traversal tool     [*]Less need to use traversal tools     [*]Less need for lighting - cave system are significantly more easier to navigate due tom higher lighting levels [/list]  [h3]Negative:[/h3] [list]     [*]There is always a bossfight and the end of each mission and you'll get to fight one of 3 bosses. In essence, you'll be doing the same mission over and over again. Once you have reclaimed one facility, in terms of main mission objective, you have pretty much seen every mission. Unlike DRG where you get a selection of mission and you can just choose to not play the ones you don't like. If you don't like this mission, you won't get to play another mission type.     [*]Only 3 final bosses. It gets old pretty quick. One of the reasons why don't play dreadnaught missions in drg unless i really have to.     [*]Armor damage feels useless. No matter how much do i have, armored enemies still laugh at my face.     [*]Reclaimers have no shield. For how much more agile the corespawn are, there are plenty of instance you won't be able to avoid damage. They will run you down eventually     [*]Ammo economy if largely dependent of your luck rather then performance. Alright, after quite a few hours and some facilities cleared, I wanted to write an actual review of the good the bad and the ugly here. I'm honestly pretty happy with the game, having a lot of fun with it. You can tell it's EA because there's a huge world of improvements than can be made, but the way it currently is results in a perfectly serviceable 7/10. With more work, I'm confident we'll have another rock and stone certified classic.  THE GOOD  - The core gameplay loop is good. Each facility is entertaining, you never quite know what can go wrong - it's fun and engaging. If you happen across the proper randoms, you can have a lot of fun, but if you don't it's still good.  - It's easy to jump in and difficult to master. The guiding cable helps a lot in not getting lost and avoiding people ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ around. The classes' abilities are useful and fun but not overly complicated to time. I feel it's more accessible for newcomers than DRG.  - The classes are distinct enough to matter, but not so critical that the game becomes a slog if one isn't present or its player is not very good.  - The minigames are fun and in character, the voice actors rock, and the critters are fun and dangerous.   THE BAD  - The negotiation mechanic is a bit of a flowbreaker, and it certainly needs a reroll mechanic. Too much is at stake in the first selection of weapons to not give the player at least a bit of wiggle room.  - I don't really have a problem with the timer, but it'd probably be better if the players had a couple extra minutes, and/or a mechanic to gain time. If the Expenite events are a gamble - gain upgrades, lose time, health, ammo - you could do something similar. Close this portal and gain X amount of timer, risk a swarm, or a sentinel, or whatever. It seems relatively easy to give options. Chalk it up to EA. - The difficulty loop seems good to me, but I'd expand the loadout options a little bit more. You have a lot of perks to choose from, and you're limited to just 6; it works against meaningful synergies, or having your dwarf be the way you want it to be.   THE UGLY AND THE PRETTY  - I feel the game doesn't really know what it wants to be tone-wise. The operator aesthetic, banter, and general tone of the classes collides a bit with the rather DRG dancing, pickaxe-waving, disapproving Mission Control and such. Give us some more military humor! Make the classes feel like the world-class operators they are supposed to be! Weird dwarf salutes, more 'operator' focused cosmetics, combat knives used as mining implements! - The soundtrack - especially the jukebox - could really use some more character. Get Wind Rose to crank up some metal! - The gym minigames rock, and really go with the whole 'l33t operators' vibe. Give us a bit more! A shooting range, an obstacle course! Some more beers, less focused on mining and more on reclaiming! - While I do miss Mission Control from DRG, Omega seems pretty cool. I'd give it a more synthetic, hyperfocused vibe rather than its current incarnation, but there's a lot of potential.

I love this game! I feel like normally I don't need to write a review to express my enjoyment of a game, but the unrelenting backlash yields mixed feelings online and has deterred people I know from even giving the game a real shot. I think there are a lot of fair and even deserved criticisms that can be levied against Rogue Core in its current state. As an early access game hat is a relatively extreme departure from the original, giving constructive feedback is great for improving the game. This isn't a comprehensive review because there isn't much I can say that hasn't already been said. I love this game and acknowledge its flaws. All I can do is give positive constructive feedback and hope to see more improvements.  The Good: Each class in the game feels uniquely fun and distinct in a way that has endeared me to each of the Reclaimers. I love the variety of builds the game affords as opposed to the more limited options per class in DRG. The overall faster pace makes runs feel more unique, and each team composition feels a lot more impactful than the original game because of the variety afforded each character. It a lot harder and more unforgiving than the original in a way that just really excites me. I find myself diving into runs more and more as I relish in the difficulty. Beating a run feels very satisfying in a way that no individual DRG run ever really has for me. There is this ramping intensity that grows throughout a dive and it is so satisfying when a build comes together. The large number of objectives on each floor means that your team of dwarves has to decide how you will tackle hitting all your objectives before the timer runs out. There's just so much to enjoy and I am having a blast!  The Bad: That in mind, I want to encourage people to continually make constructive criticism about things that frustrate them. I understand the difficulties with the timer, the shared upgrade system feeling unfair, and the clunky implementation of the roguelike mechanics in places that clashes with DRGs core mechanics. I think all of these complaints are fair and there is a lot of tweaking that could be done to improve the core gameplay loop experience. The shared upgrade system can feel limiting or in online matches purposefully unfair or targeted, although I play with my dedicated DRG group and we have yet to experience that as friends playing in a private lobby. I think some of the upgrades could be tweaked to feel more impactful, as I feel it took me a good while to find what was and was not working i my builds. I think the timer needs to be longer and the decisions you make should feel more impactful. Many critical reviews are like this and provide good and informative constructive feedback for the devs.  With those criticisms in acknowledged and with a good few I can say I greed with, those points are often not what I have seen in a lot of reviews. I have seen sexist comments about the fact that the game includes women, which is crazy and kinda embarrassing that we are seeing that as a perceived negative in 2026. I have seen review bombers being rude or outright hateful towards the developers at times. There is a degree of vitriol and hatred that is frankly undeserved and goes beyond constructive and ventures into hate and harassment. To those people, you should be ashamed. If you have spewed hateful rhetoric or attacked members of the community or the dev team online, you give the rest of us a bad name. A bunch of Leaf Lovers.  Conclusion: If it doesn't appeal to you right now, that's fine. It could use some refinement to really reach its full potential. This new take may not be for everyone. I still highly encourage people to give it a shot. I hope despite the flaws that you will love it as much as I do! I look forward to seeing how the game grows and improves in future updates!

As a DRG veteran with prior experience in rogue-likes and modded DRG, I enjoy this game overall. I am excited to watch the game slowly make it to its 1.0 release. That being said, there are definitely a few things that need polish, and I can understand why the reviews are mixed at this point in time.    Rogue Core is a much more tense and stressful game than its predecessor. While the Reclaimer’s Handbook and Tutorial do their job at introducing you to the basics of the game, new players can still be discouraged by Rogue Core’s difficulty/learning curve. The developers previously equated Depth 1 to being like DRG’s Hazard 3. Even with prior DRG experience, there is a chance that you won’t win your first run, as you will still have to adjust to the game’s mechanics. For example, you could be in a room full of Expenite, an Event, a Bio Booster, and a Workbench and STILL have to walk away from the majority of it due to sheer swarm pressure and the ticking timer. That is part of the game, as you will be punished heavily for taking your time and overstaying, especially due to the Reaper Worms that can remove you from the current stage until you are rescued in the next one (in solo you just fail). In comparison, in DRG, most missions allow you to clear out the entire cave at your own pace (excluding a few Hazard 5 missions) and get all the loot.    While it can certainly be expanded on as the game moves toward 1.0, I still enjoy the buildcrafting in Rogue Core, as it still allows you to create broken builds with good synergies. You can create builds that delete the Gatekeepers’ health with one shot, chain elemental effects to all the surrounding enemies that stunlocks them and deals massive damage, become a temp shock machine while regaining ability charge after every enemy killed, etc. That being said, it is definitely harder than in other Rogue-likes to get a “stupidly OP broken build” that I feel most people in this game want to feel the satisfaction of obtaining. It does not help that sometimes all a Workbench will offer you is a new crappy weapon, subpar weapon upgrades, and ability upgrades you do not really need but, ultimately, end up being the better choice anyway. The fact that you can only pick one option from the Workbench also limits buildcrafting, especially if the inverse of what I said happens to be true (super good weapon option, useful weapon upgrades, etc.). Add all this onto the fact that, due to timer pressure, you might be forced to skip certain things that could make your build stronger, including Expenite veins and events to obtain Expenite upgrades. Most people probably feel a degree of dissatisfaction because of this, especially since the game gives you less chances to really “savor” your build since you will be punished for overstaying.    I enjoy playing all the classes and each one can be successful in my opinion. Even Spotter, whose role is more support oriented, can create really good synergies with boosted crit and has the ability to spot Cave Leeches and other enemies on the roof. Falconer, especially with the right Bio Boosters, can deal a lot of electric damage for crowd control and have high uptime with her Falcon. Retcon can be stupid broken and can be clutch in difficult situations. Guardian can stunlock the boss, provide armor for his teammates, and does extremely well in providing space for revives, repairing the elevator drive, etc. Slicer is highly mobile and is extremely strong once melee upgrades start stacking. The gunplay, especially with some of the new weapons, feels great.     In terms of matchmaking, soloing with Cooper does have a learning curve to it. While Cooper is there to help you mine, carry stuff, and revive you if you make a mistake, you still have to juggle multiple tasks at once, with some Expenite events being an automatic skip for me since they carry a lot of risk to them. Even one of my friends, who has thousands of hours in DRG, is still adjusting to the amount of task management required for a solo D2. Soloing without Cooper is possible, but the game is definitely not balanced around that. Co-op/public lobbies can often be chaotic and unforgiving, especially on higher difficulties. One player falling behind or getting downed affects everyone, especially if you have to rescue them from the Reaper Worm cocoon. The cocoon spawns in a swarm to apply pressure onto you, resulting in a potentially uphill battle that, even if won, will still leave you “behind pace”” and force you to rush to the elevator due to the timer. Despite this, from my experiences, the community still maintains that “high trust” from DRG, where most people will respect the heart system.    While you will still be rewarded for failed runs, if the Host disconnects/crashes, you will get no tangible rewards. DRG has a system that gives partial rewards if this happens, so I hope Rogue Core adds a feature like this soon. The Reaper Worms can sometimes be glitchy and sometimes it’s often not clear right away if another player got grabbed by one until it’s too late. Plenty of other issues exist, but, based on the fixes in the latest patch, the developers just need time to cook.    If it was not evident already, Rogue Core is much less cozy, casual, and laid back than DRG is. Instead of a chill experience mining, dancing, and drinking together, you either survive together or die together. You and the rest of the Reclaimers can easily die from just a handful of mistakes. However, this does not mean camaraderie does not exist. You and your fellow dwarves can lift together to contribute to opening Omega’s Bar for beer, and you will be incentivized to fight as hard as you can against overwhelming odds for your brothers and sisters. Funny dwarf voicelines still exist, such as when you fall and your dwarf screams “Keeps happeningggggggggggggggg!!!!”    The soundtrack/OST fits the atmosphere really well and is often hype. Even if I am a bit tired from the current run and previous attempts, the boss OST crescendoing and Omega saying “Strike the killing blow, victory is within reach” Warhammer 40K style is an experience in itself. Plunge to Perdition never fails to energize me and keep me going.    Overall I recommend Rogue Core, but not because it is simply “DRG 2.” It is a harsher, spicier, and more stressful experience with a lot of potential in its current Early Access state. However, the same reasons why I like the game may also be the reasons why many players are staying away from it right now.
