I have nearly 1000 in OG DRG. Read the reviews, watched videos surrounding the criticism around the timer, shared upgrade pool, and ammo economy. In spite of it I give this entry a thumbs up.  Reasons below:  This is NOT DRG. Best way to describe it as NightReign to Elden Ring. Some of the criticism seems to delve into the hypothetical and some valid.  SHARED UPGRADES: I've had nothing but cooperation with squad mates in public lobbies in upgrade selection, no griefing or BM experienced yet. If you read the reviews you'd be shy in trying to play in pub lobbies, I did, but it was unfounded. And hardly anyone is on mics, be brave and at least call out things to make more use of the time allotted.  There is a certain level of enjoyment of not only getting my build up and running, but to help out the squad mates (the give and take). It would be nice to be reminded the class others are playing & their current perk loadout to best synergize with the team. Spotter players usually spec into crit builds, Slicer melee, etc.  It's a time waste (more about below) for everyone to have to run to Ellis to initiate the perk draft. Would be better if there was a pop up where everyone clicks to accept to begin the draft, or at minimum a mandatory draft for available perks once in the elevator.   TIMER: To do away with the timer is the wrong direction to take. It provides that friction between risk/reward, fear/greed that with time can help you prioritize what to focus on and what to skip depending on the mission state. If there was no timer, then everyone would just max scour the map (like I do in OG DRG) for every morsel and the run would go for hours with all the reward and no risk.  I would like to see an increase in the timer overall because of the time waste trying to corral people to Ellis to perk draft (that should be changed) or other things that the full squad must be there for. Creates a hurry up and wait feeling. That and the random mini waves eats up time too.  I find it unfair that you can start the extract and the worms will still spawn if it spills over to the "out of time" segment. Perhaps it would be better to cancel the worms from showing if started before timer runs out. Maybe make the extract waves harder the closer you start towards the end to a cap - would play into the risk/reward a bit more fairly imo.   AMMO ECONOMY: In my opening hours I felt the lack of ammo the most, but more playtime and it's become less of an issue. Some characters are very ammo efficient (Retcon) and some guns are more than others and the less efficient guns can be offset with Spotter's extra resupply ability.   There are 3 resupplies per level, and I would agree that perhaps they should hold up to 8 ammo packs or increase the ammo pool of the weapons.  The lack of ammo is most apparent in the "Double Enemy Health" alteration that can accompany a run for added difficulty. I've attempted many times and have never been able to successfully complete a run. Especially if you try to release the Expenite Crassis - it's health pool is ungodly.   GUNPLAY: This is a combat focused version of the DRG universe and most of the weapons feel good to use. If you took the reviews at face value you'd think the new guns are trash, far from the truth. There are only a couple guns I can think of that likely need tweaks. Since it's combat focused, the mobs clipping through the floor/grates is a problem no unfamiliar with OG DRG - but it's a bigger issue here due to the focus of the game. And the big spawn that shoots a death laser at you doesn't have sound ques, insta down you and anyone caught in its path, and goes through terrain - this obviously needs fixing.   Overall this is an early access game but any early access will be judged since it is playable now. People forget how bare bones OG DRG was and comparing EA states is a night/day difference between the two.  There is fun to be had in this game if you're someone who enjoys a faster-paced experience with a foundation of systems that will likely get ironed out and tuned during EA.

I read a few negative reviews. Boy this game does NOT deserve them, this is kind of disrespectful for me to say, but I feel like I'm watching toddlers complain that the royal palace they just moved into is slightly different. We would NOT have liked a game that was exactly like DRG, that's what DRG is for.  It's much more polished than DRG was upon Early Access and is already a blast to play. It's sorta like if you took Deep Rock Galactic, allowing silly powerful builds that Hades has, and made it less about hanging out in a cave doing all the cave things and instead blasting through hordes vermintide-style barely making it out alive. Also while sporting some crazy new abilities.  Let me hit on every negative point, and tell you why its actually a positive. For reference, I play this game on max difficulty and am 20 hours in. I played after their initial balancing patch addressing timer and ammo:  UPGRADES SUCKING? I fully expected boring upgrades, played many games with them where designers were too afraid to give players power, but I was surprised to find out each upgrade you get in a run is quite meaty and noticeable, and best of all can STACK. for instance, a gun can get 50% rebound chance. Stack it again and it turns a pistol into a horde clearing gun.  ASSET FLIP? Yes and no. I was pleasantly surprised by the fact the game features a lot of new weapons custom built for the game, with some classic DRG weapons making a reappearance. The games entire aesthetic is a lot more sleek, techy, and serious, matching the seriousness of the reclaimers as they take on a deadly mission. The UI is completely new, the lobby is new, they music is new, the classes are new, there are new abilities, all new enemies, and the game loop is far different, yada yada.  SOME CLASSES SUCK: hard disagree. Any new class feels sucky at first, give it some time, they all have strengths.  SHARING UPGRADES: Yeah it sucks with malicious teammates, but most are not like that. I kinda like the social dynamic of everyone looking over a selection of upgrades together and parcelling them out, you can see how people are building themselves alongside you. They took a risk doing this, since most games remove any and all friction of social interaction, but when they lean into the friction, it can make playing with others a much more beautiful experience.  AMMO RUNNING OUT? Honestly, this happens in DRG all the time, this is NOT new. It's a threat that needs to be present, asking players to get smarter with how weapons get used. The game also gives dwarves more ways of dealing damage outside of guns. Melee is stronger. recharging class abilities sometimes deal significant damage.  RNG? I've heard people complain you HAVE to have the platform gun, or HAVE to have a red sugar upgrade, etc.. My experience is most of my difficulty in the game was simply not knowing how to play it yet... some runs can feel heavier than others, but there are many many ways to skin this cat that at least one dwarf on your team is going to turn into a monster shredding monster. If the game was evened out so you always gain a certain amount of power, the tradeoff is that the designers are taking away those moments when you experience a high of power after not quite figuring out a previous run.  ARMOR SUCKS? so instead of a recharging shield you have "armor", a second health bar. I believe the devs made the right call making it operate like health. It asks you to build around either around planning on taking damage (and bringing back armor through goofy means), or avoiding damage altogether with fast kills or fast movement. Perks often play with the idea of always being low on armor, or always being topped off. its spicy. Its interesting. A recharging shield evens out the experience, taking away the diversity of builds.  THE TIMER: yeah so in DRG people tend to extract every goody out of a cave, do every event, etc.. in Deep Core there is a timer spelling impending doom, summon horrifying tentacles to drag your team into the depths. You can fight them off, but its legit bad news. Its kinda awesome. It turns the game into a delicate balance of trying to milk a cave for all its upgrades, and remembering you actually wanna stay alive. It forces your team to keep a move on. I hope the devs do not nerf it further as it has been balanced just right. This is a game about resource management.  YOU CAN DIE, AND WILL DIE A LOT: I dunno what players expected, yeah? Its not much of a rogue like if its easy to master. Your supposed to die, learn, try and get a bit farther, rinse and repeat. I find this game, after an initial "holy crap this is hard" period is much more merciful than a title like Hades.  My only real complaint with the game, and with DRG as well, is the UI/UX is far from elegant, with information changing positions per screen and it being difficult to digest what is going on with your build mid-mission at a glance. I really don't like that cosmetics are unlocked per class. The world of Deep Rock has so many beards and hats that this type of padding doesn't need to exist.  I love the gym. I love the new mission control. Love the gauntlet abilites, love re-experiencing the tension DRG use to hold for me.  Anyways, I recommend it 100000%. Did not regret buying early. Keep it up Ghost Ship!

It's not done by any means but the core gameplay loop is fairly engaging. Anyone claiming the timer is too harsh or ammo economy is bad are not actually good at playing video games remotely. In regards to the timer, the point is that you should not be able to full clear the floor every single time. in regards to ammo, there are ammo perks that can increase your ammo by around 50% if you equip all of them, not to mention perks which increase ammo gained from resupply's. The real issue I have comes in with variety in almost all cases. All of the biomes are just from DRG, enemy variety is boring and most notably the upgrades need to not only be more unique, but actually good. Huge fan of DRG: Survivor, so Rogue Core feels like it is aiming directly at something I already want to love.  The foundations are absolutely here. The atmosphere, co-op chaos, gunplay, enemy pressure and “Rock and Stone” personality still come through. When it works, you can see the outline of something brilliant.  That said, it is not quite seamless yet. The timer can feel too restrictive, upgrades and drafting do not always flow naturally, and the pacing can sometimes feel more stressful than satisfying. Some runs also feel like they need more variety and stronger build identity.  But honestly, if Ghost Ship can tighten the run structure, make progression feel as smooth and addictive as DRG: Survivor, and blend that with the exploration, teamwork and class fantasy of standard DRG, they’ll have a classic on their hands.  Right now it’s rough in places, but the potential is huge.

TL:DR up front: If you like DRG already, yes, if you're on the fence or new, let this cook a little longer, then yes.  Already the bones of a great game are here, transitioning DRG into a rougelike works really well, and there's a lot of different playstyles that suit each class. I was a little dubious on the hero-shooter like elements but they work really well and each one is useful in their own right, whether it be a support class, trash clearing, or diving in headfirst with a blade.   I didn't have a ton of time in the original DRG but seeing the lineage trace to the new game is pretty cool, and it carries through the great visual style. Plus they did it right, dwarven women should have glorious beards and there's plenty to choose from.   It is early access and is in need of some content and polish passes, but given that the framework of the game is good the rest of the changes should be pretty straightforward for the devs from here. It's basic stuff like balancing (the cadna projector is pretty rough if you're stuck with it) and upgrades needing a little work but none of that is a massive dealbreaker and it'll keep improving with time.  So yeah, thumbs up from me for a game with great replayability already and a really high ceiling for new content being made by devs who really care about making this game great.

EDIT: After spending time in the game and having a better grasp of what's going on.. I want to change my review to positive but everything below is still relevant and will use it as my disclaimer to whoever is on the fence. There are workarounds to the upgrade issue, the main one being just waiting till the end of a floor to go through all the upgrades.  The lack of UI indicators for almost anything you can pick up will make the game impossible to play someone who is deaf. BUT.. I love the vision and I'm in it for the journey to release. So long as the devs make changes players are asking for, I will keep this positive! EDIT 2: literally as I posted my edit, I read a dev post that literally addresses a lot of the problems i've mentioned. Just a matter of time before we get more seamless gameplay it seems!  For the average entry level player and those who have a preference for og DRG's polish and pacing.. This game is quite abnormal. In the sense that the pacing, while fast,  gets halted by having to group up to do upgrades with the REPD (this game's MULE/Molly) which is practically like trying to round up wandering cattle when it needs to be done. THEN you go into a perk choice menu which does not let you do anything until each person takes their turn to choose something. Some people won't even realize it's their turn cause the indicators that it is your turn to pick are too subtle. I think currently there are too many audio indicators for things you need to pay attention for, which imo should have UI indicators for things like the elevator arriving (if you miss the dialogue for it the first time, it can basically be a missable thing until this game's assistance AI voice thing starts yelling at you to get on the elevator lmao). Weird decisions as those really ruin the pacing of this game. For what's here, I really enjoy it so far. But the way the game slows down and forces you to stare at a screen while ppl take over half a minute trying to figure out what to pick.  OG DRG does the teamplay elements much more seamlessly and is built in such a way that there is no waiting unless it's to get a teammate to the objective to start an event. There is no HARD pausing the game for this, and I'm not at all expecting this game to be the same as og DRG, but goodness for a game that is combat focused it has me staring at a menu for longer than the og DRG would ever have you do (it's just about never).  I love mastering fast paced difficult shooters and all, but this one shoots itself in the foot to get you to simply progress.  Devs PLEASE please change the upgrading system to where you're not severely ruining the pace of every single mission to get to REPD and group up. Even going out of my way to drop Ellis (REPD) smack in the middle of the group, ppl will still wander away. It's just god awful, and somehow people constantly miss it.. like ALL the time.

Rogue Core is a fundamentally different game than the original DRG, and I think that is the perfect direction for a spin off title. DRG allows for more exploration, casual fun, and has nearly a decade of development behind it to be a much more complete game. Rogue Core places more focus on speed and communication with teammates that can cause frustration if not adapted to. This game is not for everyone but I do enjoy the shifts the devs have made to try something new.     One of the biggest criticisms I have seen is the shared skilled system, I do not think these players are wrong for having this opinion but I do believe this fits the core of this game's identity, if all players had their own sets of skills then that would remove a layer of teamwork and communication that is prevalent in the rest of the gameplay. The game plays the best when taking advantage of all of it's systems, making sure to use your abilities, making decisions based on time and resource management such as skipping events when time is tight, and stacking synergies on top of each other usually resulting in each player have a specialty.    Ellis is a great addition and while there are aspects from DRG that I do miss (more intricate cave generation, more minerals/side objectives, and a more lively hub) I am excited to see what the devs continue to work on and add to flesh out the game further. Additional new perk decks, skills, weapons, classes, and objectives will be welcomed and I am excited to see when this game reaches 1.0

I think the gameplay loop is solid, and I enjoy the different mining areas that can be found in the caves. Though im surprised, with how many cosmetics were in DEEP ROCK GALACTIC, the amouns seems low here. I also do not like needing to unlock them individually for each class, as that seems tedious when you do not get too many tokens to buy them per mission. Overall though I think its a good start and can't wait to see where it goes!

“As it stands now I can’t recommend rogue core.”  Shut up.  This is an early access game, and it’s a fun early access game.  The fact that they made a rogue-like DRG game is very fun and it offers more replay value because each game will be unique. The game is fun with friends, but it’s also almost as good with randoms as well.  I’m so tired of people reviewing an EARLY ACCESS game as if it were a full release game. Especially when the dev of the game literally encourages people to speak out about wanted changes or updates in the future (you are shown this in a introduction video as soon as you open the game,) this fact in itself should be a massive positive as long as they do listen to the community. Regardless of that fact though, the game in its current state can offer you enough fun to be satisfied with the low price of $30 (relatively cheap in today’s economy). Also when DRG originally came out as an early access game, it was missing a lot of stuff, but that didn’t stop it from becoming a more polished and enjoyable experience over time. The same applies to this game and all other early access games. If you go in with realistic expectations instead of being delusional and thinking this is going to be a complete experience of a fully polished final product, then this is just an enjoyable DRG title in a rogue-like form factor which will offer you plenty of content for something that just released about a week ago.   If you are looking for a finished product and don’t want to wait to see what this game will become in the future then I wouldn’t recommend it because you’ll be in the review section making a negative review like all these other impatient goofballs. But, if you have a little bit of patience and are excited to see what the future of this game holds and enjoy the currently available rogue-like gameplay, then this could possibly be a good fit for you.

Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core Review — The Brilliant (and Frustrating) Dilemma of the Roguelike Dwarf  Lately everyone is debating the upgrade systems, the brutal difficulty, and how well Ghost Ship Games’ formula adapts to a roguelike loop and honestly, Ghost Ship Games are absolute legends; their track record proves they make games with genuine passion and quality. However, as with any new experimental venture in the industry, sometimes you have to screw up a bit before you can reach heaven. In my time with Rogue Core, I’ve noticed a massive design flaw in its opening hours that nobody is talking about, and it’s time to put it on the table.  The Gauntlet Mode and the Design Paradox  Let’s first talk about what works spectacularly well: Gauntlet mode. If you check the forums or talk to the community, the vast majority agrees that playing a Gauntlet run is when you truly experience the game Rogue Core was actually built for. That’s where the formula explodes, the synergies click, and your dwarf feels like a true mining and killing machine.  But here lies the grand paradox and, honestly, it’s a tragedy when a studio designs a game with such a brilliant vision for its endgame, yet the barrier to entry in the first few hours is so flawed that many players will get frustrated way too early. A lot of people will throw in the towel and never get to experience the game at its maximum potential.   The Frustration of the First Workbench  Where does this frustration come from? It happens in the initial runs (those 3 levels + 1 boss level format). In their eagerness to deliver a pure, hardcore roguelike experience, the game commits a major tactical sin: at the very start of a run, secondary weapons have an incredibly high spawn rate at the workbench.  This is deeply frustrating. That initial burst of excitement and adrenaline you feel when starting a new run completely vanishes the moment you interact with that first workbench and see nothing but secondary weapons or short-range shotguns. Being forced to start a run with a weapon that doesn't help you in the early game—and actually actively hinders you—adds an artificial difficulty barrier. I am certain this wasn't the intended experience Ghost Ship Games had in mind when they coded this.  The solution is logical and clean: secondary weapons should be completely removed from the initial selection pool. Players should always start with a solid primary weapon option (except heavy choices). Later in the run, if they decide they want a secondary, they can make that strategic choice at advanced workbenches.  Furthermore, there’s a noticeable lack of freshness in the starting arsenal. It feels like they dropped the ball on creating more unique primary weapons and grenades with perks designed specifically for this new experience, rather than just copying and pasting so much content from the original game.  The Isaac vs. Satan Syndrome: A Pacing Issue  To understand why these short, initial runs feel so bad, we can use a perfect analogy: it’s like if The Binding of Isaac forced you to fight Satan after clearing only 3 or 4 floors of farming. You simply don't have the space or the time to piece together a proper build. Because you have so few floors to upgrade and evolve your dwarf, the boss battle at the end of these starter runs feels unfair and hollow—an incomplete experience compared to the wonderful balance found in Gauntlet mode.  Conclusion: Adjust the Perspective, don`t touch the numbers, yet...  This critique doesn't come from a place of hate, but from a desire to see Rogue Core shine the way it deserves to. I firmly believe that not everything in video games is fixed by simply tweaking numbers or scaling enemy damage up and down. In this case, the game's flow is tripping over itself right at the starting line.  The solution isn't that complex: it's about shifting the mindset and understanding that in a short 3 to 4-level run, the player needs to feel like they can "break the game." If we aren't getting more floors to farm, then give us double the rewards. A great fix would be that in these short, non-Gauntlet runs, every time we level up our XP or hit a workbench, we get double the upgrades or perks. This way, Gauntlet mode can maintain its current neutral strategic pacing, while the shorter runs become fast, chaotic, and deeply satisfying power-trips.  Small design adjustments like these will make a world of difference. It’s just a matter of adapting their perspective to this risky new genre mashup. The foundation for another masterpiece is right there; now it’s up to Ghost Ship Games to polish the gem. Rock and Stone!

rogue core in its current state is very flawed but still very fun. prepare for a short list of pros and cons  pros:  -incredibly satisfying gunplay, most weapons are capable of being more fun than anything in base DRG if you ask me. this includes weapons that actually are from base DRG because of workbench upgrades and build synergy  -arguably high general difficulty. I consider this a pro because it keeps the game from feeling too easy and makes busted builds feel more rewarding  -classes feel distinct and fun to play similar to base DRG. the class abilities go a long way since you can upgrade them through various means  -solid gameplay loop. do as many tasks as you can during calm periods, choose to fight during swarms, continue doing tasks, or try doing both. reach the boss fight, win or lose, and do it again. I think there’s enough variety and familiarity to keep things fun  cons:  -the co-op experience isn’t great right now. I’ve had much more fun doing solo runs where you don’t have to wait for others to come to a zone that *requires* all players to be present. I don’t mind the negotiation system itself but it’s the way you access it that’s annoying more often than not, and this of course extends to anything else that requires players in a zone  -the negotiation system. I did say I don’t mind its current implementation, but it could certainly be made better and more fair for everyone. other reviewers have already had some good ideas for how to improve it so I won’t try to come up with my own, but rerolls and card flips would be a great start  -frustrating gameplay changes, a main one for me being the slowdown when falling from a certain height. this mechanic wasn’t in base DRG so I have no idea why they decided to add it to this faster-paced more intense version… another annoying change is the armor system instead of DRG’s regenerating energy shield. armor is plentiful in this game but that doesn’t really matter when it feels like it barely helps and gets depleted in 2 seconds. then you have to find an armor source or hope to find an upgrade that gives you armor in some way  I do definitely recommend the game despite its problems which will hopefully be solved over time. the foundation they have here is super strong and I believe it will shape up to be a great game. if you’re on the fence about getting it, just be aware that you might be frustrated and disappointed in many ways and you might want to check back later when development has progressed. if you’re looking for a fun and intense solo experience though, I think you should try it

Same humor, same cool community, Same biomes although the generated caves are a bit more linear, some of the same old weapons, but far more new guns artifacts upgrades grenades. And ofcourse new Dwarfs with new abilities etc. What would Karl choose? But overall it IS a different game, it's far more RogueLite than OG Deeprock, with a new perk system, and weapons that are collected and upgraded throughout each mission. Instead of spending your gold and minerals on weapons, cosmetics, and overclocks; each item available to upgrade has its own currency you get by playing. The barrel kicking game is now a gym where you play minigames to lift weights in order to purchase beer. They obviously put a ton of time and effort, love and cleverness into it so far.  Seeing people diss it so heavily feels unfortunate and unfounded, it does take some learning to understand how the missions play, but once you get into it you'll really enjoy it imo. Rock & Stone.

This is a fun game right now, great combat feel, cool level design, lots of potential. As a DRG fan I see why they wanted to hold onto many of the core concepts in Rogue Core as in the previous title. However, it feels a bit hamstrung to me.     The level ups could be so much more interesting. Rather then mostly just stat percentage upgrades I would love to see more options like magic missile be implemented. Chain lighting, fireball, acid spray chance on hit/fire type abilities would be so engaging! You could also work in crowd control style level ups more too. Maybe a dwarven polymorph spell that changes an enemy into a mug of ale that gives you armor when you hit it with bullets? Or a summon ability that raises undead dwarves from the abandoned mines to fight for you?    Another aspect of the game that I find myself thinking when I play Rogue Core is about the classes. I felt this way about DRG too actually. I don't really enjoy leveling up multiple classes. I find myself getting attached to my class, putting all this time into leveling and upgrading everything. It feels more like my character in a classic rpg. I understand the importance of the classes for co-op, but it would be super fun if you could have multiple of the same class in a run. 3 Falconers and a Guardian would be a so fun to play. I imagine its unrealistic, but I would even go as far as to suggest eliminating the class system and make the base abilities options on the ship. Maybe that would stray to far from it being a DRG game?    The meta game level ups seem bland so far, there wasn't anything in the tree that I was excited to reach. Hopefully more interesting options will be available in future updates.     I don't really like the character/ pickaxe customization system. Maybe I don't understand it correctly, but it seems like there is very little content and you cant just pick out the hair you like, you need to unlock the hair before it first? Odd.    Changes that would improve the flow of the game would be cool too. Accomplish an objective and it adds time on the countdown? Rather than gathering around together to level up, everyone just has to rock and stone? You wont be interrupted from your current task but can join right after.     Considering how much the developers care about their games, I remain faithful that Rogue Core will eventually be a blast to play. We cant forget its in early access, so for now, it's acceptable :)

It's rough around the edges but it's still a blast, though the difficulty curve feels pretty steep. Not all options and events are made equal so learning what you can take or what you have time for can be a big deal.   Looking forward to how things pan out over the next updates :)

Rogue Core is a solid foundation built on mechanics familiar to Deep Rock Galactic fans, but keep in mind this is a spin-off, not a sequel. It focuses on a linear, hardcore experience rather than the zany shenanigans or deep mining of its predecessor. There is a good game here, and it is great to see the developers patching it consistently since its launch last week. That said, the solo experience feels like a grind. Progression is slow, making it difficult to unlock perks for higher difficulty tiers or reach new biomes.  I cannot recommend the game in its current state due to a glaring lack of accessibility. We are in 2026, yet basic features are missing. There is no always-sprint function, which is especially frustrating in a fast-paced game. The game lacks a customizable crosshair, and the HUD is even more cluttered than the original, which was already busy. The text is often illegible on smaller screens, and the lack of Steam Deck verification makes this a major issue for handheld players. Furthermore, there is no proper controller menu support.  I am deeply troubled by the expectation that players should rely on third-party mods to implement these basic accessibility features. Mod.io will require Steam credentials when it is eventually implemented, and forcing players to bridge their accounts for accessibility that should be built into a modern game is unacceptable. This is not 2007. I hope the developers address get around to implementing these changes.  The game itself is feels and looks really good. If you're hardcore about DRG, then you probably will like this a lot of what RC has to offer. It takes a long time to unlock things to make the game more enjoyable. That said, I can't in good conscious review bomb this game. It's addictive. Solo needs some balancing, things need to be reworked, but I think what's here is good, albeit a little bare.

A fun rogue-like experience.  Each run has 3 or more levels of fighting/exploring/mining your way to the elevator and one boss fight.  There's a time limit on each level, after which if you hadn't started the elevator yet, you'd have to get lucky to survive.   I like the level timer, it encourages you to coordinate with your teammates and often forces you to make tough decisions. I've only played solo or with friends so far but it may be more difficult with strangers to get the type of coordination needed to keep the hordes of monsters off your back.  The enemies are tough and get tougher as you progress in the run. Thankfully, you will have opportunities to get various perks, upgrades, weapons and equipment to choose from that you get to keep until you've finished the run.   I found most of the weapons enjoyable to use, once I figured out what they were good at. Most of the temporary perks(upgrades) you get as you make progress through the run are helpful even if they don't fit well with your build. The upgrades stack and with the exception of the weapons upgrades, I haven't run into a stack limit. :)  Starting out, you will die. As you get better, you will die less. One of the most common consequences of playing rouge-likes is that you will die. A lot. But each time you die, you get a little progress towards permanent perks(enhancements) that you get to equip and keep between runs. You will be dealt a bad roll sometimes. You may get a bad run, get bad upgrades or bad weapons or you make bad choices but you will still make progress. That is a quintessential experience of playing rouge-likes.   At the time of writing this review, it is in early access and still has a few kinks to work out:  	* As stated earlier, I like the level timer. However, the timing feels a bit too tight sometimes and it would be great to have some options on how much time we get per level for a more casual session.  	* I feel the system for selecting upgrades with your teammates needs some adjustment. When it is time to select an upgrade, everyone is randomly placed in queue to pick something. While other teammates to make their pick, people have the option to mark the upgrade they want but the game does not enforce that selection. Griefing opportunities abound but sometimes an item is picked just as someone marks that item. Not a big deal but it would be nice to have a little more time before anyone picks to read through the items and communicate what people want/need. Communication is key.  	* There are indeed, bugs in this game. And the ones you can't shoot are surprisingly rare for an early access game. I've run into a few issues while playing with friends including some crashes but they usually just amounts to a minor and often entertaining inconveniences. Once my friends and I had an issue with Ellis unlocking the starting weapon crate and decided to see how far we could get without weapons rather than restarting the run. We didn't get to the boss but we still had a lot fun.  Overall, I enjoyed this game and it’s already become a staple in my friend group. I look forward to further updates and will continue to enjoy playing.

Recommended  Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core is not DRG 2, and that’s intentional. It’s a more demanding, intense experience. If you play DRG to relax and have a chill session, this might not be for you. If you’re looking for a game that challenges you and keeps the tension high, this is worth picking up.  Pros:  Core gameplay and gunplay remain excellent—shooting and movement feel satisfying. Difficulty is tough but fair; mistakes matter, and you need to adapt on the fly. Upgrade system is core to the game. Teamwork is essential, not just four players doing their own thing. Timer adds urgency and enhances the connection between gameplay and narrative. Class abilities may feel unusual at first, but they grow on you and add depth to the game.   Areas for Improvement:  Pacing of upgrades and overall run flow could use work. Some upgrades need balancing to feel equally useful. More mission and enemy variety is needed. Dwarf visual designs are hit-or-miss. Bug fixes and polish are necessary. Mission length might feel a bit long at times.  Rogue Core already delivers tense, satisfying co-op gameplay. With additional content, balance tweaks, and polish, it has the potential to become a standout roguelite.  Rock and Stone.

재미없진 않으나 문제점이 다수 있다.    1. 런 한번 뛰면 40분 ~ 1시간 소모  이 게임의 엄마격인 딥락갤은 숙련되면 가장 껄끄러운 임무 종류도 20분이면 깰 수 있는데, 이 게임은 어머니가 안 계신지 가장 짧은 판도 거진 40분이 필요하다. 원한다면 광질이나 부가목표 전부 무시하고 러쉬뛰어도 되지만, 더 깊이 내려갈 때마다 적 스펙이 조금씩 강화되며 보스 짜르는 데 너무나 긴 시간을 보내야 할 수도 있다.    2. 이해가 안가는 업그레이드 시스템  대체 왜 적재드론 옆에 전부 모여야지 업그레이드가 가능한가? 한놈이 눈치없게 멀리 뛰어나가서 안오면 다같이 업그레이드를 못한다. 심지어 중화기나 특수장비 사물함은 적재드론을 불러야 되는데, 적재드론이 이것들에 연결하는 순간부터 모두가 모여서 사물함을 깔 때까지 아무도 드론을 못 부른다. 이젠 한 새끼가 없는 거 때문에 모두가 적재드론을 못쓴다. 하지만 가장 개같은건 협상 시스템인데, 업그레이드를 할 때마다 방에 있는 인원수만큼 업그레이드 선택지가 주어지며, 딴사람이 먼저 고른 선택지는 내가 못 고른다. 쓸만한 선택지가 없으면 체력업글을 고를 수 있지만, 계속 체력만 업글하면 결국 재미는 딴놈이 다보고 나는 쩌리가 되는 멍청한 시스템을 갖다놨다.    3. 최적화  딥락갤은 최적화가 잘 되있어서 중상옵 정도는 돌아가는데 이 게임은 엔진도 다르고 각종 효과가 넘쳐서 그런지 렉이 조금씩 걸린다. 자기 컴터가 좀 후지다 싶으면 플스1 그래픽으로 플레이해도 렉이 걸릴 수도 있다. 이건 개발하면서 개선될 수 있으니 지켜볼 생각.    결과적으로는 고스트쉽게임즈의 하다 만 숙제를 대신 해주는 느낌이다. 딥락갤을 길게 해본 유저로서 좀 탐탁치 않은 점이 많이 보이는 것도 있는데, 북유럽계 개발자새끼들은 고집 센 걸로 유명하기 때문에 이게 고쳐질 지는 미지수다. 딥락갤을 재밌게 플레이해봤어도 정식 출시때나 세일할 떄 구매하자.

Context for review: Grey beard in DRG with over 2000+hrs 100% love DRG. Disclaimer: This is not DRG 2 and this is not DRG. It is DRG adjacent.  Having said all that this game has so much potential and thus far I have had a lot of fun with it. Is it perfect? is it as good as DRG? No and No.  What Rogue Core is, is exactly what the devs say it is. A spin-off to DRG, a game like DRG but not. It is fast paced, more punishing and will push you to your limits. It is after all a rogue-lite. I mean what rogue game lets you get easy win after easy win early on? after playing for close to 30hrs I do feel I am progressing.   The Timer: The game has a timer for each level, it can be very challenging to not completly mine the entire level but again this isnt DRG. The timer is there to put pressure on the player. It NEEDS work yes, but the timer isnt going anywhere so get used to it and accept the choice to mine everything is your choice at a risk.  Upgrades: Toxic players exist. If someone picked the upgrade you wanted to have, then adapt and get over it. Find friends to play with as they are less likely to take your upgrade you want. If you play with randos then accept that there is a chance you wont get what you want. As for the pool of upgrades, it only EARLY ACCESS they will add MORE! This game doesnt need a negative review coz you can't handle not getting what you want.  Ammo and & Balance: Ammo is a bit of an issue, and needs fixing I 100% trust GSG will fix it so ammo feels fair. I also trust GSG will fix any other balance issues with the game. Yes spawn rates are a bit crazy and its easy to get overwhlemed even on easy levels but have faith they will get it right. GSG is focused on FUN and CO-OP.  Overall: The game needs a lot of work, but I think with good constructive feedback the game will thrive and be lots of fun, especially for those that want a different flavour of DRG. If you go into this accepting 3 things. 1: This isn't DRG, it plays and feels different. 2: Other players will take your upgrades if you dont like that play with friends or solo. 3: This is in Early Accesss and WILL get better.  So please dont waste your negative reviews on things that will get fixed. Rock & Stone!

I would like to start by saying that if you think that this is like DRG, it isn’t. It’s a much faster game that uses DRG’s mechanics as a base to work off of. It’s a great game and really fun for those who wanted DRG to be faster and more challenging, I personally was wanting that out of DRG sometimes.  Once you get a build going it feels really fun, but the start can be hit or miss sometimes. But as said before by the developers “you’re not going to win every run”  One thing I see from a lot of other reviews is that the choose your upgrades is annoying, but that’s if your team doesn’t want to work together. Just because an epic or legendary perk is there doesn’t mean you have to take it, some other classes in your team most likely would benefit them more. The one thing I would like to change is the fact that it takes longer to get your team together for the upgrade than a solo player. Maybe change it to only half the players need to be in the area to start the upgrade, it’s would save time considering on higher difficulties the timer is much faster and wasting time waiting for one guy is a problem at times.  My one idea for cosmetics and stuff is make it so that we could have weapon cosmetics that could be set up before a run, so that if you got that weapon in a run, it would change to your present cosmetics.  In the end, this game isn’t for everyone and can be annoying during some runs, but it’s extremely fun when you can completely destroy and pull out a win in the most closest situations

Huge fan of DRG, huge fan of Roguelites, this ticks all the itches! There are a few expected qualms from early access, the big ones people talk about are the timer, negotiations, and ammo economy. I actually like the ammo economy (it forces you to think about your build, and to play more strategically as opposed to just running and gunning), but I can definitely see how it could be seen as too aggressive for newer players. For negotiations, I don't think there's much argument here, there are going to be kill-joy players in any game you play, the favorite system is actually really nice, and rarer upgrades aren't always better. For an early access launch I am absolutely loving it, and I can't wait to see what they do as the game grows!

Rogue Core is shaping up to be a really great spinoff in the DRG universe, and personally this early access release has surpassed my expectations.  Ghostship Games is quite talented at making fun gunplay that synergizes with abilities, and we see that here just like we did in the first game.  Deep Rock tends to lean heavily on team synergy while Rogue Core is all about building your own dwarf up over time, while considering what your teammates are working for to a small degree.  Personally I have only played with friends or solo, so I am sure there is an argument that some of these mechanics could be tedious playing with randoms, whether it's waiting to upgrade or being unable to communicate what build you are going for easily.  Some sore spots I am hearing online definitely have their own valid reasons, but personally I love the timer mechanic and the shared upgrades.   For me, this game feels a whole lot like how I felt playing Nightreign.  I was not expecting major innovations, I was expecting a rogue like spinoff of a very fantastic game that myself and others love a lot.  They even share the same timer based structure to force you to think on your feet on what is most important to your build right then in the moment which I love.  I'm excited to see what is next for this title, the scope is small now but the foundation is solid.  If I had to give my biggest gripe that is some of the upgrades/boosters are a bit weak at least on their own.  This can lead to a feeling for some that the upgrade they chose was not impactful for the resources burnt to obtain it, but this can easily be fixed with number changes and/or additional upgrades to flesh out what we have now.  If you loved Deep Rock there is a solid chance you will really enjoy something here and if that isn't the case this just may not be your type of game, it's quite niche with the mechanics it uses to be honest.  Fortunately for me, I love just about everything I am seeing here.

In it's current state this game is not meaningfully different enough from DRG to justify a seperate game.  Sure, it's cool to have new weapons and abilities but I can't help but feel that this whole thing could have just been DLC.  The consequences of the rogue lite formula: each class identity is diluted and roles are not as clear. Some perks are really great and others are so niche I wonder how they justify a slot. Also, they are waaaaay too wordy and activate unreliably due to their specific conditions. Tbh, I barely notice when they activate half the time. Because of this the only differentiating factor are your class abilities. Those are alright but they lack the sauce somehow. I guess that's one of my major issues with this game. There aren't a lot of impactful decisions to make. It lacks "oomf" or "weight" if you know what I mean.  That being said, I think Ghost ship might have something solid to build and iterate on and I think they have a good track record. I don't see why they couldn't straighten this out. In fact, I'm expecting by v1.0 to have a lot of these issues resolved.  I don't regret buying it and am enjoying it but I am a bit disappointed if I'm being honest.

At the time of writing this review, this game is in Early Access. With this in mind, it’s important to review it with the acknowledgment that I am not reviewing a complete product. This is a good game for what it is so far. The three things I see the most complaints about are the timer, the negotiation system, and the ammo economy. The timer should remain in the game. I’ve seen some ideas for the timer to be dynamic in different ways but I’m happy with its current implementation. Shared upgrades should stay in the game. It creates an environment where players have diverse builds in their runs and you fight with consideration for how your teammates are playing. It feels like a rare example of something working better in practice than it does in theory. People who reviewed this part of the game negatively played one or two runs and, fairly, expected that shared upgrades might not be respectfully treated by players, but in my 35 hours so far (plus 45 in the closed alpha playtest), across all difficulties I have not seen this become a problem. I actually wish th game leaned more into this system by promoting more gameplay which synergies with your teammates. The ammo economy is hit-or-miss. In DRG you could call a resupply pod wherever you like, but with the absence of nitra in Rogue Core the pods just generate somewhere on the map. I don’t see a good reason for making ammo unnecessarily difficult to access, so I would like to see tweaks to the spawn locations of the pods such that they are at least somewhat easy to access during elevator windups and events. The total amount of ammo which generates is acceptable, the placement could just use slight changes. The reason I am giving this game a positive review is because it feels good to play. Runs are challenging in a way that makes them feel rewarding to complete. I expect that as this game gets closer to 1.0 and beyond, we will see more rewards for grinding runs and whatnot. The enemies a combat are excellent, in my opinion. They bear similarities to DRG being in the same IP, but have some key differences that make them fun and unique to fight. New weapons are awesome to use. The Devastator, Mag-blaster, Lark, and Bolt-tec are my favorites so far. Another addition I hope to see as we approach 1.0 is more of these fun new weapons, heavy weapons in particular. So far each heavy option is returning from DRG, and I would love to see new ones. Reclaimer abilities are great. My favorite ability-wise is probably Retcon, but all of them are very fun and allow you to really play into a specific niche of DRG combat. I can’t wait for more to be added for us to play with. Visually, the game looks great, and the sound design is very good too. I would really like to see more variety in the deep core biome though. This brings me to some feedback: biome selection does not affect missions nearly as much as in DRG. I think this has to do with cave generation being less intense, which is something else I would like to see evolve. One of my favorite parts of DRG is navigating the caves; I don’t see why that can’t be a part of Rogue Core as well.  I am very excited to see some thing be added to this game as time goes on. It it’s current (Early Access) state, cave generation, is fairly mediocre, but I also want to see more unique expenite events, final boss encounters, and just things to do on each level in general. What’s missing right now is something to make it feel not-repetitive, which is probably already being looked at. “More content” in general is my number one request for changes. My last bit of criticism is that currently upgrades do not feel balanced. I understand that roguelite builds are not supposed to be dominant every single run, but right now it is probably a little bit too RNG-dependent. There are also just some upgrades that are so strong/weak that they always/never should be taken. The solution to both of these issues is to tweak the balance stats of upgrades, weapons, etc. such that each one is on the stronger end, impactful and unique in a fun way that gives you something fresh each time, but not so powerful that you’ll be disadvantaged for ever daring to pass it up.  TLDR: the core (pun intended) systems of this game are fundamentally in a good place. Minor tweaks are fine, but the game works. The changes I want are what I expect will come with time and are mostly related to a desire for an increase in the amount of content.

Other thing is requirement for people to gather up at Ellis - it breaks the pace a bit. I'd rather just have a hotkey to vote for activating an upgrade instead of gathering up - would feel so much better for a fast-paced game.  The timer is punishing and I think that could use some tweaking, just like balancing - but that's something that can only be figured out as the game is played and people figure out over and under powered stuff.  Is the game hard? Yeah sure first runs are but that's like, a roguelite thing? You're supposed to fail your first runs and level up and learn. Frankly, I only failed my fourth duo run. I like the game and the idea behind it, but realistically, the state of it in this first week is bad.    1] There is enough content to play and enjoy for about 25 hours max. After that, it becomes repetitive due to the lack of cave variety, event variety, interesting upgrades, and guns.    2] It has decent polish and very few bugs, but it still needs more polish.    3] Progression is bare-bones.    4] The solo experience is 3x harder than any multiplayer lobby and probably won’t be fixed anytime soon, as this is not a solo game and the issues run very deep in the game design. Plus, the devs have bigger issues to solve right now.    The GOOD thing about the game:    It feels like DRG. The guns, the movement — it’s all here and still feels amazing.    I trust the talent of the dev team, even if they work slow as f***. The end product will be worth it, and speaking of worth, the game is cheap for what it offers and what it could become.    Conclusion — the decision is easy if you know how Ghost Ship Games works and liked DRG. Just buy the game to support the devs and end up with a better game after about 15–20 months. It is very much worth it.    If you don’t trust the devs, just wait. After a year, this will be a great game and a no-brainer addition to your library.

I'm actually surprised this game was (somewhat) review-bombed on release into Early Access. I think Rogue Core is a fantastic alternate game-mode to DRG. I will agree with those who said, "if you like DRG, you won't necessarily like Rogue Core." Although the moment-to-moment gameplay is fairly similar, everything about the progression system and even mission pacing are very different. Rogue Core is definitely the "sweatier" game, definitely more difficult, focused, "hardcore." If you are one of the people who enjoyed how relatively laid-back DRG could feel, this might not be your cup of tea. For those of us who were seeking additional challenges and a more engaging progression loop, Rogue Core is what you've been waiting for. I'm eager to see what all they add onto this game to continue what they've started; it's excellent so far. [h1] Done very well. The early access is promising. [/h1] Let me begin by emphasizing that many negative reviews (not all) suffer from two fundamental issues; the first one being that they try to play like its paced like DRG and not Rogue Core. The second being that they refuse to genuinely communicate with their team.   Now, I refuse to blindly dickride the game, as there's rough patches, but once you learn to play the game, they aren't nearly as bad as copped out to be. They will be fixed soon and will make the experience even better.   [h2] The Good [/h2] - The classes all promote a distinct, unique identity and each have different builds. The gameplay doesn't feel very samey like most roguelites tend to. I have ascended (prestiged) each character at least once, and have Falconer in particular prestige 3. Each character has unique ways of using their ability that might not be apparent immediately. - The community is, from my experience, very willing to negotiate with you on your upgrades. Something important to understand is that some classes benefit more from certain upgrades from others. Slicers benefit from melee boosts, guardians benefit from stun cooldown, etc etc. You are going to have to compromise with your team sometimes, and that's not a bad thing. Sharing rewards adds to the teamwork of a co op game.  - The game rewards proper resource conservation and time management. If you're doing things well enough, you will USUALLY, not always but USUALLY, have time to get everything done in the caves before descending to the next floor. If the game always allowed you to get everything on the floor then the time system wouldn't be punishing enough. The only way a well-designed game is going to allow that is if you're in the top 1% of players. Once you learn when to use your pickaxe vs. when to use your gun, when it's time to leave, and how to save time, the game becomes much easier. Depth 03 is absolutely doable by any schmuck if they've played the game long enough.  - REALLY fun synergies: If you build your gear right, you can have some extremely fun synergies. An example is getting an ice gun, the mag-routed heat perk for fire damage, and boom you're dead. This is an easily attainable way to get huge temperature shock area control value, to the extent you're practically 2 shotting the average grunts with any gun. That's only one example of the many.  [h2] The Bad [/h2] - Most of the elemental-focused perks are just... kinda not useful? They rely on being able to apply status effects to groups of enemies but the bonuses they give for that are pathetically low. I don't mean perks that APPLY elemental effects, I mean the ones that buff you for causing elemental effects. 1% ability recharge for each shocked enemy within range of you is one of the best instances of this. It's incredibly useless.  - Some weapons are just flat out inferior in every way. The LARK, for instance. It has no real build potential and is notably weak when compared to other crit focused weapons like the M1000.  - The tutorial is absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. This is the game's biggest pain point as it leaves players grappling with a lot of features they don't understand. The game currently relies on you reading the reclaimer handbook to understand things and I don't feel that's right. It's a very barebones tutorial that only teaches you to play the very basics and a single ability from a single class. However, it's not very hard to learn the game if you read the Reclaimer Handbook, as it covers a lot of the basics that the tutorial fails to. Still, better tutorial would be nice. - The current scrip system is kinda bonkers. You need about 150 to 200 scrip to get everything there because unlocking a cosmetic only unlocks that cosmetic for one class. I feel like this is NOT a good way to do things because it makes the game unnecessarily grindy. I somewhat feel the same for the perks system as you get 5 or so units per match and when you're getting to the very end of the perk system it costs 4 to get a single perk. This needs a bit of tweaking but it's ultimately not the end of the world.  [h3] Above all else [/h3]  This is not Deep Rock Galactic, this is not an easy game, this is not a game you will learn immediately. The game has so many nuances that even if the game held your hand through it all (it shouldn't) you'd still be learning something new. If you invest the proper time to learning the game, and being willing to fail a few times in the beginning, then defeat will be a rarity assuming you actually engage with it how you're supposed to. I'd like to say I have a mission success rate of about 90% and I'm far from the best player in the game.   The game is early access. Come into it with that in mind.

As a DRG fan, I bought this game without a second thought. I believed that the developers who created such a great game could do no wrong and truly understood how to make games. But Rogue Core is awful. It's not worth a single dollar. From the thousands of bugs to the boring, tedious upgrade system and the immortal enemies. I especially dislike the Expenite concept. You just run around and mine it over and over again just to get another boring or completely useless upgrade that merely tacks on a damage multiplier or adds 100 elemental damage icons, instead of being a cool and fun addition to the gameplay.  I wasn't expecting DRG2.I was expecting a roguelike game with a lot of weapon variations and different ways to handle situations, as that was one of the best parts of the original DRG. I feel so disappointed that I don't even want to write down all the things that make this game so bad.This is another situation where a small company gets money from a really good project made with love, and then starts thinking only about how much cash they can make. They gave this "developed-in-two-days" result to people who were waiting and expecting something great, stealing their time and money without even feeling like they did anything wrong. For all the claims from both developers and players that this isn't DRG and shouldn't be treated as such, the developers still seem determined to cling to the DRG identity of the original game. They justify it with "familiarity," (as said in a Reddit Q&A stream summary) but that familiarity is affecting Rogue Core at its very core. Yes, this is a DRG game, it is set in the same universe, but keep in mind that at it's core that this is not THE Deep Rock Galactic. It is not the original game, and it should not become "DRG 1.5."   If the developers truly want Rogue Core to stand on its own and more than just a glorified DLC, they need to reinforce that through meaningful changes to the overall design. The distinct identity they claim the game has simply isn't coming through in its current state. Right now, it still feels more like an alternate game mode than a genuinely separate experience.  There is no horror, nothing resembling a "dark" atmosphere, the structures lack anything substantial, it does not FEEL like we are deep into Hoxxes IV, there's no signs of anything happening aside from the Core enemies. I don't expect anything like Dead Space, I don't expect this to be filled to the brim with le horror and it shouldn't be, but what was said in the beginning by the developers does not hold at all.   It feels like they sacrificed the atmospheric horror and hardened element of Rogue Core they had planned in the beginning for the comedic tone of DRG, and it shows. The Reclaimers are one of many victims of this.  From the very beginning of this being announced and it being revealed that it was originally conceptualized as a DLC and then moved on to become a new game, I was excited for what it could hold for DRG. What I expected was "professional", more serious dwarves in a darker atmosphere (which was what THEY claimed when the teaser first came out), I played the closed alpha, what we got instead were unfunny Reclaimers, talking about how much better being a miner would be and how the company sucks, why would, a group of supposed professionals, "best of the best" still ♥♥♥♥ talk the company? Were they not miners before they became reclaimers? Don't even get me started on OMEGA.  Some of the male voicelines even sound like they belong to the OG dwarves (I don't mean the voice actor) which I guess is a leftover when this was DLC, but out of everything, this disappointed me the most. They just feel like rehashed DRG dwarves and a poor attempt at their humor, and as a result they have very little personality of their own. I don't hate the miner lines, but they're not serious in the slightest and I'd consider that a false impression since the beginning of this game.   I DO hope the game improves, because there is still plenty of potential here and it IS in alpha. But given how insistent they were on not having the "always sprint" option through the closed alpha until they budged for Update 1, and the pricing, I'm keeping my expectations low, and that's the problem: if it took that much effort and loud protest to get a basic quality-of-life feature implemented AFTER the game launched in alpha and not during the closed alpha, where this was brought up plenty of times, it's hard to imagine the developers reconsidering something as fundamental as the game's identity and tone.  And Ripley is a better name for the R.E.P.D. than Ellis. Calling it "too much on the nose" isn't a good justification. Bite me.

this game definitely needs a lot of work, i'd say its about a 5/10 or 4/10 right now  armor feels pretty useless, feels like every time i get hit a single time it all disappears so i start ignoring armor pickups after a while, especially with how much friendly fire there is armor just doesnt seem worth it at all  enemy design is ehhhh, a lot of stuff is missing audio like attacks or just them moving around so you can get hit randomly by some horde enemy or one of those little explosive guys (which probably eat all your armor instantly) enemy melee attacks themselves are also kinda terrible since they're extremely lacking in telegraphing making a lot of the combat just feel bad, i'd rather be able to react to their attacks even if they'd hurt more that way if i did get hit, unsure about enemy ranged attacks but they also might massively benefit from proper sound design and more telegraphing, i don't actually remember having to dodge projectiles in this game and that would defo help make stuff more fun, combat would feel way more fun and fair if i could actually react to things (staying out of range isn't as engaging anyway)  just feels like taking damage doesn't give me enough info since sometimes i cant tell if its an ally unloading an entire clip onto me or an actual enemy (maybe bringing back that friendly fire resist perk from deep rock would help? it sure would need to give some massive friendly fire resist though, people hit me more than the bugs sometimes... :(    )  balancing could be better too, it feels like slicer only has 1 ability since the dash can't really help you dodge attacks (they lack telegraphs) and the shield just feels bad lol, maybe it'd be nice to have some ability customization or swapping? could be a little broky so maybe some buffs could help instead (retcon feels like they have a WAAAY more complete kit) thankfully slicer's slice is good at least  half the guns just kinda feel pretty meh? i do like some like the charger, shock striker, devastator pistol and maybe some others but so many of the guns lack mechanics, impact or any real flavor to them, in deep rock it worked out since we had overclocks and upgrades but in this game the guns mostly stay the same through a run so they have no alt fires or effects or anything actually interesting, some don't even feel good to shoot at all and could use stuff like more vfx, better sounds or perhaps even launching the enemy ragdoll further (hammercaster should DEFO send them flying, that thing feels weak to shoot rn and is just generally really boring lol) some unique projectiles with mechanics could also help. dunno a lot of these weapons could just do more stuff instead of just feeling the same, also melee weapons would be literally peak  also the game feels a bit lacking in identity? i thought it was gonna be a more horror focused and more serious deep rock in tone but it just kinda... doesn't have much of a tangible tone to it? it sharing voicelines with deep rock definitely doesn't help, omega doesn't really feel very memorable either, not that i was too invested in mission control (i only paid attention to their "annoyed" voicelines so i replaced em with the way more enjoyable greedy texan mod LOL, they simply have way more character too) also i wish the classes just had more personality since they're quite bland, slicer could be some berserker or falconer some animal loving pacifist for example, though i'd also be happy with an option to change gender of the dwarf's voice instead too  dunno if enhancements are enough buildcrafting outside of the randomized upgrades mid mission, could have something else maybe? perhaps some stuff with upsides and downsides? dunno... enhancements just aren't too interesting 2 me, if theres more buildcrafting outside of the run a single build change could warrant playing an entire mission so i'd say it can be QUITE worth it to have more stuff  workbenches could defo have more options, they can be quite bland  keybinds in this game are very wonky for some reason? q + m1 and q + m2? why? it also stays up after you stop pressing q so you can accidentally rewind whenever trying to pickaxe something,  why does stuff need 2 buttons anyway? i'm not on controller... just kinda confused about this one, at least autorun will help i guess?? this is reminding me of how unusable berzerker was in deep rock due to keybindings  hoping content isn't main priority since a lot could be improved first tbh... better enemy design would already make it SOOO much more replayable     now for what i do like i find the pickaxe in this game to be quite fun to use and the abilities in general are a massive upgrade over deep rock's (even if imperfect right now) ramrod is cool and the introductory cutscene and ascension ceremonies are nice, game in general looks a little nicer than deep rock and runs well enough on my computer, having to work out for beer is funny and cave generation with the structures is interesting  also i haven't complained about timer since i do like it nor have i talked about upgrades since update 1 will be making it a lot nicer already  will be updating review if i have anything else to talk about, honestly doubt most of these issues will be fixed though lol... at least darktide is great and far far west has enormous potential so i can play those if this game ever gets tiring

I'm giving a thumbs down because there are some pretty fundamental issues with the game. The following are my top complaints:  1. Ammo economy is atrocious. In single player it works just fine, but once I queued in a duo, it was so over. Also some weapons like the devastator pistol have absurdly low ammo. The damage isn't good enough to justify its low ammo capacity.  2. Not enough healing. If you have to fight a boss and there's no red sugar, it'll end your run, even on lower difficulties.  3. If the boss orbs spawn on higher ground than the terrain behind them, trying to grab the orbs and go uphill or up a cliff can also just end the run. It's brutal.  4. Class exclusivity is non-optional. If you pick one class, nobody else in the party can pick that class. This should be optional. The fact that it's not is just not fun. I want to be able to go duo slicer if I want to. That would be so fun.  5. Certain upgrades are just never picked. There's an upgrade that gives guaranteed crit chance on the first round in your magazine. If the game was a slower pace, that might have interesting niche builds. But rogue core demands that you keep running and keep firing. Reloading just for that crit round is just too risky.  6. I understand the game is meant to be more difficult than DRG, but it feels like the run 100% depends on upgrade and weapon RNG, and not on your ability to make use of whatever you get. It feels bad when I lose because it feels like I lost due to bad game mechanics and not because of my lack of skill. The word I have in my mind every time is "brutal".  7. There's no sell/recycle mechanic. I think that some ability to recycle or trade upgrades would be good. Other roguelikes allow you to make up for bad RNG by selling/recycling items and then being able to buy replacement items later.   I love the franchise, and I'll keep playing the game. I bought this game on the day it released and the supporter pack. I believe in it. I'll give it a thumbs up once everything's addressed. RNS

Plays like base DRG in terms of gameplay, just with randomized weapons/equipment/grenades. Needs more time to cook and become better.  I've played most of the game as a duo with my friend.  When upgrading everyone needs to be near the mule to trigger them, and it's a shared upgrade pool instead of individualized, so everyone chooses an upgrade round robin style and the first upgrade taken is no longer available for other players.   A lot of the weapons feel bad baseline and need some good RNG to get the upgrade to make them good. For example SMGs need a recoil reduction to make them viable. If you luck into an elemental weapon (40% dmg as fire/poison/electricity) then you are able to go a weapon build and have it be actually viable considering how limited ammo feels against the hordes of enemies roguelikes have you face.   The meta progression upgrade don't feel impactful enough considering you can only use 8 of them at one time. Some of the meta perks feel useless, like HP regen threshold. If you have no armor and regen to that amount, you are still able to be downed in like 2 hits, maybe 3 if you play enough to get the 4th tier of the perk. The basic enemies in base DRG can be funneled fairly easily, but in rogue core they can jump so even when you manage to funnel them they can just jump and attack and chip away at your hp/armor. In base DRG you have a shield that regenerates, it's not a very big shield but it's a means to retain health for playing well and cushions you against some bad enemy spawns. Having some means to resist chip damage  would help with balancing.  There is a very strict timer. I get having a timer in that it forces you to move, but here you literally have to keep pushing as fast as you can otherwise you'll hit the timer and face more foes than your limited ammo can handle. By being forced to move so fast you can easily miss side objectives and miss crucial power upgrades that you need to make it through a run. There is also a reaper worm after the timer hits which is pretty much a 1shot if you get grabbed  and pulled to the next level (if aren't saved by your teammates).  The reaper worm is less of an issue considering how deadly the normal enemies are and how you are getting swarmed and killed by the time it appears.   The Jump between depth 2 to depth 3 is crazy high in terms of difficulty. Considering how badly RNG can screw you over, I don't feel I will make the choice to try and go further in terms of depths.  Comparatively to base DRG, where it does get fairly difficult going from 2 to 3 to 4 then to 5, but you can build out your kit to go into those missions, rather than rogue core where you are dealt a hand and have to try and make it work at the same level of difficulty.   Each class does feel different, but some abilities could use some work. For example the slasher has a short range dodge as an ability, but I feel that it doesn't go nearly far enough to be useful in getting out of danger or using it for getting to places too high or wide to normally reach without using your equipment ammo.   While this is a start for a DRG FPS roguelike, it seems that they wanted to change some core functions that make co-op roguelikes work. Like shared upgrade pool, needing people to literally gather around to get said upgrades. In other co-op roguelikes some builds take longer to come online and if one player gets completely screwed via RNG,  a different player can get good enough RNG to carry or at least survive until things improve.   They did a much better job for a roguelike in DRG: Survivor. It was well balanced and most of the RNG in the game comes from what weapons you choose during your run in addition to what weapons are available. You are able to reroll as well so if you get really poor RNG you can actually reroll it and hope for a better option.   If I do want to continue playing this I'll probably play on Depth 2 and wait for things to improve. It'll probably take a few months to a year or so. Currently not worth the asking price. If they keep up with the roadmap and actually take a lot of the feedback people are giving them, then it'll likely be better and worth the asking price.

Well it's in early access and boy does it show, balance is kind of a mess right now.  A lot of weapons are just awful and can't really deal with the hordes in any meaningful way so if your first level doesn't give you a viable weapon then you might as well just die and restart.  Running multiplayer is sort of mixed, you get more people to fight the hordes but the RNG aspect gets annoying.  When solo you get 4 options with 1 being a constant + hp or w/e but each extra player just adds 1 more option for rng meaning that adding players doesn't really help mitigate bad RNG with upgrades/weapons/etc.  In other co-op rogue-likes you can at least rely on one player getting a high roll to carry, you can't do that here so if your collective rng is crap then your run won't go well for the most part.  It also hurts to have 1 epic and 3 green choices and only one person grabs it, I play with friends so we try not to grief but when all the options aside from the legendary/epic/rare are bad it just feels like crap, assuming you even get above green rarity (I had a run where we got 0 rare/epic/legendary for the first 2 levels and it was awful).  Ammo economy is just bad, you can take all the ammo perks you like and it still won't solve the problem once you get to depth 3+, there's just too many and you can't kill them with the ammo you have unless you high rolled a very good weapon and upgrades for said weapon.  The timer is fine on depth 1-2 but once you get to depth 3 there's not enough time to find the side objectives or mine enough expenite to keep your power high enough to kill the hordes.  It might get better with a full party of 4 but when playing solo or duo it's a nightmare to get objectives besides getting to the elevator complete.  Difficulty balancing is also kinda ass right now, since even the basic enemies get a huge distance closing leap and instantly attack you upon landing, there's nowhere safe to be unless you're in a tunnel and force them through a chokepoint.  Unfortunately if you want to get enough power to win in depth 3+ then you have to fight them in bad spots to try and make your way to something that can give you a decent power spike (if rng favors you anyways).  Game runs fine, haven't had any performance issues so that's a good part at least.  Most zones are incredibly dark even with your basic flares and unless you're picking the flare gun it's hard to find all the enemies before they get to you (mostly because of that damn leap).  Compared to the time I spent in the regular DRG, the enemies in that game didn't have huge gap closing capabilities and there were downtimes between foes to get objectives done.  For this game the timer (again) causes huge issues, you need to kill enemies fast in order to make spare time to get objectives done but if your rng is bad and you don't get good weapons or good perks then you fight enemies for 3 minutes instead of 1-2 and now the next wave is coming in 30-60 seconds.  Maybe this game will get better in a year or 2 but I don't have any hopes for it in the near future.  It also doesn't really look or feel all that different from the base DRG so my friends who played that and didn't like it won't give this one any attention so I'll have to wait for patches to make this better.  I don't trust random pugs to not grief me with upgrade picks either so there's no point in matchmaking imo.
