[h1] When a game doesn't value it player time at all! [/h1]  [h2] Preface [/h2] this before rage commenting a gut reactions  [h3] my personal gaming background [/h3] [b] 3k hrs Deep Rock Galactic [/b] - Haz5 to 5.5 or modded - solo and squads [b] 2k hrs Killing Floor 2 HoE  [/b] - solo and squads [b] 1k hrs HD2 D10  [/b] - solo and squads  I love me some rigid PvE shooters that award aim and have a decent foundation. I became a fan with the DRG franchise and its universe after a year of slow simmer when I finally decided to give DRG Early Access a chance in 2018.  That said with DRG:RC I knew it was going into another direction, different pacing and class based abilities.  What leaves me somewhat speachless is that the same developers that delivered on DRG and had so many learning experience over the course of +8 years of active development. Had lighting in a bottle that got them on the board for Top 100 games to play on Steam. It is absolutely mind boggling to me that the same dedicated team of designers programmers artists audio engineers and QA forged this over the last 3 years. In their defence it is labled Early Access. But we no longer charge Early Access prices as Steam no longer enforces developers and publisher to cut down pricing on EA titles unless the game releases. My thoughts are based on group and solo experiences playing through all current available game modes  [h2] Arbitrary Difficulty [/h2] What bothers me to point of a boil is the absolute lazyness and short sighted approach to enemy combat in the current build.  [h3] Ememy Design [/h3] Running an Expert Gauntlet solo per say isn't hard. It is the combination of this arbitrary garbage that leads to getting swarmed by chaff enemies canceling your reload animations over and over and taking hits by enemies that phase through solid objects like half of the time. Yes this is ingrained in DRGs enemy code base, But only maybe consider some good QoL if we didn't had an enemy race that is baised on spastic jumpy 20ish frame rates movement patterns. That have hurtboxes of 140 degree sliding at the edge of the screen at the games max ingame FOV just to land on 2 pixels of a surface  up side down where the player is standing. It is outright boring to take any engagments.  [h3] CQB Combat [/h3] Continuning with these examples is the pick axe swing. While the game forces you to make quick decisions on the fly and take your fights with you rather then hold out on the spot. The game grounds you to a complete halt when a melee hit connects. CQB is needlessly crippled and feels tedious even if you set your perk points (I still call em like that) towards more pick axe damage.  I like chaining precise hits with a bull or an MM-4 but then again the lazyness shows that even the most basic enemy type such as the Creeper will jump from 15 meters sometimes to force you into melee range or pick axe damage. The game turns into a back paddeling simulator at that point were it heavily borrows negative tropes from Killing Floor 3 where the player "is forced to move". This is the lazy way of difficulty as some enemies don't even have engaging meaningfull weakpoint beside the head to deal with them in time. Rather then offering engaing combat you get cheaply swarmed regardless with no time to either use all your utility and mine or quickly help your stupid AI comrad to not light a vein of Expenite and rather mine it for you.  To me it looks like none of the fundamental lessions in creature design or combat was kept. Instead it was thrown out the driving ride mid developement and if you analyize how engaing every other DRG player thinks Corespawns are. Then boy, oh boy - guess what? The majority cheesed the Corespawn event with ziplines back then because these enemies were unintuitive to fight against and lacked all the key pillars of design for fun and engaging enemy combat.  [h3] Movement [/h3] Similar to the Melee issue every other aspect of movement got dumped down and made more unfun and less forgiving in a sense that the players dwarf feel like extra heavy untrained less elite. Grabing onto ledges is unreliable at best. Getting stuck on basically meaningless decals and slopes all the time. It adds to this cheap way of "difficulty". To make use of a traversal tool, a flare gun that is. All that does is feel unpolished to the point that the harsh fall damage and its movement penalty are just the another nagging issue. Straving is almost none existant and bouncing of sloped edges is no longer a thing. All movment tech that was fun in the previous installment is stripped.    [h3] Loadouts [/h3] There is tons of rant for that already. I leave this one short and say that there is a 60:40 ish balance of weaponary currently that is absolutely forgetably. Often enough these loadouts are offered in Gauntlets so you pick the least offending firearm.   I will add to that but I am bored out of my mind thinking of this game rn.

The good: - Slightly better graphics than Deep Rock Galactic (DRG). - The anti-gravity ball movement mechanic. - Weapon variety.  The bad: - Multi-level missions - every mission is a deep dive-like slog from start to finish. In DRG you could play an average mission in 15-20 minutes depending on skill and difficulty level. Now you're committing at least 40-60 minutes regardless of skill or difficulty level. - Exhausting and repetitive mechanics. Run in, side quests if you have time, elevator down, repeat 3+ times per mission. - Subpar cosmetic system. Fewer cosmetics than DRG. You're buying the same cosmetic 5 times (once for each class) except for pickaxe parts which inexplicably apply to every class. Less a cosmetic tree than a cosmetic matrix which seems to favor getting to a cosmetic as quickly as possible instead of having to work for them. - All the different monetary devices don't make sense. Merits, scrip, and whatever the thing is to unlock more beers. Just let me use money to buy what I need. This is reminiscent of when phazyonite was introduced in DRG; it was a useless addition to a system that already worked (scrip + credits). - No more class agnostic skills. The most critical example of this is Iron Will. In DRG you have multiple chances to pull yourself out of a bad situation if everyone on the team has Iron Will. Not anymore. Only the Retcon has instant-revive so you have *one* chance to save a mission at each level. - Everyone needs to be in the same physical location to upgrade.  The ugly: - A timer resulting in infinite waves, effectively punishing players that want to clear each level of the cave. - Roguelite/survivor style ruined what could have been a great game. I hate this mechanic so much. It makes zero sense in what should be a coop focused game. This turned the game into a grief ridden thumb twiddling simulator. - The game is completely depersonalized. It took away your ability to design your dwarves around your play style and preferred loadout and turned it into luck of the draw that pivots on a team that respects the "heart" system for picking items. - The lack of transferrable skills between classes (e.g. Iron Will) means that you can sink 60 minutes into a mission and be one hit away from failing the whole thing because only the Retcon can "iron will" after death.  The above is not an exhaustive list, its all top of mind. I really can't understand what GSG was thinking with this game...it feels like they took Deep Rock Galactic and smashed it together with Deep Rock Survivor then said "yeah, good enough" while simultaneously ignoring all the stuff that made DRG a great game.  At the time of writing I have something like 2300 hours into Deep Rock Galactic and ~40 hours into Rogue Core. It was a waste to create this as a separate game especially considering its been in the works for ~3 years; it could've been 1-2 DLCs. Its has some cool new weapons, one new traversal option, and some interesting "add-on" items like the turret item but aside from that, the game is effectively worse than DRG in nearly every measure. It also doesn't get more fun as you progress. The slog is the same regardless of how many times you've ascended. The game-of-chance is the same. It effectively takes out the potential for any long term enjoyment. I could never see myself coming close to the amount of time I've invested in DRG.  Sticking with the idea that there's enough content in Rogue Core to justify a completely separate game (there isn't), it would've made much more sense to embrace the mining and RPG-potential aspects of DRG. For example, let me name my classes, fine tune their looks, unlock more weapons, etc. From the mining angle, give me an actual mining mission with some complexity instead of what Rogue Core has done which removed mining options (no more nitra, dystrom, etc) and made it way too focused on the FPS angle.

This game is very poorly optimised for solo play. You can brute force it with a little bit of skill, a fair bit of practice and a ton of luck, but unlike the original game, you will almost certainly lose a lot of runs due to uncooperative procedural generation, and also unlike the original game, much of the progression systems shoehorn you into playing the harder difficulty levels very quickly, and it wouldn't surprise me if a lot of people end up tackling higher depth levels before they're ready to, failing many runs due to said uncooperative procedural generation, and having a much less enjoyable experience with the game because of it.     It's possible that playing public games alleviates this to some extent. I wouldn't know, I won't enjoy the game if that's the intended experience. I certainly wouldn't count on every single person running or joining a public game being pleasant to play with. I have zero doubt in my mind that the official Discord server is not a good place to find people to play, either, based on the behaviour of several members that are very active on a regular basis, and most of the people I play the original game with struggle with that, so I can't imagine them handling the increased challenge of RC any better.    I quite enjoy playing the original game solo; I have completed approximately 1000 missions alone across all four classes, many of which were in modded difficulty levels that are far harder than anything in the vanilla hazard levels, so I was disappointed to discover how unfriendly RC is to solo players. Also disappointed by the amount of people in the official Discord server whose arguments against making things just a little bit easier for solo players boil down to "uhhhh wellllll i can work with it so lol skill issue" and it's certainly unexpected from a community that almost constantly gets praised for being wholesome and welcoming.    I did read the many disclaimers that RC is intended to be a very different game from the original. I did go into it expecting it to be a different experience. I am still disappointed by the state of it, and as it is, I would not recommend it to anyone unless you're willing to suffer a bit. I'm sure it will get better as GSG progresses through Early Access, but right now? I would say give it more time at the very least.

[h1] Rogue Core the lesser DRG [/h1]  I have more than 600 hours in of the previous game (survivor doesn't count) Deep Rock Galactic and 25 hours in the Alpha of Rogue Core.  To say that Rogue Core is a disappointment is an understatement, there are a few new good things: cools new weapons, cool new characters abilities, new(ish) enemies, better graphics (nothing revolutionary). But thats it the rest is a step down from DRG.  The game tries to be an roguelite and fails at making it interesting. Where other rogue games make you play to unlock new items, abilities, upgrades, characters, etc. RG let you unlock health upgrades and ammo upgrades and crit upgrades and other boring stuff, you can only then equip a limited numbers of them. You also have by default a small deck of boosters (upgrades) you then grab in-mission, there are no new boosters its just the boosters of the other characters that you can unlock the mix and matching of. Such innovation, less is more or so they say.  The time limit, while necessary for this type of game, is badly implemented. Between the waves of enemies, the mining and various points of interest you often don't have time to complete the map on the highest difficulties. Lets not even talk of the Gauntlet game mode where the game immediately end (wtf), but in regular missions when the timer end the game then spawn some very nice tentacles monsters (one for each dwarf) which disable you and then kill you, only a teammate shooting the thing for a bit will free you.  You might think "thats not so bad i've played plenty of game with disablers before", well the thing is that they are invincible, thats right you can't kill the disabler only shoot them a bit (once isnt enough) to make them back of a few meters. Brilliant, what fun it is to die because you forgot to shoot the grabby monster every 10 seconds while dealing with a horde of enemies. Also note that each tentacles will not only target the dwarf that made it spawn so if you play solo you only have one to take care of, but if you play with a full team of four well now you can get targeted by 4 immortal grabby friends at the same time.......  In-mission when you mine enough of the green mineral the team have then to gather around to select perks further worsening the time limit problems. Waiting for your team is such good fun, we can look each other in the eyes and contemplate our own mortality while some git is running around doing something else. This is a co-op game so the devs thought it was a good idea to make us compete for perks, you only have a few choices to pick when everybody finally manage to gather around and if someone pick the perk you wanted before you well then tough luck, pick the health upgrade or some other boring perk. Same for your guns and mobility equipment you be at the mercy of RNG and your teammates so if you really dont like your gun well either restart or get ready for a boring game.  Somehow the highly trained and equipped reclaimer team have forgotten that shields (that the DRG miners had access to) exists and instead decided to wear the most advanced and cutting-edge technology: Armor. ??? A coat of mail is more advanced than a device creating a defensive energy shield around your stunty you see so instead of a nice recharging shield you now have non-recharging armor, a glorified second health bar, why? If an lone enemies you didnt see hit you and take of half your armor you then have to backtrack to a resupply or a armor box if you want to "top off". Thats good gameplay.  The personalities of the characters feel bland. Gone are the the silliness of DRG now reclaimers are voiced by professionals actors who somehow do a worse job of giving ours dwarves an unique personality than the one dude of DRG. The more "serious" voicelines doesnt help, the devs must have thought that DRG was too immature, too crass because a game about silly dwarves running around shooting stuff didnt need levity OH GOD FORBID. Mission Control has been replaced by OMEGA, an AI with a Mean Voice (not in a good way) and a mean character. So now the rightful place of a true dwarf have been taken by a bloody AI screaming in our ears to hurry up and whatnot. Awesome.....  QTE minigames for virtual beers!!!!!!!!!! Thats right to get a beer with various funny and unfunny effects (no actual gameplay effect) at the base you have to "work-out" to burn the beers calories that you will virtually ingest............................................ SUCH A BRILLIANT IDEA!!!! WHATS NEXT? A QTE TO PLAY THE JUKEBOX? A QTE TO START THE MISSION? A QTE TO LEAVE THE GAME?  I like DRG a lot, i expected DRG but greater and instead i got a mix of bad ideas mashed together to create a frustrating game. The core of the gameplay is there but thats it. It could get good in a year or two but i won't hold my breath.

TL;DR I hate the jarring shift in setting and circumstances, like no mission control (the robot guy sucks), and the time jump into the future, as well as the lack of glyphids to fight.  As a greybeard with 1800 hours in DRG, over on Xbox app on PC since my boys didn't have PCs at the time, i can say im not a fan of the direction they have taken this game, and i don't mean the rogue lite aspects. I truly don't like how its some time into the future and the space rig is derelict and you aren't playing as the boys anymore but rather characters and there is no missions control.  It's just too jarring of a shift and i don't like it. What i think would've been better is for the game to take place right after the events of the core spawn season. How Rogue Core is sometime in the future makes it feel like everything that happened in DRG was pointless, as far as fighting the other companies, fighting the rockpox, and the core spawn, not to mention all the minerals i mined!  I also have some gripes with the gameplay, but i wasn't able to play too much of this game as i was just too put off by the setting. The two major complaints I have with the gameplay is when you land from a moderate fall, not one far enough to do damage, you do this little stutter landing animation thing and this just kills a huge part of the movement for me, i view this exclusively as a downgrade. Another problem i have is the enemies. Obviously I'm not expecting Rogue Core to have all the enemies DRG has, since that game has been out for years and Rogue Core just came out into early access, but not fighting glyphids is HUGE downgrade, not sure if they plan on adding them but i do not feel the core enemies are as dynamic, visually appealing, or well designed as the Glyphids from DRG. And this is where i am honestly VERY pessimistic for the future of Rogue Core as most of the issues i have are products of the setting and the jump into the future. These are things that the devs can't possibly change without redoing large sections of the game.   Not a fan so far.

Some of the rogue-lite elements like the timer, etc. are fun and interesting evolutions of DRG, but the classes are boring, the builds being dependent on RNG is goofy, the armor layer is lame, the upgrade mechanism forcing players to gather is frustrating, the new cosmetic system feels too grindy, the difficulty is through the roof (significantly more than Haz3 that the devs claim), and a lot of what made DRG special is gone. [h3]Solid foundation buried under an incredibly frustrating design decision.[/h3]  My biggest issue with the game is the shared upgrade system where players take turns picking upgrades in a random order. Since many builds rely on/benefit heavily from specific upgrades, one inconsiderate teammate can completely ruin your run by taking a key upgrade you need. If that upgrade never appears again or appears late, you're screwed.  [b]This means the strength of your build can be determined by another player's decision rather than your own buildcrafting abilities.[/b] In a roguelite, where build crafting is supposed to be one of the main sources of fun, that's a terrible feeling. It constantly creates tension between players and makes public lobbies far less enjoyable than they should be. The game has essentially the same problems as DRG (button pressing in bad moments), and then it adds another even bigger problem on top of it. How did this feature survive past alpha? I have no idea.   [b]This issue alone makes me unable to recommend the game in a good faith unless you have a dedicated 3-4 player group[/b]. With friends who communicate and cooperate, the system is perfectly fine, although a bit annoying at times. With random players, it is an absolute awful experience when you encounter that one person that should have never left solo.  There are other issues, like performance problems (particles causing fps drops AGAIN) and balance stuff (some weapons without upgrades are way stronger than some fully-upgraded weaker weapons, some classes definitely feel stronger than others, same with upgrades and artifacts), but this is not as important, as it will be eventually fixed and adjusted. Right now, Rogue Core feels like a game with huge potential held back by a system that actively get in the way of player enjoyment. Unfortunately, they seem stubbornly attached to the shared upgrades mechanic that creates frustration rather than fun, so again, I don't recommend this game to people who don't have a dedicated premade group for it. If you do,  go for it, as it gets really fun once your build comes online. Otherwise, stay away until GSG sobers up and realizes their mistake.

The bugs in DRG were memorable and had a lot of character. These "The Last of Us"-style mutants with exposed brains just don't do it for me.  And where are the minerals? Mining was a huge part of what made Deep Rock Galactic unique. Now it feels like you're only collecting a rehashed version of Nitra. Heck, you can't even buy beer anymore, you have to work out for it.  My friends and I ended up going back to the original DRG after a few sessions because that's where the excitement still is. Pretty disappointed so far.

Huge DRG fan here, (1000 hours on DRG and 300 on survivor), always happy to shove money at GSG, but I can't in good faith recommend buying this.  It's not just the lack of content, the repetitive/boring gameplay loop, or the fact most of the upgrades are unoriginal/fun and either make you go "meh" or are ridiculously situational with no fun synergies.  There is something at the core (pun unintended) of the game that makes me worried it can't be fixed without a massive rehaul, and it mostly comes down to the fact that it seems GSG can't decide between doing a time attack fast paced survival fps, and a social multiplayer class driven roguelite where you have to sit down and discuss tactics and synergies to better optimize your (otherwise very punishing) long game. That or they're miles away from finding the secret formula to make these seemingly completely opposed and complex concepts work together.  This is all particularly worrying when taking into account all the time (3 years now) GSG has already spent in the game, considering that again, imo, the problem sits at the very core of what the game *is*... and that's without mentioning the bugs, heavy imbalances, placeholders, or simply the fact that an overwhelming amount of assets are reused from previous games! (not a bad thing in itself but you'd think it'd save them time to work on *new* things)    P.S.: Very subjective opinion here; I, for one, am very glad that they took a different direction with the tone of the game, but as of this day it kind of sits awkwardly between DRG's wholesome camaraderie (which I kinda miss tbh) and a darker more serious setting. The thing is, when the gameplay is already frustrating, repetitive, and even tedious, frankly, the sinister and monotonous voice of the very authoritative and single minded AI operator (OMEGA) makes it all the more dull and kind of forgettable... a shame. But I really hope GSG will prove me wrong and I'll get to reinstall the game!

It's just not in a good state right now. Being Early Access doesn't mean that you get to hand-wave issues, it just means that it's probably going to get better.     Things I'm really hoping to see:  - The caves are boring. I know they're supposed to be mineshafts, but it's all kind of just Orb Connected With Straight Line. It makes the biomes all mesh together too, which is a shame since weird and wacky caves were a major like in DRG.  - The upgrades kind of suck right now. At least before this update there'd be multiple times per run that a level-up just gives me absolutely nothing I want, or there's like one good item everyone wants, so I have to just take the scraps. I don't need Binding of Issac level interplay, but it feels like there's only 3 "builds" to work towards, and it's entirely random anyhow. I'll get an electric gun and see zero electricity perks, but then see 3 fire-related perks when the only fire weapon on the entire team is one dude's grenade. This is the one I'm most confident will get better, but at the same time is my largest downside. Upgrading just isn't that fun in this game, and that's REALLY important in a roguelike.   - A lot of the guns kinda just stink. There are notable exceptions and there's cool ones. Sure hope you roll them and don't need to spend workbenches foregoing upgrades to get a gun that feels good to use. AoE damage is at a severe shortage in the *hoard shooter game*, so unless you have a special perk or find a heavy weapons crate, hope you can hit weakpoints. You REALLY need to.   - Please give Omega a virus to shut him up oh my god like actually bro I want him gone holy frick. Turning the volume down in settings isn't enough, I want him DEAD. Such a downgrade from Mission Control.   - Make the vending machine work, I want more energy drinks.     It's just sort of disheartening that the recent update's content is things people have been asking for, not in Early Access, but as much as a YEAR ago in the Alpha. [h3] PREFACE [/h3]  [b] The game is currently not worth your time. [/b] GSG needs to pivot drastically and change the gameplay for Rogue Core to be worth the asking price and to justify it not being a DLC for Deep Rock Galactic.  Without re-iterating the issues brought up in other reviews, I mainly want to keep my review about the subjects I have issue with that were not commonly discussed or mentioned here. Those being:  [h3] MOVEMENT & GAMEPLAY FEEL [/h3]  The gameplay direction of Rogue Core is wrong. It should not feel like a clunkier Deep Rock Galactic. The Reclaimers were hyped up as being 'Rambo' style badasses that are sent in to accomplish one goal as effectively as possible and get out. DRG without any of the fluff and a cooler, darker, more threatening tone. But they move just as slow and cumbersome as regular dwarves- all the while being less proficient with their weapons and having no rechargeable shield.  The Reclaimers (and Rogue Core as a whole) should be a *faster* game than Deep Rock Galactic. The fact that missions in Rogue Core last on average 30-50m while Deep Rock Galactic's missions are cleared in 10-30m is proof that Rogue Core is NOT following the intended/advertised direction of a "faster pace" game.   GSG needs to acknowledge the potential that this is a *brand new* game that can be *anything they want it to be*. Take the reigns off, make the Reclaimers feel genuinely robust and different to play. There is no reason why they can't move two or three times faster than they do now. Obviously make the caves larger and the enemies more numerous or whatever else you need to maintain a balance with this new direction- it's just absolutely dismal, boring and unfun to play right now.   Even the most "broken" builds in Rogue Core feel lame and unsatisfying for the type of game it's trying to be.    Change course, it isn't too late for Rogue Core to be just as good as Risk of Rain 2 or The Binding of Isaac.   [i] 900+ HRS in Deep Rock Galactic [/i]

[h1][b]"Ya lousy excuse for a dwarf, I'm gonna rip you a new arse. "[/b][/h1]  [i]- [u]Baldur's Gate, dwarf Kagain[/u][/i]   First of all, i was the beta player of original game back when the original game was a fresh early access title in 2018 and i knew back then that the game had huge potential. From Q1 of 2020 it was [b]THE GAME[/b] I've played each week to this day with friends because of how great value the game brought to our lives. With that said at the time of writing this review i have [b]2,169[/b] hours, mainly playing as Engineer.  Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core officially shifted from being a planned new game mode of original game to a standalone spin-off shortly after its initial announcement in October 2023. Ghost Ship Games realized that the new game play loops, systems, and mechanics they were prototyping fundamentally altered the base game and warranted a separate release. [h1][i]But were they good assumptions?[/i][/h1]  [b]I don't know, [strike]though I suspect so...[/strike][/b]   The developers appear to be prioritizing changes that many long-time DRG players never asked for, while core concerns about gameplay direction remain unresolved. Whether this is driven by market trends or a deliberate attempt to broaden the game's appeal is open to debate, but the result is a product that feels increasingly disconnected from what made the original Deep Rock Galactic successful. The shared perk and weapon selection system often feels frustrating rather than strategic. A player can spend an entire run working toward a specific build, only to lose key upgrades simply because they happened to be last in the pick order. When progression is decided by turn order rather than player decisions, the roguelike element starts feeling less rewarding and more arbitrary. At its worst, the system can completely undermine an otherwise well-planned build. Replacing shields with armor feels like fixing something that was never broken. In the original DRG, shields clearly communicated how much punishment you could still take before risking your health pool. Rogue Core's armor system still allows damage to bleed into health, making every mistake feel more punishing while offering less clarity in return. A shield layer on top of armor would have preserved the strengths of both systems instead of sacrificing one for the other.   [h1][b]“A game for everyone is a game for no one”[/b][/h1]  [i]- [u]Arrowhead Game Studios[/u][/i] [h2]  What also i found that does not feel right with the title.[/h2]  [list] [*]Despite being presented as a separate project, Rogue Core often feels extremely close to the original game in terms of assets, animations and overall presentation, making the standalone decision harder to justify. [b](hell, you can find grappling hook in the files and code)[/b] [*]Constructive criticism from long-time players often appears to be dismissed in favor of preserving the current vision for the project. Criticism of Rogue Core is often met with heavy-handed moderation on Steam forums and Discord. Over time, this creates the impression that feedback supporting the current vision is welcomed, while dissenting opinions are far more likely to be removed or dismissed. [*]The original DRG rewarded careful exploration, teamwork and long-term mission planning. Rogue Core pushes the experience toward a faster roguelike structure that often undermines the deliberate pacing that made the original game memorable. [*]I understand that we are talking about fantasy creatures in space but i do not think the female dwarves are a good addition here and the decision has proven controversial among a significant portion of the community. For years, the franchise established a very specific image of its dwarven workforce. Introducing female dwarves only within the elite Reclaimer teams raises questions about the consistency of the setting and makes the change feel disconnected from the identity that long-time players became attached to. Especially when they are class-locked. [/list]   If the early access launch serves as a wake-up call for the developers, the game can still be improved significantly over the next year despite its current reception. I wish the great success for [b]Ghost Ships Games[/b] but even that has boundaries. I am not against innovations and breaking boundaries, but not like this...  But... it costs nothing to promise...

DRG rewards picking various classes to complete the team because they each have a different tools and specialties but lets you stack multiple if you want; this game forces you to one person each class even though they all get to pick from the same pool of random tools and weapons and have no synergy. DRG has persistent upgrades and unlocks that feel meaningful and interesting; this game gives you small percent increases to stats that couldn't be less inspired. DRG has lore-increasing mission control who you grow to love and like listening to and reminding you of stuff; this game has a horrible overlord computer that's annoying as hell to have gawking mean ♥♥♥♥ at you.  TLDR; DRG is fun; this game isn't.  It's mind blowing to me cause DRG Survivor is so good they've shown they know how to make a fun roguelite already. This game just had to sit between DRG and DRG survivor but they somehow forgot everything they learned making those two games.

As it is right now, the gameplay needs work. You receive next to no ammo, your guns generally are stock with their upgrades barely affecting them, a single hit removes most of your armor making you super squishy, the list goes on.   You get an ammo crate in the beginning of a level, sometimes one near the middle or end. A single ammo resupply feels stingy, at the very least give us more max ammo so we can carry more and resupply more. By the time you make it to the end of the level and need to defend an elevator it seems that almost every single time I need to pick up a resupply. Guess what? The resupply box is at the very beginning of the level, either you run to it and back in a mad sprint dodging enemies and facing the punishment for going over the timer, or you defend the elevator with whatever little ammo you have left so you can walk into the next stage still out of ammo.   The timer creates such an unfun loop where you are forced to rush through everything and you always feel like you can't catch a break. The Reaperworms you face as punishment for going over the timer are a stupid mechanic considering it already calls in hundreds of enemies all at once to fight you. I understand it's to emphasize the timer mechanic, but if I want to do literally anything else other than book it straight for the next floor I get punished.   The difficulty in missions seems to amped way up compared to what you would expect, which while I have no problem with that for a full team, if I go solo or with a single friend it becomes substantially harder since we don't have the extra players helping. Knowing I can solo a hazard 5 mission in DRG without too much trouble, somehow this game is nowhere near as forgiving.   I respect the team for actually putting effort into the game to make it feel different from Deep Rock Galactic with the differences, new classes, all new enemies, and new objectives. I just wish they saw some of the major issues that everybody seems to be having with it before release. I want to play a game to have fun, and this game has potential to be really fun if it weren't for a few of their game design choices.

NOTE: This review is from a mostly solo players experience  I don't usually write reviews for games but this one I feel like I have to. I've played the original DRG for over 3000 hours and I was excited for a roguelike version of the game. Both DRG games sharing the same universe and all is cool and the game visually looks great and it runs fine for an Early Access game.  Let's start with the upgrades. 90% of them are completely pointless, and the only useful ones are the ones that either increase your DPS in the bossfight, or let you siphon health one way or another. even the solo AI helper (Cooper) mostly has upgrades that change its base stats one way or another. So when you get an upgrade, the default always available health boost is almost always the best option. Special upgrades like heavy weapon crates or artifacts are the only ones that can change your build in a meaningful way.  Then the missions. Solo difficulty is a headache and the difficulty is all over the place. Playing depth 1 or 2 is laughably easy even with no upgrades but depth 3 offers some challenge. Solo depth 4 however is nearly impossible in many cases, since the timer is so short that you can only dream of doing expenite events or even mine all the expenite from the walls. At depth 4 swarms happen a lot sooner and they often last so long that the next one spawns basically immediately after the first one ends. Even the original DRG Haz 5+ seems easy compared to this games depth 4 solo.  The base upgrades your characters can unlock and equip at the lobby are just flat stat boosts and offer very little build variety, if any.  Then there are the missions. In short, they are repetitive like no other. Having the mining facility scaffoldings and elevators as a part of the cave is a nice change from the original DRG, but within 3 missions you've seen all the variety there is. The caves themselves pretty much have no difference at all, as in the rooms are mostly the same size and unlike original DRGs biomes, the ones in Rogue Core dont offer any real dangers or advantages, even though they are copied from the original DRG. The biomes matter so little in fact that I don't even think about it when I'm deciding which mission/facility to play. The elevator objective is always the same "protect the generator" type, that got repetitive during my first mission, since you have to do this between every single level of every facility. Bossfights are fine as is and are one of the only good things about the game.  Cosmetics are lackluster at best but that is expected for an early access game. Not really important right now but if you were looking for that, you won't find it here.  The good thing about the game is that, it's mostly bug free in my experience, which can't be said for a lot of Early Access games. There are a few bugs here and there but no major game breaking ones from what I've seen.  In conclusion. Do I recommend this game? Not right now, but possibly later on, after which I will change my review. I have a feeling that Ghost Ship Games can turn this ship around, but for now I recommend playing the original DRG instead if you haven't already.

I've struggled a bit to rate this game. I hate leaving negative reviews, but I know I can't leave a positive one for this either. I am basing my rating on whether I could literally recommend this at its current price and state.  I would not recommend this game. I am someone who has hundreds of hours on DRG and roguelites.  I also think this game has a great foundation and a great potential to become a great game, and I have enough faith in GSG to listen to the community and bring it to that point over time. Their track record with the base game has proven them to be good developers. When the game is improved or the Early Access price is reduced, I'll update the review.  If you want to support the studio (which, I could be wrong, but imagine has enough funding already) or just see what's in the works, it might make sense to buy this game. But don't expect anything amazing yet. I am having fun because I'm playing it with someone who I can enjoy anything with. If I were in public lobbies, probably not.  Main points of criticism:  The timer should be a toggle, a mode, or a setting. For some people, the added challenge can be fun, but for others, it can make something that's otherwise casual and lighthearted become stressful. Yeah, it's not hard to know when you need to leave and call it quits on a level, but I also don't find it fun to rush it.  The negotiation system needs to be reworked. I play with just one friend, so we just talk over our build synergies in VC, but it would suck so much in a public lobby where maybe not everybody is co-operating or even in VC to respond in time. Even then, I doubt it'd be fun to try to coordinate it with 4 people for a pool of 4 abilities. Maybe give everyone their own pool of equal rarities to choose from? Or maybe +1 reroll per turn in the pool? Even keep the same system but increase the ability pool size? I don't know, but what's here doesn't work right now.  I think the game needs to lean into its roguelike side a bit more. Most of these abilities aren't very interesting, fun, powerful, or even synergize super well in comparison to many other games in the genre. It makes you wonder why the roguelite elements are even here if they're not going to bring some of the best qualities of a good roguelike into the game. I rarely get excited when I see an ability available in the pool. Most of them are just a minor stat-up. Sure, after a while, I can start to do a lot of electric damage with Falconer after I take enough electric/stun abilities in succession (assuming I get lucky), but that's about it. I hit a bit harder. Stun a bit more often. Do a bit more AoE. It's just not that interesting. It would be more fun if they introduced new mechanics or modifications to the properties of things more often, instead of just taking a few basic elements and effects and upgrading their damage and frequency. I want my bullets to gain bounces. I want to look down while firing a minigun and start floating. Swap places with the falcon and deal AoE damage + knockback. Progressively destroy more terrain with my bullets the longer I fire. Give an assault rifle's bullet the hitbox and penetration of a breach cutter. Spawn friendly corespawn minions when I kill them. Make my ammo my health but give me lifesteal. As of right now, the roguelike elements are just kind of doing what a more traditional progression system could also do, but worse.  Also.. lower difficulty levels would be nice. I can't imagine trying to bring people inexperienced with the base game into this.

Okay, first of all - choosing upgrades in a queue between players sucks.   You gotta remove it. It creates tension between players. Making all the players come to get an upgrade also sucks. Due to that I had an experience of players insulting each other in first 10 hours of playing a game. In DRG that almost never happened. I think players gotta pick upgrades independently from one another, so that they won't take upgrades that others may want.  I see 2 ways of doing that:  1) When upgrade is available, then everyone, no matter where they are, get the upgrade screen and choose together the upgrade that everyone wants. But all people can pick same upgrade if they want. That way people can easily set up command builds for freeze/fire/sludge/crit/AOE etc. This will also force command communication to discuss what options to choose. To favour each others' builds. This will remove this awkward time spend waiting for your turn while others probably will take your preferable upgrade.  2) Do the same as in option 1 but with the difference that players will pick up upgrades absolutely independently. So that each player gets random upgrades. This makes things more complicated, cuz you can't make a 20 upgrade options on a screen to make people see what upgrades others are choosing. Nobody will look through all that))) So I think option 1 is the best.    Secondly - the timer. I understand how it works, and why devs added it. But the penalty is too ridiculous, especially with large worms. You basically can't play game after timer ends. Maybe with top tier perks etc it is possible, but I don't wanna struggle couple hundred hours just to play normally. And I've played 2000h in DRG. You need to find other way of making penalty for going out of time. Maybe create some sort of side tasks that motivate players to do mission objectives quicker, or otherwise they will be locked. At least, make these worms unable to reach so far. Make them sort of stationary enemies that guard certain dedicated area. Enemy spawn after timer ends is also insane, and it increases instantly. A LOT of players aren't used to play with limited time. They wander around the cave, then the timer hits and everyone is done, cuz enemies start to spawn insanely, players are apart and can't protect elevator. I'm thinking about making it grow through stages. Like, for example if you're late, then enemies start spawning continuously, if 3 minutes late - then more heavy enemies spawn, if 6 minutes late - then they start spawning in insane amounts like right now.    Third. Fire napalm grenade - PLEASE remove friendly fire damage and make it progressive like in DRG. When a greenbeard smashes napalm under your toes and you burn instantly - that SUCKS. Make   progressive indicator of heat, where if you are too long into a fire, then you burn. Cuz right now this grenade is almost unplayable.    Also the digging in this game is now almost useless. And that was huge part in DRG. And everybody loves it. In Rogue core it's kind of and accessory rather than necessary thing. MAybe add some secondary objectives where you need to dig through to get to them. That will make digging more necessary.    Ammo crates economy sucks. I don't like it. Why not make nitra spawns and call for ammo when needed. It was fine in DRG. This also sucks to search for these crates especially when you're running out of time with no ammo. This happened to me continuously. If you don't wanna add nitra to make people struggle wasting time while  searching and digging it and running out of time, then I see 2 ways of fixing ammo problem:  1) Ammo crates drop continuously throughout the mission. Make it drop for example on 2nd minute on a mission and 10th minute in a random location nearby one of the players. Or maybe next to elevator so that players won't waste time searching for it. Because searching for it sucks.   2) Or make it as a skill that is shared by all players. And players can call for 1 ammo crate each 5-8 minutes. So if they don't need it - fine. If they do - they call it instantly where it's most needed. This will also improve communication and keep ammo economy scarce, but entertaining. That way you can add some negative traits for missions where for example ammo crate calling taking more time. I understand that in deep reclaimer missions there is no connection to the outside, so calling ammo crates precisely makes no sence, but at least launching them approximately inside the cave as I said in option 1 DOES make sence.  3) Or even better option, for each mission at the beginning drone can grab couple of ammo bags from start elevator and carry them for dwarves to access it whenever they need. But of course, drone will carry limited ammo. But the players will be able to ask drone to drop it whenever they need. It is also possible to make drone require some expinite price for ammo drop. Thus, no nitra needed, no search for ammo crates, no extra time wasted.    If these problems are fixed - game will feel nice, it will keep it's core, allowing players to play and improve. Leaving a lot of time for devs to think about other mission types, weapons, skills, cosmetics etc to add.

So let me preface this by saying that I really do enjoy this game, but it needs a LOT of work to make even trivial runs feels satisfying.   I've been an avid DRG fan for about 6 years now and I was also on the hype train for when this game got announced, playtests, yadda yadda. When you first play the game, it feels incredibly fun and hectic, and it feels like you need to coordinate with your team and clear stages, which is all well and good and makes certain runs feel incredible once you finish them. However, once the honeymoon phase of the game wears off, you quickly realize how rough a shape this game is in currently, and I know GSG will buckle down and make it as fun as Deep Rock, but here's where my issues lie.  - Guns, while fun as they are, really struggle from a lack of ammo, and certain guns like the Charger pistol and the Shock Strike *devour* ammo and mean that in certain runs, one wave will be all you need to expend all your resources, which is an unnecessary tension builder. - Resources are highly limited and coveted and as such, I have the biggest problem with the queue system for upgrades (for the uninitiated, every upgrade initiation that is received must have ALL players present in a small radius -- which can be modded to not be like that, but even then it will cycle up 1 time every time of each player that can receive priority for upgrades. This leads to an honor system, which ever so rarely will be violated by select players who will grab upgrades just because the word Legendary is slapped on it, and there's no take backs on upgrades.) Ammo, as mentioned before, is so unbelievable scarce, with some rounds not having any except at the very beginning and never more after that, which if you don't have a Spotter, can make for some stressful runs. - The armor system is very inconsistent and causes a lot of frustration for most teams. It is not like Deep Rock's shield system, where the shield takes the brunt of all damage before breaking, and then dealing health damage. No, the armor only blocks x amount of damage on hits, so it doesn't matter how much you have sometimes, you can get rinsed by Clamorheads and Edgestalkers in record time because your armor doesn't protect you that well on higher difficulties (D-03 and D-04), not to mention that on said higher difficulties, regular Creepers can 3 shot a surplus of 80 armor (which doesn't regenerate unless you have certain Epic/Legendary upgrades, which are also limited to one person being able to get them at a time), and this is exacerbated by the fact that the enemies in this game are freakishly agile and mobile to the point where hitting them can be a challenge because they quite literally bounce around surfaces like chimpanzees and have weirdly long melee ranges, so if you're not playing Retcon and all three other players go down, you might as well say goodbye to that run. - Oh my GOD Vanguards are so damn annoying. They literally are the grunt/tank hybrid from hell. They hunker down for like 3 seconds after you shoot them once, and armor penetration and armor breaking are so negligible in this game that you can end up dumping tons of ammo into them before you kill them because their defense mitigates about 90% of incoming damage. It's not worth trying to engage them sometimes. - Merits are just too hard to earn consistently, and the fact that all the perk slot unlocks and perk upgrade trees rely on them makes it a little too grindy, in my opinion. You don't get them from completing missions in the traditional sense, you only get them from completing accolades and you need to buy extra perk slots *for each character,* which can easily be in excess of 10k merits overall. The only other way to earn them consistently is through Gauntlet, which is a mode that is so annoyingly overtuned in difficulty and unfun to the point where everyone I know that has tried it will only try it about twice and then not again anytime soon because you only get your upgrades AFTER completing a stage, and the enemies are never-ending on Gauntlet, so you never get time to breathe, and are hanging on for dear life the entire time.  All in all, it's fun for what it is, but like I said the frustration as soon as you hit higher difficulties sets in quite fast, and I'll be playing it but it needs a lot of work to make each run have that equilibrium of challenge and fun.

I wish there was an uncertain rating, instead of just thumbs up or down. The tl;dr is that rogue core is currently a true early access game: filled with flaws, but the developers are working on them.  I plan to revisit this review after some of that work is done, but do not think it is currently worth the purchase if you want to have fun now. Just play DRG instead.     Rogue Core aims to be Deep Rock Galactic, but with rogue like elements. It currently falls short of that, with respect to the Deep Rock Galactic elements. There is only one mission type: you follow a power cord through a cave, completing side objectives along the way. Side objectives generally unlock upgrades for your run. You repeat this in four to six caves, then kill a boss in the final cave. The mission then ends immediately: no frantic extraction to escape the angry swarms.     This brings up the biggest problem: this game has none of the variety that DRG offered. No different mission types or different cave types. Every mission is essentially the mining mission type and cave generation of old DRG, but with no extractions.  Where the game does have variety is in the random upgrades you can accrue for your character. Though it is currently missing several classic guns from DRG. (or maybe I've just gotten unlucky and not seen them ever spawn).     The class design is interesting, but limited. Each class gives you about three abilities you get to run into missions with, along with 2 decks of random upgrades that can spawn from bioboosters. Level up a class enough and you can swap out its decks for those of other classes. So you have a little bit of influence over the RNG, but not much.  What really irks me here is that the game devs have forced lobbies to only have one of each class. This was not the case in DRG, and it should not be the case here. It works in a game like Vermintide, where the classes are actually unique characters with unique dialog, but we have none of that here. It just results in people feeling bad that they can't play their favorite class.     The roguelike elements have potential, but currently I think they aren't great. You get relatively few upgrades each run, so you don't have a lot of room to define a build. Instead the best strategy seems to be to just take generically good upgrades each draft (weakspot damage and crit chance mostly), rather than leaning into specific niches. I think I would prefer the game to be balanced around harder enemies, but more upgrades for the player.     Whenever I play this game, I find myself looking back fondly on DRG, and wondering why not just play DRG instead? I love rogue likes, so in theory, DRG plus roguelike elements should be my jam. But currently, the game loses too much from DRG, and the roguelike elements added are not good enough to replace it.     That being said, the developers are very responsive to the community, and they already have created one of my favorite games in DRG. I am sure, if given enough feedback and time to cook, Rogue Core will turn into a great game. It just isn't right now.
Undercooked, not raw. It just needs a little more time in the oven and community feedback!

I'd recommend if it was on sale or marketed it for like $15, for $30 its simply not in a good enough state. For context I've got well over a thousand hours in drg and over 200 in gunfire reborn. For a rogue like the systems simply don't encourage interesting builds, and for a coop shooter the systems don't make me feel good about playing with teammates. Shared upgrades is a fun idea and it has its moments where forfeiting an option altruistically feels good, but never being able to build into melee because you have a slicer on your team compounds the lack of upgrade diversity and makes runs feel even more similar, and then you are last pick at the weapon terminal and get stuck with the devastator and want to flood the room with a deadly neurotoxin. Having the class ability upgrades and weapon upgrades in the same terminal feels a lot less fun than them being separate as building into your ability feels like ♥♥♥♥ when its on cooldown and there's a plethora of ♥♥♥♥♥♥ guns you can get stuck with which compounds this feeling. Class designs are interesting enough but the ability upgrades are pretty boring and often just ability duration or ability damage, make some simple upgrades like falconer giving two drones with 66% damage of a larger drone or something, or slicer ults in a circle around him instead of straight forward. The timer mechanic isn't the worst but overly punishing and having no way to increase time feels pretty ♥♥♥♥. Being the last one alive and trying to res everyone next stage which spawns a wave and then you die just feels like ass every single time, and even if you do survive you're just playing from behind the rest of the time because it takes like a third of your timer to get everyone up and healthy. Run difficulty is either overly hard and you are struggling against the timer, or overly easy and you are steamrolling completing everything every stage. I'd say players should be rewarded for completing events with an increase in timer length, there's tons of events that are just awful and not worth it because they take so long and don't reward you enough. Movement feels like complete ass with the addition of a slow down when you fall a small distance, I don't know why this is even here because the core spawn don't have any trouble keeping up with you anyway unlike glyphids. Build variety is seriously lacking and you feel like you get power in the same way every run. In particular building into fire is broken and cryo and poison have not nearly enough upgrades to make it any good in comparison. also the elements can probably be consolidated. Why is there poison, radiation, and acid? That's so asinine and just makes it so much harder to get synergy between elemental upgrades and weapons, just consolidate like all three of these together or something. legendaries are way too rare as well. You're lucky to have one person on your team get one in a max length run, ideally each player should get one legendary every run and that single handedly can guide your build, but the legendaries are honestly pretty damn boring. Why not make it so a legendary causes you to radiate heat, or every 50 shots causes you to fire your grenade? Why would I be enthusiastic to pick 150% more health from red sugar? It also just feels like ♥♥♥♥ having to wait for everyone to upgrade at every terminal. I'd say just making it so 2 or three people wanting to upgrade should be able to start a cooldown to prompt the upgrade ui, making everyone have to meet up just makes managing the timer feel even worse as you have some guy who has no concept of his own existence pissing time away. The movement tools and grenades all being from DRG feels pretty ♥♥♥♥. Having to make one player the sacrificial flare gun loser is annoying, these are dedicated facilities anyway, why is light still such a difficult resource? I'd move more of the burden of lighting the caves onto abilities and classes, the head lamp shouldn't be this wimpy in terms of light output anyway. I don't understand why biomes are just ported 1 to 1, if you just invert the primary and secondary colors or make the hues generally darker with depth it'd make the games identity feel a helluva lot more unique, also the max depth biome is ugly as ♥♥♥♥ and contrasts horribly with biomes like REZ and glacial strata as it becomes more prominent. For a comparison to gunfire, what made that game succeed in its early access was being able to buy a bundle and get all your friends to play, why no bundle was made for this seems insane. $30 bucks is incredibly greedy for early access and prohibitive for a bundle is my guess I suppose, charging people $60+ bucks for a four game bundle would have no one purchasing it, compared to $45-60 bucks for four copies of a game that retails for $15-20. Hope they can turn this one around.

i'll just cut to the chase. rogue core is a somewhat mediocre spinoff that feels like it should have been shot down before being approved for full production. the slower paced objective oriented gameplay for drg has been substituted for a linear design where each floor is just a footrace to the elevator with some uninteresting side objectives on the way. the upgrade system has been replaced with a rougelike perk system, which is a mesh between the vampire survivors level ups and the risk of rain 2 item system. not that it's any fun, as the outcome of your run is determined *entirely* by luck, as nobody on the development team has considered adding upgrade rerolls to a rougelite game (they put this on the roadmap, but that doesn't really excuse the fact that this isn't a feature on day one.) while i love the original game, this just isn't it. it's not fun, and there's not much of anything to sink your teeth into for a game that's priced equally to the original experience. i hope it'll get better with time as it is an early access release, but as it stands right now, purchasing this game at full price is a massive misstep.

Rogue Core is, to put it bluntly, a game with problems. While I like the idea of a DRG Roguelike game, there are serious issues that are heavily holding this game back and prevent me from recommending it. I will highlight the most egrious in no particular order:  1: Difficulty. The Difficulty of Rogue Core is overtuned in terms of enemy dmg. You won't feel it until you hit Depth 3, where the game suddenly spears in difficulty and enemies are hitting you for high amounts of damage. And if you get two waves overlapping with each other, lights out. You're not recovering from that. The high agression paired with the high damage makes for a fustrating experience, but its fine at Depths 1 and 2  2: Multiplayer aspects. I know a lot of people have issues with the timer, but I don't think the problem is the timer. See, if you're to play the game solo, the timer is long enough for you to get almost (if not flat out) everything in the level before the game starts actively trying to end you. As long as you keep moving, and keep doing something, you'll be fine.  Mutliplayer turns the timer from a challenging but fair aspect of the game into a fustrating aspect that that will make you turn on others. Having to wait for others is annoying, but when you're under a time limit, it goes from annoying to infuriating. I've had to kick people from lobbies because they were taking their sweet-ass time and effectively throwing the game. I have NEVER done that in DRG, and I have hundreds of hours in that game. It doesn't help that the timer isn't dynamic: If you hit max, the game throws out all the stops. Why not do something like Risk of Rain where the timer steadily increases the difficulty? And why the hell do we have to wait in circles for others? Why can't it be an F4 like Mann vs Machine, or save it to the end of the level?  3: Underwhelming options There are too many upgrades that are either too niche, too weak, or require too much setup to work well. Not to mention some items feel like they're supposed to be used as setup, but there's nothing to capitialize on it. I think it's saying something when so many people just skip for health often.  Overall, Rogue Core needs some serious improvements before I can recommend this. Even if you're an avid DRG fan, I'd stay away for the time being.

So after several hours diving into facilities and trying things out, I have to say I don't recommend Rogue Core as it is right now. They are on the right track for what they want, but as is? Wait until they hammer out the fine details.    A lot of the gameplay is right where it should be. Side objectives, getting exp to level up together, upgrading weapons. All that is just right. But there's a lot of minor things that keep hurting the entire experience. Boss fights? Pretty brutal. Can be done. Almost all of the difficulty comes from where the boss spawns as opposed to the fight itself. If it's on a sheer cliff face you're bound to have a bad time.    Traversal weapons are either completely useless 90% of the time, or relatively useful. Comparing a low ammo count of a zip line to the platform gun is just a joke. And having the flare gun feels awful when the flares won't stick half the time. At least the drills still feel great.    Guns? I've yet to find a gun that feels strong. Everything is lacking. Either the damage is laughable, the accuracy is terrible, the time to kill is way too high, or it's all three. And with basic enemies requiring two point blank shots with a shotgun while also hitting the weak point? It's a terrible experience. Starting off a run with a bad weapon is awful enough you might as well start over. Upgrading guns is nice, but with how limited your options are when you get to upgrade? It's basically left to RNG if you get something useful.    Enemies have some nice variety too, but with them being able to swing (and hit you, or objectives) from insane distances with no wind up, from other sides of walls and platforms all while they are crawling up and you can't even see them below you? It doesn't feel like something you can play around. You just have to take the hit. Getting hit by melee without even being able to see the enemy is straight up awful, and since there's no wind up it's just free damage they constantly get.    The class powers are great though. Everyone feels like they have a role to fill, and they can do it well. Cooldowns are not punishing, so you get to use your stuff often and you can get a ton of value out of everything once you are comfortable.    At the end of the day I like what they are going for, but it's just not enjoyable in its current state. It just came out into early access, and these devs seem to have a good relationship with the player base so I have hope at some point I will get to say. "This game is great and you should give it a shot."

I was very excited to play this game, and was looking forwards to a more roguelike take on DRG, however, currently this does not feel like a roguelike DRG, it simply feels like DRG with 'randomized' kits, new classes, and a more heavy focus on combat.  My biggest gripe is definitely with the upgrades that are available as they feel like can simply be boiled down to do more damage or take more damage but without any sort of conditions that make you play to the strengths of the class you are using, or change playstyle according to the build you create during each run. (see games like Skul or Risk of Rain 2). For instance, you could offer overclock like changes to weapons as upgrades rather than simply more accuracy or more damage, then offer upgrades that play into those changes at level up  I def will continue to play and see how the game feels after playing through a few more missions, however, as things are now, it simply feels like a watered down DRG.  Now, all that being said, the new weapons DO feel really nice, and I like the fresh take on classes that are more combat and recon focused as opposed to the more utility based classes of DRG, and I think that they fit the current theme and play patterns of Rogue Core very well, and look forwards to seeing how the games development changes as we play through early access. It's just... Not good. Not yet.  The lack of balance across the board is appalling. Taking a step from Deep Rock to go for a more roguelike experience is a fun idea but the game is almost entirely lacking in what makes roguelikes fun. There are very few weapons and perks all things considered and a large majority of the perks are massively uninteresting and often nearly useless. So many of the actually somewhat interesting build options are nearly pointless to even try going for. With how difficult it is to get matching elemental perks AND a weapon with matching elemental damage it doesn't seem worth it to bother unless you stumble into it on accident. Most of the AoE perks are absolutely tiny. Crit seems to often be the only interesting thing to spec into and it's... Not interesting. There are a few more interesting perk options currently in the game but most level ups you're going to be seeing +12 armor, increased heavy attack and skill regen while standing still (why are there so many perks centered around standing still in a game where you HAVE TO keep moving constantly?), or +10% melee damage per frozen enemy within arms length from you. You always have a health boost option if all of your perks suck which you will have to consider a lot more playing with a full group and it's not good and it's not interesting.  Speaking of not interesting, the classes are just not good. The controls for abilities are strange and clunky, much of the controls in general seem to have issues like melees randomly cancelling, heavy objects you pick up immediately dropping, the falconer's drone immediately recalling if you dare to try to melee shortly after sending it out. Controls aside most of the abilities are hardly useful and rarely worth even bothering to use. There are some perks will give you bonuses while your abilities are on cooldown but you have three and it doesn't elaborate on what that means. Does it apply when any are on cooldown? Is there a specific main one that counts? Most of the main abilities have a relatively short cooldown as is so lo and behold another perk that seems almost entirely useless!  There's only a small handful of guns in the game and most of them don't feel great or are relatively niche in usefulness but worse than that ammo feels absurdly scarce most of the time to the point that I often don't want to bother shooting most enemies. Even without ammo being considered the guns tend to be so weak most of the time that hitting them with your pickaxe is just better and far more efficient.  Mission timers are a thing in this which is a fine idea and the pressure adds to the game so you can't just casually farm out every scrap of XP per floor, however the way this is currently executed is terrible. When time runs out core worms will start spawning that will seek characters out and try to grab them like a cave leech would. You can shoot the worms to make them retreat but they will immediately start coming back towards a target. If they happen to spawn right next to you or on an objective you can't really do much about them, they will immediately grab you. Worse is that the closer they grab you to where they came from, the sooner you'll be killed. The moment they pull you back to their base you're gone for the floor with no way to be revived. So if one spawns close to something important or even just right behind you, then you risk being immediately grabbed and killed. Why isn't there a radius around objectives and players that they can't spawn in? Why isn't there a minimum timer for the execute? This feature feels like it hadn't been thought out in the slightest.  Part of what makes these worms so frustrating is the timer is largely at the mercy of the maps random generation. Random generation is a core part of deep rock but in this case if you end up with a long path with difficult terrain and important objectives behind the escape elevator... Your set time limit is far more punishing. Many of the objectives you can do for bonuses are also massively imbalanced with the amount of time or risk they take. Sure you could just ignore these, it honestly seems like the most optimal option to almost exclusively ignore them, but then what's the point? The point of a roguelike is to get that scaling and level ups and become powerful. They really would have been better off making the time limit an endless horde that intensifies.  Much of the enemy design is frustrating all around in this game too. Most enemies are nearly dead quiet so they'll easily run in behind you to get a surprise hit in so you can spin around and smash them just for another enemy to run in and hit you from behind while you do that. I get that the point is you move as a group and keep alert but it feels horrible that enemies are often quietly running in to get a free hit of chip damage before being killed while you're busy racing the timer. A lot of enemies are absurdly tanky for no reason which makes the ammo problems far worse. The worst offender are edge stalkers. Thin and tall enemies that keep their distance and spam projectiles from afar while strafing around often. You would think an enemy who is thin and agile and spams projectiles while running away to keep distance would be delicate and yet... This is the second tankiest basic enemy in the game. Why?  The bosses also just suck. They're incredibly tanky with invulnerability phases and all of them have near unavoidable damage options that have little to no interesting counter play. It's a tedious chore to fight them at the end of a mission instead of an interesting and fun finale. The bosses in Deep Rock were fine, how did they get so much worse here?

The game is super fun and makes you want to keep playing, but it’s just brutally hard. Even as a veteran roguelike player, I find it really tough. A lot of the mechanics feel unbalanced—way too many enemies, insane damage numbers. Honestly, winning or losing is just pure RNG, not about teamwork or skill.

Huge fan of DRG and veteran of many many rogue like games including a lot of shooters. I'm not making this review based on a comparison to DRG, they are in different genres and i understand that.  There are some cool features here but the design seems to indicate a fundamental lack of knowledge about the genre. The classes are extremely boring, and they abilities don't scale enough with upgrades to make them interesting/relevant.  There are some cool upgrade synergies and I know a lot more will be added in the future so I think they're doing OK in that aspect.  The biggest promblem here is the movement of the player characters. The painfully slow, unresponsive clunky movement was serviceable when playing DRG but here in DRG:RC its an absolute travesty. You are facing hordes of melee creatures attacking from all angles, with extremely limited ammo and little to no protection abilities/equiment.  So much of the fun, creativity and skill cieling in games of this genre comes down to free rapid movement and movement tech/abilities. And here you just have to waddle all over the place and tank damage from 100 enemies.  The most egregious example of this is [spoiler] The flying whale boss thing, part of its attack cycle is a standard charge attack that you're supposed to dodge and let it run into a wall to get stunned. Well, this thing can start its charge from 100 feet away and yout stubby little dwarf legs still wont let you move the 6 feet you need to move to the side fast enough to dodge the attack. Meanwhile it ♥♥♥♥♥ landmins all over the place adding to the woes of your inability to move around.[/spoiler]  Losing a 1 -1.5 hour run for design this bad is making me put this game down until I read patch notes about movement buffs, hopefully inclduing an any direction short cooldown dash for all classes. Maybe they need to include into the lore that reclaimers get mini exosuits to improve their movement or something but whilever it remains the same this game is dead to me.

Games fun, but also has no shortage of issues. I like it a lot but will be holding off on recommending to others for the time being  Short Rundown: -Timer shouldn't be so oppressive  -Opportunity for high-risk, high-reward -Negotiations need to be more seamless/friendly  Long Rundown:  Timer gets really suffocating after a while, binary life/death style becomes really dreadful, I kind of wish that they dropped the reaperworms, they feel like a copout for making an actual unstoppable tide. A never-ending wave is already enough pressure given the limited resources the reclaimers have to work with, just up the intensity as the wave drags on, maybe add some unique enemies that would be to suffocating to use in normal waves, adding to the eldritch themes of the game as you face more and more twisted enemies.  I also feel that the timer should be part of some kind of high-risk, high-reward choice, stay and test your mettle for further riches or leave with your run still afloat. It would give something for the seasoned reclaimers to hone instead of whatever the hell the gauntlets are. I really just feel like there should be a higher ceiling of what is achievable, because there's a finite amount of expenite in each stage/run and that means there isn't a difference in build progression between the reclaimers that barely got everything in the stage and ones who picked it clean with half the timer still remaining.  I haven't played enough multiplayer to get an accurate read on the negotiation/barter system, but I feel like such a hardline social contract is in antithesis of DRG. I do not wish to compare it to DRG, but RC should still beholden to the rules of co-op shooters even if its older brother also is one. I can see why the barter system is in place for things like weapons and equipment (so that we don't have four homunculi with flare guns trying to kill the gatekeeper via blindness) but I see no reason why it needs to be in place for main upgrades seeing as it's a massive pool that cannot be influenced by the players and each individual upgrade doesn't have nearly the same impact on gameplay.  Either the upgrades need to be personalized (each player gets their own pool), or, if GSG is committed to their choice, I would allow players to tag out their place in line with other reclaimers and have upgrades be replaced with a different random upgrade once selected, effectively letting players pass their turn in line to someone who has their eyes set on an upgrade, the tagged-in gets their upgrade and the tagged-out gets to see a few more upgrade choices if the current pool isn't particularly appetizing. Players shouldn't be punished with having less options because they chose to play with others, most of my (especially early) gameplay was solo and I would have dropped the game immediately if I saw every fourth upgrade only had two options and one of them was the boring health upgrade.  Also I threw an empty beer mug at bartender omega and he said something along the lines of "was that a pathetic attempt at unionizing?" lobby experience is still the best across all of steam

I cannot recommend this game at the time of writing this review. There are too many obtuse systems. The roguelike aspects are shallow and not fun. You barely make any choices of your build, and you barely making meaningful choices from the few choices you already make. The base game loop in Deep Rock Galactic worked better. I love roguelike games but this is not a good one. The metaprogression is too slow and artificial stat boosts. The ability upgrades are few and mostly cheap damage increases for the classes I played. The guns you get don't matter and you barely get any gun options during a run. There's also a lot of 'players ready' waiting points. The game will not even let you go to the weapon selection section of the mission without all players being ready. The station already makes you wait but you still have to wait before that.  There's also nothing encouraging experimentation, and no explanations of any mechanics. There's no training zone in the ship. There was no explanation on how to use character abilities in the tutorial mission. There is no space to learn about the damage elements and whether they matter.  One class has an ability that is literally not usable in singleplayer. The other ability is completely useless even with upgrades. A lot of manual samuel activation and barely any payoff if at all. 20 seconds cooldown on a slightly better melee slice.  When selecting upgrades you have to choose in turn with other players - this makes the whole game slower for barely any coordination. It also leaves some players sour if they both want the same thing. The choices themselves can sometimes not be condusive for any build. A lot of them don't even present themselves outside the UI - no one wants a +damage on a melee attack you can only use every 20 seconds and doesn't even 2x its value.  Missions are also not varied whatsoever. You can see the grand majority of everything this game offers in your first 5 runs. A run can last upwards of 50 minutes with the final boss just being a gimmick fight and damage sponge that cheapshots you. Infuriating is the least I'd describe the lack of payoff for going through the slog.  You can't even feel yourself sprinting, and they've been hesitant to add a quality of life feature in the form of auto sprinting...  I wish the devleopers well - they have a ton to fix. And to change.
