If you prefer solo-play, don't buy this game. Your time and effort are not respected at all as the game is exclusively balanced around group gaming. The regular corespawn are far more bullsh*t to fight than any group of enemies in standard drg, tankier, faster, jumpy and climbier while also coming in from pretty much everywhere makes it an absolute slog to do solo, even with a better Bosco-esque drone to help. The tankier enemies are super-roided up with their health and spawn only slightly less often than drg praetorians, while having health that feels far more excessive (as again, the game doesn't adjust for solo in any respectful manner) Meta-progression is pretty average, in-match upgrades for weapons and skills are mostly bland. In four hours of play, aside from the usual dirt walls (which are much thinner and less effort here than drg) I only had to dig terrain Once. Maybe this changes at higher depths or complexities but it makes me wonder why they bothered including the drills as a secondary instead of the grappling hook which would have arguably more versitility. Ship hub is fine and new if bland, the ai overseer has less charm by far compaired to mission control.  Exercise system could be interesting if it was tied to something else like a slight stat buff depending on what you used last, instead it's to pay for the same beers from yester-game  Maybe it's fun if you're playing with a group of friends but for me? Dissappointing, and I don't see that changing anytime soon.

The core gameplay is a lot of fun, but the issues of the timer being too punishing especially at the early depths / higher complexity levels / earlier floors inside of a mission - and the fact that all players are expected to gather around the drone to apply upgrades (not bad in solo or 2 player missions but a pain in 3/4 player lobbies) put me solidly into "can't recommend" for the time being.  Shared upgrades and items is also not super great, though I would feel better about it if many of the lackluster upgrades were buffed to be better across a variety of character classes - and just a balance pass in general.  Friendly fire also fit fine in DRG but for Rogue Core I think it would be better left out. Though because it can be mitigated somewhat, it isn't something that would ever "make or break" the game for me.

People wrote a lot longer and with more insight than I care to, I already sent proper feedback through the right channels, but it genuinely feels like nobody at the studio actually plays the game they're making. I don't think the concept of "DRG with roguelite elements" is inherently bad, but this execution of the concept feels like a joke.     As it stands right now, DRG Rogue Core is to DRG what Back 4 Blood is to Left 4 Dead 2.

This could've been a DLC/Elite deep dive mode. The game fundamentally plays like DRG in terms of player feel, but objective wise is entirely different. This WOULD be fine, if it weren't built off DRG in both design, gunplay, and aesthetic (not to mention community, who have been starved for proper gameplay content for a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ WHILE).  While people will try to say "It's entirely different! It's not gonna be anything like DRG!" the game is named after, copied off the skeleton, taking place in the same universe, and played BY the DRG fanbase. As a result, it's rightfully going to be compared to it's counterpart.  Levels are barren, linear speedrun formats with 1-2 XP side objectives, a bio upgrade, and a worktable with a chosen vector reward present. No extra ores or anything of real note. Cosmetics are cool, but limited since you don't HAVE weapon loadouts, thus can't customize weapon designs in any notable way (might change, it is in EA after all. it's a fun note though since THIS supporter pack is missing colors+frameworks due to that problem). Small note is that pickaxe customization still hasn't been updated to allow for head parts to be put on either side in base game. The perk system is fine, but barebones and flavorless in comparison since the vast majority is just "have 15% more max ammo" and the like. Classes, for what they're worth, are great and I like them as an idea. Having equipment be separate has removed some charm and inadvertently builds/styles, but they are a cool idea and I hope more is done with it. The NEW guns are mostly sick as ♥♥♥♥, and the old copied over ones have small updates and tweaks that make them feel better (sometimes). Of course, these could've easily (and still hopefully can be) retroactively added to DRG as well. Bugs and other interactions from DRG are still present here in so many little forms, mostly in the stuff that was copied over, like the flaregun shots sliding off/disappearing when shot at specific generations (and normal terrain somehow now). The good news with this is that catching people to prevent fall damage still works :) Armor over shield is... fine. Not all that interesting of a change. It gives guardian a decent purpose and is used as an aspect of various upgrades, but due to said things and refills galore it doesn't actually change all too much in practice. Beers serve no purpose. I mean yeah I still like the flavor of it, but there wasn't going to be a reason for beer anyways since all the buffs have no purpose when all the CONTENT NEEDING THE SPECIFIC BUFFS ISN'T PRESENT.  Overall, not a good state or impression in any capacity, and mostly just makes me sad that so much of this will never be put in DRG for a better, more interactive game environment. The roguelite design limits it drastically so far, and despite the fun little quirks it gives, may be strangling a lot of possibilities unless sweeping alterations are made later in development. Honestly, I was originally going to give Rogue Core a good 10 hours before making a review for it, but I just can't force myself through the drudgery that is Rogue Core's overly stripped down missions. I expected a lot better from the devs as Ghost Ship Games.  And I'm sorry if this review becomes kind of long, but I have a lot to say, so please bear with me.   And this is to the devs at Ghost Ship Games specifically.   If you guys can't handle criticism from users, then I will just say this to you guys.  Either learn to maturely take and act upon fans' criticism like adults or get the hell out of the gaming market and go do something else! Because you are [b]NOT[/b] entitled to success nor are you free from criticism just because you're a smaller game studio!  I don't care if that sounds rather harsh, I'm no one's yes man nor will I give any devs a completely free pass without some damn good reasons for it. I refuse to only tell people what they [b]want[/b] to hear, and these guys need to actually listen to fans and not get on any kind of high horses and fight with their fans or whatever.  Don't be like the stupid higher ups at companies like EA, Blizzard, or Activision who just do stupid things that come back to bite them. I don't think you guys are like that, but don't start down such a path.   But honestly, the GSG devs [b]SHOULD'VE[/b] made Rogue Core a proper DRG2 to solidify the DRG series instead of trying to go off and make a spinoff game like this.   And I for one think most of us would've probably been fine with the GSG devs taking current DRG and copy and pasting its data and using it as the foundation for building upon that to make a proper DRG2, but with many new additions of course.  But Rogue Core isn't a truly horrible game by any means, but it feels like a giant mess of a game none the less, like the GSG devs wanted to just make a roguelike FPS game, but then decided they had to tie it in with Deep Rock Galactic, and hence we have this messy game.  I probably would've liked this game more if the devs really worked to either go a bit further with making the game more of a roguelike/lite game, with no classes but maybe some skill trees that allow players to choose a bunch of different generalized roles, or, like I said, make the classes far more distinct like in DRG, but with a few new classes as well, because the classes in Rogue Core feel very lackluster and like major downgrades from the ones in DRG.  But honestly, game feels incredibly underwhelming because of how the roguelite elements seem to work against the fun aspects of a co-op shooter game like DRG, like how there are no persistent loadouts to drop into a mission with, it feels like there's no point to even trying to choose different playstyles, as it's all just random nonsense.  And as many have already said. That damn mission timer [b]has[/b] to be removed as a fixture of all missions.  If it was a little longer and only as a type of difficulty option to select for a Lethal+ level of difficulty, that'd be one thing, but it feels like it goes by so damn quickly that's it feels oppressive rather than actually adding any kind of legit challenge to the game.  But I think that the GSG devs should not try to keep Rogue Core bound to all the bog standard roguelike stuff, like random missions and weapons and so on, but try to break the mold and add in some elements that are not only different but could potentially still work within a roguelite game.  I could see a Weapon Forging mechanic for either Rogue Core or a possible future DRG2 where you can essentially forge your own upgraded versions of the different types of weapons in the game and drop into missions with them, but there's an element of risk to it.  I would say that the risk for such a possible mechanic could easily be that there's a chance to lose such powerful custom weapons if you fail a mission, so it could become a bit of a meta game of sorts where you weigh bringing powerful custom weapons with you or not.  But I honestly think that Rogue Core has plenty of potential, but the devs seriously [b]NEED[/b] to listen to fans and not just try to stubbornly stick to keeping the game as overly restrictive as it currently is.

Every gun feels terrible to use, only a few are somewhat decent after you upgrade it. I don't necessarily mean their strength but the gunplay in general feels bad. It's like the game was made with ADS in mind but never actually implemented it, so you're stuck with an inconsistent feeling hipfire profile for everything that feels like it has an identity crisis.  So if the game doesn't really want the guns to be the focus, then maybe the abilities of the characters are the main combat incentive... Nope, your abilities have long cooldown times, effects that last a very short amount of time, and that feel nearly decoupled from the character as a whole. The power of the abilities is also fine here, much like the damage output of the guns. The difficulty / damage of this game isn't the main problem, it's that it feels like the game is created in a way that makes everything inconvenient to use and clunky.  The boss fights are uninspired copy pastes of eachother with minor differences between the actual boss mechanics. The bosses themselves are huge bullet sponges and the game doesn't provide you with enough ammo to kill them in half of the runs, so you end up needing to backtrack through the cave to find an ammo drop that you didn't already collect, all while the boss ignores the cave walls and snipes you while clipping through the environment. It doesn't carve holes where it went, doesn't blast open the caves as it trudges through rock, just phases through until it does a move that locks you in where you can't do anything but take the damage and wait to die.  The start of each run has an entirely unnecessary time bloating entrance sequence where you activate two robots and run through a small cave into a room where you pick up your weapon and grenade for the start of the run, nothing too egregious here, but it wastes a lot of time for something that could have been implemented on the drop pod you get sent into the mission on to the same effect while saving all of that time.  You are playing a cooperative game that has you competing against your allies for perks where the person choosing first is randomly selected and then rotated for every pickup after that. This can often cause one person to get completely shafted on their drops because people take the strongest perks first, and when it's your time to pick, the whole table rolls commons. So not only are you limited in the quantity of pickups in a single run, but you have to double roll on RNG to even get useful items and also in a way where you can actually pick them.  The character upgrade tree seems quite expansive at first, until you realize that this huge tree you're grabbing is actually just selectable perks that you can slot into 8 slots that you unlock per character, you do not gain the effect of the whole tree. This isn't necessarily a problem, but does lead to the fact that these early bonuses are almost entirely unnoticeable on your character. Sprinting for a few seconds to gain a 5% sprint speed boost is not useful in the slightest. So you're stuck feeling like the worst version of the character until you reach the end of the entire tree to be able to select a perk that may have a 15% effect on one aspect of your character.

"A coop game, that fails in coop"  in it's current state I cannot recommend this game. Good Point: It's Deep Rock Galactic with the dwarves.  But the "Rogue-Lite" Game is currently a big let down because of many decisions, that are just bad.  The game itself looks well made.  Design, Dwarves, Enemies etc. are looking fine and the core of the game feels promissing.  The rouge-lite aspect is bad though! Balancing is definitely not good. Lots of the Upgrades are useless, or just garbage. The shared upgrades are a bad choice. Good upgrades are taken and then you just take more health. Honestly... this shared mechanic sucks so hard. Especially because everyone needs to group up. Love it when 3/4 are waiting for the last player and he decides "nah, not upgrading"  Also other decisions: for example when players are down and the team progress deeper. The start of the next stage is just annoying, takes too much time and in 80% kills the run because you have to fight waves without the team.  This mechanic is just annoying. It's so stupid when you die and go deeper and have to wait 3-5 minutes until you can play again.  The timer is also stupidly annoying. You feel permanently rushed.  The fun exploration part from Deep Rock is completely gone becuase of this. And when some randoms decide they want to explore even deeper when timer is at 90% you can basically quit the run.  Then there are the waves. Most of the time you go through the cave and it's boring. Then comes a wave, and with one mistake the whole group is killed in 1 minute.  It feels frustrating. Not really like a "difficult" game. Just like a frustrating, unbalanced game.  And then there are so many other annoying parts. It takes about 2 Minutes before you "actually" start the run (this portal stuff is annoying)  I also never really notice, when I get damage. I see my armor not going down and think everythings fine. But it is reducing after some time. You dont see enemies, they come up behind you. no real hit feedback and then you die.  Compare this to maaaaany Rogue Lite games that are currently out now. This game hasn't anything to offer and is massively unpolished. Only plus-point is the Deep Rock universe and the dwarves.

In other words satisfying others' needs in RC won't benefit your game experience, it may help the team to complete the mission but it won't make you happy during this whole time. whilst I understand and appreciate what GSG are driving at with how the games progress and 'every run is different'...it isn't fun for the most part. Confusion reigns supreme here, especially if you're fresh into the premise of DRG and are unfamiliar with its weapons and gadgets etc.   The entire armament and upgrade system can be very frustrating, you may recognise a certain weapon that you KNOW you enjoy using and would make your run a lil more crafted to taste? got to wait your turn and hope no one else takes it. A certain perk perhaps that would benefit your character the most over others and would fit really well with what you have acquired so far? again.....wait your turn and hope no one else takes it. You get your trusty pickaxe! but its relevance is almost naught considering you'll use it to salute your 'colleagues' and mine expenite..or whatever it is....and that's it for your pickaxe really.   Why am I picking a certain character? I don't know...again the relevance and difference comes down to some minor abilities that are VERY situational, and when those abilities are used...the effect on the gameplay doesn't feel enough to warrant them being specific classes with those abilities and not just upgrades, rather than having YOUR dwarf be fully customisable from the starting line, such as body type, voice pitch, armour type to name the obvious starting points, and the run THEN dictates your characters abilities, this would allow a little more of a PERSONAL touch. I own NOTHING, I CONTROL NOTHING and are given scraps during a run to make my loadout with but this fully customisable dwarf is MY OWN that I will spend hours customising and doing challenges for said cosmetics etc...  NOW. I really really enjoy the IDEA of what GSG are getting on with here, but its execution feels like a pie with very strange filling...…and its not very warm, its not that I don't like it! its just...strange. What would really work great for your average player who just wants to hop in and have fun for a round or two? compile all the upgrades and weapons gadgets and tools into lets for example say a deck of hypothetical 'cards' right? that every individual player has to either unlock or buy or in some cases maybe keep it if they complete the mission with it? and when you get to a weapon cache or tool bench or get an upgrade, YOU ALONE can have a pick from random items from YOUR OWN DECK. this would ensure that for someone wanting to have a more relaxed experience, they have a LIMITED capacity to control what weapons or traversal tools they might encounter for example. You want to play with a certain weapon or upgrade? well fine....unlock it first through challenges or what not...put it into your deck, and hope it pops up on your run. Could even have a bunch of the same weapon on offer but with different overclocks perhaps?  The whole experience of going through an entire run playing with the scraps of what other players have left for you even if you make it known you are interested in certain perks etc, is INFURIATING and very demoralising. Human nature being what it is and not what devs think it is denotes that, more often than not or no matter how politely you might ask your companions to leave a weapon or upgrade for you or even how many times you chose not to to take a certain item because you saw someone else hovering over it and hope they'll return the favour?...*pats shoulder* they ain't gonna.  now the games themselves, or how they are laid out rather...need some love. You drop in...what am I doing? I'm not sure...where are we going? I'm not sure? all I know is I follow this glowing pipe...because that's what the guy said. what guy? the one that announces the few and far between events in the run in very broken robotic speech that stutters half the time and takes a lot of screen space up leaving me semi blind when I'm fighting for my life against an unholy amount of core spawn and no one is helping because they cant see each other because the narrator is blocking their SCREEN FOR AGES! and partly because you asked the flare gun and some other knuckle headed leaf lover took it and hasn't touched it since so we are now once again  FIGHTING IN THE PITCH BLACK! The levels become difficult to negotiate when the people who have traversal tools either do not use them, or use them for not exactly the intended purpose, possibly because they didn't want what they got in the first place.  I LOVE THIS GAME. I think its beautiful and a lot of love has gone into it!! I love that GSG is giving us a DRG with a refreshed palette and many MANY different ways to play. But it kinda sacrificed a lot of the things that kept me coming back to DRG for almost 400 hours.  I wanted to use a weapon? I EARNED it, I wanted to look a certain way with a certain character? I EARNED IT, I had the the chance to earn overclocks and perks and upgrades that I unlocked by giving the game my time and attention and in return I got rewarded with what I wanted and then played with it and rinsed and repeated because I ENJOYED WHAT I HAD UNLOCKED, it made the game fun and opened up different ways to play my favourite class or help my team. Everyone in DRG knew what role they played in the team, in rogue core....you ain't sure. HERES SOME STUFF THAT NOONE ELSE PICKED. HAVE AT IT TIGER.  uhhhh thanks.. but I don't enjoy this very situational weapon, this perk is only activated in a situation that probably wont happen from what I've encountered so far so is kind of using up a slot for nothing, and these grenades I ended up with I cant even use because we are all so tightly packed in these small corridors and weirdly laid out rooms. I do not enjoy the dungeon hobo simulator style of play it forces on you.  HOWEVER! these are just my thoughts and feelings so far and I am thankful to GSG that this hasn't ended up as a f*** a** pvp extraction shooter or Hoxxes battle royale. In short, MORE reasons to use pickaxe, MORE ways to personalise your experience, MORE MORE reasons to pick the character you do besides its got a blade or a darn metal BUDGIE!

Refunded.  First of all, stop slapping the term "roguelite" everywhere. "meta gameplay aspect, with permanent character progression that carries over between missions." So any game with unlocks or New Game+ is a roguelite. Yay. But I digress.  The game design doesn't work. They've built a randomized shooter in the exact mold of the first game, but the first game was built around teamwork and a greed-balance loop. In this game there is no teamwork—I see no synergy between classes. Weapons and abilities are random. No mining. Yes, I can spend time mining under constant pressure to eventually get a random level‑up, but why would I want to? The chance of getting a random reward that's both useful and synergizes with teammates is astronomically low.  In the end it’s a very dull, boring experience. It feels like a fan-made mod for DRG. Oh, and female dwarves. We all wanted them. Riiiiight.

Do I want to like Rogue Core?     Sure I do! I love Roguelites! I've played great amounts of Hades, RoR, both Spires, Astral Ascent, OSFE, etc. I love DRG! I've got hundreds of hours in it!    Do I actually like Rogue Core? No.     Rogue Core, as it stands, is simply not a particularly good game. Somewhere inside Rogue Core, this is an earnestly good game, but Rogue Core profoundly does not want to show you that. It is difficult not just by virtue of having frustrating basic enemies and questionably designed boss fights, but also because the perks and upgrades that it hands you flat out suck. In what world am I interested in a 15% chance to heal 10hp on killing an enemy when basic enemies deal 30 to 40 damage apiece, and maluses like "base enemies have double health" or "elites have double movespeed" are required after every floor.    Rogue Core is a roguelite which is profoundly uninterested in you having a build, and more interested in you cobbling together a few perks which maybe don't completely suck, and then hoping that's enough. Very occasionally, it comes together into something magnificent, but far more frequently, you're just scrounging by, barely holding on - and you are ALWAYS on the clock, consistently rushed and under time pressure, which means that requirements to gather the entire team to level up feel particularly grating. Runs snowball into collapse at a moment's notice, and the time taken to stabilise is time not taken progressing.    I sincerely hope that in a year's time I can change this review to a positive one! Perhaps the devs will listen to feedback in the early access window that was ignored during the early alpha. But for now, I cannot help but assert that even if you love DRG and love Roguelites, or love only one of the two, you simply should not buy Rogue Core. There are so many better games out there.

Having looked forwards to this for a while, it's a shame I can't recommend it to anyone. It's supposed to be a high-action version of DRG, but it's full of pointless waiting and chafing jank.  A 50 minute match will have almost 10 minutes of standing in circles or doing nothing in menus. Even if you play efficiently, you're constantly badgered by muster UI popups and dialog.  Basic class functions are bedeviled by awkward weapon swaps and delays that force misinputs. Fundamental aspects of the game like LIGHT AND VISION are gated behind giving up power/mobility, resulting in "you get to be the flaregun cuck or you play with a washed out contrast filter". The core DRG gameplay of using tools to manipulate enemy pathing/spawns (and enemies forcing you to adapt these strategies) is almost completely absent, even the most basic enemy types have a 'jump' that allows them to reposition directly inside/behind you. Ranged enemies frequently attack from inside their spawn fissures (which can appear right on top of objectives) or directly through solid structures and terrain.  Without getting into anything specific about the game itself, just [i]playing it[/i] has so much frustration and friction that I can guarantee that if you aren't playing with DRG veterans you'll lose friends playing this.
You guys have a long way to go on this...an extremely long way to go

Really wish Steam had more review options beside "Yes" and "No", but in it's current state, to an overall degree, I cannot recommend this in confidence the way I can with the first DRG. Rogue Core feels somehow just like DRG, but so different, but it's not the differences that make it unbearable.  If you are a DRG player just looking for more fun "haha dwarf say funny line, oo~ shiny rock, gun do warcrimes" gameplay, this is not it. (you will be told you are a Reclaimer, not a Miner.) Rogue Core was advertised as "DRG with Roguelite elements" Rogue Core feels like "players said Haz 5 DRG was too easy" with Roguelite elements.  Many things feel way overtuned against you, and it feels like a constant uphill battle against the timer, ammo economy, and RNG deciding to be merciful on your drops. Alone, all of these things are fine, when combined, they created a tense experience of feeling rushed that just doesn't work.  Rogue Core is obviously designed to be a challenge. You will, of course, not win every single run. However, a game being challenging should be secondary to it being fun. And the challenge this game offers to casual/solo players, is not fun.  Like many reviews state, the game struggles with crowd control despite being a horde shooter.  The game simply doesn't provide enough tools to manage the number of enemies it throws at you, making Guardian feel less like a build choice and more like a requirement for basic survival.  This issue is amplified by the game's ammo economy. Enemies are highly aggressive and surprisingly durable, even on lower difficulties, yet ammunition remains scarce.  Because firearms are the player's primary method of dealing with hordes, every encounter eventually becomes a lose-lose situation: stand your ground and burn through precious ammo while enemies swarm from multiple directions, or retreat constantly and spend the mission running from fights instead of engaging with them. (you better love prey animal gameplay)  Rather than feeling challenged, the player often feels deliberately under equipped for the situations the game creates.  All of the things that provide a challenge would be fine alone, but its because they are together, it goes from a fun challenge to an annoying race against RNG that you will probably lose anyways.  I feel DRG does far superior in terms of being accessible to both hardcore players seeking a challenge, and casual/solo players looking for just, well, a casual experience.   I REALLY hope it's only uphill from here, but unless you are a gaming god or a masochist, I cannot recommend this.

Before we begin i will preface this review by saying this. 1) I am a deep rock veteran I have been playing deeprock since it first released and it is still one of my favorite games 2) This review is not coming out of a place of hate but love for deeprock  Okay where to begin. first impressions of this game are a mixed bag. there are some things i like and some thing i do not like. for instance. The swapping of "armor" for "shields" would imply that the armor is better than shields but it really isn't. its shields in all but name and frankly, way worse because it does not replenish itself like shields do, armor pickups are available sure, but they are finite, and also the guardians ability to replenish armor only works to half your max armor. this combined with the fact ammo crates no longer restore hp like they did in DRG means your health is constently being nibbled away at by creeps that get the jump on you. combined with the fact there is a frankly obnoxiously short timeframe to complete a mission each floor before you are infinia-swarmed means you cannot be slow and strategic like in the original DRG you have to go go go from the start and im not a fan of that. even on the starting difficultly we felt like we were on a level 3-4 DRG mission and barely got a moments rest to get our bearings. sure there was the dopamine high of getting a good upgrade or big heavy weapon. but other times your left with 2 dinky SMG's that chew throw bullets faster than they chew thro the enemies. while each class did feel unique and fun the button combos to use thier abilities are not easy to imput in the middle of combat when you have to be constently moving to avoid getting swarmed and losing precious armor and hp. sure the abilitys are cool, flashy and fun to use but they have long cooldowns. finally the enemies in this game while, visually cool, basic enemies seem to have the ability to jump and lunge at you. compared to the relatively slower gait of the gliphids you have far less time to react to danger and as previously stated since armor does not regen and hp regen is more sparse then in the original DRG your franticly sprinting to the end of a mission often missing extra pickups, bonus objectives and other things because as metioned before there is now a very obnoxious time limit to missions. while in the original DRG you were free to take missions at a pace determined by the player. taking the players ability to chose thier pace makes missions far more stressful. all in all. this game was hyped up massively and when i played it i was very dissapointed. I now know how every payday 2 fan felt after playing payday 3 for the first time.

Let me order my beer with my hard earned cash!  A lot of people have already written about the gameplay of Rogue Core, and I agree with many of those points. But I want to point out something else that worries me. While I am pretty sure Ghost Ship is going to polish the mechanics of Rogue Core, this game is missing something deeper.  DRG is one of my favorite games of all time, not only because of the gameplay, but because of its charm, humor, and stupid little rituals. Rogue Core has the dwarves, caves, and beers, but right now it feels like it is missing the soul.  The game tries too hard to be serious while still keeping some of the funny DRG stuff around. The result feels odd. In the original, getting dead drunk and dancing before a mission, or shouting “we’re rich” at a chunk of gold until Mission Control got a headache, made perfect sense because the dwarves were reckless miners in a corporate deathtrap. In Rogue Core, the Reclaimers are supposed to be more serious and elite, so those same rituals feel strangely out of place.  The best example is the new workout system. Workouts could be a great way to give mission buffs, like the special beers in DRG. But making me play a repetitive minigame just to order a round for my colleagues is annoying and kills the fun. I know Rogue Core is not DRG, and the gameplay can change of course. But the series should go back to its roots when it comes to silliness, humor and dwarven nonsense. That is what made Deep Rock Galactic truly special.  Also, I never thought I would say this, but I miss Mission Control.  Rock and Stone.

Hi fellow miners, and hello Ghost Ship Games!    After spending around 60 hours in Early Access, I feel like I have a decent grasp of the game and can provide some constructive feedback. My intention is not to bash the developers. I am not a game analyst, just a longtime Deep Rock Galactic player who enjoys challenging games and isn't afraid of difficult content. I've attempted Hazard 5 many times (with varying degrees of success), so I understand that not every run is supposed to be easy.    First, I should mention that I play exclusively solo. I haven't tried the group lobby yet, although I've watched a lot of gameplay videos and discussions. Because of that, I already suspected that the current negotiation system combined with the timer would be controversial. However, this review focuses specifically on the solo experience.    Let's start with the positives.    The core gameplay is very solid (no pun intended). When you manage to put together a decent build, the game becomes incredibly fun and satisfying. The combat feels great, the progression system has a lot of potential, and the overall gameplay loop is engaging.    I also believe the timer should stay. It is one of the key mechanics that makes Rogue Core feel different from Deep Rock Galactic. Without it, the game would risk losing part of its identity.    That said, I think the current difficulty is too punishing for solo players.    I don't think it's reasonable to expect players to deal with 20–30 enemies almost immediately while having very few upgrades, weapon enhancements, or levels. I spent an entire day trying to complete Depth 3 and simply couldn't do it. At some point it stopped feeling challenging and started feeling frustrating.    The biggest issue is that the timer creates a difficult dilemma:    If I rush to the elevator, I arrive at the next floor underleveled and underpowered.  If I stay behind to gather Expenite, complete events, and gain experience, I fall behind the timer.  Even when I do gain levels, the upgrades offered are often too situational and don't always help me survive the next floor.    As a result, solo progression often feels overly dependent on luck rather than player skill and decision-making.    I believe solo play needs additional balancing. Here are some suggestions:    Reduce the experience required for the first few levels.    The first 3–5 levels should require less experience. This would allow players to establish a basic build more quickly and feel like they're making meaningful progress before the difficulty ramps up.    Introduce quick objectives that reward power.    Consider adding small side objectives tied to existing map activities. For example, hacking a data terminal could reward the player with an elemental weapon upgrade or a higher-rarity power.    This would create additional opportunities for players to become stronger during a run and would provide more strategic choices. Early elemental upgrades can make a huge difference in survivability.    Rework early-game enemy waves.    The enemy waves are currently too large, especially on the early floors.    I understand increasing the pressure later in a run, but during the opening stages players simply don't have enough tools available to deal with the number of enemies being thrown at them.    I would also suggest:    Pausing the timer while a Core Spawn wave is active.  Slowing the timer during these encounters.  Allowing players to recover a small amount of time after successfully defeating a Core Spawn wave.    Perhaps players could temporarily seal the rifts from which the enemies emerge, creating a short event that rewards successful defense with a small time bonus.    Allow limited respec options in solo mode.    Please consider adding a way to undo a power selection in solo play.    For example, clicking an already selected power could allow the player to choose a different option instead. This would reduce frustration caused by accidental picks or upgrades that don't synergize with the current build.    Rebalance some Expenite events.    Today I started the double-drill event on Depth 3 at the beginning of a run. I managed to survive the waves without much trouble, only to discover that I had lost almost all of my available time.    Additionally, it feels strange that the Core Spawn enemies focus almost entirely on the player instead of attacking the drills. It would make more sense if protecting the objective was the primary challenge.    Some Expenite events also feel poorly balanced for solo players and could benefit from further review.    Overall, I think the game needs more ways for players to become powerful during the early stages of a run.    Right now, despite enjoying the core gameplay and seeing enormous potential in the project, I cannot fully recommend the game. I think it needs more time in the oven.    My advice to players would be to wait a few months and see how the game evolves. Personally, I'll be taking a break until I see some balance changes and improvements to the solo experience.    That said, I have complete confidence that Ghost Ship Games will listen to feedback and address these issues. Once meaningful changes are made, I will gladly revisit and update this review.    Until then, I'll be waiting.    Rock and Stone!

TL:DR, It's just not there yet, and definitely not at the price point.   As a long time fan of the original DRG, but also of rogue-likes, my main takeaway here is this brings the lesser elements of each, rather than drawing on their strengths.  - The level layouts are fairly prescriptive and repetitive, which lends itself to a very samey feel, unlike DRG.  - The builds available feel either poorly balanced, or limited in their scope, which encourages players to shoot for familiar safe builds rather than experiment in the manner of a rogue like.  - A significant number of potential weapon options are so poor as to immediately compromise a run from the outset in solo, or lead to a massive power imbalance in groups.  - The frequency and style of enemy encounters mean you WILL take chip damage by the wave, which means certain healing powerups become essentially mandatory picks, but the scaling means that once you have them, damage is almost meaningless. - Very limited enemy variation, combined with the limited maps mean that almost every fight feels the same. - The negotiation system on upgrades means that build variety in groups is extremely hit or miss, with typically one or two players of the group getting synergisticaly interesting options, and the rest of the group being left with very generic choices.  I really want to recommend this game, but as things currently stand, feedback and suggestions from the numerous beta testing phases do not seem to have been taken on board, and it comes at a hefty price point for what it offers. If it were ~40% cheaper, or offered more long term playability I would recommend it, but currently I cannot. If you're new and looking for a roguelike shooter with good variety, check out Roboquest or Risk of Rain.

Where do I begin?  As someone who bought at least a dozen copies of the original DRG for friends, this game sounded pretty damn good on paper. More DRG, more combat focused environment, more importantly NEW CONTENT (that could of been in DRG). I applied for betas, I got denied, but the game rolled out and without hesitation I bought it.  Man.  Rogue Core is suffering in it's most simplistic rogue-like elements.  Pray to RNGesus for a good gun at the start of the run or suck it up. Pray to RNGesus that your teammates are laser focused otherwise you will be dragged down for not holding their hand and tugging them along. Pray to RNGesus that your teammates will be polite enough to not sabotage you by taking the perks that compliment your build. Pray to RNgesus that you get a build at all instead of "if an enemy is burning, get more burning" perks or just a handful of + critical %  Don't take in the scenery, keep moving at all times.  Set aside 30-60 minutes minimum for a single damn mission to get a chunk of progression. Hope to god that your team is actively working on all pistons to scan the room for the mcguffin resource. Hope to god they actually will listen to you in a timely fashion that they will use their mobility tools and actually assist in getting said resource. Don't take in the scenery. As hammered in by your AI overload OMEGA, inefficiency is practically a cardinal sin which the game is all to ready to beat you over the head with a pickaxe for committing such.   What's worse, is in the time I've spent playing with pubs and strangers, not once, not a SINGLE DAMN TIME, has anyone ordered me a damn beer or worked out for one. If you're a DRG player, you already know this is a sign.   As of right now I can't recommend this. I'll keep playing it because it's at least a damn time vampire and allows me to fast travel through the day by playing a single mission.

It looks like developers wanted to distance themselves from original game as much as they could, and if that would be the case, why use the same IP?  No more interesting mission objectives, every playthrough is going to elevator couple of times, and then fighting boss. The main problem with the game is, in my opinion, ultimately the name. As a passionate DRG player (3500 hours), I associate a certain in-game feeling, a state of mind, with these three letters, which unfortunately doesn't arise here. For me, DRG is an place associated with teamwork, chaotic battles, and above all a lot of shenanigans. Despite the intense moments, the game and thus the world created within it has a certain SPA-day character for me. I never feel hunted; I am the master of things and ultimately determine the game speed – even in missions like Point Extraction, since the difficulty level also increases there in a relaxed, leveled manner for me, and it's up to me at what point I exit the ascent process. In Rogue Core, I feel like I'm on the run; the above-mentioned DRG characteristics can't even arise for me in this scenario. There's neither time for shenanigans and thus a not-insignificant part of DRG's team feeling, nor do I determine the actual game speed.  And yes, one of the main counterarguments will be: "Then the game simply isn't for you" and "The devs did say it would be hard." This is also correct and unfortunately also a main problem of the devs, in my opinion, that they created themselves. Upon the initial announcement of Rogue Core, they announced it as originally planned update for DRG, which simply wasn't really implementable with the mechanics present in the original DRG. Therefore, DRG Rogue Core became a standalone game, which could be realized with a new engine and thus new possibilities. It's just that what it ultimately became within this framework is nothing that transports the lightness feeling of DRG. If someone says: "I'm making an extended update of the said game," and the feeling that makes up the original game is missing in the new product, it's understandable if people are disappointed to a certain extent.  Thus we come back to my initial thesis: The problem is the name. To be completely honest, if Rogue Core didn't have DRG in its name, the setting was different, and the rest ultimately stayed one-to-one the same, I don't believe the game would be facing this review debacle like now (compared to DRG and DRG Survivor, the devs probably won't see it differently either). But it's now consciously a DRG game, BECAUSE you want to have the fanbase as your playerbase. Rogue-lite enthusiasts who have nothing to do with DRG might even feel more repelled by the branding.  I personally like rogue lites very much. Gunfire, Abyssus, and Dead Zone Rogue are three games for me that I find very successful in this genre and accordingly hoped for something in that direction with a DRG rogue lite. This is of course wishful thinking, but what all rogue lites I've played so far had in common was the feeling when my build was finished: I felt powerful – something I basically never really had here. Honestly, I was surprised that according to the announcement and game lore, I'm entering the race with the elite of the elite. Factually, I felt stronger with every class in DRG than with the end-run builds in Rogue Core. This may certainly largely have to do with the strength and behavior of the new enemies, but the movement in Rogue Core also gives me the feeling of a downgraded character.  GSG created my personal GOAT game with DRG, ultimately through constantly working on it and being creative. Therefore, I simply hope that whatever comes in the future in terms of updates and changes can transform my feeling from hunted to hunter and thus bring back some of the lightness that DRG embodies for me. As I said, the feeling could ultimately also be transferred to DRG Survivor, which can also get wild. Whoever the target audience may be – I'm unfortunately not it currently – but I'll keep checking in to see which direction the game develops.  Thus, I simply cannot recommend the game in its current state due to the name. In my opinion, it's not what's inside that's on the label. If you want an challenging rogue lite with what I consider a survival character, then this could be something, although I don't see enough content in the game for €30 either. I'm sure a lot will have changed in a year, but until then, we'll continue listening to the diligent pingin' of Mushrooms and Gooold by teammates. Rock and Stone!

Missing the "Rock and Stone" Spirit  As a huge fan of the charismatic, "Leave No Dwarf Behind" energy of Deep Rock Galactic, I came into Rogue Core hoping to find that same welcoming and wholesome co-op experience. Unfortunately, several design choices actively work against that friendly vibe and turn the game into a stressful experience.  Here are the main issues holding the game back right now:  The Timer Ruins Exploration: In standard DRG, I love playing like a true miner, exploring every corner of the map and gathering resources. The timer here completely kills the joy of discovery. It forces you into a rushed, speedrun playstyle out of fear of failing the mission. I want to explore the cave complexes, not stress over a ticking clock. I hate speedruns.  Shared Upgrades Create Friction: Having players share upgrade picks instead of simply giving everyone their own 3 options to choose from is a huge mistake. It just creates unnecessary drama and annoyance when players want the exact same build. Co-op should be about synergy, not fighting over loot. Please remove mechanics that cause friction between teammates.  The Missing Extraction: Why was the extraction removed?! That frantic, cooperative dash to the drop pod is literally the most fun part of the original game. It gives you that triumphant feeling of "We completed it, TOGETHER. For Rock and Stone!"  A Depressing End-Game Vibe: Right now, the game lacks the joyful energy of the original. When a match ends, people just disconnect from the lobby. There is no "GG", no post-mission drinks, no dancing. Just grabbing your points and leaving. It feels depressing.  Please, shift the focus back to co-op, unity, and camaraderie. Remove the mechanics that punish exploration and cause player friction, and bring back the triumphant, cooperative soul that made DRG a masterpiece.

It has everything I love about DRG, but now it's all shuffled and mixed around with no cohesion. If the game wants to be a separate part of the DRG "universe," the game should reflect that, instead of being a lazy copy paste of the orginial

pain points: 1 the 'talent tree' feel basically pointless, why not just have enhancement slots as they do now and the option to upgrade individual enhancements? why not also make them more interesting. instead of this generic "+25hp" maybe we could have some options that give actual enhancements to your abilities. maybe some class ability boosters? (slicer getting an additional charge to his weapon for example) or maybe just the base class abilities can be upgraded. (like preventing the pickaxe from slowing you down when you use it)  2 melee builds absolutely suck. player hitboxes are way larger than they seem they should be and you tend to get hit from a melee enemy from a distance several feet longer than what their max wingspan would reach and because of this, trying to melee anything ends up with you taking damage.  3 the weapon, armor, and ammo system is just lackluster. there's often, theoretically, enough of the armor and ammo to sustain the whole team if you utilize all of it. however, its nigh impossible to utilize it all since you would have to massively backtrack to actually get the stuff. the weapons feel fine by themselves, but the whole system of upgrading weapons within each run is so lacking that it almost feels like there's no point in actually doing any of the side stuff and you'd be better off speedrunning each level.  4 the difficulty ramps so fast compared to the speed at which you gain upgrades. the enhancements at the top of the talent tree are really weak and feel like they don't actually do anything since the stats they give are just so low. the fact you can only regen armor via boxes or ammo crates, you will often have low health and armor due to the aforementioned hit box size issues. I often found that, even when i just filled my hp and armor, id quickly lose half my armor because of that same hitbox issue. basically the whole armor system does not work and they should bring back the shields from DRG and have armor as an additional option between shields and hp from the enhancement system  5 the timer system for each mission feels out of place for what the game otherwise tries to be. it currently incentivizes ignoring everything that isn't in the direct path to the elevator. I think they should do away with the individual level timer and instead do what risk of rain does and incrementally increase the difficulty over time. it doesn't have to work exactly like ror but the concept, i think, would work perfectly for this format. this wouldn't fix the be-line issue, but it would help.

- Ammo is available when you already have 90%+ and never when you need it.  - IMPOSSIBLE to solo.  - The timer function is really a fun killer.  - Only way to gain upgrades is to mine the exp ore, which just isn't fun.  - You have to skip every node that takes time or spawns enemies because you won't have enough ammo to kill 10% of the enemies that spawn.  - Hugging the ore depositer to level up, boring.   - The reward? Waiting for people to choose between 3-4 usually completely pointless upgrades.    I loved the original game, but this one isn't it.     3/10    My opinion:  - Remove exp ore and replace it with same minerals from first game, allow us to "call down reinforcement" and choose when to resupply.  - Allow upgrades to be chosen on the fly and independant from anyone else.  - Gain experience by completing side missions and kill monsters.  - More and actually interesting upgrades.  - Rework the timer.. maybe spawn a colossal enemy you can take down? Missing timer punished by having to spend precious ammo.

I simply cannot recommend Rogue Core in it's current form. I believe it has potential to be a good game, but it is very unbalanced, both due to the timer which disincentivizes  completing optional objectives, and the amount of enemies sent towards you vs the resources you have to deal with them in terms of ammo is wildly out of sync. The game is also horribly balanced for solo play, which I understand the game is meant to be played with other players, however why bother having a solo option if it's virtually unwinnable? The original Deep Rock does not have as extreme of issues with solo balancing. Also the lack of mine-able resources aside from expenite really rubs me the wrong way, I don't oppose expenite being the primary currency, however given this is a Deep Rock game there should be more incentives to mine beyond just getting expenite or environment destruction. Overall this game is just a really hard sell in it's current state, and while i'm open to playing some more after some updates, I don't see a reason to play this over the original Deep Rock Galactic, which I consider a damn near perfect game.

Like playing DRG but without any of the fun.   After putting time into this game I'm left with generally sour thoughts about the experience.   Friendly fire is too damaging, considering how enemies will mob yourself and teammates and bullets will be flying and grenades detonating. Can't defeat a mob of core spawn (that come with melee and ranged attacks from the start) when you're too busy killing one another by accident. I've probably lost more armor to friendly fire than to enemies.   Speaking of armor, does it mean practically nothing in Rogue Core? I spend more time hunting for a crumb of red sugar and armor than doing events - all for a single ranged attack to swipe the whole armor bar down, regardless of stats. There's simply not enough health and armor to account for the density of enemies and the average damage by their attacks.   Once you're down, the mechanic for reviving teammates after the elevator departs is excessively punishing. Retrieving dwarves from cocoons while pursued by a mob - before you can even revive them - adds nothing but frustration. It's comically punishing, kicking the team while they're down. Yeah, I get it's supposed to be a challenging game but the fun factor doesn't exist here to balance it.   What I liked about DRG was the fact that a mission didn't have to go perfectly or even remotely smoothly. The best times were spent limping out of the chaos, but Rogue Core punishes anything less than perfection. Given that most players are joined by random teammates, this is a steep ask for a casual player. Yet I can't imagine a serious player enjoying the game since the end of each mission doesn't justify the means; it only saps an hour of your day.   Really tried to enjoy this game for the sake of DRG. Considering that the last few times I've played it's felt like a chore I realized that I don't have to pretend to enjoy a game that doesn't reward you with a good time.

After putting 260 hours into Deep Rock Galactic (DRG) and spending some time with Rogue Core, I finally feel ready to share some of my thoughts.  [h3] EDIT: June 11th [/h3] as of today (in the experimental branch), most of my complaints about the perk system have been very much improved and made friendlier for you and your team. Excited for the game's future!!. consider this as a mixed review more than a negative, I want to wait for the main branch to update and other future updates before I rewrite my review in detail, I'll leave it here for now  [h3] Disclaimer[/h3] DRG and Rogue Core belong to completely different genres and shouldn't be directly compared  however, you can't ignore the fact that both are horde shooters and they share the exact same setting, core mechanics, and dwarf characters/weapons.  As a Gunner main in DRG, Rogue Core is right up my alley. I love shooting the creepy crawlers. This game doubles down on the shooting mechanics, the timer keeping you always on the move (although sometimes i find it a bit short) and the perk upgrades system makes the combat shine brighter than ever... if you actually manage to get the perks you need.  That brings me to my biggest grievance: passive-aggressive fighting with random players over the perks is easily the most frustrating part of the experience. If you happen to pick last during a round, you're essentially screwed with only things like "random elemental/melee bonus you can't trigger"  or "default boring HP boost" for choices, you end up with either a sloppy, weak build or an inflated health bar that doesn't save you as you hit like a wet noodle and the corespawn can just gang up on you. I can't fault the other players, as they want to build a fun, strong build too.   some people are gonna say [i]"Just play solo if you don't want to fight over perks" [/i] But that completely misses the point. tell me.  [u]Why would I buy a cooperative shooter and then have to play alone just to have a fun, viable build? [/u]  [h3]Long story short[/h3] Rogue Core has a fantastic foundation and brilliant combat with its classes and weapon design, but the current multiplayer progression system ends up being annoying to deal with. this is Early access and GSG has said they're addressing these pain points, so I'm really looking forwards on the next updates to come  also idk if it's an Early access thing  or UE5 shenanigans but this game doesn't run as smooth as DRG even thought they share the same low-poly visual style

The only thing GSG got right with their "big 3" improvement plan is the ammo economy issue.    The timer is important, and a big part of the gameplay loop. The main issue with the timer is A) leveling up is counterintuitive with the rushdown sense of a roguelike and B) "wave has ended" might as well just be a lie. Many of my runs had issues with leveling up via expenite farming or machine event objectives. Most of the events are slow and do not fit in the focus to prioritize what you do per level. Even worse when the event takes so long to complete(even if playing optimally) that a core spawn erupts. This leads to constant swarms with a lack of ammo. Skipping the event or other upgrade opportunities to maximize my efforts still has problems. Core spawns last so long in the higher difficulties that another is right around the corner, resulting in time to upgrade and progress the level being replaced with a constant fight. Omega telling me that the swarm is over is just not true.    The negotiation "issue" has been said enough so I won't detail my thoughts. Make upgrading map wide instead of huddling together or just upgrade in the elevator next floor, and make upgrades feel more impactful. The sharing system is the best part of this game, and people who dislike it are just plain gluttonous/need better friends.    Several runs went my way because I was able to get the aoe and healing upgrades. Without these two, all other upgrades might as well be cosmetic, especially in higher difficulties. A lack of aoe or a form of self sustain on D04 might as well be game over off the start.    My biggest issue though, is the understanding of a rogue like/lite gameplay loop. Difficulty should reflect an exponential graph, where the beginning is at its lowest. It is the level where I decide what gun I pick, what upgrades/builds I want to push towards, and a challenging but fair amount of enemies to fight.    In Rogue Core, the difficulty at floor 1 is the opposite. Almost every run feels like I'm fighting a 4th floor horde, mixing in edgestalkers and vanguards that should not be there yet. I have not been given time to begin my snowball effect, and if I don't get the godroll upgrades within the first 5 minutes of expenite farming, I might as well reset. A roguelike should not hit the point where you reset a run 2 minutes in because I didn't get the right upgrade/gun, but it certainly feels this way when I die/barely make it to floor 2 for not having a necessity perk.    I think the developers should look into the gauntlet mode more, as several issues with the main game are fixed in gauntlet. Upgrades between floors, no need to scour into a random corner away from the next elevator for one upgrade, no extra events/expenite past the elevator. The elevator SHOULD be the end of the cave, and not have a random event or toolbox 150m past the elevator in some cave. I understand this would make the game feel even more linear, but that feels like the intention of this game compared to DRG original.    I only have good spirits to the devs for this game. I hope my feedback is helpful to them improving rogue core. I agree with everyone that this is not DRG, and I want that as well. Rock and Stone.

I really hate to leave a negative review, because unlike many of RC's detractors I'm actually quite enjoying a lot of the gameplay/mechanical decisions. I think there's a steeper learning curve barrier-to-entry than DRG had, and it takes significantly more willingness to *work with* your team rather than *tolerate* them if you want to succeed (and have fun.) I think there are some creases to iron out, but frankly I reckon I'll enjoy this just as much if not more than DRG when it leaves early access. Even so, I'll lay my issues out below. (TLDR at the bottom)  Firstly, and the primary reason I'm Not Recommending RC: the game crashes a lot. *a lot* a lot. Of the 12 missions I have now participated in, 8 of them have ended prematurely due to CTDs, network errors/DCs, bugs related to late-joining or rejoining, etc. I have actually gotten to play from start to finish four times - and one of them had two of my four teammates CTD, so we lost anyway. That's a 25% success rate on the game actually running properly. With a run taking 20-40 mins, it's just too much time to lose over and over again. This is exacerbated by the client-host system of DRG and RC. I was really hoping RC would bring a restructuring of the network functions so that the game could still be completed even if you DC unexpectedly, but... no.   Secondly, there is a tonal identity crisis with Rogue Core that comes almost intrinsically from it being a DRG IP. The marketing, promotional material, and teasers all suggested that Rogue Core would be a more intense, serious, grittier title relative to Deep Rock Galactic. I was excited to see GSG release a game where the dwarves are truly the best of the best - no more antics or silliness, just four people getting in to clean up the miner's mess.  What we actually got was the *exact same* tone as DRG. There's still a dance floor, there's still a bar, they still crack goofy jokes and yell in the same voices. Even Omega is basically just Mission Control but with annoying audio stuttering applied. I wanted dwarves who were more like Max-Tac from Cyberpunk and a Handler who knows they're in charge of an elite squad - but the only thing that seems to make the Reclaimers the "best of the best" is the marketing.  Onto the mechanics.  The traversal tools feel largely outdated and clunky within the context of RC's level generation and pacing. The power-drills and platform gun are the main ones to suffer here, while the anti-grav emitter is just obviously always the best choice. Tying the flare gun to this is also an interesting choice - I don't hate it, but I'm not sure if I love it either. The level generation itself somehow feels overly linear and overly clunky. I'm hoping there'll be more variety once RC leaves early access, because right now I feel like I've seen every room three times already.  I like the timer, shared upgrade/equipment pools, and the overall decision making of events vs elevator. With that said, the radius for actually triggering the upgrade is SMALL. Make it like 50% bigger. And weapon crates should probably not be reliant on every dwarf coming to the crate, there's always someone who runs off - an entire weapon being left stings a lot more than waiting an extra minute for my +10% power attack recharge rate. With that said, I don't like that ammo/armour resupplies have less in them - and that the random upgrades have a decreased pool - when there are fewer players in the lobby. The game is still tough, I still need bullets, this feels like a needless cruelty. I understand GSG may want more players to feel like it's inherently stronger, but it makes playing with 2 instead of 4 feel pointlessly hard.  Last is the audio. I understand that GSG's original audio designer for DRG left, but the clear, crisp, and distinctive audio design elements of DRG were (and still are) some of the best of any game ever released. Not just in terms of music, but weapon/equipment/enemy/event sounds are all balanced and presented *perfectly*. It is seriously a masterclass in how to do audio design for a videogame. I've heard OSTs that are as-good or better, but there are very few titles that come close for actual in-game sfx. Rogue Core is.... *lacking* in this regard. Many sounds are borrowed from DRG, so it's not like it's just bad, but it's an obvious and unignorable step down. This makes it feel more chaotic/fuzzy in a bad way - it's not that I'm yet to learn it, it feels like, it's that this time you *can't* learn it.  TLDR Rogue Core has mechanics and a gameplay loop that I, personally, really enjoy. However, the too-frequent CTDs, network errors, and other instances in which 30 or 40 minutes of gameplay are entirely voided makes it very difficult for me to want to play this game, and make me unable to recommend it. Outside of the mechanics (which many who feel differently to me have already spoken about), there are also notable issues in the game's tone - which is far too similar to DRG - as well as the audio design, which lacks the clarity and complexity of the original game.  While Ghost Ship have made it clear that Rogue Core is not DRG, it is my opinion that they have attempted to borrow too much of DRG's "vibe" (audio design, dialogue design, visual design, etc) for it to feel like a clean cut for most players. I believe they need to either re-orient their design philosophy and move RC *away* from DRG, or they need to bite the bullet and align it *closer* instead. I would prefer the former - I think many would prefer the latter.  In any case, check back in a year's time.
Needs a lot of time to cook.

I cant recommend this in its current state. A friend and I have been playing this and the scaling just feels way off for 2 players. The boss can 100 to 0 you with one of its tracking nukes (no we arent just standing there and watching it hit us) and the past few attempts even with additional upgrades to our characters have been fine until we get to the rift guardian at the end and it either ends with us taking damage that feels way too much for being clipped by the explosion or just straight up running out of ammo. While on the subject of ammo I just want to touch on the fact that it feels like about half of the games guns just feel terrible to use. They either have next to no ammo or they hit like a pool noodle or in some cases, both.     TLDR; Scaling for 2 players is bad. Many weapons just suck. If you dont have a full group to play with then dont bother with this game as it is right now. Wait for some patches to improve ammo economy and rebalance player scaling.

Gameplay loop feels unfinished. Upgrades are not impactful enough and even getting your first couple upgrades is incredibly slow. You will rarely feel even as strong as you do in DRG despite this game being a roguelike where I would expect to feel more powerful near the end of a run. There are only a few end bosses, very few structures and most upgrades are stat buffs so runs start to blur together very quickly. Overall I was stupid hype for this game but have found myself just not wanting to play it.

Devs should look into changing the weapon and perk choosing mechanics, gameplay feels constantly interrupted by the need to 'huddle up'.   Otherwise, the game isn't bad, rather a bit soulless - having played the first game, which actually had creative mechanics and exciting NPCs, I don't see how this game is supposed to be a successor of any sort. Feels like a 'product', plays like a 'product'.

The game in the current state is collection of AWFUL mechanics. Starting from death timer to saving teammates that were oneshot by some unkillable worm and having a choose between leaving BIS Crit+15% card or some common garbage for teamates that are forced to choose from same pool. Even Depth-01 can feel like Hazard 5+ in DRG due to how much RNG is involved.    Also, no duplicate 'classes' allowed in the team??? Really?  The classes are not even real to begin with, the do not fell like classes, might as well remove them all and replace with ability roll at the start of the map. The only thing they add to the game is grind for cosmetics and perk slots, etc. per 'class'.    Great job. "this is not what I expected" from the team that made original DRG what it is.

I know that this is early in development, but there's nothing even here yet. It makes no effort to iterate on Deep Rock Galactic's strengths, and actually forcefully pulls away from them to create this 'new game.'     The various objectives and the classes' ability to impact how they're done in DRG is absolutely absent here in Rogue Core, but that's partially since every objective has been enshittified in case you don't get the class tools you would need to navigate the amazing procedurally generated worlds they had to strip away from the game. If you're lucky, you can get a level modifier that makes every enemy within 5 meters kick you in the ♥♥♥♥ in exchange for the level having a single glowing mineral boulder inside awall (for a high quality player upgrade). Do you miss DRG yet?    The game devs love to choke the pace of gameplay! They love wasting your time.    Before we even get into a mission, you must spend a few minutes with the gang hitting the gym. You cannot earn the means to buy a dang beer. So the side objectives to earn buffs & beers in the gameplay loop itself has been stripped out for a watery lobby minigame, when space rig basketball was already the apex of the art form. But now we can get going, since we hardly have to worry about class loadouts.    Start your mission!  But it starts you in a lobby outside the mission area,   Cross a hallway so you can go and press two buttons and just to walk another hallway,   Ask nicely for god's least efficient drone follower to take the child locks off the weapon chest,   Press a button to find a seaparated toolbox so you can upgrade said weapon or get a 2nd one,   Do a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ little hacking minigame which loses its appeal in a saferoom for another slow-roll upgrade, And, as slowly as humanly possible, negotiate a set of characteristics for yourself and your team.     All of this time spent with no guarantee the parts you get will even fit together enough to get you through your mission, much less create a fun synergy to play. It bakes a failure chance into the formula, unfixable to the player, because you can actually just roll losing combos.    Thinking back to good old Deep Rock Galactic, you drop right into the main area your mission is in, and you're already underway before your dwarf is done complaining about the Drop Pod ride. There's objectives all over the first room you find yourself in! A barley bulb here, a fester flea there. Your class comes with a set of basics that work well enough together for a level 1 character to succeed, so you really don't need to have any levels or upgrades to start completing missions. Your character and your mission are underway, and you are playing the game straight away.    Back to Rogue Core: why have the start of the mission take so crazy long? Why not have a mini drone swarm rush the front room of the facility, bring everything to a round meeting room table next to the kitchenette (these are workplaces, right?), and do the set of negotiation drops as fast as possible?     Why not make the drop pod punch through the barrier and put us inside, instead of making us hitting two buttons in an empty room for a swirly dirt butthole to walk through? The drop pod has always left us 'cut off', why create this second, clumsier method of communicating our isolation, at such a high cost of clunkiness?    DRG's classes were such fun (even the red-headed stepchild, the Gunner) because their tools create the gameplay!   I am grappling great lengths and chasing down fleas and extracting aquarqs, lighting as I go,   I am putting up platforms and turrets to keep the minerals flowing in and keep the bugs down,   I am drilling out escape routes, killboxes, and getting us through a huge wall so we can skip a detour on the way to the drop pod,   I am putting up a slow-moving zipline to keep us off the ground as the waves come in, these are CREATING the gameplay!     We have the flare gun, platform gun, drills, but they've been stripped of their ability to create gameplay. We are going in a straight path and the worst you might need to do to get around is mantle a ledge. Small flares are more than adequate in these tiny facilities. The platform gun seldom comes in handy to get anything besides optional upgrade minerals.     The classes are all specc'd into their combat potential here in Rogue Core, but the combat in DRG is great because of these class tools they aren't guaranteed to have in RC and besides, they aren't in an environment to use them to their fullest because of the gutted level design.    What is it you hope to create without any of the things that were great about DRG?    PS: I work in 3D mapping, and if you take anything I say seriously, please update the terrain scanner to present a Triangulated Irregular Network rather than square gridlines. I understand the purpose of grid lines to communicate surface elevations on a static model viewed from one angle only, but since users pan the terrain scanner view in 3D, that benefit is lost.

(i know this game is in early access!)  after waiting for months and months we finally get to play this game, and if im being honest it should of been released a bit later. after you have played like 3 or 4 games you've basically seen everything this game has to offer. all of the biomes feel incredibly similar unlike the main DRG, the enemies that spawn in each biome are also the exact same every time.  every upgrade in this game feels insanely boring as they mostly are small stat increases, theres definitely some cool ones but there needs to be much more. since the upgrades are boring it ends up making every run feel identical.  another big issue i have with this game is the timer, its way too short on the higher difficulties. the reason why i don't like it is because it spawns invincible enemies that can fully kill you (you cannot be revived until the next level) once it turns red.  the classes are pretty cool but the falconer has an ability that is literally useless in solo (the reviving one). i wish you could upgrade all of the abilities on each class but its only ever just 1 or 2 of them.  as of now i dont think this game is worth buying, wait for some more updates to be released and then consider buying it.  overall im pretty dissapointed with this game so far, ive been waiting for absolutely ages to play it...

I cannot at this point in time recommend Rogue Core to anybody. There have been many others pointing out the bads of the game so i'll keep it simple:   - The Timer: it's a base mechanic and cannot be removed, however, it could be adjusted for some more leniency or maybe remove the reaper worms? The worms are just unfun, and incredibly punishing. The endless swarm is hell because:   - The enemies: they're mostly melee however they get in range to strike in like 1 second while you, the player, (except for Slicer) have limited mobility, take high fall damage, and have non regen armor that gets stripped away in 3 hits...  I often feel like a brick trying to jump and outmaneuver a wave. Mobility tools  that are ripped from original DRG won't help much, except maybe the zipline... Only 1 tool  out of many   -The dwarves:  brittle armor that doesn't regen and some extra temporary HP to inflate difficulty, why not give us a shield like before and then make waves hard? It'd be like high hazard DRG. Class skills are...okay. Base usage is limited , improves with upgrades, but to me they're a bit underwhelming compared to the enemies you're facing. (I'm looking at You, 4 seconds Guardian push skill ) The third skill such as armor regen field, thunder rod, ammo box, etc. feel like a thing they added last second as an afterthought tbh. "Ehhh let's throw this in why not."  Small skill thing that I often forget to use.   Lastly: -The upgrades: .... Small, flat % increases, an extra jump being near Legendary , many don't synergize well... These could've been permanent small unlocks like the ones you get at the Ramrod and nobody would've batted and eye. They're uninteresting, only kind of helpful at best, forgettable at worst. (Literally, i keep forgetting i have x or y sometimes with how small impact they have).    I'd say this game needs work to live up to the hype , and hopefully time will tell if they make it interesting and balanced. But as of now it's more a "get run over by twitchy, jumpy , same-y enemies with small crit  locations while you flounder about and shoot for dear life."   ... At least the  Double Barrel Shotgun is better than in base  DRG! ...   -The upgrades:

Lastly you have people with the mental brain power of a bread, that will light you up like a christmas tree, becuase moving slightly left to shoot past you is somehow inconvenient. I can't recommend the game in its current state. So far, it feels quite repetitive and lacking in variety.  The ammo economy feels terrible, and the upgrades are barely noticeable, not moving the needle at all apart from a higher damage number and occasional special effect. While the out of mission upgrade tree is a nice feature, it needs way more depth. And same goes for in mission upgrades, they need to be more game altering.   The game also needs a wider variety of materials. Finding the same material repeatedly becomes tedious quick. And I understand its to streamline the progression for upgrades, but, this is a MINING game. Additional materials could be integrated into the out of mission upgrade tree, which would help make progression more of a grind and feel more rewarding as you could up the effects of the perks.  These are my thoughts at the moment. I hope future updates help the game become the roguelite/like experience it aims to be. However, in its current state, I will be refunding. I would rather spend my time playing the original DRG.
