it feels like a stripped down drg with rougelike elements tacked onto it, the characters are fun but the player upgrades feel unimpactful, the progression is a slog, the gameplay loop gets dull after the first couple missions you play, the timer makes difficulty feel forced rather than organic, and forcing players to all stand in a circle is bad pacing. i wouldn't reccomend buying until they update it

I can't say I recommend it in it's current state. Having to worry about time management as well as hearding cats to access upgrades makes the game feel draining after a single run. Imo it doesn't help that the pace and difficulty is toned up on top of worrying about 'what your upgrades are gonna be' and how many players you have to account for in the hopes you get the gear you want  From my current experience; you're spending roughly a fourth of the whole game trying to get people together to get upgrades and gear, followed by waiting for a good 15-30 seconds for each selection screen. I can understand it's a matter of acclamation to a new system that people aren't used to, but I **DO NOT** see shared upgrades as a permanent core mechanic, period.  My key notes:  *I think progression could be better, I get it's in early development but it's still being sold to people to play, but I'd be less harsh just because it's ghost ship games and to me they are among the company with the highest good will standing I've had, and I use them as an almost ad nauseam example for 'how to make a good gaming community' (rip helldivers community they would've been my second)  *Fix or change the timer system: being rushed in every run feels draining and anxiety inducing in a negative way. It's hard to really embrace the environment you're in when there's a visual timer ticking and you have Omega screaming in your ears  *INDEPENDENT UPGRADES! On top of a rush timer, waiting for people to group for upgrades before waiting in selection screen is like you're at a drive thru with 4 indecisive hungry stoners and it's honest to god the most ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ exhausting and stupid part of this game, it absolutely kills it for me personally  *More interesting upgrade selections: A majority of the upgrades imo feel particularly linear, 'kill something and get red sugar' 'kill something and it blows up' 'sit next to someone to heal with them slowly' I feel like the only upgrades I really care about are from the weapons / class ability, or things like being able to spawn magic bullets. It would be nice if there were more risk >< reward upgrades, I feel like that's the essence of a roguelike which this game is trying hard to do with the DRG IP but it just feels like ♥♥♥♥ being thrown against a wall in the hopes X implemented factors will just work with it. I think if upgrades were more eye catching and build provoking rather than mostly being number changes or hit/kill effects it would make selections faster, and people wouldn't be wiggling their cursor around the choices like they're mosquitoes in a nudist colony  *This might change in the future, but I'd like to see environmental resources for making drinks like in DRG, working out is boring imo and takes from the comradery of having simpler access of slamming beers with homies by just playing the game normally instead of sidetracking to weird gimmicks that aren't enveloped with the core gameplay alone.  *I truly hate to say it but Omega narrator is one of the most nails on a chalkboard voice I've experienced in gaming. I think they either need to tone down the glitchy yelling based off of time based urgency or just change it entirely. I get that the players are mercenaries so their narrator would be different in kind to fit the vibe of being like space pinkerton esque cleanup & coverup crew, but Omega just spends 90% of their time gloating or shouting at you and it's kind of a major turn off. Maybe in the future there could be different narrators based off of 'who hired you' in a quadrant, and allow that idea to be manifested in the personalities of different mission voices to make a more meaningful 'I love to hate that guy' style. As of right now though, I can't help but think of the mission control voice from DRG, and how after like 700 hours of DRG I've never even thought of myself being sick of their lines, even if I've heard them thousands of times. IMO I think it's the delivery, Omega's lines alone are just tonally stochastic and obnoxious. If you want particular tones in your narrators, I think the idea of getting company CFO's or COO's as mission control voices either as primary or as side narrators to Omega would be pretty sick and add to the worldbuilding of just how dystopian the company you've mined for yesterday and have been hired for today *CAN* really be    Positive notes:  *Weapon designs are awesome, reloading feels crisp and gritty - especially the devastator pistol reload (reminds me a lot of the Johnny Silver-hand pistol reload)  *Character variety and their abilities are really nice! I think there's some work to do with the fluidity of abilities but overall I'd say it's the main thing that keeps me coming back for a run or two before my mood kinda dies   *Enemies and boss fights are really neat! I was skeptical at first with corespawn as default enemies, but I feel like how they've been tweaked for this game works well, and the variety on top of their unit pallet feels fun and welcome  *Biomes are sick! The atmosphore of 'going deeper' in the world of Hoxxes feels tangible and not just as a passing mention from the synopsis of the game. You feel like you're going deeper for what time you do get to collect per depth mission, and being able to rock in stone your way down the hellevator loading screen is truly a charming feature  This game is rough right now, but I believe the vision of this game for as long as Ghost Ship Games listens to the more realistic criticisms and makes decisions that help to make the vets and the more casual minded players happy overall. There's no way to make everyone happy in any game, but I think both the developers and the players deserve to be happier with the state of the game right now, Rock and Stone!

I recommend you wait to buy this game (even for an early access title). As a roguelike, it's not great (yet). As a co-op shooter, it's not great (yet). If you can get this for like $10, then I'd say it's worth picking up. If you really want to scratch that itch, then buy Vermintide 2. The Chaos Wastes mode is this game but better imo.  Before I vent, I want to say I like this game and I think it has great potential. I hope this review does not age well.  In its current state, the game *can* be fun but is held back by baffling choices on the developers' part when there was plenty of closed alpha feedback AND examples that already work. Edit: I usually play depth 3 (depths are difficulty from 1-5) because any higher is imo too frustrating for me to enjoy myself.  - Bad movement (allegedly it's intentional, to make the game harder); Deep Rock Galactic has about 50% better movement than this game.  - Every fight is essentially a DPS check. Can you kill the enemies before they swiftly run up and claw your face? No? Guess you'll die. Running or kiting is NOT an option due to the neutered movement. Mortal Sin does this better.  - Temporary health mechanics do not come standard and are subject to RNG to even get them, on top of the possibility that your teammates might just take them before you can get them (since they probably need them too).  - Forced cooperation. The game is obsessed with forcing everyone to do things at the same time, which only serves to irritate you especially if your last run was ruined. Cooperation shouldn't be forced, it should be *encouraged*. Vermintide 2's Chaos Wastes mode does the roguelike co-op formula much better and that's not even the main part of the game.  - Runs are VERY long for a co-op shooter (45 mins is about the average in my experience), which doesn't help the frustration you experience when you die to some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ (probably the movement system screwing you over). Your survival is essentially RNG-based (even for roguelike standards). Megabonk does the timer mechanic AND movement better than this game.  - Poor balancing with upgrades. Some are 100% necessary regardless of build (the temporary health ones) and others are only relevant if you are playing a certain class (you cannot feasibly pivot); Balatro and CloverPit do this better.  - Very little QoL improvements despite the modding scene on Deep Rock Galactic. For example, there's no 'Run by Default' setting, something they refuse to implement in Deep Rock Galactic. This is exacerbated by the bad movement. I hope your pinky doesn't mind pressing Shift to run about every second.

Fun idea on paper, terrible execution. Feels like this game was developed in a bubble without any critical thought applied to how random players interact with each other. The game is barebones currently. You can burn through all the main progression goals in just a week. What remains after is a pointless, endless grind to increase your rank. No new rewards, no new cosmetics to unlock outside of missions, no deep customisation.   My main concerns reflect the community consensus about the timer, the communal upgrades and ammo economy. All three features need more time in the oven and significant changes need to be made. Firstly, this is not a deep rock. It may superficially resemble deep rock galactic, but shares little with its predecessor. If you expect an experience involving exploration and resource gathering, you will be disappointed. Rogue core strips these elements and cherry picks some minigames from deep rock to produce what amounts so far to a mediocre roguelite. Controls feel refined but there is little else to praise. The timer in conjunction with the map generation spreads objectives out such that players inevitably separate to pursue them all. However, leveling and upgrading requires players to stand together in a circle. The upgrade system is standard roguelite fare except that you share a pool with your teammates. Thus you find yourself competing with teammates in what is meant to be a coop game. In its current state, the game is filled with contradictions like this, not to mention some balancing issues all while charging full price for an early access experience. Only buy this game if you are an idiot like I am.

The game is ok, it has some fun mechanics, however its horrible to play with random people (haven't wone one single mission with a group of randos, there's allways a bastard that's afk or goes off to die 200m away from the upgrades that are only available when all players are gathered in 1 spot at the objective). That's a stark contrast from original DRG where joining missions were a blast. Guess the game is still new and people haven't got used to this more fast pace gameplay. Overall the game has potential but 70% of the players got to go. Such a shame, playing with new players was a fun aspect of the original game but here it just unbearable

After the revive you can't get health or armour back meaning its basically game over. Maybe there is a lore reason for the lack of regenerating shield from the main game, but that system is infinitely better as at least you have a chance to recover if your smart about it. Not to mention the resupplies in the main game gave health and this doesn't, they should at least do that. Insanely frustrating how you either feel like you aren't making any progress towards the challenges by doing only the "easy" missions or you get demolished in anything harder than depth one. Game needs to be balanced desperately for solo play to be viable.

Same as what most people are saying honestly. I've put over 600 hours into the original deep rock, and I knew going in that this would be a different experience, yet rogue core is such a muddied mess at the moment that I can't stand much more than 10 hours.   The game seems horribly confused as to what it wants to be. It wants you to go through the levels quickly to beat the timer, yet you only stand to lose out doing so. Much like the original deep rock, you benefit from clearing out each floor, but gain no extra benefits from moving fast. It's baffling game design and it doesn't feel good at all.  On top of that, you share perks and weapon choices between your team. This system requires everyone to gather around an object and slow down the pace of the game even more despite everything telling you to keep moving. Once in this menu, everyone has an insane amount of time to sit and choose between often lackluster options, wasting minutes of your time in a supposedly fast paced game for boring stat boosts and weapons that feel worse than swinging your pickaxe sometimes.  Much like the original DRG, the perks in this game somehow remain the most boring aspect, and sadly those are the only upgrades that linger between runs. Increased health here, faster running there, it's just so uninspired and it makes me sad. Even a good portion of the perks midgame share this issue, ensuring you have to pick several upgrades you'll be lucky to notice, and most likely, had to settle for because you were last pick.   The game also feels unfairly difficult at times, especially at the start. Some of the starting weapons are insufferably dinky, and feel nigh useless against the wildly flailing creatures we're set to kill. The ammo economy also feels so much worse, further forcing you to take your time and search for more, only to screw yourself with the timer. Maybe I've just been unlucky during some games.  I know I said a lot of negatives, but I do think these things could be mended with time, but as it stands the game just feels bad to play. You're slow, yet everything is screeching at you to move quickly as if you have any real ability or reason to, leaving you feeling as if you never stood a chance at all. Hopefully the developers listen to feedback and smooth out the very rough edges of whatever this game is trying to be, I'm not giving up on it yet.  I wish I had more good things to say, but aside from a few new weapons being decent, the art style still rocking, and the IDEA of the game being interesting, there just isn't enough at the moment to recommend this high of an asking price for what little fun there is on offer with its singular gameplay loop.

Timer sucks. Balance sucks. There's not much to do, and the fun events basically need to be skipped due to the timer.  Just a huge miss in terms of game design, in such a fundamental way. At their pace of development it'll take years of changes to make it actually fun, and the devs haven't indicated that they even care to do that.

The game is filled with good ideas balanced by terrible execution.  I was constantly reminded of Yahtzee's video review of Alone in the Dark, specifically the part regarding the two hypothetical designers. Ultimately, by the time I failed enough missions to unlock some decent gear, I was starting to get the hang of the game and what to do, and specifically what not to do (basically: do not do anything other than bee-line to the elevator and get a few upgrades/gear on the way).  I don't know who the intended audience for this game is.  Rogue Core takes a very accessible base game (Deep Rock Galactic) and mixes roguelite elements into it which make it more difficult and less rewarding, seemingly to appeal to enjoyers of a much more niche genre.  There's some things that also just don't make a lot of sense, given the intended lore:  the Reclaimers are top-tier combat operatives but they send them into the deep layers of the planet without regenerating shielding or grenades?  Why is ammo so scarce in a combat-oriented game?  There are class abilities which are kind of explained, but take a bit of time to get used to and to see how they are best deployed.  I found them less useful for getting through the levels than I did just surviving a few more minutes to see an unreasonable amount of Core Spawn dumped onto my party while we were already starved for ammunition and were resorting to pummeling monsters in the face, all while our health ticked down rapidly due to corpse toxins and being attacked from all sides.  Overall the combat is much improved, weapons that were trash in DRG like the burst-fire pistol have gotten another pass on them, but new trash weapons like the slow-traveling laser-quad pistol are added in, and all the weapon loadouts are randomized per mission ... so if you didn't get to pick one of the non-trash weapons first, then maybe you'll have better luck in the next random drop. Still,  not a fun game loop and it only seems to irritate my friends.  I didn't mention the immortal worm that appears if you take too long to escape any given level yet.  I will mention it now: It sucks, and really takes my enjoyment of the game down a few pegs because if you're playing with just one other player, you might as well just abort the mission and try again.  Again, I have to ask:  Which audience was this game made for?

Not only does rogue core completely misunderstand what made the original DRG enjoyable it also completely misunderstands what makes roguelikes as a genre entertaining. As a continuation of the Deep Rock Galactic universe its terrible but as a roguelike its laughably ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ taking all of the least enjoyable mechanics from the genre without any of the parts that make them fun and squeezing them into a single game.

I am a LONG TIME fan of Deep Rock Galactic, however, in good conscious- I just cannot recommend Rogue Core; and I believe there are 4 large issues pertaining to this.  1 - The Pacing, As a lot of others have mentioned- the "Timer" system applied to each floor of an expedition makes it feel like you can't always get the most out of the new content. Exponite events are scattered through floors, even moreso on higher complexity or depth missions- but because of the timer, these are essentially locked out unless you're able to rush through everything else, which can be costly given the next issue.  2 - Balance Long in Short: Too many enemies that hit far too hard, even on level 1 missions; combined with the waves as a result of the poor pacing that can make boss events borderline impossible to complete at times. Another issue in this regard is the weapons; most are fun to use, sure- but that doesn't mean they're effective. 90% of equipment, new or old, just feels like throwing pebbles at a brick wall. Combined with the strict ammo economy and tough enemies, this can make reliably completing expeditions a frustrating task.  3 - Poor Roguelike Elements in a game called Rogue Core Upgrades feel either invaluable or worthless, leaving not much room for viable builds that don't rely on the same 3 or 5 upgrades / weapon choices- which you're not guaranteed *because* of the roguelike elements. Also- Why are upgrades shared in multiplayer? Just give each player their own choice of a personal upgrade.  4 - Armor System Why even remove shield from the first game? Given the spike in difficulty, making armor that has a hardly noticeable effect 80% of the time just feels more frustrating than intuitive. The resupplies also **Not** refilling health like in the first game also feels like a frustrating choice. It's just not fun to do- especially in boss floors where, while resupplies are usually scattered around the general arena area, they just aren't enough since shield doesn't take priority over health- only share the damage taken with itself. This means that, in solo- where you have Cooper- you can get locked into this rhythm of taking a resupply *(or trying to, since there's always a ludicrous amount of projectiles or basic enemies being thrown at you in a BOSS FLOOR)*, then going down almost immediately after because you didn't actually refill any health; just reduced the damage taken for the next few hits; before eventually running out of revives.  Sorry if this is worded a bit strangely at points- There's just so much that feels wrong about this game, yet it all feels so strange to try and convey properly. It's frustrating, not fun half the time, and feels like it's trying to hide more precise issues under visual and baseline gameplay polish.  It all feels like it *Could* work just as well as Deep Rock Galactic, but refuses to.

As much as I love base DRG, Rogue Core feels empty compared to it. There's not enough in the game, and the bits that are in the game don't provide enough substance.  Upgrades range from useless to absolutely overkill, with some upgrades being straight-up unusable because of the game state (Glass Cannon is the worst Artifact upgrade in the game, and it's not even close). Some upgrades have extremely niche application cases, or just don't provide anything, such as the sticky flames on walking upgrade, or exploding in a small radius when taking a chunk of damage. Selecting upgrades isn't any easier, and the way selection order is delegated feels favored against the player in most cases, where teammates can indirectly nerf you by taking upgrades; the small pool of actually good upgrades lowers the chance of a build working when one or two upgrades are taken. Having to wait for players to get together and gain upgrades is also just as annoying, especially when all players share the R.E.P.D.   The gun selection is interesting, with some arguably being worse than the base DRG guns. Half of the new guns for the game don't account for the massive swarms at higher depths, and it doesn't help when the starting weapon and equipment crates don't give anything good.  Cave generation is rough, especially with how much there is scattered between the cave. Each cave past Depth 1-2 is built with the idea that teams are either going to have to skip upgrades, or deal with the timer punishment in order to keep up with the floor's level. Some objectives are just inaccessible if you don't have the time or right traversal tools.   Damage is extremely unforgiving, especially with how swarms and friendly fire is amplified. One stray creeper can take out a good chunk of your shield if they sneak up from behind, and the speed/lack of space in some caves leads to situations where you're bound to take damage regardless of how much mobility you have. Every hit chunks your health regardless of the amount of health/armor upgrades you have, and it doesn't help when friendly fire is not only easier to deal, but heavier. Items like the Incendiary Grenade are notorious for shredding shields and dealing heavy friendly fire damage even to the user.   General difficulty scales pretty fast, even on lower Depths. The basic corespawn are as much of a threat as any other enemy in the game, with some being extremely unfun or difficult to fight against. Some ranged, fast and tanky corespawn such as Edgestalkers not only have too much health, but have too much damage to reliably dodge and fight against, while dealing with potential corespawn waves actively in front of the player. Based on your gear and ammo count, you either anchor or run in circles until the elevator arrives or everyone is downed.  The bosses of Rogue Core are arguably harder than all the Dreadnoughts, and even minibosses like the Tyrant Weed or BET-C. The non-flying bosses have too much projectile spam, in a game that lacks proper cover and projectile mitigation; certain abilities such as the Slicer's shield don't even fully block projectiles, with some phasing through the shield.  (I'm running out of word count) Nerf Ramok Gatekeeper  While I don't see any issue with the timer, the punishment is rather harsh, especially with how unreliable it is. Worm spawns vary based on cave generation, and they tend to clip through terrain or important structures such as the elevator lift. Sometimes, shooting the head or body doesn't retract or drop a grabbed player, and some worms move faster when grabbing players. Some worms may even spawn during intense waves, with some spawning extremely close, or right on-top of the elevator lift; in cases where they grab a player, every other player has at most 1-2 seconds before the grabbed player is downed for the rest of the floor. It's difficult to discern the worms from a swarm in most instances, and by the time you notice a player is grabbed, they're already pulled under.   Ammo economy is even worse in this game, with how much is needed to clear waves, even simple spawns from corespawn rifts can cost a good chunk of ammo. Ammo resupplies can range from too far to be considered grabbable, or are available when the player already has ammo available, such as the immediate start of a floor, where the elevator generates an ammo resupply on the side. Taking double guns or sticking with a single gun, either option expends your ammo at the same rate, and it's all dependent on corespawn difficulty and spawn rate.  Solo play is outright unplayable, even at lower Depths. Depths 1 and 2 are doable if the player has game knowledge of DRG, but new players won't make it past Depth 1 without a team. The option to remove Cooper from solo play also just doesn't make sense to me; I know the option to remove Bosco is also in base DRG, but there are more systems in play that allow solo play in DRG over Rogue Core. Cooper doesn't do nearly enough, is too slow, and if he's mining or isn't actively shooting, he's a detriment more than anything. The only saving grace Cooper has is his 3 revives, but that doesn't get capitalized upon when all the corespawn group around your body (They do flee when Cooper revives, but they move a total of 3-5m before they can just walk back and hit the player once). Mining and doing objectives takes too long to do solo, and it shows in the lack of Expenite upgrades you're able to get at a time.  My ideas for some fixes: - Add a refresh option for upgrades, Expenite, Weapon/Equipment Crates, and Artifacts With how most upgrades are, most players either choose the shiny, Epic upgrade that does nothing, or skip and take health. An option for players to refresh a page of upgrades, or at least one upgrade would help in getting players closer to builds they actually want. These refreshes shouldn't be too frequent and shouldn't be able to be spammed, maybe one refresh per floor. - Add indicators for worms An indicator for worm spawns, and a more obvious indicator that a player was grabbed would help. At the point of the mission where worms spawn, players are already going to be occupied running towards the elevator, or actively fending off waves while the elevator arrives. Worms can easily spawn anywhere, ranging from extremely far to right next to objectives. They aren't as discernable when fending off waves, and their grab isn't immediately known to the team. - Upgrades are individual Instead of team-wide upgrades, players should be able to gain upgrade choices independently, based on the amount of players in a team. Players should only be able to select one in a set of upgrades at a time, but they should be able to claim which upgrades they see without another player stealing their pick. It doesn't make sense in a roguelike like this to share upgrades, especially given the small pool and lack of unique builds for upgrades. - Better upgrades As it stands, I'd estimate only 25% of the upgrades are actually worthwhile, with the other 75% being niche or plain bad. it doesn't make sense why some upgrades even exist, as they not only don't provide any actual benefit, but don't build into anything at all. - Make solo play playable While Rogue Core is designed as a team-based game, it makes no sense to have a solo option when it almost always results in failure. Solo play is locked as singleplayer, which should mean that since no other player can join, the sole player should be able to act as four players. Increasing solo player health/armor, ammo reserves, or even just allowing two upgrades at a time to be chosen can allow solo play some leverage. - Rework the R.E.P.D. upgrade system When the R.E.P.D. is attached to a locker, it can't be called to deposit Expenite; players are forced to group up in order to open the locker and unstuck the R.E.P.D. Forcing players to group up for upgrades is not ideal, especially when players have to split and collect Expenite/finish objectives.

I really want to like this game. It looks engaging from the outside, but several design choices keep pushing me away.  1. Timer: I went in expecting to explore every side objective. The previous game offered plenty of side missions, collectibles, and hidden content, which gave it a stronger sense of discovery. Since you have about 10 min per stage, this is largely missing here.  2. Ammo economy: The ammo economy feels punishing. Depending on your weapons and upgrades, basic enemies can become bullet sponges very quickly. The game often sends large enemy waves at you without consistently giving you the resources to handle them. As a result, the challenge often comes from running dry rather than from the combat itself.  3. Armor: This is subjective, but I preferred the shield system. Having to balance another health bar, but brown is a slog.  4. Upgrades: The upgrades I have seen so far feel underwhelming. Most of them seem to be simple stat increases rather than meaningful changes to gameplay.  5. Objectives: Some objectives feel poorly suited to what made DRC enjoyable. The hacking mini-game is questionable, and having to identify the correct numbers and letters when your options are lIIIII, IIIIll, and IlIlIl is genuinely frustrating.  There may be a good game here, but it takes too long into a run to know whether you are having fun or just losing time. At the moment, I cannot recommend it.

Posting this for my friends interested in the game.  Huge DRG fan and was very excited for this. The opening lore vibes and the vibe of the world hits the spot.  And then everything from there is downhill. Honestly I'm not sure exactly why it isn't fun to me, but part of me worries it's the fundamental Rogue Core formula that just doesn't work. It sounded so fun, combat focused and roguelike elements, but instead it just feels like a DRG DLC with a ton of sluggish pauses to choose upgrades with people.  There's also the other two issues, the timer and the ammo, but I think those will be addressed somehow.  I also don't really enjoy any of the five classes so far, but I'll keep trying them out. I think making more of them combat focused would help, or maybe it's that they are so confusing to me. It feels like the classic DRG classes add a lot more to the game. Maybe the abilities are just quirky for me. But having two press TWO keys at the same time as Slicer defeats the point of a dash.  Also... what the heck is the "core" that went rogue? It's not very clear in the intro video.  Also why are none of the mobs from DRG in this? I'm stoked for new mobs but shouldn't there by Glyphids?  I dunno... I hope they can fix this game and make me love it. I sure want to! But as it stands it's not worth the price tag. I feel like it should have been a $20 DLC to not get people's hopes up. I assume they improved the game engine though? Maybe modding will turn it into a masterpiece.  EDIT: Some idea to fix upgrades:  Have a "hearted" upgrade automatically unlock, maybe even allows users to prioritize a few upgrades so if their first choice is taken then it automatically chooses the next. Then the upgrades would go by WAY faster.  When weapons are chosen and boths slots are available, automatically choose the slot. I don't want to click twice every time.  Use more/better symbols or something to make the upgrades more intuitive. I hate having to sit there and read as much as I do, that's part of what made the whole thing not fun for me.

This is Deep Rock Galactic without what made it so good.  The missions last longer than the deep dives from the original. Now, one of the most common complaints is the mission timer, and im not gonna be a contrarian here. The caves have a bunch of optional side objectives that give rewards, thus encoraging you to take your time, but the timer makes it so you can't even do that.   The upgrade debating system makes missions only take longer because there is one person thats on the other side of the galaxy while everyone else is trying to get their upgrades. The upgrades themselves change very little and don't have any real synergy with eachother. This is a roguelite, yet it doesn't feel like one.  The classes themselves are also underwhelming, and you don't have much of a reason to play a specific one, as they all get the same weapons anyway. Their abilities don't affect gameplay much. Each one has a combat, defensive and utility ability, and they all blend together and feel the same.  Performance is another issue i ran into constantly. My computer is far above the recommended system requirements listed on the store page, yet i lag whenever there is more than 3 enemies nearby.  I sincerely hope it improves and makes me want to play it again, but i am done for now.

Eh. It's not that fun.  Edit: Well, since I have time now. This game is a bit... undercooked. And not in the "early access" kind of way, it's more of the "as a game" kind of way. There are so many various issues that I have problems trying to concisely list them. But, here we go.  [h1] Baseline Kit and the progression system[/h1] After playing this game I went back and played nearly every single action roguelike and horde survival game in my repertoire. Gunfire, Dead Cells, Gungeon, Star of Providence, discount RoR, etc.   Base power is too low. [list]     [*]You're too slow to avoid damage     [*]Dying is extremely punishing unless you use one of your few meta progression slots (notice the plural) to add forgivness to revives     [*]You don't even have a proper cleave in this game.      [*]Ammo sustain is awful     [*]Survivability and baseline health/shields/regen are all terrible     [*]Health seems balanced around doing 50-100% more damage than you can do without taking the mandatory meta skills [/list]  It's like they took what [b]any[/b] other game in this genre gives you and then removed it to sell it back to you in the meta progression. "Builds" are less choosing different flavors of playing and more putting crap onto your character that should already be there. It feels like ♥♥♥♥ to have a melee weapon that ends up objectively bad to use in a row because you got exactly 0 ways to make it spread.  In something like literally any game above and the majority of every single horde survival/shooter I've played, incoming damage     [h1] (the lack of) skill in the equation of success[/h1] Nothing says skillful gameplay like precision weakpoints on non hitscan weapons on enemies with nearly completely unpredictable movement patterns, vulnerability windows, pathing, and speed.   [list]     [*]An enemy can properly walk at you, and then skitter backwards     [*]Enemies have Vermintide 2 Chaos Warrior punch hitboxes where if you weren't already moving before they started their windup you just got hit with no counterplay.     [*]Enemies walk through and attack through solid objects with no sight, visual indicator, or even audio queue     [*]Chip is completely unavoidable (and it's telling that one of the win conditions of every single solo run on depth 4 is to get one of the 2.5 sustain rng cards that completely break and brute force sustain automatically). [/list]  As a result runs are devolved into nothing more than brute forcing the game mechanics to the exception of the boss (who is the easiest part of every single run). In something like Nightreign I have pulled off the wiliest comebacks off of pure adhd twitch reaction, enemy knowledge, map awareness, and mechanical skill. Meanwhile you will NEVER do that here because some seeds (on solo Depth 4) with some dwarves are flat out unwinnable.  [h1] Sound Design[/h1] Getting it's own shiny point (because of how important it is) and part of the above, the sound sucks.  Every single game I've listed thus far (alright not Providence), you could blindfold me and I could tell you where the enemy was, how many there were, how far they were, if they were currently swinging at me, and where on the Y-axis they are. All very important things to be able to decipher.  Conversely the sound in this game is terrible. You'll have a general idea of vague direction and there the audio knowledge ends.   [h1] Bad enemy design[/h1] I too wish that I had a tanky, higly mobile enemy that can sit on the ceiling and throw ♥♥♥♥ at me. I love that every single enemy hard requires weakspot hits and yet your starting pool of weapons can give you slow projectiles.  I love enemies whose gimmick is that they take no damage unless they do an aoe, requires seconds of uninterrupted fire (in a horde shooter), to kill, directly punish melee unless the snowball has already started, and whom take 4x LESS damage when hit anywhere else.  There's not one, not two, but three  enemies whose gimmick is that they play keep away and move over 5 times as fast as you do. Two of which spawn crap constantly. Oh the artifact orb went down to the bottom of a complexity 3 cave and through an adjacent tunnel at 7x my traversal speed. No yeah that's great. I'm glad this exists.  There's sacs that spawn suicide bombers and I've seen the procgen put in the things in the wall that require digging to. Every single enemy is annoying waste of time. But not in an engaging way like Darktide. No it's a waste of time in the literal "you can't even interact with the enemy because it [b]Completely unpreventably[/b] LoS'd you". That's not fun.  [h1] Multiplayer[/h1] The loot here isn't instanced and the rolls barely expand per additional player. You go from having 3 selections at every negotiation (solo) to randomly only having ONE choice multiplayer because you were 4th pick.  I've seen insane amounts of friction just from a player not being as good as the other three were. I've seen some guy flame a literal newbie. They've made a game in which the amount of in-fighting seems to be baked in. As this game continues it's player drain spiral towards a single player experience,   This isn't like dropping an item for somebody else in Nightreign where the other player had their own rolls and you had your own rolls and you're doing it out of the kindness of your heart to the betterment of you both. Sometimes in runs a player is plain old going to get screwed. Haha. How funny.  Which itself wouldn't be bad if the player base level tools (mentioned above) weren't complete ass.   [h1] Speed[/h1] This game has a timer. It's contentious but I get it. Nightreign has a timer. Nightreign also has better (albeit boring) map gen. You'll seldom full clear the map but you'll never be lacking in player power. There's choice towards power progression and ways to adequately map your route.  In this game, none of that ♥♥♥♥ exists. You can't even see the map let alone plan around it, all roads are linear, cave seeds are random so you can fluctate between a map in which all objectives are possible and ones where they flat out aren't.  On top of that so many actions are too slow for no reason. Go ahead and repair that generator on depth 4 layer 5 solo when all of your guns remain ass, you got no elements/chain shot, and the repair speed is 5 seconds. You won't. You're not that guy.  [h1] Solo[/h1] Having run solo runs with every single character I can confidently say this is by far the worst solo exxperience I've ever had in an action roguelike or multiplayer games. Darktide can sometimes FEEL impossible when you're still ass at the game Inversely D4 actually IS impossible dependent on RNG seed.  There's not much more that needs to be said than this. If you bought this game to play/relax solo, you bought the wrong game.  Cooper is like a worse Bosco.   [list]     [*]Cooper mines as useless slow as an unpgraded bosco     [*]Coopers rockets can't be controlled/manually fired and they can't be stocked as you only get 1     [*]Coopers base movement speed is useless slow [/list] Alright cooper, take the 10 seconds you need to actually travel to that node and the 30 seconds you need to mine it. There's also this stupid acceleration it has. When I was still getting grabbed by leeches I'd see that thing drift behind a rock (due to admittedly poor terrain gen) and just never be able to get me.  That is, without a doubt, the most frustrated I've been at a game in over three years. All of this and more is also part of the baseline problem where Cooper just flat out sucks ass because there's less control and all of it's tools are worse so they can sell it back in meta progression   [h1] Roguelike[/h1] The power ups aren't fun The guns suck And large amounts of this overpriced game were ripped wholesale out of DRG  A 10% chance to dodge while sprinting? Yes I'm sure that'll be extremely useful in a game in which you can't attack and simultaneously sprint. Cool.  TL;DR Don't buy this.

Neither currently are there for me.  The best part of a roguelike is having all the stars align to create absolute chaos or having luck not on your side to still win feels amazing. As it stands, it’s hard to recommend this game.  I’m not really a roguelite/like enjoyer, but as a long-time enjoyer of DRG, I decided to try Rogue Core. Unfortunately, while it retains the DRG aesthetic and overall design, it loses much of the identity that made the original so enjoyable. I wouldn’t call it bad, but right now it feels painfully average and undercooked - more like an Early Access title released a year too early.  The biggest issue for me is that Rogue Core shifts heavily away from exploration and sandbox-style gameplay in favor of a constant, unnecessary rush pressure. DRG worked because it balanced action with exploration, mining, traversal, and downtime. RC feels much more linear and nervous by comparison.  Some of my main issues so far:  [h3]General / QoL[/h3] - Sprint by default should exist. - Falling and pickaxe slowdowns feel unnecessary in a game built around constant movement. - Cave generation feels far too linear. I understand this was intentional to support combat pacing, but exploration was one of DRG’s biggest strengths. - Combat and visuals become cluttered very quickly. More visual/ragdoll settings would help readability a lot.  [h3]Gameplay[/h3] - The timer fundamentally clashes with what I enjoyed about DRG. Being constantly rushed removes a lot of the fun from exploring and interacting with the caves. - The shared upgrade system is unnecessary. In practice, players usually just take what they need when their turn comes up. Separate upgrade pools would feel much better. - Mobility feels lacking on most classes except Slicer. In games like this, movement is a huge part of what makes combat engaging. Rather than nerfing Slicer, the other classes should be brought up to that level. - Replacing shields with armor feels like a downgrade. - Character progression currently feels shallow. Outside of skills, classes lack identity and there’s little meaningful build development yet. - The Merit economy also feels poorly balanced. Since progression is tied heavily to class-specific goals, playing mainly one class can slow progression to a crawl. - Gauntlet becomes exhausting in co-op later on because constant combat leaves almost no room for mining or exploration. A lot of the systems that gave DRG long-term depth - overclocks especially - are still missing.  I do think the foundation is there, and the gunplay is still fun, but right now Rogue Core loses some of the charm and freedom that made DRG stand out in the first place.  Hopefully Early Access gives Ghost Ship enough time to refine the systems, improve class progression, and better balance combat with exploration, because there’s definitely potential here.

I loved the original DRG, and naturally super hyped for this one as well. I think it's enjoyable at times, but overall it's lacking something that DRG had. And I don't think it's about content. I don't actually like leaving bad reviews; I only do it for games I want to see succeed.  Here are a couple of points I feel hold Rogue Core back:  [olist] [*][b]Enemy spawns[/b] — The way enemies spawn I find uniquely tilting. In DRG there was some level of predictability of where enemies spawn, and I think this was quite important to the experience. In Rogue Core, you can be holding an angle and consistently get backstabbed. Your teammates are actually pretty dangerous to be around due to the pure chaos of where enemies are — the amount of friendly fire I'm experiencing is next level. The backstabs from a flank you checked two seconds ago feel like playing free-for-all in Counter-Strike. It feels like there's nothing you can do to prevent that damage; you can't check all angles all the time. It breaks the physicality of the experience. [*][b]Level generation[/b] — Feels stale and repeatable compared to DRG. There's very little verticality to the maps (I haven't tried complexity 3 yet, so this could change). In DRG you got the sense that there were long caverns stretching up and down, so much variety, and you could actually get lost pretty easily. Rogue Core feels more horizontal. [*][b]Class identity[/b] — Character classes don't form a unique roster of responsibilities. In DRG, each role has a strong identity and a specific job. Rogue Core's classes feel interchangeable to me, and I missed that feeling of having a specialized job to do on the team. [*][b]The opening of each run[/b] — Going in without weapons to open the magical wall, then heading to a crate to pick your loadout, feels gimmicky and gets repetitive fast. It adds a solid minute and a half every game just to go pick weapons. That said, I don't mind the system where players are randomly ordered and pick weapons in turns — it adds variety and makes you adapt and sometimes work with the scraps. That's a fine feature to me. [/olist]

Because of Hyper Light Breaker, I will be hard pressed to say that this is the most mediocre, frustrating, poorly-balanced, undercooked, and/or belabored follow-up to a game I loved. However that bar is so low that Satan's using it to floss between his toes. The core here is not unsalvageable, and I don't even think egregious changes are necessary, but this is a pathetic showing after years of development. Constant rookie mistakes that were solved in DRG. Unacceptable amounts of variety even for an EA launch. Legendarily poor balance.   Anyone who has played enough DRG to have the mechanical familiarity required to play beyond the first hour will be able to see this as a shallow and broken experience; it's simultaneously too married to DRG's design decisions to kill its sacred cows but completely unable to stand on its own merits without that pedigree.   This will be a fine game after a few years of work, I'm just naïve for assuming those years took place already.

Timer sucks- No time to do any of the several side objectives that spawn on each map or explore at all.  Weird/unimpactful weapons and upgrades- Often time feels like I'm fighting with my friends over who gets the weapon/upgrade that sucks less.  No clear path- Very easy to get turned around or lost, the only indicator is a dark pipe on the ground which sometimes clips through terrain. Compounds the oppressive feeling of the timer.  Boss arena can generate terribly- Had the rift spawn on a cliff so the orbs would fly off and it took forever to climb back up, all the while being killed by the constant boss attacks and swarming enemies. Also the red sugar spawned om the ceiling which is horrible trying to reach in the middle of a boss fight.   Ammo and healing is incredibly scarce- Drawing the short straw and having to take a pistol feels even worse when you run out of ammo and there are 30 corespawn coming at you.  Fall damage/buggy small lifts- Have fallen through lifts/elevators several times and it always results in losing half my health for what seems to be a small drop. These lifts are also very small for a 4 player co-op game on a short timer.  Long intro/duration of matches- Seems like it takes 5-10 minutes to even start a run with the arbitrary tasks you have to complete in order to even start playing. You have to ride the elevator down, activate the machines to open the rift wall, call over the flying mule to open the weapon chest, wait for your teammates to gather around the box, wait for your teammates to pick a weapon, unlock the grenade box, wait for your teammates to pick a grenade. It just feels like everything is so drawn out and slow. Then the runs themselves take around an hour or more unless you're just rushing through, which will leave you weaker with less upgrades and gear. People that don't have infinite time to play will probably play a round a day, which can make a bad run feel like a waste of time.  There are some things I like about Rogue Core but I just find myself more frustrated every time I try it out. The classes are cool and interesting and the 1/10 time you get a good weapon it can be a lot of fun.  I've played hundreds of hours of Deep Rock Galactic and think it's one of the best co-op games out there. I do not think Rogue Core comes close to it. I'm confident that Ghost Ship Games will fix it in time, but for now I do not recommend buying it. It just feels like worse Gunfire Reborn wearing a Deep Rock costume.

The core of the game is very solid and it very much shows that there is room to grow and get better. As it stands right now however the game is just kinda frustrating at points. There is almost no power fantasy and a bunch of different design choices that clash against each other. 1. The switch from Shields to Armor. I don't know why we keep trying to make armor like this in sequels to massively popular series but apparently it continues to be a trend. Similar to the original Payday 3 armor system the armor system in Rogue Core works as an "allowed fun meter" where once it's gone you might as well accept your death. The only way to replenish armor is to either find ammo pods or armor crates around the map and once those are gone or you're too far and have too little time to go back well you're ♥♥♥♥ out of luck. 2. Ammo economy is just abysmal. You are actively punished for wanting to take a back up secondary weapon by having your ammo reserves gutted for both weapons to the point you'll be wishing you made sure to take a melee build because the guns are going to quickly become just for show. Ammo resupplies are spread out *juuuust* enough to where you will pretty much just *barely* survive the hoard and god help you if you have back to back hoards, events, escape etc. 3. The Timer is just too short. I don't really have much to say other than yeah it's too short. I don't mind the concept of the Timer or the punishment for running out of time since you kinda need it for a roguelike with Deep Rock's mechanics but it's just too damn short. You have barely any time for proper exploration and you're under too much pressure to just keep moving. It's this constant clash of  don't stop if you stop you're dead even though you need to stop and look around and collect resources and oh get this upgrade and do this minigame and OH WAIT TIMES UP RELEASE THE DEATH WORMS DIE. 4. Upgrades affect too little/don't do enough. So far the upgrades available just don't feel like they do too much. The bonuses you get from most are too small to really affect how I feel while playing or give me this feeling of meaningful progression. Some of them for the classes are a bit redundant too from what I've seen like getting the option to pick between DAMAGE UP or DAMAGE UP like oo such variety!  If I think of anything else I'll add it but right now playing Rogue Core just kinda makes me want to play Deep Rock Galactic instead. I'll keep playing because like I said the core of the game is solid and I trust GSG to turn this into a gem but right now I would probably wait for the first few major updates to start rolling out especially with a $30 price tag

If there was a change to the picking system for the weapons this game would be a solid 4/5. Having other players take one of the new weapons and you are being force to pick from what's left is not fun.

In its current state, I'd have to say no. Why? Well, let me explain some of my recent frustrations...  Level ups and weapons choices are all shared between the entire team instead of being instanced. If you aren't playing with friends, guess what? Random people will take the best things if they go before you, which they will 75% of the time. Upgrades should be an "instanced" set of upgrades only available to each player.  Additionally, the game far too often will give you trash tier common side arms for your starting weapons. This slows you down a lot, and then you're forced to decide between clearing the entire level or trying to rush the elevator to proceed before you run out of time/ammo. Starting weapons in a run [i]should[/i] be primary weapons like ARs and Shotguns at a [b][i]minimum[/i][/b]. The way the game is currently set up is NOT good game design. I have played a lot of roguelikes and the games do tend to feel hard or challenging when you're starting out, but being hard because of poor or obfuscating design choices just isn't the way to achieve it. Also, the game currently has very little in the way of cosmetics or meta progression (think small passive boosts from the DRG game).  Finally, the sheer number of enemies and swarms when you're on a very limited ammo count just doesn't feel good. Gone are the days of farming Nitra and being able to call in ammo when/where you want. Good luck if you run out of ammo in a random swarm. The game loves to do large swarms back to back, when you have neither the time nor the ammo to deal with such things. The time limit is not an addition that makes the game feel better. Sadly, when you go overtime, tentacles start trying to abduct team members. Good luck freeing them or surviving if you have no ammo. Everything just ends up combining into a jumbled systems of gameplay elements that are actively not fun that makes me wonder if these guys even seriously tested things before they released this into early access.  All of these complaints aside, the foundation for a good game IS THERE. They just haven't made it there quite yet. All of the issues I've mentioned are things that can easily be changed with some patches and updates that focus on fun over frustration. If or when they do that, I'd say it's an easy game to recommend and play with friends. Until the day that they do, I'd stay away for now.

I don't usually write reviews, but by Karl I loved DRG and see so much potential in Rogue Core. There is a lot of great ideas and designs that could make for an immensely fun game but isn't fully realized.  Firstly, my positives. So much obvious love and passion was poured into this game, and it would be a sin not to give appreciation to GSG. At its core (heh) the concept of the game is fantastic, the design elements are great, and its ties to DRG are present but minimal. Despite being such a deeply connected game, Rogue Core feels completely fresh and different from DRG. Perks, characters, events, and enemies are overflowing with creativity. There is not a single enemy type present in Rogue Core that I hate on a cellular level (unlike Stingtail) which is amazing given the sheer number of deaths I've faced at their hands. The mix of ranged, heavy hitters, sneaks, and creeps forces players to be adaptable in their playstyle and build choices, which is good. DRG allowed you to sit back and blast glyphids, corespawn do not give you the luxury of a one-size fits all loadout. Challenging the player to juggle weapons, grenades, and skills makes for fun and tense shootouts. Also, despite absurd hours in DRG, this game has FORCED me to watch ceilings, so honestly respect :) I love the structures included in the mines, and more importantly I love how accessible each is. Stairs, elevators, and lifts make it possible to quickly explore terrain and structures, even with corespawn breathing down your neck. Finally, so many new weapons and ideas have been added that I have picked up and after the first shot went "WHY WAS THIS NOT IN DRG?!" Guns feels great, and upgrades to them feel meaningful.  Negatives: Roguelike elements: -Permanent Upgrades: You can bring in perks which add a slight amount of benefit to future runs, but are ultimately limited/overshadowed by the expenite boons you get. I don't mind that each run is heavily randomized with minimal permanent unlocks, except... -Poor loot pool: Most lost runs of Depth 2+ are lost in the opening minute. Because you don't bring any loadout to the mission you are completely dependent on that first equipment crate, and all too often you are choosing between three side arms which might be great for big targets but will do nothing for you against the hordes.  -Inconsistent Boons: Expenite boons are a bit underwhelming at times, particularly for Rare and Epic grades. I love the expenite system; its a perfect design of stay and grind at the risk of difficulty. But when you stretch for the next perk and are offered three buffs that require you to stand still (which is suicide) it feels bad.  Ideally, implementation of some form of reroll, banishing, or giving players a way to skew the draw pools will let players more consistently get kits that work while still making each run unique. Potentially consider classing perks so that one of each type always appears? Like Offense, Survival, Special  Bleed-through from DRG: While DRG is a fantastic game and obviously the two games will never be separated there are a massive number of design elements retained from DRG that simply do not fit in Rogue Core. -Terrain Generation: DRG terrain generation was, at best, silly. Which was fine in a game about digging the living mess out of a planet, but feels very poor and inhibitive in a fast-paced survival shooter. The worst of these by far is ledge grabbing which frequently will launch you away from cliffs instead of up them. Your traversal tools are next to useless as the game rarely allows you time to stage arenas like DRG. Rogue Core actively dissuades you from back-tracking, which I like, but makes drilling, flares, and platforms a waste of time when most places can be easily reached provided you aren't being eaten by the corespawn, to which point you are better just shooting them than a platform. Ideally generation should include more soft edges, make things interesting by tightening corridors, but make caverns more traversable. -Hit detection: This is painful, but true. DRG had bad hit detection, mostly on the enemy side. If you were remotely in the vicinity of an enemy, you are taking the hit. This was perfectly fine when you were constantly getting chipped, because in DRG you could shield regen it. Rogue Core does not afford you that luxury. Static armor makes the game significantly harder, and I don't disagree with having done that. I like the challenge and pressure of only having a fixed, non-regenerative health pool. What I don't like is losing health to a creeper 10 meters away from me, or from a projective that missed me but landed too close. This is especially problematic when viewed in the lense that enemies can hit you from a range your pickaxe cannot reach, despite neither character model overlapping. -Movement speed: DRG imposes a lot of restrictions on movement speed. You take a penalty when shooting, hitting with your pickaxe, when getting hit, etc. While most of these make sense, they feel bad in Rogue Core. When you are trying to move with limited time through tight corridors against enemies that can hit you from beyond sight, getting slowed and forced out of sprint with each hit just hurts. Sprint should be a permanent toggle, and move speed reductions should be removed but still take you out of sprint momentarily. -Heavy load carrying: It is slow and cumbersome by design. Understandable, and fine, until the boss fight. If you were to ask me, throwing the gatestone at the end would not be a pick/throw but rather a QTE/rhythm minigame and you dunk the stone on the gatekeeper. Heavy load is also just annoying during the pressure plate side quest, and I never actually engage with it, just wait for the next horde and let the corespawn be the weights because tossing barrels is horribly unfun.  Anti-Fun Elements: Most of what has been stated in the negatives are what I refer to as anti-fun mechanics. These are design choices that specifically add difficulty to a game that do not add to the overall experience, and instead detract from it or frustrate a player. Nothing feels worse than picking up a gatestone for the third time just to have it knocked back for a fourth time to the boss's burst move. That adds nothing to the game; it is not fun, it is not cool, it is not meaningful. It merely adds a barrier for players which ultimately comes off as un-fun. I am not anti-challenge; I rather love the difficulty of this game. What makes me irate is the lack of agency I as a player have to overcome said challenge. Too many runs have ended with me dropping the mouse in frustration because there was genuinely nothing I could do. Only drawing pea-shooter guns, getting poor perks, and dropped by a swarm I couldn't outrun is not a challenge, its pain. I get there are time when poor RNG will get you, but as it is bad draws are the standard and not the exception, and it is not fun. The game is also lacking considerably in the whimsy that DRG had. I, like many other players, will take goofy and crazy perks that are terrible everyday over minor, boring buffs. Put crazy stuff like eating dirt instead of red sugar to restore health, or deal damage when landing on an enemy. DRG played at its best when using crazy builds against the hordes, and Rogue Core will benefit from many of those same ideas.  Tl;dr: Rogue Core has immense potential but in its current state lacks fun. For every great idea incorporated to the game lies some underlying mechanic that nullifies any fun it could add. Roguelike elements lack the consistency to empower players to overcome the game's challenge, while copious annoyances grate against patience leading to an overall frustrating experience. I know GSG are watching player response intently, and will improve as fitting just like they have time and time again. Although it pains me to my core (heh) I would wait on this one until they work out the rough edges. Until then, its better to save out on the frustration.

I love DRG and I am a Regular Super Helldiver (if you can relate). If they could fix even half of these, i'd happily change my review to a positive.    	Citiques:    1) Difficulty - it is difficult when you start Rogue Core, little selection of how difficult you are allowed to choose, ranges from hard to insane - usually more difficult than a Super Helldive.  I am Glad I have Hundreds of hours into original DRG, otherwise i would have been completely lost on the missions.    2) The Ammo - it is usually scattered in far away places so it makes you question if you should get it while you're there when you don't really need it so it's wasted   or risk going back far to get it later wasting time and under attack when you really need it. Just have it closer to events and elevator or let the Expedite have a dual purpose for   upgrades and reserve ammo to call supply pod where you want it or have the RAPD have some sort of ammo make function to give out conditionally,   like the more you mine, kill, accomplish side objectives or revive the more ammo you can access and perhaps able to ask permission to access from someones elses supply that's carried by RAPD.    3) The timer - It needs to be delayed by at least 10 percent after completing a side objective to give incentive.    4) Weapons with spread damage are underwhelming, except the breach cutter and thunderhead cannon.     5) Shared upgrades - it is more competitive than cooperative. Instead of taking from others in front of them, they could have the RAPD visit whoever's turn it is to get upgrade and take from the pool of upgrades anonymously   or each a different fixed set to choose from, and have a large shield the RAPD puts up for them while the individual chooses so they aren't attacked. I love the upgrades, but it should be free from pressure.    6) Delayed death - Damage inflicted upon a core spawn are delayed by at least .2 seconds, so you can shoot a core spawn as it swings toward you, have it hit you, and then it dies from the original shot.    7) Player selection - would be nice to choose same characters rather than forced to be a different one if someone already has chosen the character you want.    8) No Mission types - halfway through Hoxxes and it's still the same mission just longer, go through 4 or 5 levels to get to the boss to clear The Factory - very repetitive. Still Early Access, hope they get more Mission types.    9) No Beer Buffs - As far as i can tell, there are not beer buffs and you have to workout in weightlifting minigames to earn the right to drink beer, NO!     10) Core spawn outbreak - usually fine on each level - even though there is a lot of them, but an outbreak on the boss level... seems like the boss should be the focus or have a small group near the elevator and with ammo near as you exit elevator,  so as you get closer to boss the anticipation builds with silence. (I think i'm being particular on this one though)       	Likes:    1) The Loadouts are ability focused rather than weapon focused, the weapons are found and upgraded in mission, could use more weapon focused abilities for my Loadouts, but still nice take.    2) Incentive to use your skills initially in battle since they have a cooldown while you fight - free damage or buffs in unique ways.    3) Music is great!    4) Some of it is really colorful and artistic, other times dark and dreary.    5) Nice to see new weapons and support tools    6) Promotions or "Ascensions" are fun to watch.    7) It's a fresh take on DRG, not really a sequal, more like a heavily inspired spin-off. Cosmetics, salute and dialogue are similar.

Definitely needs more work, I don't enjoy this at the moment but hope GSG turns things around, it's only just in early access after all. Though it sounds like lots of what people aren't fond of with how Rogue Core is upon early access launch were pieces of feedback received frequently throughout alphas, so that's concerning.    I gave most of my thoughts already via the in-game prompted surveys, but the big stuff is:    - It feels bad right now to experience what feels like very little run variance, besides what build you make over the course of the run---you do the same basic most rewarding tasks (get bio boosters, get workbenches, mine some expenite, go elevator) and then leave half of the side objectives offered over the course of the run undone, because of trying to beat the timer, or simply the reward isn't worth the risk and you'll win fine without it. I've never had a run where something happened that I distinctly remember different than any other run.    - This game, with the timer and so many other design decisions, feel like it's asserting that this is faster-paced than Deep Rock. So then why do you move basically just as fast as Deep Rock's player control? Why are animations at best only slightly faster, if unchanged? This game feels like it created retroactive complaining about the pacing of Deep Rock missions, despite them going faster the more skilled you became at that game, made especially true if you were on classes with more movement agency like Scout or Driller. But then the actual movement and player control here did not change to reflect a "faster, more frenetic game" enough. It feels like the most unsatisfying way possible to force less passive or slow gameplay, something I wasn't experiencing in DRG to begin with.

I have over 600 hours in DRG. I play Haz5, typically with the more bugs modifier. I was not expecting DRG 2. But I was expecting some of the magic of DRG to come through. Below are my thoughts after 40 hours of gameplay. I have Ascended all classes at least once.    Co-op is broken (Us vs. Each Other vs. Hoxxes)  What made original DRG so legendary was how seamlessly it forced cooperation. Class roles, traversal tools, and map layouts naturally taught players to back each other up. Rogue Core ditches that. Mechanics like weapon selection and the negotiation screen just create artificial friction and put teammates at odds. It doesn't feel like "Us vs. Hoxxes" anymore; it feels like "Us vs. Each Other vs. Hoxxes." You need to build in mechanics that actively reward good behavior, because just winning the run isn't enough to stop the toxicity. The forums are already turning into a "Git Gud" cesspool, and the current design is driving the game straight down that path.    Scrap the forced difficulty progression  Honestly, just remove it. It sucks. You did an amazing job with DRG by making the full game experience available from Haz 1 to Haz 5 without gating anything. Right now, Rogue Core is rushing everyone through the difficulty settings, and players are quickly getting way over their heads. Let people play where they are comfortable.    The Roguelike elements are way too shallow  Flat stat enhancements and bioboosters aren't enough to influence a run. Good roguelites rely on a deep mix of variables—weapon choices, character milestones, and perk boosters. More importantly, the core loop of dying, learning, and gathering rewards to build up your permanent stats needs to feel meaningful. Rogue Core is missing that depth right now.    The weapon power curve feels backward  Too many of the baseline weapons are just boring. Struggling through levels 1 and 2 while waiting for anything to click so you can actually start having fun feels terrible. The game starts out punishingly hard and gets easier as the run goes on, which feels completely backward. It’s artificially difficult early on simply because the starting arsenal sucks.    The Autorun response is a massive red flag  Shutting down the community's request for an autorun toggle with a flat "No" and zero explanation is a terrible look. This is an Early Access game—the entire point is to gather feedback. Ignoring a basic quality-of-life and accessibility feature without explaining the design rationale behind it completely kills player confidence. It makes it incredibly hard to recommend the game to anyone else

The game has flaws and I'd say as of now it's a bit scuffed: ✔️The shooting is good like in DRG and when you got a good build you feel like a pro ✔️The Soundtrack is pretty cool ✔️After putting a decent time into learning the mechanics it can be fun with a good team ✖ The guns are very unbalanced, some are better than the rest and you gotta hope you get to pick them ✖ The whole game seems very "Alien" inspired, it's a lot darker and the enemies also look a lot uglier, no longer Spiderbugs, but grotesque creatures ✖ The sorta lighthearted characters clash a bit with the art direction as mentioned above ✖ Mining and getting minerals is no longer an important thing you need to do besides getting more upgrades  ❌You have to assemble the team at the resource drop-off and everyone will be running around and it can get quite annoying even if you intend to upgrade: A big ongoing firefight might just stop dead in its track for 2 minutes cause people look for upgrades ❌People WILL take what you want and there's nothing you can do about it + You may encounter tryhards and people that don't know what they're doing which is not as forgivable as DRG is + some ppl can be quite nasty and I've seen people get teamkilled for taking stuff someone else wanted ❌The dirtpatches look like buttholes and with enemies that look like walking pps it can be a bit offputting  ❌Every mission takes 40+ minutes  ❌Ammo on the map can be griefed and you may be left without anything and can do nothing about it  ⚠️Early Access bugs like stuff inside walls, missing voicelines etc. are stuff to expect  All in all I'd say: If you like DRG a lot you will certainly get whiplashed by how different this is.  You certainly can no longer bounce around and be drunk like a dwarf like in DRG, because you will be punished by how much teamplay is required here.

[h1] This Ain't Deep Rock Galactic 2 (It's Worse) [/h1]  Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core should have been an additional mode to DRG not a full-fledged game. It needs way more time to cook; maybe in a year or two the game will be good but right now I would avoid buying this game. It’s as if the developers removed the best parts of Deep Rock Galactic, stripped it down to a bare-bones shooter, slapped a timer on the missions, forced it to a be rogue-lite, and had the balls to charge $30 for it. The upgrades are boring and underwhelming, shooting is average, resources are scarce, and the game is just not fun. Even if you had friends to play this game with it wouldn’t makeup for the shortcomings this game has. There are better games in this genre that are more worthy of your time and money, so avoid Rogue Core for now and hope it improves in the future.  [b]6/10[/b]

Just a strictly worse version of the original game for me.  It's too similar - instead of leaning more into combat and giving some fun traversal options so you can jump into action straight away and have fun it just forces a way-too-safe roguelike progression. That just means you start slow to get to the fun part later.. maybe. So I feel like I'm playing the exact same game as DRG but slow and boring. Also it creates some completely forced and needless friction between players in a co-op environment - the game already has incentive to coordinate and work with the team. That means shared upgrades just slow down the game and make it even harder for you to create an interesting build. It has worse vibes (annoying robot voice, weird dwarves etc..) - now that can be a very minor thing in a good game, but it's just more bad vibes from the game...  All in all, maybe it gets somewhat better if I suffer 20+ hours of boredom, but instead I'm gonna refund it and go play scout, which reminds me why I'm doing that and don't have enough faith in the devs. The devs have only 1 class in DRG out of 4 that has good enough tools to deal with the darkness in a game that's all about that. That shows clear divergence from what I see as good game design.

If you have not played any deep rock game yet and are thinking about whether you should, please go play deep rock galactic before playing this game.   The concept is good, but a lot of the game needs to be worked out some more.   Upgrade trees need to be flushed out some more and have some more creativity and versatility. They are not beneficial enough to motivate you to push for them, especially when you have to buy 20 other upgrades you don’t want in order to get something like an 8% ammo boost. Needs to have some more upgrades centered around boosting character abilities.  How you acquire loot mid run, and the frequency you acquire it needs to be reworked some.   The upgrade system needs to be reworked, as of right now you vote on your upgrades often times having to take a trash upgrade in favor of letting your teammate grab another. Maybe have it more like vampire survivors, where you a selection for one person comes up, then it moves to the next, and you have the option to send a selection over to your teammate if there is something that benefits them a lot more.  Character abilities and the way you upgrade them need to be changed some,  The only way to get an upgrade for them is at a workbench, which is already pretty scarce. And then you have to choose a minute upgrade for an ability rather than a weapon or upgrade to your weapon. And choosing one leaves you tough out of luck for the entire floor if your other weapon or ability sucks. There needs to be another option or item on map that specifically focuses on upgrading abilities , and the enhancements you get back at the base should have some options per character that either benefit or rework their ability some.   I understand this is early access, so I am not angry at all the flaws that I see. But that is also why I don’t recommend this game right now. I feel like playing this game in its current state will push people away from the game, especially if you have not played the main deep rock galactic game. My advise is to wait for some more updates and let the devs flush this game out a bit more before trying it out.
