I want to start out saying that I believe I will like this game in the future as GSG has a good history of work and continued support.  That said as of now I can't recommend this game.   The first problem for me is the price tag. $30 for early access to a game that is less complete than other early access games less than half the price just feels bad. The devs tried to justify it recently by saying everything is more expensive and that's the sole reason why. The game currently does not have enough in it or diversity of gameplay to be $30.  The largest problem with the game is it being at odds with itself. This is echoed in lots of reviews. The game wants you to work together as a team, and on higher difficulties requires a team, but forces players to either sit still and work together or attempt to diversify and do the various POIs that can range from wildly simple, decent, to wild tedium that shouldn't be in a game this fast paced. I think the main problem here is the timer and how difficulty ramps. Each level has the same timer and the same amount of events, however the levels themselves get harder regardless of the timer as you progress through a level. As the timer ticks down it triggers two waves at random times throughout the level. This seems to be all the timer does to my knowledge after about 11 hours of playing. This doesn't match with how levels are currently laid out and it doesn't work as being separate from the difficulty. I get that the game requires time management and you won't always get everything on a level every time. I have no problem with that. But currently each level having the same amount of events but slowly ramping enemies that then balloon twice per level just doesn't work. Either POIs need to decrease as you go down (or maybe in addition to appearing less they're more complex and require teamwork) or the difficulty needs to ramp more slowly and have the levels within the levels be more like waves themselves. I don't want to say the timer and difficulty should work more like Risk of Rain but... kind of? RoR overwhelms you with items and POIs, none of which require more than one or a few button presses, because it knows you have to be moving or you will die. Rogue  Core doesn't quite seem to grasp this yet. It still feels too much like DRG to be a different game.  That is my base complaint and I think the most fixable thing that is causing problems. People complain about sharing upgrades and such but I think if access to things was smoothed over the course of a mission and the difficulty ramp was adjusted people would be happier overall.  I feel a little differently about the theming of the game. Like I said above it just feels too similar to DRG. Would the reclaimers even have pickaxes if they're combat dwarves? Spending time mining in this game sort of just feels bad when you know there are other things to be doing. Exponite itself just feels like a weird way to do an upgrade system with the listed issues but also just in terms of gameplay. I think the idea of mining still being necessary for these dwarves might be driving a larger problem with the game. These dwarves are miners quite specifically and clearly. They are literally reclaimers and reclaiming sites for other dwarves to work. Why do I mine? If management really wants us to be efficient here as we are part of the core of their operation and we already have access to better gear in lore, we need to be gathering resources differently.   Got a little off topic there at the end but I think there are odder and larger problems with the game than just the timer, difficulty, and upgrade system. On a certain level I'm not sure the core idea makes complete sense.

This game is not a finished product, there is no depth inside it, and worst of all its a horrible attempt at a rougelike. The  problem isn't with the ammo shortage, the difficulty, or the mission timer, but with completely zero options that change up the gameplay. The level up perks are abysmal, most of them provide very negligable buffs that don't matter throughout the run, workbenches are laughably bad, always only providing increased dmg, ammo capacity or elemental dmg. Perks from hacking pods are selected for the class you are playing, so if you will always get the same boring options with each run, however there is an option to get other character perks for a different character, but that requires engaging with the worst part of the game itself. The absolutely mundane gameplay loop for each run. Each run looks the exact same, you will look around the area for workbenches/hacking pods/events which provides you with the same perks you have seen countless times, as you make your way to the same elevator defense mode, and you have to do this loop for minimum of 4 times. However, devs decided that the "Big 3 problems" the game has going for it, are the lack of ammo, difficulty and time restriction, ignoring the agonizingly boring and mundane gameplay  Do not buy this game in its current state.

In an environment where the friction points are obviously understood, I would call this an unfortunate example of creative vision keeping the material from being what it could be. It's hard to recommend this to people I know that enjoy either roguelites or DRG.    Just to address some of the bigger points:    The timer is... fine. It's annoying at times. It's not fun to have the end of timer wave spawn in the middle of the elevator defense wave. The timer itself does not exist outside of the realm of what to expect in some roguelites. I think it's the only thing keeping ~hour long runs from turning into 1.5-2 hour runs while simultaneously flirting with disincentivizing exploration.    Ammo economy is *okay-ish*, but I do think there need to be stronger vectors to be less reliant on your gun if the things stay the way they are.    Shared upgrades are... a choice. Fostering friction in a co-op experience is a design philosophy that is unlikely to resonate with most. People enjoy making the comparison to RoR in this respect while ignoring just how abundant those same upgrades are by comparison in RoR - or how RoR doesn't ask you to deal with that while also dealing with variable weapons, ammo economy or bio booster perk deck choices. You can start a match of RoR with a friend and know at the start that you will be prioritizing different items.    The power fantasy here is a bit underwhelming. Unless I made every wrong turn imaginable, I should end the run feeling as though what I arrive at would consider what I started as trivial. You do not need to conflate power fantasy with difficulty to arrive here, nor do you need to give the player the ability to clear the screen because they stepped on site. FTL is a perfect example of being incredibly punishing to poor decision making, while still allowing you to become something that would never drop a bead of sweat if the end of run version of your ship encountered the base start of run version of it in combat.    I WANT to like this game. I really want to feel excited to push a new depth - try a new build - level a different character, but it's just not there. Not yet.

I'm having fun and will keep playing for a while, but there's a lot of work to be done before I can recommend Rogue Core.  Rogue Core feels more like a reverse-engineered roguelite game instead of being built on a fresh idea. The result are multiple systems that are at odds with each other. Direct comparisons to DRG are also inevitable because the combat is 1:1 the same, but the enemies are twice as mobile and all of the essential DRG player tools that made combat work have been stripped away to be redistributed as inconsistent, random upgrades. The class abilities just don't cut it (except Slicer).   [h3]My biggest gripe[/h3] For me, the most pressing matter that needs fixing is access to crowd control. In DRG, all classes have crowd-clearing weapons and/or a variety of tools to herd large swarms and open small windows of opportunity for revives and objectives. This is totally lacking in Rogue Core since you start with a ballistic single target gun and grenades that won't last half a swarm. Reviving a teammate often results in a death loop because you can't mag-dump a crowd-clearing weapon or pop a shield, unless you randomly aquired those later when the run is almost over anyways. My point is: There needs to be a consistent way to access at least some form of crowd control at the start of each run. To achieve this, I propose:  - Add a tool workbench to each floor that functions identical to the weapon workbench, except for tools. Upgrade your drills/platform gun/flare gun or craft a new tool to have semi-consistent access to a shield generator, stun boomerangs, sentries, lures (as a tool, not a grenade) and so on. - Add 1 heavy weapon to the initial weapon pool so you can have one guy with slightly more firepower. I don't care if this adds friction because ultimately you'll have a guy being a better killing machine. Maybe also add Drillers Flamethrower, this game needs more non-ballistic weapons.  If the devs can find a way (not necessarily mine) to fix the issue of lacking crowd control, I'll consider changing this review.  [h3]Other small gripes[/h3] - A lot of unavoidable damage, problem compounded by having armor instead of recovering shields. Can't reliably kite because the creepers can just leap at you, or even outrun you in some cases. - Friendly fire is terrible, once again made worse by having armor instead of a regenerating shield. Don't make it a perk again, this shouldn't be a choice in a high-stakes cooperative game. - Mission intro sequence takes too long, maybe the drones should open the gate automatically while you run there. This adds pointless minutes to every run. I was amazed when the Gauntlet dropped me at the weapon crate directly. - Depth 1 and 2 timer could be more lenient for smoother onboarding. There's a lot to take in and you'll have to move on to D3 and 4 for progression anyways, so this should be no issue. - I don't mind shared upgrades, but I wish Ellis would send out little drones that slap an iPad into your face to initiate the negotiation remotely. - All on-kill upgrades being useless against bosses, unless you like the occasional red sugar chunk that will give you back the health you just lost from a sneaky creeper. - No Vampire perk of some sort - Mechanical hissing of Bio Booster station and elevator sounding a lot like Cave Leeches. - A shorter mission type with only 3 floors would be nice to have.  Edit: The music kicks ass, you owe it to the composer to mold this game into something beautiful.

The game pressures you to move fast... then asks everyone to come stand in the circle over and over, slowing things down.  The caves feel very similar. I've played a lot of DRG and I can recognize cave shapes on occasion, but rogue core caves tend to feel similar mission to mission.  The fact there is only one real mission type. Back to back mining missions with events scattered about... then a boss. That's it. Every time.    This game needs more work. More time in the oven. Wait to buy this game.

I say this as someone who has put hundreds of hours into DRG. The core movement and combat mechanics of Deep Rock were never one of its strengths; when moving to a genre that puts movement and combat at the center of the experience, it really exposes how lacking these aspects of the game are. It feels bad that most of the classes don't have any sort of movement ability and you can't reliably know what equipment you'll get. Don't get me started on why they chose to have the inane double press system for abilities rather than just assigning them all to individual keys, which they literally could have done without any additional keybinds. Finally, perhaps the most CORE problem is that most of the upgrades do not feel like they change anything about how you play the game. The way that upgrades are given out, it's almost impossible to assemble a synergistic build that feels like it doesn't anything unique even when teammates are kind enough to let you grab the stuff you want. I think it fails at providing any interesting sense of progression or buildcraft within an individual run, and that is a fatal sin for a roguelike in my book.

It is easy to see people enjoying this game if they enjoyed the core combat of DRG. It is not that I am not having fun, but that fun is coming from the parts from DRG, and mostly being used worse here. I should note I played Rogue Core for about 40 hours in the close alpha and stayed active with discussions, and most of my complaints to this public build are the same issues talked about in the closed alpha.   Rogue Core is fun for it is just more DRG, but the parts that are suppose to make Rogue Core unique, are disappointing. Rogue Core is suppose to be a Roguelit, but fails to make interesting builds and spice up runs enough to keep me coming back.  -The builds: Most of the player upgrades come from a shared pool of Expenite upgrades, collecting Expenite is already a bit dull and repetitive, unlike games such as Risk of Rain 2, where you kill monsters for money to buy chests, Rogue Core has you mining Expenite off the walls and adding that to a Mull, along with side-objectives that are all just reskins from DRG to get more. Once you collect enough all players group up at the drone to share upgrades.  Now, I don't mine the sharing upgrades, and I think that is a key part of this game, but I do mind the upgrades themselves. Because all upgrades are shared amongst all classes, they are left being pretty generic and just boring. So few are going to help you make a "build" and there a few builds to even be had here. Expenite upgrades NEED to do more to make this game interesting to play. Getting a "Legendary" upgrade after like 12 runs and for it to just be "150% more healing from red sugar" really just pissed me off, it is lazy design. Legendary upgrades should be there own, unique upgrades, not just state ups/better versions of more common upgrades. Why can't this "Legendary" upgrade have a small chance to turn killed enemies into red sugar statues or something? Expenite upgrades are boring, too few to select from in the pool, and need more creativity to them.  - The weapons: I like most of the guns, there is talk about ammo issues and the such, which I do think are a problem for some weapons, but the weapon upgrades are also just boring stat-ups. Nothing wrong with these, all Rouguelits have them, but the workbench where you upgrades weapons and abilities need changes. DRG's "Overclock" system needs to be looked at here for weapon upgrades. Something that changes a weapon in a new and fun way, so instead of adding more weapons that start to feel similar, work on a couple Overclocks for weapons that change them. I saw that the grenade launcher had a full-auto mode and that was awesome! That is the type of stuff I want to see on all the weapons that you could choose to upgrade at the workbench or something.   Now the second big issue I have with the game, run predictability.  Now, boring upgrades contribute to predictable runs, which kills replay value, but more than that, the runs are too easy to predict for the following reasons: - Caves are mostly hallways, even at complexity 3. - Swarms come around the same time every run depending of depth level. - To few side-objectives that are new to Rogue Core and not just reskins of DRG. - No "Oh ♥♥♥♥" moments like a mini-boss showing up, or a "Witch" (From Left 4 Dead) style enemy that players could choose to engage for a high reward.  The caves need more work, either make them feel like a mineshaft or go more into more complex caves and increase the timer by a few minutes to compensate.  Swarms need to come from the timer and maybe random events, such as digging up an Core Spawn egg for a powerful upgrade triggers a swarm.  Add more side-objectives, which seems to be coming on the "First-up" board from the Devs.  Add unexpected "Walls" to player power in the run. Mini-bosses showing up to test how the player is doing in the run for example. We see Risk of Rain 2 start spawning bosses that the player can engage with for more upgrades, similar idea could be applied here, something that breaks the monotony of running down hallways and mining Expenite.   I could say more, it is not like I hate the game and will play a little with friends, but this game could have been a lot more, and I expected more from GSG, considering a large portion of this game is just assets from DRG. I hope to see more done with this game, and sooner rather than later as I want to love this game.

there are a unfortunately lot of issues with the game at the moment.  of course upgrades, timers, ammo economy like most reviews say but there is no need to say it another thousandth time.  other issues i do not see discussed often are:    failure in visual and sound design:  most of the enemies have similar/same colors and siluette so they are less easy to distinguish especially during a intense battle. in other games including deep rock enemies are often made to be particularly visually distinct like how they have very different colors or siluettes.  you wouldnt confuse a praetorian for a warden if you look at it from the corner of your vision for half a second but you have to look closely at core spawn because they are not as visually distinct.  the game also seemingly lacks distinct impactful audio queues.  in deep rock if a enemy like praetorian or scorpion spawns you have a loud and distinct audio queue that warns you. almost every enemy has this.  in rouge core it feels like enemies dont really make distinct noises, if you notice any noise at all its just a soup of indistinct noise.    bad voices/voice acting:  mission control was replaced by omega which is a robot/ai that talks in a very annoying, slow and glitchy way. omegas voice lines feel like if some out of touch corperate designs a ai assistant that they want to be fun and sarcastic and they failed completely at their job making it just cringe and annoying.  as for the dwarves, the male dwarfs retained the same voice actor which is fine. the female dwarfs voice acting feels very bad however. it sounds like a regular woman overly shouting and not like a female dwarf should sound in my opinion. the female voices in my opinion are quite bad and very annoying to listen to.    lack of mission rewards:  there is no more money or minerals in the game. you actually get no rewards for completing missions at all. the only rewards you get at all is reputation currency you get from challanges/dailies. missions do not feel rewarding.  the system with having to excersise for beer is also no fun, you cant even quickly drink a few beers to pass out before you leave anymore. its so in your way that it massively dis incentivises you from having fun in the space station. it devolves to not wasting time on the space station and either do mission or leave immediately.    bad enemy design:  several enemies suffer from bad design. the artifact dude runs away incredibly fast and spawns a bunch of corespawn as well as having no obvious vulnerable phase. this makes it a huge ammo and time sink that is basically never worth it.  the fish boss for whatever reason has a un telegraphed unavoidable huge instant kill attack when you deplete its shield.  the bosses overall just have way too much health and relatively little interesting mechanics compared to the variety of bosses in deep rock like the 3 dreadnaughts, caretaker as well as several encounters like korlock and nemesis.    inconsistent experience:  for example one of the biggest confusions i have is that  expenite upgrades, weapon crates, grenades and tools all requite all dwarfs to be there and then pick together in a popup menu that stops time.  bio boosters you get to pick alone and you click on floating icons in the world.  toolboxes you get to pick alone in a popup menu.  why is there such a distinction made? it creates strange fragmentation for seemingly no good reason. either have everyone pick every upgrade together or none of them, either have every upgrade have a popup menu or none of them. but why the strange mix.    incentives:  conflicting incentives arent nessesarily a bad thing, you have to balance what you do. but too much is going to make the game not fun.  the game is full of counterproductive incentives and conflicting incentives. for example you want to explore the cave and do all the objectives, but the timer sits in your neck and you have to be fast.  you want to fight enemies but fighting enemies is a waste of time and ammo, so you get incentivised to rather run away and avoid fighting whenever possible.  you want to collect expenite but upgrades barely do anything interesting so its a waste of time.  also everyone has something they want to do at the moment and you have to frequently try to get everyone to drop what they are doing to come together to get upgrades. you can mitigate that somewhat by saving up multiple upgrades for some time but that doesnt work for weapon boxes for example.    things lack clear identity:  in deep rock most things like weapons and enemies have a clear identity and purpose. for example the engineers shotgun is great at self defence and killing individual enemies that are close, the grenade launcher is great at killing many small enemies from afar.  the praetorian exists so that you have to have something against spongy big targets and move around more. get out of its spit and shoot its butt. if you take only aoe weapons you will have a hard time against this enemy. the enemy has a clear purpose and was designed around that purpose.  acid spitters require you to pay attention to not only whats right in front of you and also keep moving. you will have to pick weapons that can also deal with enemies on a distance.  i feel like enemies and weapons in rouge core are less designed like:  we have some gameplay element that we need to fill in lets design something that fits that.  and more like:  we have this interesting idea for a weapon/enemy lets just put it in the game and see what happens.  not even thinking about the overall structure of the game.    there are many more issues like for example:  lack of mission variety  lack of interesting weapons: due to the lack of distinct upgrades/builds to choose if you have a weapon it will always be the same. to make the matter worse almost every weapon is basically some minor twist on a generic machine gun.  meta progression is unnessesarily grindy and not fun.  why can i not move my weapons around in my weapon slots?  it would be great if i could try out a weapon and if i dont like it get my old one back.    actually just put all the upgrades into a in between level rest area like in roboquest and have the timer be stopped in this area. problem solved for the most part.    there are even more issues that i did not list here.    of course not all of these issues individually are game breakers but they compound together to make the game not very fun.

I love both DRG and first-person rogue likes, so i thought this was gonna be a slam dunk for me, but it wasn't. It particularly fails on the rogue like front. Some key changes i'd like to see:  1. Remove shared upgrades  It's one of those gameplay mechanics that sounds intriguing on paper, but gets old real quick. Waiting for everybody to choose simply takes too long. It's also extremely limiting to build variety.  2. A shop after every depth Instead of huddling together at the minecart, after every depth the squad gets to meet a trader, sent by DRG, in a shop room. Together in person you decide on the upgrades and the shop refreshes for how many Expenite upgrades you've collected. This fixes the abrupt pacing of throwing everyone into a 2d upgrade screen in an otherwise fast-paced action game.  3. Actually interesting, impactful uprades The bland stat uprades are just not cutting it. It's not that there aren't any synergistic upgrades, but even the few that exist are not impactful enough to be intriguing. I get that devs don't want semi-broken snowball runs, but multiplicative synergy is exactly what makes rogue-like upgrades interesting. I've never cought myself wonder what any particular build would do; it's just stack the best stat upgrades and hope for the best. A perfect counter example on how to do it right would be Roboquest.  It's not all dark and gloomy, the game still has its undeniable DRG charm going for it. To say it like Senator Palpatine "We will watch your career with great interest".

Edit: Theyve added autosprint. Glad pressure from players worked. Glad Mike and Mikel arent leaflovers. Im still not enjoying it that much but it looks like the game is heading in the right way, considering theyve implement pretty good qol stuff that was often requested.  ---  Im struggling to enjoy the game right now and I know Im not alone. Other reviews go into more detail about most of the issues Im also facing but I wanted to highlight a specific moment from the recent May 26th Dev Stream (Rogue'n'Core, baby!).  At 22:45 a message about adding an 'Always Sprint' option was read. The developer responded "Dude I fully agree. Ive had that conversation many times and I have no trouble saying that it boils down to Mike and Mikel dont want it." (might have butchered the names)  It seems most of the friction in this game comes from a few individuals imposing their preferred playstyle on everyone else. No one is asking to remove the current mechanics. They just want the option to toggle them. Adding this wouldnt change the games core style for those who prefer the default but it would solve a major hurdle for others.  Its disappointing that such a simple addition is being blocked because reasons. While a mod might eventually fix this relying on the community to patch basic qol features is a symptom of a bigger issue (theres already a mod on nexus but its buggy af). I do hope to come back to this review and change it to a positive one once major issues are fixed and hopefuly they stop resisting player feedback and choice.

Not yet, but it will get there eventually, DRG was kinda rough in early access too. Here's a few things I dislike as someone who put thousands of hours into DRG.  [h3]The Bar[/h3]  You need to do minigames in order to get beer from the bar, as a result I don't even bother touching the bar, unlike DRG where I was there after every single mission.  [quote=Note for devs]Please remove the minigame requirements.[/quote]  [h3]Locked Classes[/h3]  Classes are locked, if someone has a class you like you can't pick it, in DRG this used to be optional but here in Rogue Core it is forced. This design decision also makes playing with friends harder, in public lobbies you can work around it by just not joining that lobby or hosting your own as the class you want, with friends? You're stuck.  [quote=Note for devs]Please, just like DRG give us the option to disable this lock. In my lobbies I liked to let people play what they liked, I can't do that here.[/quote]  [h3]Shared Mid-Mission Upgrades[/h3]  The random upgrades you get mid mission are unfortunately shared, you each take turns picking an upgrade. I thought this was a co-op game? Apparently not, you're competing with your own teammates too. The upgrades themselves are pretty generic, lazy stat boosts, nothing exciting. You also can't really customise it in a way that you want, don't like X stat and want to swap it out with something better? Too bad.  [quote=Note for devs]We should get our own individual upgrades to choose from, we shouldn't have to compete at all. The upgrades themselves need to be more exciting too.[/quote]  [h3]Dwarf Designs[/h3]  The new dwarfs are pretty generic, I don't think they're very memorable. How did we go from the incredible designs from DRG to this? Just how?  [quote=Note for devs]The designs in DRG were simpler, and they each stood out and no matter the skin you still knew which dwarf was which instantly, in Rogue Core I can't tell who is who and the guy that looks kinda like a Driller... is not a Driller?[/quote]  [h3]Generic Classes[/h3]  Classes are pretty generic, in DRG they felt distinct with all their equipment, here though they decided to take away said equipment, throw it in an RNG pool and instead give us generic abilities. First thing I did was look for a class with a sentry... they removed the sentry, no class has a sentry.  [quote=Note for devs]Not sure what to say here, really I'm just lost, I thought I was buying a Deep Rock Galactic game, this ain't it.[/quote]  [h3]Female Dwarfs[/h3]  They added female dwarfs which is cool, no problem with that, what I do have a problem with is being unable to switch between male and female, if you're going to add both male and female please let us choose instead of forcing it.  [quote=Note for devs]Self explanatory really...[/quote]  [h3]Mission Timer[/h3]  There is a mission timer, if you take to long the game basically says screw you time to lose, its like a worse version of Extraction missions from DRG, yep they decided to make the whole game basically that one mission from DRG. I'm not a fan of this approach to difficulty.  [quote=Note for devs]Remove the timer and readjust your difficulty, you did this in DRG you realise this right? Extraction missions worked because IT WAS ONE MISSION! It wasn't the entire game. And for other missions difficulty was added in a variety of ways, not through a mission timer. This needs to go.[/quote]

This game is not balanced for anything other than a 4 person team of friends. The difficulty goes through the roof with fewer players/randos.  As is common for games with the "pick a random upgrade" type mechanics, it's way too reliant on RNG. More than half of your runs will basically need to be thrown due to terrible upgrades or poor placement of points of interest in a map.  It also doesn't help that mechanics like stunning/shielding/pushing back enemies don't seem to work consistently. Been killed through deployable shields or still attacked in melee by enemies despite having the guardian's repulsion field up plenty of times. Would be nice too if the enemies didn't beat on the elevator from inside of it or far up on the ceiling.  If you thought glyphids were annoying sometimes with their pathing/clipping through terrain, just wait until you have these core spawn starting attacks while around corners or leaping up to you from 30 meters away and just popping into your face for half your health/armor in a single swipe.  The core loop/mechanics are alright, but it really needs a good pass at giving players a little more control of their runs and fine-tuning some of the upgrades/level generation that make for a poor experience.

This game is in a rough place. The player base is expecting fun and whimsy like regular DRG, but is instead receiving punishing and forced gameplay instead.    ◘ The ammo is seriously scarce, it's often what ultimately decides if you get to finish the mission or not.  ◘ Bosses are straight forward massive HP bullet-sponges with gameplay of "Do enough damage, drop something into the hole, repeat 3 more times."  ◘ The weapons themselves are mostly balanced, although ammo is purposefully slim on some of them, making them weak picks unless you've built yourself a specific way.  ◘ Speaking of building yourself, you'll find yourself taking ammo traits primarily just to make do.  ◘ Classes have two balance categories: Strong (Slicer) and weak (everything else)  ◘ Surprising amounts of downtime - between the start up at each mission, waiting to grab your weapons, grenades, to requiring all players to click "ready" before attempting to load each mission, standing in upgrades waiting for all players (you can't upgrade unless all of you are in a 5m radius).  ◘ The problem with the downtime? There's a timer, and that bad boy is ticking non-stop. It's quick and forces you to miss content in order to not die.  ◘ Events are fun but are incredibly dangerous for an otherwise unimposing enemy. Seriously jacks up the pacing as you can go from exploration to fight for you life at a moments notice. Anyone can start an event, even if the rest of the party isn't ready. Reminder that basic upgrades require the entire party, no exception.    I like the spirit of the game, but it feels forcibly difficult instead of interestingly so. The rig is so full of life, options, mini-games, and sights. It really seems the devs wanted you to be immersed and having fun, that is until you launch the mission. If you're interested, I'd recommend waiting until it's on a good sale or until they patch some of gameplay traps out of it.

I guess I'm giving this a negative review because it's too far from Deep Rock Galactic. I know it may be unfair to do so, but making a sequel so far off from one of my favorite games of all time just seems wrong. I know it isn't DRG 2, but here are a few things that I'd like to see changed:  -Each class should have at least a base primary, secondary, and utility that can be viable with upgrades. The player can freely swap them during a mission, but at least give us some consistency run to run. -Players should not be pitted against each other with upgrades during a run. Each player should have their own choices to choose from. -Bullet sponge bosses are bad design. Make them more interactive with better gameplay being rewarded instead of slapping on % HP for difficulty.  Knowing the team behind Deep Rock Galactic, I know this game will become another one of the greats, but at this time, I cannot recommend it.

I really want to like this game but I can't in good conscious pretend to recommend it to anyone (unless you are a masochist, in which case you might be very happy.) It is punishingly difficult but the real issue is that we are too time and ammo constrained to do any of the side bits that actually give some enjoyable variety to this game.      Most mobs are bullet sponges. You only have nerf guns. On very rare occasions you will get a loadout you are happy to run. Grenades are a trap and will kill you as often as you manage to kill something you are actually interested in killing with them. Upgrade chests get pushed way down out of the way corridors, and if you are dumb enough to chase after them, you have a 50/50 chance of making it back to the elevator with enough ammunition/time to actually summon the exit before those ridiculous worms show up.  There will be next to no red sugar at the boss arena, perhaps a couple crystals if you are lucky. No health in resupplies. Armor does not regen, you have to get it from resupplies or armor crates. I'm not sure it's doing anything anyway. There is an astronomically expensive upgrade system with perks that may or may not actually be doing anything.     Even your home ship is going to be hyper obnoxious. There is a roomba which putters around looking for people to bump into. If you dare look away from your screen for 20 seconds, your AI overseer will incessantly - INCESSANTLY - piss and moan at you then entire time you have had the temerity to be taking your attention off of the screen. I cannot properly express how annoying - and f'ing pointless - this is.     I think there's a good game in here, but if so, it is buried under many hours of misery. I am still trying to make up my mind whether this game is for the hard headed or only for masochists. I'm currently leaning towards the latter.  For reference, I've got about 40 hours in, have been playing (mostly) with a few friends who have played DRG for quite awhile. We've managed to defeat a boss 3 times (one run of which we all went down immediately  after the boss died and stone deposited so we didn't get credit for clearing the facility...).

The weapons are boring, most of which are weapons I've already used for hundreds and hundreds of hours but without all the things that make those weapons fun (overclocks) and you just use the same ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ white weapon u get at the start of the run for the entirety of it unless you take a green weapon that is just as boring. All the upgrades are boring as hell too but that's already been talked to death.  A huge part of DRG's combat and exploration was terrain manipulation. In Rogue Core, that is completely gutted. The environment is totally flat as if Hoxxes' tectonic shifts were sentient and said "I need to make sure my caverns can accomodate a bunch of Dwarves that want to mine in me." Things like repellant additive do not exist as far as I can see, making shelters with drills is useless as theres barely any impactful CC in this game at all (in a horde shooter, ffs), and platform bridges to avoid/funnel enemies are largely useless for the same reason, so the combat has much less strategic depth and skill expression with traversal tools.  The best part of DRG was terrain manipulation for traversal and combat which created a naturally emergent need for cooperation. All this game has to offer is parallel play with forced cooperation in the form of negotiations. The only time I'm reminded I'm playing with others is when I'm waiting for them.  The metaprogression exists only for the sake of it. Funner ("funner"), more involved experiences being gatekept behind meaningless stat upgrades that you're meant to grind for is nothing but padding out gametime in the laziest way possible. This is a one bite review. I love DRG and own almost everything they had to offer on steam including survivor on my phone. I love roguelike.  I have played way too many hours of both, but I was excited to check out this new twist. I passed around the $50 gift cards and brought my 2 regular gaming buddies on a drop.  Short version is, at this early point in my experience, it needs some work to recommend.  I will admit that I'm very low on competency at this point. I love the franchise and it has potential, but I don't feel it competes well with either DRG or DRG: Survivor yet. I will update this when I can get my gang to give it some more time. I expect we will see refinement based on previous games.  Things I liked: It feels a lot like DRG. Maybe too much like DRG. If you enjoyed mining and killing bugs, you will probably like this. Upgrades are fun. Fun co op play.  Things I didn't like: Gameplay feels more rushed than DRG did. Not sure if the tutorial really prepared me for the first drop. The upgrade choosing popups are jarring halts to the gameplay. The upgrades are shared, so you have to choose who gets what. I would like to see rerolls after each choice, maybe with an option to "like" one if you want it to stay on the list They are chosen serially in (maybe?) random order, under pressure from a timer, and everyone is waiting until completion. You are either rushed or waiting. Not very enjoyable You still need to manually aim and fire everything I saw so far. You still need to worry about ammo. The armor wear mechanism exists for a reason I cannot fathom. Another consumable to manage.  It feels like they took a successful game, DRG, and tried to check off a few rogue boxes. I wasn't immediately in love with it, but I do look forward to diving deeper and unlocking the things which are denied to me on the first run. I expect things will improve rapidly but it feels a bit rushed to early access.

I've honestly given this game a fair chance, and right now it's genuinely getting unbearable to play. It feels completely cobbled together in a way that's starting to get on my nerves with a lot of obviously bad design decisions that apparently have been the core of feedback for a year now (with closed alpha). While I'm waiting to see what the devs have planned to address it, what you get for buying now is:  - A horribly constraining timer system that basically forces you to rush through every stage if you don't want to die from timeout infinite horde + instakill tentacles and also guarantees a wipe with clueless randoms that try to full complete the map and take their time searching everything - Disappointing upgrades. Some common upgrades are straight up significantly better than rare upgrades because they're actually useful on all builds (I'd rather have +35 % ammo in mag than a % boost on energy damage I don't use) and overall just forces you to tough it out with a disjointed and completely RNG kit - Incredibly stingy weapon and perk progression which caps your enjoyment of the game, especially if the RNG screws you and gives you a hilariously bad starting weapon (3 pistol 2 shotgun starting crates anyone ?) or keeps giving you terrible options (+50 % armor break when you can't break enemy armor off, yay...). Not helped by the fact it's all split up (workbench only gives you EITHER an ability upgrade, a weapon upgrade OR a new weapon, and you only get 4-6 in a match! And no, the upgrades are not THAT impressive) - Some of the most anti-social "co-op" features I've ever seen. Negotiations are first come first serve affairs with randomized order, meaning a weapon or perk that'd finally rescue your build can get stolen by a random that doesn't care and just wants an extra rare goodie. Upgrading requires everyone to go back to a tiny 3 m wide circle when most teams split up. Events and elevators can be started by one person and start a massive, deadly horde. - The enemies are genuinely anti-fun in the worst way possible. The basic grunt leaps around like crazy, making them miserable to shoot at. The elite grunts can absorb an absurd amount of damage or even shield their weakspot. The main ranged enemies are either full of health and move ultra fast, or spam you down with crazy accuracy and speed. Ironically the big armored boss energy is by far the least threatening since he's slow and has a very telegraphed attack. Combined with the tight ammo pool, the full attrition-based gameplay and the drip-feed of upgrades, this is far more frustrating than DRG. - The balance is genuinely ridiculous right now. Some of the mid-stage downsides like double enemy HP or double enemy damage are basically begging to wipe your team, compared to making a single enemy a bit faster, adding more leeches (some will spawn even if you don't pick it) or slightly tweaking enemy spawn percentages. Same with classes, Retcon's passive is that she gets to self revive once a mission if everyone else goes down first... which only matters if you've already lost, great, plus a mid active ability that basically allows you to play for 10 seconds and then "revert" unless you go down, compared to Spotter's passive that gives you a free permanent radar plus a nearly essential auto-crit dart that nukes all enemies you shoot at. - The difficulty progression is like a cliffside. D1 is basically brain turned off and forcing you to intentionally run out the (elongated timer) to wipe even on solo, D2 is a bit spicier but doable, D3 is a slap in the face and often requires locking in to beat, and D4 will genuinely just crush your skull in if you misplay even slightly. - The currency progression is genuinely insulting, especially merits. You earn a lot quickly, giving you a false sense of security in splashing in all directions, and then the drought comes as you need it the most to prepare for D3/D4 difficulty. No merits for just playing a mission, really ? Why ? It just encourages people to play endless D1s to grind out the challenges.  All and all this game feels rushed, which is genuinely shocking given how long it's been baking in the oven. I seriously hope the dev team pushed this into early access to turn around major monthly changes, otherwise this game is dead on arrival with the genuinely sorry state it's being presented to the public in.

I am frustrated with RogueCore right now because I really want to like it - no, love it!  While things like ammo and upgrades have already been named countless times and the devs mentioning that they will work on it (which is great!), I still want to talk about a few issues I've seen less talked about so far.  I feel like the game lacks visual clarity. In a lot of ways. The way the upgrades look make them not easily distinguishable and I essentially have to read any upgrade multiple times to remember what they do. Especially compared to the original DRG, the dwarves no longer each have their own, very distinctive silhouette and colour scheme. Well, the Falconeer and the Guardian are very distinct, I think the other 3 visually are sort of similar. While in the original DRG all 4 classes were immediately recognizable from silhouette alone. Finally the enemies also sort of start blending into quite the mess. I have a really difficult time telling apart what kind of enemies are attacking me at times, which again in the original DRG  was never an issue since every bug's silhouette, colours and sizes were so different from each other.  Also one of the bosses seemed to have an unchoreographed attack that just pulls in any player near and basically 100 to 0s them. Idk if that is skill issue on my end, but it definetly felt unfair.  Lastly, and I know this is more like personal preference since that is just the vibe/theme this game goes for. But man, I miss Mission Control, the goofy humor, the game not taking itself too serious. Omega is just mean and not really fun to listen to or even tease (besides I find his voice with the glicthing quite irritating during gameplay). Also, I just want to celebrate a good run with a beer afterwards, not have to burn enough calories for like 3 minutes first. Missions should just give a whole bunch of calories and it would be fine to be honest.  The game has so many good additions to it. I love the gameplay loop, I love the class abilities. The builds can be super fun. And heck yeah for female dwarf representation! (Although I do not get why she constantly screams)   TLDR: I want the game to be amazing and rn it's okay, kind of inferior to the original. I wouldn't recommend it right now, but hopefully soon (it's in  early avcess after all) I can without a shadow of a doubt!  Rock and Stone everyone!

Cannot recommend.  It's Deep Rock, and that sells.   It's a sub-par exponential power creep game in that subgenre of games with "rogue-like elements."  Short Term: Extremely rudimentary, poor gameplay and mechanic decisions. Some could be tweaked. Long Term: Core mechanics and vision for the game is unlikely to change.   _________________________________________ From the FAQ: _________________________________________  ***How is DRG: Rogue Core different from Deep Rock Galactic?*** "you start each mission with only the most basic equipment, and you collect random upgrades, gear and abilities as you go. This makes for lots of variety in your build, and the potential for extremely powerful upgrades and synergies."  This is false.  Far less variety, yes potential for extremely powerful stats.  -- 1. You get your gear at the start. The gun you're left with is likely what you'll be using.  -- 2.  Bonuses apply to the player, not their equipment. There is no customization of primary /secondary weapons to be synergistic or complimentary in a loadout.   -- 3.  The weapons themselves do not have the variety, overclocks, etc they have in DRG. Again, you receive player buffs, not weapon modifications.  -- 4. The buffs themselves are bland, inapplicable, or a simple power boost. The only potential is to snowball in power, similar to games like Risk of Rain 2, though with uninteresting options.  GSG developed perhaps the greatest weapon customization and load out design in gaming history, only to scrap it for a trend of arbitrary buffs and power scaling. _________________________________________  ***It's faster-paced.*** "You’re under more pressure, and time is a resource you need to manage. Each level is on a mission timer, and you may not be able to complete every single side objective before your time runs out and you reach critical threat level."  The gameplay is not inherently faster than DRG.   The rounds are shorter linear levels in which you follow a cable to an elevator, on a timer. You speed run.  This is to keep the several segments in a short play session. _________________________________________ ***It's harder and more intense.***  "The baseline difficulty is more challenging. We’d say the game probably starts close to the 'Hazard 3' experience from Deep Rock Galactic, and it scales up from there."  It's more difficult because: 1. You don't have a complete loadout. Often stuck with a weapon you wouldn't choose yourself. 2. It can be completely trivial if you get decent buffs. _________________________________________  ***There's more focus on combat.*** "In Rogue Core, the cave generation is more linear. Where DRG had more focus on traversal and terrain modification, Rogue Core introduces fighting in and around industrial structures in the caves."  DRG has the same combat mechanics.   Rogue Core is linear and lacks those other elements. It only has DRG's standard combat, though limited. Rogue Core has one weapon you're screen clicking with, with more or less buffs.  DRG's combat is much more engaging and dynamic with full diverse and synergistic loadouts.  _________________________________________   ***Is this game a Roguelike or Roguelite?***  It's nothing like the game Rogue (1980), in any way.  Rogue is a dungeon crawler. DRG is much closer to Rogue.  The game has exponential power creep of other indie games marketing "Rogue-Like" elements. This never existed in Rogue.

Deep Rock Galactic: Identity Crisis  This has nowhere near the polish that Deep Rock Galactic had when it arrived in Early Access. Which is shocking to me.  It looks and plays like a student project that's re-using assets from a popular and beloved title. A student project that needs to go back to the drawing board. Somebody mentioned that this plays like a mod. It does. A mod with a steep price tag.  The core issues? The core issues were brought up during their very selective closed beta tests. Even those selected people complained about the very same things yet this developer still chose to ignore their feedback.  That unskippable developer introduction video was jarring and eye opening too. I had no idea how stubborn and out of touch this developer actually is. Who are you making this game for? Yourselves? Your shareholders?!  And this coming from someone who supported this developer by purchasing every DLC they put out for DRG over the years because i truly appreciate how they handled that game. From the content releases to stuff like being able to select and unlock Season passes at your own pace at no cost either. I mean. The state this industry is in? It's unique and noteworthy when a developer doesn't ♥♥♥♥ over and exploit their fanbase.   That's why i purchased this. Not because i was expecting Deep Rock Galactic 2.  But because i was expecting a polished Roguelite title with a solid foundation.  Instead i got a speed runners wet dream come true with very lackluster Roguelite aspects and very questionable design choices in general.  That timer? Ok. You want me to rush? Sure. I can rush. But no! I have to get side tracked to mine ♥♥♥♥ and do repetitive tasks just to unlock "Now gun do more damage!" or "You now have more armor!" Oh! Yipee! How exciting! You barely notice the extra damage output and the endless swarms of leaping and no clipping enemies will just melt through that "extra armor".  There's no respite in game but it wants you to hold still while you're interacting with the new, flying mule and selecting your perk/upgrade. Meanwhile the endless swarms of enemies are reducing your health down to almost nothing.    Those things just clash against one another. Those are not only oversights. That's concerning to me and where this game is headed.  They replaced the shield system for an armor system for some reason. Even playing Guardian. You can only partially restore it with him. An armor system that seems very inconsequential.  Then you have that really dumb, toxic way the game deploy perks/upgrades in co-op. Which is why i stuck to solo.  Also. Why do i need use my mouse AND keyboard just activate one of three class abilities? It should be one key for one ability! I know why. Console controllers. Newflash: I am not on a console! I am using a PC!   Power fantasy? In this game? Forget about it!   For example, i am currently playing Crab Champions too. One run, i unlocked a perk that deployed a temporary Turret whenever i used my grenade. This game has nothing like that. Nothing actually game play changing and impactful.  It's just a boring, uninspired numbers game when it comes to that.  The overall solo experience is miserable. You're constantly barraged by enemies. Enemies that will clip through the environment. You'd think switching to UE5 would have fixed some of DRG's issues. Nope. Just more power draw.  Cooper? The Bosco replacement? He may as well be a bird that just pecks at the enemy. Again. So inconsequential.  Did you not play test any of this stuff?  Ammo economy? There's a reason why most if not all of the recent Roguelite shooters feature unlimited ammo.  From Roboquest to Deadzone: Rogue. With this amount of enemies? Ammo economy should not be a thing! Yes. You still have to reload and recharge weapons but ammo is not a concern. There's only two resupplies on those boxes too. They want players to be fighting each other over everything in the co-op. From the upgrades downright to the already scarce ammo. At least let us call in ammo like in DRG instead of having us battle your randomly generated level system just to reach it.  "Well this is not DRG!"  I agree. Not even close.  This? In its current condition? It's just pure, utter frustration to me.   P.S.  Please do something about the GPU power draw! Even with resolution scaling enabled. Your game is drawing way too much power. Especially when accessing all of the games systems (Customization, Enhancements etc.) You at least should be capping the FPS to 60 when accessing those systems if you can't fix it otherwise. This seems to be a common issue for UE5. The gift that keeps on giving!

As a huge fan of both Deep Rock Galactic and Risk of Rain 2, I’m really struggling to enjoy the timer in Rogue Core.  In Risk of Rain 2, while time increases the difficulty, you still have the agency to thoroughly explore and clear out a stage without facing an artificial, punishing cutoff. In Rogue Core, however, the strict timer feels incredibly restrictive, especially when combined with cookie-cutter level designs. This rush-heavy loop actively undermines a big aspect that made the original DRG so special in the first place: the satisfaction of organic, thorough exploration.  Instead of blending the strengths of its inspirations, Rogue Core ends up with a frustrating middle ground. It fails to match the strategic risk-reward of Risk of Rain 2, where staying longer is a calculated difficulty increase rather than a hard cutoff, while completely stripping away the classic DRG satisfaction of clearing a cave of all its threats and resources at your own pace.  I understand that Rogue Core is meant to be its own distinct experience rather than just more of the same, but I'm really not a fan of how this was executed.

Unfortunately this is currently neither a great DRG game or roguelite. Upgrades are boring and don't really change how you play throughout a run, or from one run to another. Then everyone has to select a modifier. I was never much of a fan of the Deep Dives from DRG but this is a Deep Dive every mission. It's exhausting, this is DRG but with too many extra steps.  Extraction was always one of the best and coolest things in DRG and this game doesn't hit the same.

If I could have a "mixed" review rather than a straight up positive or negative, I would.  The game is good in places and gets lots of things right. This is a very good start to a rogue-like experience, but falls flat on its face more times than I can count.   ALOT has already been said about the timer and other frustrations such as the armour system. Which I can only agree with. Being forced to find armour as a resource rather than the original shield premise that the original game had is a straight downgrade in my opinion. But many of the perk systems are tied to armour, so this system probably won't change and running around for ammo, as well as armour, becomes a massive chore.   The timer is meh, I know other rogue-likes implement similar time-based systems to prevent you from become extremely powerful etc, such as a "day" system, but too many elements are just out of your control to justify it's existence. The game is preventing you from hitting key upgrades that you really do need because the difficultly spike compared to base DRG is massive. You will know how a run is going within about 2-3 missions because you've either received, or not received the upgrades you desperately need to make your build.   Which ties me into the next topic.  Upgrades - Most of upgrades are straight-up U S E L E S S. I can't stress enough how annoying a pointless upgrade is when you have invested precious time and effort into a long objective. Sometimes you will receive JUST pointless melee upgrades and you will be forced to choose the default health option. That doesn't help anyone on your team and definitely won't help you when you're getting swamped by a horde.   Speaking of team, I don't know who decided to SHARE the upgrades, but this is quite possibly the dumbest decision made for this game. I can't even begin to imagine how annoying it would be in public lobbies, just for some idiot to pick a purple rarity upgrade when it clearly doesn't suit their weapon or class, or, just pick it accidentally out of ignorance. This either needs removing, or revamping entirely. The only place this can and will work is with 4 friends using communication, not random lobbies, I have no idea what the devs were thinking when they thought of this option, but ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ exist and I can imagine a world where they will not be team players and just pick what is best for them. Very stupid design.   Furthermore, the upgrade system, which is very key to all rogue-likes, requires you to stand near Ellis and pause the game, sometimes leading to a "tug of war" and people not picking upgrades and commanding Ellis to move (Who actually thought this was good?)   In addition, the game is entirely paused during this segment when selecting upgrades, leaving you to sometimes wait (and physically age) for some random player to choose what they want. It messes with the entire flow of gameplay and turns it into a crawl, feeling like I am locked in purgatory every time a upgrade appears.   Characters/Classes - Most of these are really solid. I love the new classes and their systems and they are all pleasing to play, Omega can get quite annoying to listen to sometimes though, which is strange, considering that Mission Control from DRG was totally fine and I could listen to his rambles all day.   The weapons and ammo seems to be a big issue amongst the community and I don't blame them. The worst thing that can happen to you is ending up picking from a pool of terrible weapons that don't suit you at all and this happens, a lot. I don't understand why you can't CHOOSE your starting weapon at a lower level, like most rogue-likes and take it through the level with you and maybe have a choice to change it down the line.  These are just some gripes. I think the game will be great with time, it just needs to bake a little longer. I have made comparisons to DRG and I understand it is trying to be its own Rogue-Like experience.  I've also read many reviews about GSG's alpha testing phase and the feedback given not being implemented into the game. If this is true, they really need to regroup and start listening to their players.

DRG Rogue Core got an "okay" gameplay loop for an early access, but still fails at some things:  [b]Post-edit:[/b] Added friendly fire and ammo economy and short review for classes.  [h2]Shared upgrades pool[/h2] [b]1)[/b] First thing first, the main idea of "shared upgrades pool", this thing open a HUGE can of worms if you are a solo player. I understand the concept of this decision by the devs, however, since the game is pretty challenging, this is not applicable in this case. People will selfishly chase meta, best upgrades, etc in order just to win the game. With this concept you are basically designing this for 4-people PREMADE teams, which is dead-on-arrival concept for this kind of game IMO. (GTFO never got high player numbers, despite being a great game)  Another thing is that a lot of upgrades are utterly useless or an extremely low statstick value that have zero impact and gameplay and combat perfomance. If you are picking 3/4 in line, then it's ggwp. Another thing that in order to trigger this "event", everyone need to gather together each time, which is just annoying at this point (if you play with random people aka PUGs).  [h2]Meta-progression[/h2] [b]2) Meta-progression[/b] isn't bad, but it consists entirely of statsticks values and there's ZERO mechanical or gameplay changes to make things more interesting.   [h2]Weapon balance[/h2] [b]3) Weapon balance[/b] is pretty bad, for two reasons. First, there's some weapons that are bad no matter what upgrades and perk you use. Second, there's some weapons that are bad, but can work really well with certain upgrades, but there's a HIGH CHANCE you aren't getting the perks or Workshop upgrade you need. This kind of casino can be your downfall in D3+  [h2]Enemy scaling[/h2] [b]4) Enemy scaling[/b]. It seems this game only has two types of scaling, either solo or 4-people. Duo is the worst kind of experience you can get. For example, i can leisury enjoy my casual D3 SOLO run, while inviting my friend and helping him with some D2 missions turns the game into hell on earth (it's not comparable to a solo D3 at all). Basically, either play solo or gather a full squad.  [h2]Timer[/h2] [b]5) Timer[/b], actually, isn't the biggest problem, because problems with timer come from stupid things like "CAN WE GET TOGETHER FOR 15 TIME TO GET ANOTHER +4% CRIT CHANCE PERK?", aka if other issues that waste your time are sorted, then the "TIMER" itself will be less of a problem. And some RNG events take too long to complete, so there's that.  [h2]Friendly Fire[/h2] [b]6) Friendly Fire[/b] is insane on some weapons and also on Grenades. An idiot with a Cryo grenade has the potential to kill everyone with one bad throw or himself. As for the weapons, some weapons are capable of extremely high FF, while the other's aren't. High FF weapons and grenades should be toned down a bit tbh.  [h2]Ammo economy[/h2] [b]7) Ammo economy[/b] problems comes for a few reasons: usually, ammo boxes spawns only at the start and at the end of the level, adding a guarrantee spawns on the mid would help a lot with this and some weapons are only designed as secondary weapons, if you got no choice, but to get them as first pick, then you struggle for a bit till you get good main weapon.  Spotter ammo box also in a weird spot, imo it should be changed from a -SINGLE USE 50% AMMO REGEN- to something like -ENTIRE TEAM CAN USE IT ONCE(including Spotter), but regen like 30% of max ammo-. World War Z did this great with Technician class, legit best class in the game. Not only the boxes have multiple uses, they also enhance around 2 full mags of your weapon with EXPLOSIVE bullets.  [h2]Class Balance[/h2] [b]8) Class Balance[/b] is also in a kinda weird/bad spot. The Devs basically screams at your face how they demand teamwork from everyone, yet some classes got either garbage or ZERO team utility and are just selfish DPS classes.   [b]Spotter[/b] - the best designed class, his entire kit is all about team utility and he has zero selfish DPS options.  [b]Slicer [/b]- strong selfish DPS option that focused on melee perks and ability cooldowns. His supposed team utility is an absolute ass and is useful only in a few niche situations (while you rez or repair something, as example).  [b]Retcon[/b] - another strong (probably strongest DPS in the game) that has INSANE FRIENDLY FIRE potential due to her builds usually focusing on spamming Grenades and Grenade Launcher(s), aka AOE spam. Has absolutely zero team utility.  [b]Falconer[/b] - this is probably the best designed DPS right now, this class got both great damage, interesting team utility (dron rez) and amazing team damage buff aura field.  [b]Guardian[/b] - legit the weakest class in the game imo, his team utility is not enough to compensate low damage potential. This guy can't do his job as a tank (your best option is to avoid damage, not to tank it, because enemies and bosses hit like a truck on D3+) You could potentially do a full armor build with armor regen perk, but armor regen is so rare, i saw it like 4 times at most in 30 hours+. This is ass casino that i wouldn't recommend at all.  [b]Classes TLDR [/b] Top - Spotter(insane team utility)/Falconer (great damage + good utility) Great - Slicer/Retcon (selfish DPS classes, no team utility) Weak - Guardian.   TLDR There's nothing that's impossible to fix in the game and overall the replayability is okay.

I have 344 hours in Deep Rock Galactic on Steam. I have played DRG more than most all other people. I consider DRG perhaps the greatest game of all time, and I mean it. But I do NOT recommend Rogue Core. Rogue Core is an unbalance, unfair, unfun waste of time. The random rogue-like elements are lazy and completely unbalanced. DRG had AMAZING overclocks for your weapons that made each weapon play COMPLETLY differently than the base-version of the same weapon. Are these SAME Overclocks that already exist included in the potential Upgrade Options during a Rogue Core run? NOPE! Just boring generic lazy stuff like 4% Crit, +15% Crit damage, +20 Health, 50% more red sugar... BORING! Few options are actually helpful, and with no rerolls like are available in other games with random mechanics like this, most runs just end at the final boss when players realize they can do NO DAMAGE and 3-4 players feel like they just wasted 50 minutes of their time. And that isn't even the worst part, EVERY Rogue Core run just feels like it FORCES the player to SPEEDRUN a BADLY-Designed DRG Level with guns and upgrades they DONT want. Probably the worst aspect is that players aren't given enough time. The FUN of DRG was exploring. Who was Rogue Core even designed for? Surely not DRG players, their LOYAL Player-base and paying customers. EVERYTHING in Rogue Core feels like it was added to waste the player's time. Go play ONE level of Rogue Core and watch how fast the 5+ minutes of unskippable BS at the beginning of each level gets old. Who in their right mind thought THAT was good game design? They should seriously consider offering a refund to the players who supported DRG FOR YEARS after completely disappointing their fans with the unbalanced, unfair, unfun, lazy MESS that is Rogue Core.

The goods are really good. The bads are so baffiling bad that it drags the entire game from a solid 7-8/10 to a 4-5/10.   The Goods +Gunplay surpasses DRGs when you dedicate an entire run on building up a single gun with the workbenchs. +overall higher difficulty level for those who found DRG to be braindead easy after hundreds of hours into the predecessor title +More focus on PvE, with waves that are capable of overwhelming the team if not prepared  +3/5 of the classes are fun and viable to play, gives some replayability +Workbenchs always spark some neurons in my head  The Bads -A lot of game design mechanics that disrespects the players time. Emphasis on a lot. These mechanics are what largely drag the game down and will be what make up most of my bads list. -The games spicier difficulty IS good, but its implemented in a way where you MUST have a team of minimum 3 players whom are all competent if you want to have a winning run. -As such, you'll have to wait for others to join your game or find a game that matches your needs. With the rapidly descending player count and every match having a forced one class clause this becomes a time burning task. -Every match starts with a pointless march from launchship to starting gear room. The only task is to simply holding interact on two barrier devices to let down a wall of pain. They advertised that every run starts with just a pickaxe but they do absolutely nothing with the idea. EVERY run will have an extra 2 minutes slapped on to it for no reason.  -Cave layouts are boring and time wasting. Lots of long corridors of nothing to traverse to get to the next big room that has a handful of things to interact with before running along the next hallway of nothing. Either copy paste the cave layouts from DRG or rework the entire thing because right now its really bad. -The negotiation system needs to be scrapped entirely. It doesn't work. It also doesnt help 90% of upgrades found from negotiations bring nothing to the table. Most players will pick a simple Hp up over the other choices offered more than half the time.  -Have to huddle the team everytime you want to get some upgrades from the mule of this game. This needs to be scrapped ASAP. -Way too much asset swapped content from DRG. Makes me believe they made this a spinoff title just for the $$$ when it was originally supposed to be another mode for DRG.   Not worth the price tag in its current state by a long shot. The game does have potential but needs more time in the oven.
Needs a lot of work. Collecting minerals to level up feels bad, runs take too long, upgrades are boring af.

The sluggish game pace could use an overhaul. Just starting a match, taking the elevator down, and opening the cave entrance to select a weapon/grenade takes a while, even though it's kinda neat and immersive the first few times, it seems this whole part is unnecessary and could be skipped to speed up run time. There is just too much waiting for stages to load, teammates to pick upgrades, teammates to rally at an upgrade, etc. The game has sprawling caverns that allows and often promotes a team to split up, but when upgrades are found it requires the whole team to be present, so the game is sort of working against itself to keep the fun going by letting players explore but then constantly yanking their leash back to rally for an upgrade. The overall rogue-like mechanics are not bad, but with dives lasting up to an hour while you're stuck with the same loadout it gets kind of annoying. A few modifications to pacing could make the game better and keep those dopamine hits rolling in instead of the stagnation it has now.

This game is a tech demo. You make your way down a linear tunnel to an elevator, fighting the same couple of annoying enemies, get in the elevator, choosing one of a few modifiers, to than repeat this same loop until you fight one of a few bosses, and that's the entire game. There's sometimes side events, but they are uninteresting, or not reasonable to be completed in the amount of time you have before the Reaper Worm comes. I wasn't expecting DRG: 2. But I was expecting to enjoy myself at least a little? Even if you remove all the major friction points people commonly (and validly) critique about this game (ammo, timers, competing for upgrades) I still don't think you are left with a fun game. The classes don't have a strong identity, and just aren't memorable. The cosmetic system is bafflingly lackluster, and there just isn't anything here that draws me in or makes me want to play further. I feel like several decisions were made not to the benefit of the game, but just to be different from DRG, to this games' detriment. DRG has the finest cosmetic system I've ever experienced, with my only complaint being that you have to unlock the same item 4 times, once for each class. This game doubled down on that mistake, and completely gutted the good elements of cosmetic customization, when you have a perfect system already figured out, why not port it over directly? I just don't see why anyone would play this, and I was pretty excited going in. Also this game makes my pc scream, when DRG doesn't even make it warm, on account of Rogue Core not being very well optimized.

I have 3,6k hours in DRG, and now that I did some runs in RC, here are the impressions, from the outside to the inside. I do not expect this to be DRG 2.0, but it was your choice and you won't be able to distance yourself from it. This is feedback for the devs.     - I like the biome/dept/length unlocks. The mission selector is probably visually better in DRG.   - The meta progression is much better than the DRG perks, looks as if it will provide more variety in builds.   - There will be probably half must picks (ammo&armor and/or resupply) and half for free use once you have all 8 unlocked.   - Cosmetics will probably be just recycled from DRG, it's hard to come up with completely different ones. I don't really care about this.   - Class duplication is not such an issue as in DRG, thus forcing different classes should be optional.   - The class has a substantial impact on what perks should be chosen during gameplay, maybe too much so., pushing to the same instead of experimentation.   - Weapon variety is good, but needs more balancing. I'm happy that grenade selection was moved to the game start.   - Cave generation in nowhere near where it should be, there should be no issue to reuse some from DRG. Currently if you are unlucky, the elevator is at the start of a long tunnel which doesn't loop back and you may have to leave sooner than you were able to explore properly.   - The timer. Oh, boy. It would maybe be fine but currently the cave generation doesn't support it. I haven't played top difficulty yet, to see how the number of enemies affects it. But on Depth 2, if you get a large expenite event, you probably won't have time to explore the level fully and maybe even lose the bonus what you were promised for the additional mutator. Once the timer runs out, maybe don't dump the full weight immediately and have it ramp up with time. The unkillable worms are just lazy and if you're not in a voice chat, it's easy to miss in the chaos that someone is being abducted.   - Expenite upgrades are underwhelming with some being simply trash, it's often better to just take HP and heal. There is no need for utility upgrades, those are fine in the meta progression. Having the stars align for them to work is also disappointing. The need to always gather up is detrimental and people are having a hard time realizing that you don't need to do it for every upgrade, but should be done in bulk if the team has to gather for some reason. Workbench and bio-booster (plus events) are already gameplay interrupting enough, and then you get eventually crates on top.   - Ammo is a bit tricky, it's maybe fine when you only have one weapon (and the resupply is in good position) or you have made some progress in the meta tree and you pick perks for it, but without those, it's nowhere enough.   - The social aspect of the perk picking. I watched most of the stuff you released about it and I understand the reasoning, but it just doesn't work. It only works if you're playing pre-made, don't try to make me remember who has what build beside me. Come up with something different. It also takes too much time.

Rogue Core feels like it wasn't made for how it is actually played  I want to briefly describe my pain points with the game that have made me fairly quickly (~20 hours) stop wanting to play the game.   Some of these are game design or balance issues, some I think are unavoidable due to the nature of the game. The tl;dr of this is that this game feels like it's designed to be played with an organized group rather than randoms, and the upgrade systems are bland.  0) A fundamental issue that exacerbates every other issue is simply the length of each dive and that there is no drop in and drop out. I'm not sure anything could be done about this, it's just how it's designed. This is a game most of us are going to be playing with randoms, and can end very quickly after a long time investment.  1) Waiting on teammates is painful: This is probably the main issue, and I think this is unavoidable. require 4 people to group up to do upgrades is painful, waiting 40+ seconds if you have a slow reader or a new player on the team is painful. There's just a lot of waiting.  2) Most of the gameplay is sprinting from upgrade station to upgrade station.  3) The upgrades are simply not interesting. I think this is correctable. I compare this game to something like Hades, where the roguelike upgrades are dramatic, fun, flashy (I mean literally affect the art and graphics), and allow for some alternative gameplay styles by mixing and matching them. Generally the only upgrades that matter in RC are DPS or durability upgrades, they change little.  4) You mostly can't start with the fun guns, it's mostly pistols, and if you put upgrades into them before you can get a heavy weapons crate then your build is kind of split.  5) This one is personal, but I do not enjoy meta progression systems. I want to play the game because it's fun, not because I need to do unlocks. I know I'm in a minority among gamers and every multiplayer business model knows that but it's still how I feel.  Anyways, that's all. I think about multiplayer games that I greatly enjoy, since I play with randoms, Vermintide, Helldivers, DRG, etc, and they are all designed with a low emphasis on player coordination and player persistence (drop in/out) except perhaps at the highest difficulty, and RC I don't think was designed that way.
