The mission timer kills the game beyond depth level one... It sucks that you can't do a single secondary mission without losing your 30 minute run. For the first few hours I was having heaps of fun, and thought that other gamers leaving negative reviews were just being picky... until I tried progressing. What makes DRG fun for me is being able to explore, mine, and work your way through points of interest - rather than kiting enemies and playing a "get to the elevator simulator". DRG has so much charm and has awesome DEVS, so it is a bit painful to see the game launch at this state, especially for the team working on it. Hopefully everyone takes it on the chin and comes up with a good solution :)

Bit of an overview.  In a technical sense, the game is solid, there's not really any performance problems or major bugs, but from a design sense they messed up big time.  The timer is too short, which encourages everyone to run around like headless chickens, which means any sort of tactics go out the window.  The group-upgrade system is horrible. Having to get 4 people to stand near the drone can take over a minute if your allies are not paying any attention. Half the upgrades are super niche or straight up trash, meanwhile the other half are good, but good luck getting them when your allies constantly steal all the good perks that you need for your build.  The enhancement system (perks you take into a run) need fleshing out and reworking pronto. They're very generic, boring and you can't equip very many of them, not to mention some are terrible. The dev should copy what Far Far West did with it's perk system and also make some more interesting or wild perks.  The amount of cosmetics is very low compared to DRG.   Many weapons from DRG are missing and seems to entirely lack drillers weapons.  The Bio-Deck system is alright, but I'm not sure about only being able to change one deck out, would be nice if both could be changed, but it's whatever I guess. Would like to see some extra decks for that class unlocked when you promote a class for the first time as well.  The enemy design seems like a downgrade, it's kind of hard to tell some enemies apart and they move around like a bunch of maniacs so good luck hitting their weakspots. The glyphids and other enemies from the main game never really had this problem or at least not to this degree.  Solo is horribly balanced and basiaclly unplayable once you get to Depth 3.  Is it worth a buy? Maybe, but only if you're ok with the major design flaws the game has at the moment. I really wanted to love this game.  Heck, I'd even settle for liking it and I truly hope that the developers make some major tweaks in a few spots to make this game attractive to an audience wider than the hardcore speed runners it is currently aiming towards.  At the moment, I cannot recommend it.  Yes, I know it's not intended to be a sequel to DRG and that it's its own environment set on the same planet but it should at least be close especially considering it's called Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core.  We were told this was going to be like DRG at difficulty 3.  Umm, no.  At the very least it's pushing well into difficulty 4 and probably 5 in some places like the core spawns when the timer runs out and boy does it run out often because of the first problem.  1) There is no difficulty setting.  This is probably the largest issue with the game and it's implementation could easily resolve all of the most common complaints we are seeing.  Just like DRG with difficulty levels, have the same sort of system that would make the Timer, Ammo and Red Sugar spawn quantity appropriate to the level.  Make the current iteration Difficulty 4 and adjust from there.  Lower the rewards for a less difficult run but at least allow for a difficulty tweak as a basic function.  I play on an experienced three-man team with thousands of hours of DRG experience and after six attempts, we still haven't managed to get the first boss down and yes, we play DRG at difficulty 3.  2) Timer.  Yes, everyone is complaining about this and for good reason.  It's horrible.  Considering the random nature of a roguelike environment, you're forced to gather just about everything you can on the first two levels just for a chance to survive the boss encounter.  If you really must leave in a timer, consider removing it entirely from D1 and double the time on D2.  Tie this into number 1 above so on Difficulty 1, you don't even have a timer or it's extremely long.  3) Ammo.  Oh boy, another hot topic for everyone and yes, the current limited ammo system is insane.  The number of times we're going into melee because we're out of ammo is again, tied into #1 above.  The game is extremely difficult and even with decent percentages of critical hits, you're still extremely limited on the ammo total.  Getting a second weapon just to have another ammo pool is critical and if you're unlucky enough to get a run without a Heavy Crate, we'll, just kiss you ass goodbye on the boss fight.  Again, see #1 above.  4) Weapon Choices.  Another touchy subject because if someone gets what you need, you're just SOL.  At the very least, make the Health choice on the right side always available for everyone because with so little ammo and red sugar, people are grabbing those over major upgrades just in an attempt to stay alive.  Again, see #1 above.  5) Red Sugar.  Ahh, the wonderful crystal we all know and love and wish there was at least twice as much on the map as we see.  With so much damage being dealt, due to a lack of shields and switching to armor, we're seeing every scrap of red sugar being consumed on several of these maps and people are still not surviving.  In the last game I played tonight, during the first boss fight, I literally provided first aid 10 times during the fight to get teammates back up but it's wasn't even for us to win.  Again, see #1 above.  6) Armor.  Okay, I can see how adding armor instead of shields from a developer standpoint would make sense to provide another level of risk to a difficult game but imagine the scene in DRG headquarters.  "Hey guys, we need to send an assault team into the core of the planet and they'll be having massive fights against waves of extremely hostile aliens.  Do you think we should send in a few shields?  Nah."  I really want to see Rogue Core succeed and become another DRG success but in it's current state, I don't think it's going to get there.  Considering the current rating for the game, I'm not alone.  Being Early Access, there is still time to fix some of the least popular elements and I really hope they get addressed but as I mentioned, not having a basic difficulty level will really hinder the overall appeal of the game.

[[Edit] - June 3rd 2026, first major post-EA release announcement  It's nice to see that some generally approachable fixes to gameplay roughness are being worked on right now, with some solutions that I've seen suggested elsewhere. Rogue Core's perk selections will hopefully feel less clumsy and more interesting after the changes, but we'll see! All perks being offered at the same rarity for all players was something I was hoping for. That should also help GSG find how to rebalance the rarity of the perks they have, as well as figure out how to design more appropriate perks at each rarity level.  Also great to see that the heels are no longer being dug in on not implementing an auto-sprint option.  I'm a hair more optimistic that Ghost Ship Games will, over the next few years, excavate Rogue Core from the depths of Deep Rock Galactic's design choices and find how to make their new title have its own (more enjoyable) identity.]  I can't currently recommend DRG Rogue Core to general audiences or even general fans of Deep Rock Galactic. I also cannot recommend it to fans of most roguelikes or roguelites. The core design pillars of DRG Rogue Core have a lot of unintentional friction between them as a result of Rogue Core being built upon the skeleton of DRG. I'm not wholly dissatisfied with what currently exists, but there are some major pain points that keep me from having fun the whole time I'm playing.  I would say many players who have experienced Rogue Core so far might be struggling to accurately describe what is not working for them, or might misinterpret what they believe their issue(s) to be. General complaints are around the timer, perk drafting, gameplay pacing, and resource management, often in the context of multiplayer. Many of these elements are unsavory since Rogue Core doesn't do a great job presenting its gameplay patterns appropriately as a result of its deep(!) connection to Deep Rock Galactic. This has led to Ghost Ship and fans of DRG driving home this statement of "It's not Deep Rock Galactic! It does not play like Deep Rock Galactic!" In spite of that, it looks like Deep Rock Galactic. Deep Rock Galactic: Rogue Core literally has Deep Rock Galactic in its name. It has character designs, weapons, a visual style, voice lines, exploration, levels and level generation, gameplay mechanics, all from Deep Rock Galactic. It's no wonder players are confused about why Rogue Core might play differently than Deep Rock Galactic.  On lower difficulties (referred to as Depths, currently Depth 1 to Depth 4), the challenge is very much still there, but not so dramatically as to teach the player that they might be "playing wrong". The fact that players can approach the game incorrectly is a major pitfall; if Rogue Core's expression of its gameplay were different, players might better understand how they should be approaching the scenarios presented in its missions and how to prioritize their use of time over complete exploration of a cave. Sometimes, you just have to move to the next floor or you'll spend resources you don't have for marginal improvements. Most classes you can play as do not start a mission with enough movement to correct their positioning quickly, whether because they need to rejoin their team, collect an upgrade, or navigate to the entrance to a new room. This is exacerbated by random level generation that might keep some side objectives you would prefer to do too far away to be reasonable. Traversal tools also feel at odds with the terrain generation in Rogue Core. The leftovers from DRG like the ziplines and platform gun are for a game with an entirely different pace and set of challenges. On a Depth 3 mission, misunderstanding of how to use your team's time or resources will get you killed and sent back to the lobby. It isn't exactly clear without repeated failure (and missions take a while to start) what one's priorities should be and what, sadly, should just be ignored. Looking at you, flashy expenite events. This disconnect is only mildly expressed to the player through its mechanics on lower difficulties, and not at all in its tutorial. Driving home its intent for the player to feel tension through the timer and to move with purpose seem to come about through experiences that feel bad to the player, instead of rewarding.  I believe the timer is important to Rogue Core, but I also believe that Rogue Core and its timer are not relaying the proper information to its players. As a result. players feel that the timer needs to go away, since they're trying to play the game that Rogue Core keeps erroneously telling them that it is in spite of its timer.  Communicating with random players in a multiplayer lobby is more of a learning curve than a design challenge. I don't think that over time players will have trouble knowing what they should take from an upgrade choice (or identifying when it doesn't matter since every option sucks). Killing the pacing by regrouping for every upgrade from Ellis/the R.E.P.D. is something that players have to train themselves out of, instead of the game offering this up through HUD information or some tutorialization of the idea. Ideally, Rogue Core would focus on teaching its players how to play optimally for a more enjoyable experience. Regrouping for an equipment or heavy weapon draft is a different story, since this action can't really be delayed until it's "more convenient". As such, that regroup requirement just feels sloppy.  The act of drafting perks and upgrades with your team would feel less bad if there weren't so many "filler" options in the pool to pick from. Perks should feel exciting or more impactful to pick for your character's growth; right now, general "level ups" from expenite upgrades can sometimes feel like a bit of a waste of time unless you get lucky. Junk items in roguelikes and roguelites are nothing new, but it feels especially bad when you get so few chances at perks in a typical run of Rogue Core, compared to something like Risk of Rain, Vampire Survivors, or Hades. With a full team, it's inevitable that someone will end up with a box of tissues when their teammate wound up with a steak dinner, several times in a 30 minute span.  Progression honestly feels worse than it did in Deep Rock Galactic by a country mile, and with ~1100 hours in DRG, I gotta say, progression in DRG wasn't very good either. Persistent upgrades are slow to unlock and spread out across multiple unlock trees and sometimes for each class as well. It is not interesting or exciting to engage with, is bloated and unorganized, and feels like something you work towards over dozens of hours for minimal impact on your play experience. I don't think players should be expected to unlock personalization options like beards and hats for each class one at a time at the pace that GSG has set with its currencies. Everything needs a few passes on progression, it's dismal.  I don't think Ghost Ship Games has a strong idea of what Rogue Core's design elements should be or how they should be presented in order for the game to have wide appeal and be more easily understood. I don't think I have the answers since I'm not in their offices, I don't know what has been discussed behind closed doors, what has been tried and did not work. But I do know what exists now is convoluted, confusing, and indicative of a dramatic internal identity crisis. If Rogue Core has to explain to its playerbase in words how it should be played to be enjoyed, then it is not succeeding in communicating this through design, and that is the real problem with Rogue Core for GSG to solve.

The early game is far too hard.  We've done 5 or 6 missions and have yet to complete one.  I can see that earning the enhancements will be a grind, which is fine, but we should be able to complete the first mission without needing anything other than the base load out.    The timer sucks.  Either get rid of it completely until you start the final battle or only have it be a swarm timer.  We spend so much time along the way trying to pick up upgrades and gear so that we can have a chance at the final boss that by the time we're there the frenzy starts within a minute.  The timer turns every step into a zergling rush to get to the elevator and the group does not have the time to pick up sufficient firepower to to the final poss.    The random weapon selection on each mission sucks.  How about you allow each player to pick a starting weapon before they leave the ship?  We just had a run where we ended up with NO heavy weapons.  You simply cannot do the final boss without heavy weapons.  You run out of armor, health, ammo, and ammo resupplies too fast.    At this point, I am shelving the game until they come out with a major patch.  I have even considered asking for my money back, but I suspect that I'd simply buy the game again once it's better.    In short, give it time, give it a few months for the devs to balance it and otherwise tweak it before buying it.

The basic core spawn move too unnaturally to the point they fly short distances and they aren't fun to shoot. I strongly dislike the timer/reaper mechanic causing you to not be able to completely explore each floor unless you have a full stack and then it is extra frustrating to die to the reapers having your run ended by a timer rather than skill. I think that going back to nitra and rechargeable shields would fix some of the issues with gameplay. Can we get some bosses without I frames being massive parts of the fights. The characters feel like their abilities are never worth upgrading over upgrading weapons and I find myself hardly using them. I don't feel like most of the upgrades are good at making unique builds. There are some upgrades that hardly effect the gameplay even after being stacked a couple of times and then there are like 5 upgrades that are actually good and impact the run with hardly any in between these categories.  I feel like the game will be solid in the future but as things stand now, I don't recommend buying this game if you are expecting more than 10 hours at most.

Past a depth 2, missions are impossible with less than 3 players minimum. This would be fine if the random-queue gameplay was fun, but sharing/negotiating with people who more often than not are not communicative whatsoever, it is not fun. DRG had the capacity of making playing a horde shooter with any random a pleasant and cooperative experience. Sadly the cooperation and seemless experience didnt translate to this game where every part of the co op just create friction with the other players.  Negotiations could exist if it didn't kill the flow of the game. A solution exists in a secondary mode called gauntlet where the upgrades happens between levels. Negotiations are always fine with perks, always frustrating with weapons and tools.   I believe the flow and agency of the game would have been significantly better if you could choose your base equipement before lauching. This whole 5 minutes intro sequence every game is just a chore after a few hours, some weapons are objectively really really bad at horde clearing and the balance is all over the place..   Joining a random game is a big slog and will make you take random weapon and tools that you didn't choose then put you trough the beginning of the level alone wich results in almost certain death if the host decides to launch a wave in higher difficulties. The game is a far greater experience in solo than in co op with randoms. (Shocking for a spin off of DRG)  The moment to moment gunplay is great, but the different systems fail to create any kind of meaningfull agency over the way you tackle your advance. The timer is a good addition but your only choice is either: "if you got time do everything in the linear hallway"/"if you aint got time just do the 2 best time to power ration upgrades and leave directly'". The roguelike perk thing is sadly not very inspired in it's power ups and since you are divising them every time you have no real individual agency over it.   On the progression side it's nothing new or interesting. It's doing it's job of making you stronger the more you play and that's it. No variable build for a class or special perks that changes the way you play. Pure brut stats. On the customisation side there is nothing for the moment and it's kinda baffling.  The only solid foundation this game has is it's moment to moment combat. The new ennemy faction is well designed and pretty fun to fight (when not overbuffed by modificators like invisibility, double speed and double hp pool). The bosses are also an highlight even tho I believe the game would have been way more intresting with a boss at each level instead of the elevator.  Sadly the different moving parts of the roguelike aspect are just not making it and feel like they are contradicting themselves.  Idk how they can salvage that one. What makes this game good are not it's Rogue Core additions.  Also add auto-sprint. Also make spotter scanner reveal invisible ennemies.

In it's current state, Rogue Core is the wrong kind of difficult. Enemies are bullet sponges, resources are exceptionally limited, you can easily get punished if the map generates side-events in less than ideal locations, and the reclaimers feel weak.   Every failed run has felt less like I have played poorly and simply the RNG decided I wasn't allowed to have fun. Bad weapons/upgrades, waves of corespawn than are arbitarily harder than others, along with the most basic enemies soaking half a clip worth of ammo.  Why don't the reclaimers start with some basic gear? Why is armour worse than shields in every measurable way?   The game feels awful, and I will not recommend anyone play this until there are some *serious* balance changes. Do not believe the post by GSG saying the difficulty is approximately hazard 3, it's a lie [or they don't know what hazard 3 feels like].

I would give this game 5/10 currently.   I do not think the guns are well balanced. The amount of fun  seems entirely dependent on the gun you get at the start, and if its a bad one you're in for 40+ minutes of a miserable slog.  Normally I would just restart but that's pretty rude in a 4 person game. Some guns having built in upgrades that become luck of the draw to whoever gets first pick is also just a bad game design.   The guns are also shared, so you have to hope you're not last in line with the scraps no one else wanted after they have their pick.   Randomize the guns and stats, have one person pick, than repeat the process with a full new randomized set.   The anti grav mobility grenade is far and away better than any other mobility tool since time is the biggest limiting factor in a run. Who cares about the zipline or platforms when they take 10x as long to use. The drill has niche uses but 90% of the time doesn't do anything, and flares don't seem as useful as any other option.   The grenades are fine.   A new perk card should spawn after someone choses theirs. It feels awful when you're trying to build something but the team is ignoring you and always having a full set to pick from would be better than the current shared system.  The timer is fine and hopefully people will learn better time management and upgrade management   Many of the perks feel very lack luster and incremental. Far from being a power fantasy I spend most of the game feeling weak and like I'm barely scraping by.
Honestly? don't buy it, just wait till more stuff is out  The game is awfull to play and it gets very boring

Played 2 hours and for now don't recommend folks pick this up assuming you're like me and will be playing with randoms.   Pros: Gunplay, traversal, humor from DRG are all here and pressing V to say Rock & Stone! with your team in the dropship remains as compelling as my first DRG run years ago.  Cons: Public play with randoms is not well designed and this has killed my appetite to sink more time in the game.  I had a great run where we cleared 2 floors without wiping, had secondary objective rolling and out of nowhere I got ejected from the match citing "This was meant to be friends only". Loaded back into the base ship with the meta progression from the run lost.  Why is that an even option for the host of a public match?    I chalked it up to bad luck and played a public match again only to have another run ended by a capricious host. Cleared a floor with 2 down then immediately host ended the run.  For a second time, was sent back to base ship with nothing to show for the last 30 minutes of playing.    As the game is now, it doesn't respect your time if you're a solo player going into public matches.  If you have a crew of 4 and can avoid matching with randoms then there's a lot to enjoy here.  For everyone else, I'd stay away until the developers change the game design.

I've been a huge DRG fan since it's release, logging over 1500 hours. I was eager to try Rogue Core and had a pretty open mind for what it would be like.   I played one game with a cohesive group of DRG buddies. They already had some Rogue Core experience and showed me the ropes. After that one game, I have no desire to play anymore.   It's like DRG with most of the good bits stripped away. It's a drag having to get gear piecemeal during a run. Having to constantly link up for upgrades breaks any flow. The special class abilities are gimmicky. Core spawn < bugs. The time commitment sucks; each run is the length of a deep dive. I wanted to like this game.  I really did.  But so much of the games seems averse to letting people actually have fun.    For one, starting a run takes way too damn long.  Having to go through the stupid barrier at the beginning of every mission before you can even roll for your starting gear is just a complete waste of time.  There's no reason for the wait.  It doesn't enhance the gameplay.  It doesn't add anything to the level.  It just slows down the start of every run.  Part of what keeps roguelikes/lites fun is how fast it is to start a fresh run.  Died?  No big deal, 30 seconds, you're back in the fun.  Not so with RC.  Next, too many weapons just don't feel good to use.  They either don't do enough damage, or don't have enough ammo to keep up with how spongy and numerous the enemies can get.  Weapons being random makes this an extra pain point.  Didn't start with a good weapon?  May as well restart your run and waste the 5 minutes to go through the barrier and reroll again.  The armour system does not feel good to interact with.  Armour gets shredded extremely quickly by even the basic crawlers, there are precious few ways to restore it, and without armor, your health gets shredded even quicker.  These aspects, plus the lack of a mercy window or some armour recovery when revived means that even a single party member dropping almost instantly scrubs a run.   Enemy density, bullet-sponginess, and their damage feels overtuned on even the lowest difficulties.  Basic crawlers spawn by the dozens from every angle during wave, and move far too fast and erratically to be dealt with in a timely manner, especially when defending the elevator.  Their leap is so quick and has such a long range that they may as well just be teleporting to the objective.  Item and objective placement is also detrimental to player experience.  Optional objectives placed at the opposite end of the map is not engaging gameplay, especially when the timer wants the players to hurry up.  It's never a complex choice.  Optional objectives will always get ignored with how punishing every other aspect is.  Several Exponite events take so long as to completely ignorable.   Terrain generation is another pain point.  The number of chasms, gaps, holes, catwalks, and other terrain features is also far too punishing when characters lack any meaningful dedicated movement abilities outside of getting lucky when they start a run.  It's too easy to fall to your death when just trying to navigate a level without fighting, let alone when you're cornered by a swarm.  This is exacerbated during boss fights, as you are required to move around constantly, yet the terrain is generated in a way that often prevents that, or makes it far more difficult than is warranted..  The ability control scheme also doesn't feel great to use.  Needing to hold down a button, and then tap several other buttons might work fine on a controller, but on a keyboard, it's awkward.  And the inability to properly remap all three ability to either mouse buttons, or simple key taps is a baffling choice.  Sure, roguelikes are supposed to be hard.  But there's a line where difficulty goes from challenging to tedious, and Rogue Core is firmly in the tedious category, at least as it currently stands.  Maybe in a year or so when they've ironed out the kinks and made a good game, I'll come back to it.  But until then, I won't be in a rush to excavate it.

[i]As the game is right now[/i], I can't recommend it. I really hope I can come back and change this to a recommend, because the core gameplay loop is fun and it does hit a lot of the DRG and shooter beats I like.  But some parts are so overtuned right now. I saw all the reviews saying there are balancing issues, and I shrugged and had the game anyway so figured I'd try it. My husband and I aren't new to FPSes and we're the losing-is-fun types, but when you can get through the first 3 floors of a Complexity 1 run with no trouble and then get two-shotted by the boss—I dunno man, maybe it's us getting skill issued, but the tuning plus the incredibly stingy ammo economy makes it feel more unfair than challenging.   I want to be challenged; I don't want to feel like I've run out of options short of trying to plink the boss in the ass with my pickaxe halfway through a fight it took 30 minutes to get to despite having been thoughtful about upgrades, stacked ice to freeze anything that gets into melee range, stacked weakspot damage and crit chance, so on and so forth.  Like I said, the core gameplay loop has potential and I'm going to wait and see how the game develops in EA. I think the devs have a good enough track record that I'm hopeful, but as it is right now, I wouldn't recommend it to anyone who's not like, a veteran Dwarf Fortress player or similar "I enjoy getting my head kicked in for laffs" type.

TLDR: This game takes so little risk it's safer than a handjob with a condom    This is a game that hardly stands on it's own. There are a lot of borrowed elements from DRG, which makes me wonder what the ♥♥♥♥ did they do the whole time this was in alpha.  This feels more like a mod or DLC than a standalone game. This comparison is unavoidable.  So, where does the game fail?    Pacing: The game wants to be played fast, you are on a timer, you must hurry otherwise an endless horde of enemies keeps spawning. But whoever designed this game decided to put time waster objectives, with little new mechanics compared to objectives (even secondary ones) from DRG.  This is further exacerbated by the occasional extreme cave gen, where in DRG it's a fun thing, here it can be a major pain in the ass (and a further time waste) if you or your team has no traversal tools whatsoever.  When you drop into a mission, you have to do another ready check, then waste even more time selecting weapons and grenades. Things you can very well do in the lobby or the loading screen.  It gets worse, to get your meaningless upgrades, you have to stand around the deposit drone along with your teammates, and then wait until each one of you selects an upgrade. Interrupting gameplay from 20-60 seconds for each upgrade you get. Contrary to what other people are saying, I do not find the shared upgrade pool a problem, because the upgrades are so generic I literally do not care if someone else takes something that my build "needs".    The upgrades you get are mostly meaningless. There is nothing particularly fun, it's all mostly +% of a stat, or when X then +%. Occasionally there is some funky upgrade, but it's usually more underwhelming than it sounds like.  The workbench upgrades are more effective, but still boring. They allow to add some spice to your guns. Two shot grenade launcher, more fire rate, use the entire ammo pool as magazine, nothing particularly fun or stupid fun stands out.    The classes themselves are okay. They don't have anything stupid broken with them. They work. Some are more boring, some less boring, personal preference I guess.  The permanent unlocks, the perks, take a while to unlock, and the really good stuff probably takes tens of hours to unlock, as they are unlocked by doing challenges like in DRG. Various levels of these perks can be stacked. These are all passive buffs, with no active perks. But it's still +% buffs. The UI for it is just bad.  There is some semblance of build identity, but nothing that fundamentally changes how a class is played.  At the very least, they didn't manage to butcher the gunplay. They are still fine to use, and some of the new additions are interesting.    Design failures, in no particular order:  No class stacking. You and your friends want to play multiple of the same class? ♥♥♥♥ you then. It was a toggle in DRG (for late joining even!), it is not here for some reason.  If you late join, you are blessed with a random loadout. Wonderful when you get a super ♥♥♥♥ gun you will be stuck with for one or two levels.  Omega, the new mission control, is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ annoying. It has no charm whatsoever.  Enemies are boring and uninspired. Visually uninteresting, they have nothing going for them.  The cosmetic shop (that you buy with scrip) is just ass.  Why the ♥♥♥♥ is there no filter on the server browser to hide full lobbies?????  Even with only 7 hours of play time the side objectives are starting to feel the same old ♥♥♥♥.  Difficulty seems to be all over the place.    I fail to see what the game wants to be: it wants to be a fast paced rogue lite, but also wastes your time with SLOW side objectives. It punishes you wasting time in combat instead of just ignoring the enemies and going forward.  The roadmap amounts to nothing. It's just "more content", a given since this is an early access game. What about progression? What about new mechanics? Nothing on that front. The issues are structural, and more content won't fix it.  The game does not take any risks into making anything overpowered even by accident: after extensive alpha testing and ignored feedback, the result is a game scared of it's own potential, it's like the devs have PTSD over the original carpet bomber overclock.

Virtually none of the upgrades, both in runs and overall progression, were something to get excited about. The vast majority of them are tiny % boosts to things like ammo capacity, crit, or health.  The game also seems intent on stalling forward movement by making you wait to gather everyone at the drone for upgrades, where for some reason players have to compete with their own teammates over the same selection, again many of which literally give bonuses for standing still.  There is also an awkward flip-flopping between UI elements that pause the game to go fullscreen and diegetic UI that projects over top of objects (and can be mistakenly selected even when the option is not in your FOV).  Overall, a lot of odd choices that trip up the momentum of the game and do not reward the time investment with any exciting abilities or upgrades. Unfortunately just made me want to play either original DRG or another roguelike altogether.

So far it is very "Meh".  Coming from someone who's clocked over 300+ hrs in DRG, and from someone who loves Rougelites/likes, this game ain't it.  This game underperforms in pretty much every category.  Let me just describe the things I dont like:  -Upgrades. Pretty much every upgrade is boring, make them stand out more. Now the weapon benches and Bio-Boosters are fine, but you wont be seeing them as much.  -Ammo Economy. I see alot of people complaining about it, and yes, could do with more ammo.  -The timer. Now ive only played up to descent 2 and so far the timer seems fair, im not sure how it is on higher difficulties so im not gonna jump in on that.  -R.E.P.D. or Drone/Upgrade thing. This is one of my main complaints, you have to get everyone in a small circle to huddle up and choose your upgrades. So you get to have fun standing there like an idiot with your finger in your ass while the rest of your team is doing other chores. Then once you all come together you have to pick from a pool, whoever is first gets their choice of the best upgrade, possibly leaving you screwed. Very Cool feature.  -Character customization. Why can't I swap the gender of the characters? Sorry but I dont like playing as a female dwarf. Call me a Chud or whatever, I dont care if anyone else wants to play as one, I just dont want to.  -Class Skills. They pretty much all suck and offer nothing special. Slicer gets a melee attack on a 2hr cooldown, Falconer launches a bird, Retcon is Tracer, why you need that in a PVE game i wouldnt know, Spotter is prolly the only one that offers any utility, he can highlight enemies for everyone and shoot darts at enemies that make them take Crit damage, havent played Guardian yet so idk.  -Late Game joining. If you join a late game from the server browser you are sent into the mission at the beginning of the stage, sometimes Ive joined in while the team is at the end fighting the elevator horde and you have to run all the way through the stage solo just to catch up. You also start with a random weapon and gadget, which are usually ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Just make it like DRG and spawn me next to my team, and let me pick from a few weapons dude.    Overall not really a fun experience, needs alot more work, and from my understanding alot of problems with this game were made aware of by Alpha/Beta testers and GSG decided to ignore them. So my hopes for this game arent really high.     4/10

Sad that I feel an unshakeable need to give this a negative review in its current state. My feelings about this game as I've been playing it have gone  -It's mid  -It's actually pretty good solo!  -It's awful  They somehow managed to make a worse DRG game while reusing a lot of assets (uninteresting and unfun enemies, less exploration, classes that are both more complex and more bland), an experience that is wildly inconsistent while playing solo (either too easy or get swarmed and hit from all sides while trying to interact with anything at all), an awful multiplayer game (PAUSING the game to SHARE upgrades), and simply a bad roguelite (upgrades and meta progression are generic stat boosts, why do we need a colour-coded rarity system, repetitive loop, bosses are bullet sponges, unsatisfying ammo and armor economy). I want it to be excellent, but there are some fundamental game design choices that subtract from the experience and need to be addressed.

Unfortunately in the current state I really cannot recommend this game - although a lot can still change and be improved, given this is early access after all...  At the core (hah) it is fine - a more combat-focused DRG with a different and more challenging set of enemies, simplified cave design, and more weapons to select from. Unfortunately a lot of it just falls apart - to go over the reasons: [list] [*] The armor system. Instead of shields of the miners, the Reclaimers have armor, which just act as an extra layer of health on top. It can only be replenished via resupplies (which no longer restore health - leaving red sugar as the only real option of restoring it) or armor resupply points - it also [b]does not regenerate[/b], which I cannot stress enough - every damage point you take deducts from resources that are not easily recoverable. You have to avoid getting hit as much as possible, which makes me wonder why are Reclaimers so poorly equipped if they are supposed to be more combat-ready.  [*] The upgrades you get during runs aren't particularly interesting. There isn't anything game-changing, and a lot of what you see are either % boosts or conditional % boosts, with nothing that gives you either unique abilities or heavily transforms how your kit works. A lot of Legendary tier upgrades are literally just lower tier upgrades but with bigger numbers attached. Even the Artifacts which are supposed to be very rare and very powerful are just boring flat number modifiers maybe with a conditional attached.  [*] The timer. You get about 10 minutes per stage to get through to the next one, if you take too long you are punished with an endless swarm and creepy grabby hands that, if they grab you, you're yoinked away, instantly killed with no way to revive you, and, if your teammates survive to the next stage, you will have to be recovered from an alien nest experience, costing your team more valuable time and manpower in the process, only making the issue worse. This makes the whole experience very solo unfriendly, as Cooper (this game's stand-in for Bosco) can't do much more than carry one thing at a time or mine some Expenite vein on the side. The amount of time you have while solo is only about 1 minute longer than a 2-man expedition, and 2 minutes longer than a 4-man. This encourages your team to split up and start doing their own things to optimize the amount of time you are given, which sometimes feels like too much of a pressure. And also leads me to my next point...  [*] The negotiation system. Every time you get an upgrade or a weapon crate, the game wants the entire squad to gather up. While you can hold off on getting the upgrades until you all are gathered up later (the drone that gives you these is very mobile), there is no such luck with weapon crates, not to mention the individual-pick upgrades like the workbench and bio-boosters will require everyone to go to that location individually anyway, burning precious time. The second issue is the more obvious one - to simulate the experience of scavenging upgrades and weapons that you find in an abandoned facility, there is a random picking order assigned every time - and if one of your teammates picks an upgrade, it becomes unavailable to everyone else. On one hand this forces you to strategize on who picks what, on the other it kinda sucks when someone yoinks the upgrade that would be a massive boon to your build.  [*] The general meta progression outside of your runs. The only way you progress here is by unlocking and equipping enhancements that make you start the run with higher values. The issue is, all these enhancements are extremely boring things like "more health", "more armor", "more pickaxe damage" etc. There are also 4 tiers of these and you can stack the same upgrade of different tiers. This basically means that in-between runs you barely feel like you're getting stronger at all - until you suddenly hit a massive power spike at the final tier; plus a few specific upgrades at that point will feel pretty much mandatory, as they will be the only way for you to significantly beef up your damage output. (Why would you not take Marksman IV with +50% weakpoint damage? Or better yet stack Marksman IV and III for +75%. This is such a massive damage increase that playing without it is just gimping yourself for no reason.)  [*] Merits. They are earned the same way as perk points in base DRG - from challenges. However, they are used for a variety of things, including: unlocking enhancement slots for your classes (with exponentially increasing costs), unlocking new enhancement tiers ([strike]tier 4 is locked behind 7500 merits what the actual-[/strike] Toned down as of the latest update, thankfully.), unlocking dailies (yes you have to unlock [i]dailies[/i]) whose entire point is giving you more merits (meaning until you get like 4 or 5 dailies done in that specific slot, it's just a net minus to your merit budget), and ascending (promotion equivalent). Main problem is you only get merits for challenges done and dailies completed, and you do not get any [i]at all[/i] for runs, as those only award XP for enhancement unlocks - and I do not understand why you wouldn't be able to get some extra like that, especially that it feels like you have to do way too much to get any significant amount of stuff unlocked.  [*] The Clearance level progression. You are forced to do higher Depth levels (think Hazard level) in order to go past certain clearance levels (which until then Hollow Bough, Magma Core, Altered Sites and Complexity 3 will be locked off to you) - most notably at least reaching the Depth 3 boss, which is not going to be an easy task. This is a massive issue if you are not a hardcore player (from my experience even Depth 1 of all things can be surprisingly vicious at times). There's a reason why in the original DRG, the only thing locked off behind high difficulty missions were even [i]higher[/i] difficulty missions. [/list] This game has unfortunately way, [i]way[/i] too many issues to really be recommended. If you know ahead of the time what you're getting into, you can probably enjoy it - but it will take a lot of work before Rogue Core can be truly good.

The most interesting or fun things about DRG were frankly, the mining/mission objectives. Dealing with the issues of a weird cave generation while you strip Hoxxes of all of its goodies and the teamwork between the classes to make that all happen is fun. The combat plays a bit of a secondary role to what you're trying to accomplish (e.g. acquire aquarks, refine liquid morkite, recover requipment) - it's there to harangue the player, to create obstacles, but it isn't the goal. The combat is fun enough, but there are other games with a greater combat focus that feel more polished, developed, and fun.   L4D is a classic, and is amazing through polish and fun, though by modern standards potentially lacking more mechanics. Vermintide/Darktide have great teamplay mechanics, positioning, build/weapon diversity, and challenge as you ramp up the difficulty. While there always seems to be some argument about the quality of Helldivers 2, it has a more epic scale, and definitely 'big boom'.  Rogue Core's combat is derivative of DRG, using enemies that at least for me are the least interesting of everything Hoxxes had to throw at you - and removes the more 'mission objective' type play, leaving a somewhat bland combat experience that pales in comparison to other offerings in co-op PvE games, with some not terribly interesting 'Roguelite' elements on top of it, where incremental stat gains are priotized over perks/choices that can radically shift the dynamic of a run.  I started playing DRG early on in it's EA, and it was fun to play right away, even without the avalanche of content that came thereafter - whereas I wouldn't say Rogue Core is fun - yet.  Yes, I'm well aware that it's not just the 2nd DRG game, and i do like when studios change things up rather than churn out the same stuff over and over again - and I like many of the games that fall into the space of what they're going for, which is why I feel confident in saying that it isn't measuring up to those other products in quality, again, for the time being.  I'm hoping that further development of the game will change it into something really enjoyable, but at the moment I think some of the fundamentals aren't quite there for it to be something I'd want to keep plugging away at - particularly since for an early access release, where in exchange for getting to play the game early, the players get to be an expanded testing group (either playtesting or bugs) it is on the pricier side of things. Wouldn't recommend just yet, but fingers crossed I suppose.

The game is, surprisingly, really really sluggish. The dwarves seem to run slower than in DRG for some reason. There's still no auto-sprint, and everything seems to slow you down. DRG has a lot of CC spam too but offers more possibilities to avoid it/counter it (grappling hook, dash). The enemies are far, far faster and harder to hit in this game which makes slowing down far more dangerous.   It wouldn't matter if the game felt slow if it didn't also feel that I'm being punished for being slow (???). I feel like I'm moving in 0.5x while the enemies are in 2x.  I feel that the wave of enemies summoned while defending the elevator should be bigger but core spawns breaches shouldn't be able to occur during that period.   The first boss is alright. It's fun for sure. However, the nature of it's projectiles plus the slowness of the game in general make it feel obligatory to tank damage. It's not impossible to dodge its projectiles but you'll probably be hit by the second volley anyway.  The second boss is a lot less fun. Again, the sluggishness of the game makes it really hard to dodge the ram attack, especially if it rams 2 or 3 times in a row. The tornado attack is kind of ridiculous if you get caught and underwhelming if you don't. Haven't made it to the final boss.  Upgrades aren't really all that fun. A few are, but in general they're kind of boring, especially the weapon upgrades that are just stat buffs.   I don't quite get what I'm playing towards either. The meta progression is very very limited so I kind of feels like I'm going to be playing the same missions over and over again, except maybe with more health or weak point damage or whatever.    Overall, it's decent and quite promising. I don't recommend solely because of the price of the game. The biggest downside for me is the pacing issues.

Unfortunately it's just not quite there (or a finished product) yet. I suppose the core framework for what Rogue Core should be is there, but theres some real fundamental issues and also a lack of content that when combined just make this game feel really eh.    What's actually good (Potentially controversially):  - The timer: its a fundamentally necessary mechanic, and its well designed and implemented. It is basically the main mechanic that actually distinguishes this as a faster paced and different experience from DRG  - Upgrade/equipment negotiations: I really like this, at least in concept. Its a great tradeoff to showing players a lot of options per upgrade while also letting players plan around eachother which is a fun teamwork mechanic. the upgrade pool itself is however quite bland and forgettable for a lot of upgrades, and the issue that higher rarity upgrades show up alongside low rarities often creates a lot of friction i think. but overall its a quite good mechanic imo. just actually speak and discuss with your teammates  - character abilities: The balance for characters feels like its in a good enough spot, and they each play pretty differently while not being over-complex.    First Issue: Lack of mission variety  There is practically no variation in mission objectives past just mining stuff on walls on your way to the elevator event every stage. Expenite events and upgrades were GSGs attempt to fix this but they do not impactfully influence or alter the game flow at all, making every stage and every mission feel basically the exact same.    Second Issue: Poor pacing  Holding E is such a meme in DRG's mission design, but this game's version of that would be "standing in a small circle. Upgrades, equipment, and many expenite events require you to stand in small circles while doing things which absolutely kills the intent of fast-paced missions. It doesnt help that upgrade selections often can take north of a minute which again greatly impacts the pacing of the missions. Upgrades should maybe just take place in between stages, not during them. The worst part is that "holding E" wouldve actually been effective game design in rogue core as implementing a time and attention tax to players when theres a constant timer, but instead the devs just made it so you have to stand in a circle for most things for some reason.    Third Issue: Equipment/Weapon balance  Equipments are in a pretty weird balance state where some are incredibly helpful while others feel like an active fun tax. The flare gun for example requiring you to entirely sacrifice a mobility tool just to help your team a bit is not fun to have to take even if it is very important and helpful.    Weapons also generally feel like they have quite poor gunplay (especially the rogue core specific ones, unfortunately) at least until you stack them with a ton of upgrades.    Fourth Issue: Poor progression  Progression is not only boring and poorly designed, but too important and impactful to meaningfully disregard.  - Perk system is walled by an unbelievable amount of resources at the max tier, which are non-renewable for the most part and the SAME RESOURCE USED FOR PROMOTIONS (ascensions) and Unlocking perk slots  - Perk system is ostensibly overpowered and gives incredibly powerful flat stat bonuses that feel pretty mandatory to actually feel powerful at higher difficulties    Unfortunately almost all of these issues were apparently repeatedly aired and mentioned in alpha testing, which makes me very disappointed that we have to still deal with them in a game released with a full price tag. I would pass if you're looking for an experience that is worth the money right now

I'm sure there will be balance patches and the game will improve in the future (and I'm noticing some shadow-patch), so I'm not asking for a refund. In fact, I'll keep playing it. But in the meantime, I'm leaving this review as a note:  Some players can experience motion sickness when the reclamation start (When you activate the magnetic devices to open that large rotating rock wall);  The Expedite Detonator is a completely bugged event that is prone to glitches when a dwarf enters the cage before starting the event. Additionally, the detonator releases the expedite inconsistently (sometimes in blocks, sometimes it fills the cave's walls).  Obvious balance issues on complexity 2+ and depths 2+: single player is a piece of cake, two players is a nightmare;  The rogue-like element goes to hell because once you've unlocked the slots for the eight perks and the major perks, there are no further rewards (higher rarity for found weapons, higher perk rarity, etc.) in the face of a difficulty that, from depth 3 onward, is extremely punishing;  Ability inputs, especially when pressing multiple keys, aren't always responsive;  Not all weapons from the original DRG are present in the game;  Beardless dwarves? No; Not enough cosmetics for beards? Hard no;  Some enemies leave zero room for interaction (e.g., leeches);

[h1] Game Version [/h1]  May 2026 - RC:EA:00.07.09  [h1] The Price [/h1]  I cannot recommend this game in its current state, at its current price. For all of the folks telling us to not compared it to DRG, DRG is a complete game with many extra years of content, at exactly the same price as this early access product. I've played plenty of other Rogue-lites that were complete games, and had a normal price less than this too.  [h1] The Timer and/or Events Need Changes  [/h1] It's okay that a timer exists, it adds to the pace of the game, keeps you moving. What isn't okay is when a single event takes such a massive proportion of a single level's timer. On higher mission difficulties there are events that the time to complete them is simply so long you should never start them, or you are going to run out of time. Even with a well equipped team and plenty of ammo, the events just take too long with too little reward. You are far better off going for simpler objectives like workbenches. My suggestion would be to make the long events actually subtract from the timer if successfully completed. If you are prepared you can squeeze out an event even at high difficulty if you have the skill and ammo this way.  The other complaint I have about the timer is in regards to cave generation. Due to long empty tunnels between rooms, backtracking is rather punishing on the tight time limit. Let's say you have a cave generation with rooms A-B-C-D-E, where room E has the elevator. This is great, no backtracking, always push forward, you got lucky. However, I've seen cave generations like A-B-E-C-D. Due to the amount of time wasted backtracking, you might be able to get to room C and get a small objective or two, but there's no way you're getting to room D without some extreme movement speed stacking. This is especially punishing if you pick a stage modifier that gives you a reward such as an artifact and it spawns in the furthest room. There's a difference between putting it in a spot that is annoying to get to, and putting it in a spot that is simply not feasible to reach.  [h1] The Shared Loot System Needs to be Removed  [/h1] You know what a fast paced, timer driven, rogue-lite FPS needs? A system that requires everyone to drop what they are doing, run to one spot, stand in a tiny circle for a few seconds for a popup window to come up, and then spend the next three minutes fiddling around with who wants each upgrade.  I'm not even annoyed about the negotiation aspect. For the most part folks are just as respectful as in DRG and there isn't really much conflict. I just hate how it grinds everything to a halt.  Give everyone their own loot pool, and let them go get their upgrades/weapons/equipment at their own pace like the workbench. I've had a number of times where I had a 10s down period in a swarm where I really would have loved to quickly pop into an upgrade/level up menu and select something that could drastically improve my survival chances.  If someone gets their face eaten while picking upgrades that is a skill issue.  [h1] Lack of Class Specific Upgrades  [/h1] If you take away the shared loot pool suddenly you have a bunch more space to add cool class specific upgrades. Make the Retcon explode when they rewind, give the falcon a lightning tether, let the guardian produce earthquakes, etc.  [h1] Lack of Other Interesting In-Run Upgrades  [/h1] The unstable overclocks from DRG that drastically alter how a weapon functions are great, and are entirely missing. Let me have something like the Fatboy for a grenade launcher, mortar rounds for the Thunderhead, or Hipster for the m1000. Make these upgrades epic or artifact level upgrades or something so they aren't too common.  [h1] Lack of Interesting Meta Upgrades  [/h1] Number-Go-Up upgrades are the most boring type. Especially if the improvements aren't terribly large, and you can only equip 8 of the things at once.  [h1] Some Other Minor Complaints  [/h1] Ammo economy feels a bit bad for the most part. Slicer and Falconer do better in this respect due to their abilities doing plenty of damage, and Spotter has an extra resupply.  Not much in the way of cosmetic options yet, though I suspect that will add more shortly.  No beers at the bar give you buffs for your runs. There hasn't been nearly enough drinking for this to be a proper dwarven game in the lobbies I've been in.  Electricity feels substantially weaker than other elemental types right now. The slow doesn't do much as the enemies just jump at you, and the damage isn't as substantial as DRG. Conversely all of the other damage types are great.  Red Sugar gives 1HP per 1 chunk just like in DRG. Unlike in DRG dwarves have a base health of 200 instead of 100, and take substantially more damage to lack of shield. It makes having a red sugar perk of some sort essentially required on a higher difficulty.  Getting attacked by a quiet enemy around a corner or through a thin wall is more punishing. In DRG your shield would absorb that first hit, but here you just take damage from your limited health pool.  I've only crashed once, and fallen out of bounds once, but other folks have reported significant issues. I hope that gets fixed soon.  A number of the ability activation keybinds seem to not work consistently. In particular any of the Q+E button press combos.  Finally, I miss Steeve. He was a good bug.

I really don’t like giving Ghost Ship Games bad reviews...  I've put over 2,000 hours into the original Deep Rock Galactic and about a fourth of that into DRG: Survivor. However, Rogue Core is a completely different beast..  Sure, it’s fun in many ways, featuring wicked cool details, a great ship, and a funny robot AI. But the core concept of the game—where you "level up"—requires players to stick together. In reality, you get teammates who take forever to choose their upgrades, which is incredibly annoying, or games where people refuse to gather up at all, completely blocking you from getting upgrades. I get the loop; unlike regular DRG where you mine every single mineral, here you have about 15 minutes max per level before you need to move on.  But that is the major catch right now.  If you try to play solo—and god forbid you do—it becomes virtually impossible past hazard/difficulty 1. If you play with randoms, you honestly have better odds of rolling a Yahtzee on your very first try. Now, if you have a dedicated partner/partners/friends to play with using in-game voice chat or Discord, then sure, it works. But that is a lot to demand just to enjoy the game.  When it comes to difficulty 4 and beyond, things get rough. You start with random weapons that feel seriously underpowered. You can't even pick your loadout from the start; instead, at the beginning of every single run, you have to watch the annoying cutscene of the barrier separating before you finally get to pick your weapons. To make matters worse, the weapon rolls aren't guaranteed to be good, or even something you are skilled with. While each class has its own nifty specialty, it mostly feels like a "paint job" that doesn't really matter on higher difficulties.  Then there are the enemies. You know those annoying crystalline threats from DRG that you absolutely hate? Yeah, they are standard here. You are constantly fighting crawling, monkey-like, face-hugging jumping SOBs with a weak spot the size of a pea. I expected the classic bugs from the other DRG games, but nope—not here.  As it stands, I cannot recommend the game in its current state. It is still in Early Access, so hopefully, it will improve over time. Who knows? I will definitely give it another try down the road and happily update my review to a recommended one if things change!

In its current state the game has too many issues and too little interesting content to justify the full price tag. To point out a few:  - Pacing and the general wasting of player’s time. The gameplay is regularly broken up by having to get everyone together to draft perks or equipment which takes like 30 seconds every time. Also just starting the mission got old very quick, why not have the first weapon and equipment choice in the drop pod. Just having to use the veil breaking drones every game does not add anything to the game. The gauntlet mode starts you off in the equipment room immediately, so why not make it an option for the main mode?  - Mission variety and room layouts. Every single mission is structured like a DRG mining expedition, but even more streamlined. Each mission is a linear chain of rooms in which you can follow the cable to the exit, with a possible appendix after the exit elevator. Along the way you get bio-boosters, workbenches and side event. And that’s how it goes every single mission except the final ones, where exit and side event are replaced by the boss. Game grows extremely monotonous quickly, at least in DRG there was enough cave and room layout varieties for missions to keep it more interesting for a while.  - Class and perk balance. The classes power level difference is way too big, guardian seems to provide very little value compared to to the others.  - Weapons and equipment selection. Sure some of the new added weapons are nice, but why weren’t some more interesting options from DRG ported, like the crossbow, or coil gun, EPC or just any of the driller’s weapons? Also every single equipment and heavy weapon crate always contain the same choices of base items every time. Having rare equipment such as grappling hook would also add some variety.  GSG seem to have accidentally stumbled into success with DRG and thought they had it figured out, but the in reality their spinoff board games and video games seem to be unsuccessful.

I'm sure this has all been mentioned, but I wanted to reiterate my own thoughts after a few hours of gameplay:   The upgrade mechanic is interesting, but making it so only one character can choose one specific upgrade is a pain. Not to mention requiring the entire team to be there to even start the upgrade "negotiation". The old system of 3 random choices that you can choose when you have time was a lot better in my opinion.  The weapon mechanic can make or break a run. I'm not a try-hard by any means, so getting a run where all my friends and I had were weak pistols, minus one that could charge up for a more powerful shot but had low ammo, was annoying and ruined the run from the start. A re-roll option, at the cost of credits or whatever, would at least make it more bearable. Not to mention locking a class once someone chose it? That eliminates the fun of incredibly stupid loadouts with friends. Updating so multiple people can choose the same class, and maybe adding a preferred starter weapon would be minor but greatly appreciated changes.  The difficulty mixed with the timer is frustrating. It feels like COD in space with no real story line driving it even though it feels like there should be? Right now it just feels like lots of action with minor breaks of boredom in between. Maybe increase the timer by 10-15%, and more of a story?  I trust Ghost Ship to make a great final game, but right now this is missing the mark from the prior games.

Hoooooo boy this is a tough one. I have 1.5k hours approximately in DRG and while I was enjoying my time with this game I think it has a lot of glaring issues that make the experience incredibly frustrating.  I don't really have anything new to add that hasn't been said already but the negotiation system as it stands sucks pretty hard, I'm sorry. A lot of the game seems to have been built around this system but I'm finding it just doesn't work. In addition needing to get all four players in around Ellis to activate negotiations is as frustrating in itself. You'd think there's enough visual indicator to imply that your team is looking to upgrade but that doesn't stop some people. It feels like a lot of small things in this game come together to create an experience that feels like it's not encouraging teamwork in the way that DRG did. Deep Rock Galactic was and still is the only multiplayer game that I ever felt comfortable playing with randoms; this game misses that mark by a long shot as it stands.   In DRG playing with greenbeards was part of the experience for me; being a greybeard and helping people in to experience the game was a rewarding experience because all of the game's assets were working together to encourage teamwork and communication without even needing to use the chat feature. Rogue Core feels like it's failing at that spectacularly and one person can and will mess up a run. People rushing off so you can't upgrade, hogging upgrades, starting events with no time on the clock, etc. It just doesn't really work and it's oh so frustrating. I don't really blame other players more than I blame the game design.  A lot of the new guns are hit or miss and will need balance passes for sure. A lot of the standard guns from good old DRG don't feel like they keep up with the demanding crowd control needed in this game where single target damage focus on things like the M1000 hardly feels valid.   One thing I haven't felt negatively about is the timer; I think the tweaks they made to timers in the first few patches here was good enough for me I find that the timer is a fun way to keep you on your toes in a game that's clearly roguelike-centric and not the slower paced exploration focal point of deep rock. It's not the timer that's the issue in my opinion but other points of the game that need working like enemies and negotiations.    The devs have also not learned how to make upgrades/perks interested and this was an issue with Deep Rock as well primarily with the perk system. The upgrades are hardly interesting and feel like small nuber-go-ups and just why would you take a feature like this and have the players fight over each other for things???? It makes NO sense.  I have faith that the devs will do right by the players as they always have to bring the game to a place that makes it something good or even great. But for now I'm shelving this one until the game's had some significant updates. Above all else I think we're curious to see what they end up doing with the negotiation system.

The Game has issues, but I think those issues will be compounded by the community's opinions on how to solve those issues.   - [b]The Buffs[/b]: They are lame, uninspired and feel meaningless apart from 2-3 in game.  The Selection process involves you selecting against the others in a [i]Coop[/i] game. You must all be within range of your new Moll-E to even commence the process of selecting your upgrades, as you're on a timer someone may have ran ahead, which means you need to wait for them, or move up and try the selection process again - you must be within proximity of the upgrade to start the process where you select the upgrades from the resource mined during missions.  Selecting against your team sucks, you can make the entire team worse off because you chose an ugprade that helps you, but isn't optimal for the team to make through each level/section. Selecting upgrades in the format as currently constituted feels labourious, contentious, and you end up sacrificing your own enjoyment to ensure the team has an adequate build from the upgrades available.  Some of them just suck: [i]Extra HP from Red Sugar,[/i] or [i]recoil reduction while standing still[/i] - very little in the way of "[i]do +__ dmg when within 3m of an ally[/i]" something that would improve team cohesion.  Are we slower in Rogue Core than DRG? It feels so, and in a game with mostly combat, and some mining - as opposed to DRG which felt like lots of minining, with combat and waves/events interspersed it's not exhilarating, or tense; it's frustrating.    - [/b]The Timer[/b]: A game like this - with up to 7 stages iirc; needs some sort of 'funnel' to keep your team on the move. Personally, I don't see the timer as the issue, but it could use some adjustments; particularly once it reaches the end on the mission. Once the timer runs out you get max pressure until you get the extraction (elevator) zone. If the pressure gradually stepped up this would likely be an easier implementation.  On the issue of the timer, I don't find it a critical issue like Buffs or the Selection process.    [b]The Upgrade System[/b] needs to be reworked entirely - You should select your OWN upgrades that your character rolls, independent of the other players. You would then be selecting the optimal upgrade for your character, and the team.   Right now it offers 5 upgrades, 1 of them behind 20+ Extra HP, and fully rehealed. So unless you need that one, there are FOUR upgrades for 4 players to choose from. Well the last person doesn't even get a choice.  If you had 4 choices + Full Health to make for your [Insert Class here] and the other 3 plays made their choice independent of yours it would likely be a better experience. After all, we upgrade our guns at the workbench this way - no one is 'stealing' an upgrade for your weapon. You simply choose between the upgrades offered on your weapon(s).  I wouldn't recommend with this game until drastic changes have been made to allow you to enjoy playing the game.

The game absolutely needs some time to readjust balancing issues. The timer, the bosses, the potency of upgrades, the ammo amount, the ability to recover health, the entire armour system, all of it needs work.  This is part of the problem doing multiple closed betas with limited players. Absolute garbage feedback in terms of balance. So much of the games difficulty needs adjustment, and the RNG needs alot of work. Unlike most every other rogue lite game out there, there is no way to reroll, save, or remove certain modifiers from the pool, making every upgrade a losing proposition. The chances of you being able to make a single coherent build is practically 0%, and most runs end in failure due to there being no way to direct or influence how your build is assembled. You end up losing runs due to luck, not skill, since the game sends harder, enemies, more often, and expects you to have a decent build to counter them, but offers no way to assemble that build.

I went into this game very excited and bought the game at launch with the supporter DLC and soundtrack.  Boy, do I regret that purchase now.  This game is a subpar roguelike with a DRG skin.  It needs a lot of work and I wish that I'd waited to buy it, if at all.    There are a few big issues with this game.  One of the biggest is that the upgrade system only works with a dedicated group of friends.  Everyone has to return to the drone to get upgrades, and it's hard to get randoms to do that.  Then everyone picks upgrades from a pool.  So the odds of you getting the upgrade you want are pretty low.  Someone else can just pick the upgrade you want.  Your friend will probably let you have the upgrade, but a random won't care.  There's also the fact that some of the upgrades seem useless.  Sometimes you get nothing but useless upgrades and end up just taking more health.  So my reward for collecting expenite is ... more health.  Not a cool ability to strengthens my build.    Secondly, there is a timer.  I don't think the timer really adds much to the game.  It forces you to keep moving, do a couple events, and go to the elevator.  That's all you have time for.  You don't have time to explore the level or just look at anything.  In DRG, there's a strong exploration aspect to the game that I like.  That's gone from Rogue Core.  I think they could remove the timer entirely and it wouldn't really impact the game that much.    Third, I've seen a lot of people complain about ammo.  It's true the game gives you barely enough ammo to get by.  This is exacerbated by the fact that the basic corespawn creeper is very, very fast.  They can honestly just be hard to hit with how much they jump around.  And the final boss of the run is always a bullet sponge for some reason.  There's got to be a better way to design these bosses than just giving it tons of health.      I sometimes like to play solo.  This game is not balanced for solo play.  If you are a solo player, skip this game.  You won't get far.  You really, really need  a group of friends to play with.  Randoms won't cut it, unless you happen to get a group of great randoms.  It's rare, but it happens.  Anyway, the game seems to be balanced for a full group of 4 players.  Any less and you are handicapping yourself.    Another issue with this game is the currency for upgrades and how you get it.  You spend this blue currency for upgrade slots and to unlock new levels of enhancements.  These upgrades are the only way to make your character permanently stronger for future runs.  You only gain currency from completing specific goals during a run.  You don't get any currency just for completing a run.  Thus, you run into situations where you simply don't have enough currency to buy the upgrades you want.  I need a another 5000 currency to unlock the next tier of enhancements.  That's going to take forever to get at the rate that the game gives me currency.      Another thing is if you fail a run and go back into the same map, the map remains the same and you get the same weapon choices again.  So if you originally failed because the game offered you bad weapons, you're going to get those bad choices again.  That means there's very little reason to re-try a run after failing.      Add of all this together, and you have a bad roguelike.  Sure it has the cool DRG skin, but you could go play Deep Rock Galactic and you'll probably have a lot more fun.  That game has been out for awhile and is basically a complete game with tons of content.  Rogue Core just needs too much work and who knows what the devs are going to fix or not fix.

Of course, I understand that you’re saying “this is a different game”, but how could the sequel turn out worse than the first part?  First, you’ve reduced all the collectible resources to just one type — not counting sugar, but that’s not really a resource, just simple health packs.  Second, you’ve decided that a game about dwarves doesn’t need a digging system — you’ve treated it like a relic of the past and turned melee combat into fighting with a pickaxe? Yes, you can still dig, but you only need to do it to get to the next mission, and it takes just 5 seconds. In the first game, though, digging was almost the core mechanic. “Dig, collect valuable resources, and fend off bugs” — isn’t that the perfect formula that made us fall in love with your game?  Third, how on earth did you decide to make weapon and perk choices shared across the whole team? This completely kills any chance of building a character. Even the developers of Far Far West figured this out on day two and let players pick cards individually for each character. You had plenty of time during the closed alpha to figure this out.  You’ve already made one roguelike — was it really successful enough to warrant another one? By the way, that game was way, way better than this one, because it had resources, character builds, and you had to dig all the time. Overall, it was a much better roguelike, too. The gadgets feel useless now. The only useful ones are the one that shoots lamps and the anti‑gravity device.  Fourth, what’s up with the progression system? Why does it look like a leftover from the previous game? Didn’t you know how to make it more varied? Meta progression 0, the skin progression tree is terrible, and the character progression is awful   I’m not telling you how to make games, but you’ve got great examples of successful titles right in front of you. Just do something similar, but in your wonderful setting.  Artificially making boss fights harder by just giving them tons of health isn’t hardcore. You’re forced to pound away at that green thing or always pick the same options between missions — otherwise you just can’t win. That’s not difficulty, it’s just frustration that you’ve created.  Running along some hose in super‑short runs from one elevator to the next isn’t interesting at all. I don't even want to talk about weapons, your new dwarves are better than commandos I was probably too lazy to come up with something new, and instead of just adding an alternate fire to the weapons, you added it to the perks. And by the way, why can't you choose the same characters? You don't have any builds A how do you sleep after that?  WHY DID YOU DECIDE NOT TO GIVE US A REAL SEQUEL TO THE FIRST GAME? I DON’T GET IT.
For now dont buy it.  you see all the game has to offer in 5-8 hours.  wait for big and better updates.  I love hairy dwarf men
For now i cant in good faith recommend  this game. some of the bugs are quite........annoying. wait till a few patches.
