The game right now is a 6/10. It has some potential, but lacks in build and run variety: needs double the current amount of unique upgrades, at least 3 more bosses, more types of events to do in the run, and 3 more classes to make it feel better.  The class balance is messed up. As the biggest offender, 1 of 2 of Falconeer's abilities doesn't do anything unless someone on your team dies, making the class worse the better your team gets. Weapon balance is alright, except for the Devastator Pistol, but the weapon variety is lacking, 5 of the 20 weapons are hitscan rapid fire guns with medium damage and medium clip sizes.  Upgrades are at the same time uninspiring while getting unlucky spikes the difficulty of the run too much. The same mission on the same difficulty will range from trivial to incredibly painful just depending on what kind of rolls you get, and you hit these edge cases far more often than you'd expect of a well balanced Roguelike.  There are better Roguelikes to play out there, but I do have my faith in developers so I will come back to this and rewrite the review when it gets some content updates.

Unfortunate that Far Far West came out so close to this. Because really, it highlights every issue so well.  Minimal run variety, so very little to do in each area. You hardly even dig anymore! Upgrades all just feel... Bland. Everyone's complaining about fighting their team for upgrades but, none of them really felt even that worthwhile to me. It's mainly just percentages and small status effects. Corespawn somehow just feel boring to shoot compared to bugs. Very little feedback, just tanky health. Especially the boss, worse than a Dreadnought, and nowhere near as creative as the Caretaker. And the Ammo economy feels so punishing, that you're left with such long periods pickaxing away if you don't make *every* shot count. Or just get mediocre weapons, I guess.  Yes, I have low hours. Yes, I've only completed a single run. But it's enough to see the formula. To see the issues that are gonna be present on every other run. And I already know this isn't a loop I'm gonna enjoy running for hours on end. Especially after FFW showed how this style of game can be done. How much variety, content, and depth there can be, even in Early Access. Maybe it'll get better over time, but it needs a [b]lot[/b] of work. And in its current state, I really can't recommend it.

I want to like this game more than I do, really, but in it's current state it's just not worth playing over DRG.  Being forced to "share" upgrades doesn't meaningfully make the game more cooperative, it basically only does the opposite, and as many, many other people have pointed out, having to get EVERYONE TOGETHER to get 5% extra crit chance is not fun, and ruins the flow of the missions, which are already way too short and spawn way, way too much ♥♥♥♥, especially in earlier stages.   And maybe, this wouldn't be so frustrating, if it weren't for the fact that for some of the problems this game has, were so painfully obvious, and you could think of a million ways to fix them. Me and my friend both expressed annoyance at having to group up for upgrades, because why doesn't it work like the workbench? Why can't you just have your own upgrades queued up? Why do you need four people who are all likely miles away from Ellis to get one upgrade? Like imagine you're playing Mann Vs. Machine in TF2, and ANY TIME, ANYONE wanted to get an upgrade, EVERYONE had to drop EVERYTHING THEY WERE DOING, so you could get 20% bullet resist, does that sound fun to you? Does that sound interesting?  Another thing I have an issue with is the amount of wasted time some things create, like when you start a mission you have to stand in a circle after activating the drones that open the facility... just because, even in SOLO. This goes for some of the events, which a lot of the time, boil down to "wait next to thing", or if they aren't that, they're the bulk detonator, which is cool the first time, but gets really, really, REALLY ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ annoying after the fifth one.  So all in all, don't get this game, not now. I have faith in GSG, they made one of the best horde shooters ever, after all. But in it's current state, Rouge Core is something I can't see myself picking up more than a couple times a week at best.

Minor Issue's: -Timer, it is tunned in a way so that it feel's like it is slightly lenient on complexity 1 and just right on complexity 2 after that it is very unforgiving, but not in a good way, more in a 50% chance you will get everything and still have half the time remaining and a 50% chance that you will have nothing and ran out of time, this might be more of a complaint against the randomness of the level making which was fine in DRG but put a timer on it and it's not pretty.  -Ammo, it is very weapon dependent after playing around with them for a bit it's mostly ok but their are just some amount thing however their is way to many swarms between ammo crates for those type of gun's to do well, if we could see gun stats (ammo count damage ect.) when picking a weapon then that problem would kind of be solved cause then you would know what kind of gun your getting  good thing's: The character's feel very unique and have their own playstyle (which make's me madder that we can't stack character cause that make's it easier to try them out in a party of people that once they find a character they like they will play with that character or not at all)  The perk system has some good variety you can do with it and with it you can fix most of the ammo issue's (but that still make's it so that when starting the game it a slog)  the gunplay and movement is still just DRG so it is good the game is basically just doing constant kill missions which where the best kind of missions in DRG so overall it's pretty fun  all thing considered it's a fun game still deep rock but I don't like how I feel more comfortable with cooper then I do with another dwarf, also dislike how lootbugs are now to useful to ignore in DRG it was debatable if you really needed to pop them but now their is no excuse to not pop them.  Anyway I would not recommend this until they fixed the Major issues i had and in turn fixed the multiplayer, though if you played DRG antisocial and only by yourself anyway then this game is fine, a bit more hectic and less "chill" but still fun.

Rouge Core Review The game is undeniably fun and shows huge potential if the developers address its issues and listen to player feedback. However, several major flaws currently hold it back: Gameplay Bugs •	Reaper Worm collision – Reaper worms can phase through walls, grabbing players unexpectedly. This ruined one of my runs and feels unfair. •	Platform phase-through – Shooting the platform gun at the right timing can trap your character, causing them to clip through terrain and fall into the abyss. This often results in death or a reset to default. •	Movement lock bug – Rare but game-breaking: mouse and keyboard inputs stop responding, leaving the player immobile unless teammates shoot them free. Design Flaws •	Static level design – Losing a level and retrying presents the same layout and choices. This undermines the roguelike unpredictability. By contrast, Deep Rock Galactic reshuffles its caves while keeping their unique identity, striking a better balance between consistency and variety. •	Ammo economy – Several new weapons lack sufficient ammo to survive a level. For example, the Devastator gun consumes too much ammo for low DPS, making it weaker than standard DRG weapons. •	Co-op upgrade choices – Negotiating upgrades with random teammates is frustrating. The system needs refinement to make co-op progression smoother. •	Boss design – Current bosses feel like damage sponges with predictable phases. Their gimmicks are interesting, but they need reworking to be more engaging and fun. •	Map layout – Maps feel generic and lack variety. More dynamic layouts would improve replayability. Presentation & UI •	Voice lines – Female dwarves need more unique and detailed voice lines, especially for actions like hauling heavy objects or customizing cosmetics. •	UI feedback – The lack of elemental status indicators (fire, freeze, ensnare, slow) makes it harder to track player conditions. These should be restored for clarity. •	Cooper upgrades – Solo gameplay with Cooper would benefit from more specialized gimmicks and unique upgrade paths.  Conclusion Rouge Core is fun but flawed. Its bugs, static level design, weak ammo economy, and stale bosses make the challenge frustrating rather than enjoyable.  As of my stance now, it is negative. I would not recommend the game in its current state. However, if the developers address these issues, I may change my stance, as the game shows strong potential to become something truly engaging.

I cannot recommend this game in it's current state.  It feels like a huge step down from the main game, in every single way. It feels like there's less enemy variation, worse weapons, the randomization usually does not end up in anything interesting or helpful (+10% chance to spawn a red sugar chunk when the enemy is affected by fire and it's a Tuesday). You end up with weapons that you don't want, the choice dynamic slows everything down, you end up getting frustrated with teammates, and the whole elevator mechanic seems like a huge step-down from the exploring that was so well-done in the base game.  In it's current state it feels like a mission-type that I would never ever select in the base game.

First off this is not a more combat focused casual nature of base Deep Rock Galactic (DRG), not that this is why I recommend against it just a fact that is important to know since I feel most people who will get the game right now are DRG players who might not be looking for an intense shooter.     The game has a fundamental flaw. They used DRG's combat and gameplay exactly and tried making a combat game from it. This would be fine if they made some changes to the combat but all it feels like they did was give the enemies a massive glow up while giving you another limited resource of armor for protection and varying levels of power class abilities. The class abilities that you now have access to are absolutely not strong enough to make it feel like you are ever ahead of the issues flying at you. The game wants you to feel the pressure of low resources and a timer but does not give enough tools to feel like you can get ahead of these issues, which to me should only happen when you are on higher difficulties not the very first mission.    Games like this can be done well and be fun with the difficulty but a lot of times these kinds of games just bring a community with the "get gud" mentality being the gold standard that I never felt DRG's goal was to be. Given how DRG's life cycle has gone so far I am sure they will listen to community feedback just not sure what they will actually change because of it.
Playing this game just makes me really want to play DRG instead.

Not a winning formula IMO. While the game might be salvageable with some MAJOR tweaks, at present it just doesn't make for an experience that I want to continue.  Many others have already stated this in better detail but as the devs already noted from feedback there are some major flaws with the current formula, and IMO they are going to have to give up some of their existing designs to get the game into a state that will not only bring in new players but keep them around without burning them out quickly.  Those are: [list] [*] The shared upgrade/negotiation system does not benefit the game at all, it only creates frustration and more problems [*] The timer is too short and threat escalation is too high given that players must explore to earn upgrades while fighting off enemies [*] The ammo economy is bad (and using melee attacks can't make up for it) [/list] I'd also add these as major flaws that are creating a lot of additional problems: [list] [*] Completely random weapons/gear/upgrades gives you no way to go into a mission with a plan [*] The game has many mechanics that force players to group up close together, but also punishes you for doing that (friendly fire, no ways to protect each other, most common enemy jumps in the middle of you) [*] The missions are simply too long (4-7 levels with a boss is an absolute slog, and probably a reason why the timer is also so short) [/list]  Overall, it feels like many of the systems that made DRG so co-op friendly and "jump-in"-able were removed in favor of this much more hostile design, while some aspects of DRG which were left no longer make sense in this new game. Finally, it's clear that the game still needs a lot more content, which I'm sure is coming, because the current gameplay loop gets very stale very fast.  [list] [*] mid-game shared upgrade process interrupts the flow of the game (works for single player, doesn't work for multiplayer) [*] exploration and searching for upgrades requires team to spread out rather than stick together, while upgrades and some objectives require players to gather [*] shared upgrades limit your ability to synergize your build and create needless frustration [*] having to complete 4-7 levels just takes too much time, which limits the "jump-in"-ability of the game, and requires the level timer to be too short for each level [*] ammo and health limitations create a natural time limit, which makes the level timer feel forced [*] classes feel flat, with only one providing mobility which is critical, and having very limited synergy [*] mid-game upgrades more than likely provide no additional synergies or ways to play [*] cave design is uninteresting and lacks the variety and "grandeur" of DRG [*] terrain manipulation is mostly negligible, rarely helps you other than to move slightly faster [*] coordination and cooperation is critical but when limited to text chat makes things frustrating [/list]  My suggestions to the team: [list] [*] Loosen the timer up (ammo and health should be the primary consideration) [*] Reduce the number of stages in a mission to 2-3 for normal missions [*] Make upgrades unique to each player [*] Allow for upgrades to be picked with a button press rather than being in a location and pausing the game [*] Let players pick a starting weapon and support tool before going into a mission, or limit the weapons/tools by class like in DRG and DRG:Survivor [*] Class ability upgrades should be part of meta-progression and customizable before a mission [*] Killing enemies should provide rewards, since you can't do anything else while fighting them [*] Reduce friendly fire, especially from grenades [*] Rework some class abilities to be more synergistic (could be done through ability upgrades as well if those are pickable outside a mission) [*] Remove the speed penalty when pickaxe hits a target [*] Add more objectives/terrain that might encourage use of support tools [*] Add more biome specific "flora/fauna" to the caves [*] Increase cave diversity [*] Introduce "gates" to the levels which require players to open to progress to the next room (and may use expenite or require "keys" to open), which extend the timer but will provide increasingly valuable exp and upgrade possibilities (but less ammo/health) [*] Every stage ending with a fight at the elevator gets boring, add additional ways to reach the next stage (DRG has multiple mission types, this game has only 1 and it gets too repetitive too fast) [*] Don't allow corespawn creepers to jump, but you can add more of them or some additional crawlers mixed in to keep enemies threatening (DRG is fun with more bugs, but corespawn in that game are notoriously hated/frustrating because of their jump which makes them impossible to kite without taking damage) [/list]

I'm only barely recommending this game currently. To be very very very clear, I think Ghost Ship is the best developer out there. I've loved every single release they've done, and have spent hundreds of hours playing their games. But I'm about fifty fifty with single player versus multiplayer, and this game is really difficult to play single player. You definitely need some friends. Further, the vibe is just... off? It just doesn't feel as joyful as DRG.  All this said, it's early access, and the company LISTENS to people and feedback, and I have no regrets over buying it early. I don't imagine I'll be playing it much in its current state, but I have absolute faith it'll get bigger and better, and if you've got some friends I think you're in for a good time. But for now, I would recommend playing Deep Rock Galactic if you haven't already. Or even if you have. It's a nearly perfect game, and I can wait for this to cook as long as DRG exists. :-)  Update: After playing a bit more, I just don't like it. It's probably much better with multiplayer but as a single player game (how I played a lot of DRG until I got used to the mechanics) it's pretty abysmal. It's frustrating to a fault, and honestly it just isn't fun enough to keep me chipping away at it so that I can figure it out. I'll check back eventually, but for now I'm just yanking it off the drive and giving the devs time to cook it. So, while your opinions may vary wildly if you're playing it multiplayer, as a single player I have to downgrade it to No for recommending it until the fun factor is addressed.

It has female dwarves. Regardless, the game is really not that well implemented. Maybe it will get WAY better, but for now, it's not worth playing.  You get locked into your starting weapons, because you get upgrades for them as you go along, and then there's no reason to ever switch to something new. It would be cool if new, different weapons dropped throughout the game that enticed you to use them instead, but that's not how it works.  You mine XP, which isn't a terrible idea, but isn't interesting at all. There's not enough ammo drops and there's no way to get more, so sometimes combat is reduced to just melee and there's nothing you can do about it. The rooms all look the same and aren't completely linear, so it's easy to get lost and backtrack halfway to the start without realizing it. The monsters are hard to see so you're constantly getting hit and you're never sure where it's coming from so combat is a hectic free-for-all as monsters infinitely spawn around and above and behind you, so there's no real strategy. Plus they all look the same so you're looking at a tangle of limbs and it's impossible to prioritize targets. Monsters only appear during swarms so you're totally safe most of the time, when you're wandering around looking for the upgrade stations that appear in the same exact amounts on every level.  The roguelite talent upgrade system isn't interesting. You level and buy dozens and dozens of minor incremental upgrades, like, +5 resource carrying capacity, then you have to pick which of the sometimes actually totally identical upgrades to slot at given time in a limited number of upgrade slots. The talent trees criss-cross over eachother constantly so there's no reason to even have trees, and the items at the bottom aren't any better than the ones at the top. In the base game you can unlock pets and rocket boots and such with talents, but this doesn't have anything that interesting to unlock.  I really like Deep Rock Galactic, and I really like roguelites, but this is a terrible representation of both.  Again, maybe it will get better, but unless it's drastically redesigned, and I don't think it will be, it's less than mediocre.

Loads of hours spent playing DRG. Love the genre and had high hopes for this title. I love playing with friends and after 1 mission we agreed that the shared upgrade system in place was a flawed design philosophy and promotes selfishness. No one wants to feel screwed over because they couldn't choose what they wanted or simply were forced to choose something unfavorable.  I hope the developers realize that this issue is a common complaint and address this. I will not be recommending this game to anyone else until I see some positive changes.

incredibly disappointing.   any aspect of DRG that was interesting (clearing new rooms, strategizing for swarms, playing efficiently/quickly as a team, build crafting etc.) is not present here.  you don't have repellent on platforms. you don't have time to drill bunkers or nests. you can't mine down any of the objectives to more preferable terrain. you don't have C4 or EPC to expedite your mining. there aren't any interesting movement overclocks or items. everything is linear (caves and combat included) with the only difficulty that appears being the lack of any good items/weapons you get in half of your runs. there isn't even any inherent counter-play to this either as the devs said themselves that not every run is possible by design (wow!!!). i wouldn't mind certain runs requiring more effort/skill to beat but often bad runs feel like a slog if they aren't outright impossible from the get go. you either get items that actually help you or you don't and on either end of the spectrum the game doesn't feel good. there is nothing here which rewards careful play or mastery.  all of the gameplay depth is substituted for upgrades which either make you god the untouchable or a sniveling gnat that could die to a gust of wind. run consistency is not high and your agency as a player is even lower. there are no interesting build decisions beyond what you choose for your bio-booster deck but that is already set in stone (why ever choose something that doesn't just give you damage or ability cooldown?). even in game all of the upgrade selection is essentially just putting the square peg in the square hole. the real power that you get in runs almost always comes from bio-boosters, toolboxes, or artifacts all of which are more likely than not to give you damage, reload, firerate, or any other actually useful boost to your stats or weapons. successful runs in virtually every circumstance end up with a build that just boosts your base stats with a strong primary rather than forming an interesting synergy or being based around a specific way of playing that isn't just shooting the bugs.   obviously this game is EA so it is a moot point to harp on how underbaked some parts of the game might be. however, there is a lot of stuff that for 3 years in development and $30 should not be the case. why can't you repair elevators? often corespawn just group up on it and break it instantly. if you are solo this always means you have to tank damage to fix it. why is the sound design of the enemies so poor? it was one of the greatest strengths of DRG which any experienced player took full advantage of. in this game more often than not you will take damage or die outright to something which you never could have predicted as sound cues are lacking. why is the balancing of the weapons so poor? the start of your run can either be the hardest thing ever or a complete breeze entirely depending on what you start with. there are just almost too many things to count that made me mildly frustrated that shouldn't be the case. as much as EA is a panacea for all small scale technical criticisms of a game it gets to a point of being too much even with that veneer.   this is just watered down DRG - akin to a high effort mod. it will probably be ironed out down the line to be more approachable but it is not worth anywhere near $30 and is incomparable in quality to DRG even when that game was likewise still in early EA. beyond that for a rougelike shooter this game doesn't do anything interesting or unique. the only appealing aspects of this game are the remnants of DRG's shooting and mining still being fun here. i tried really hard to enjoy this game since i love DRG so much but it is quite rough.

The most damning things I can say about this game are that it feels like a DLC instead of a full product and that most of the major complaints (class balance, timer issues, ammo economy, shared upgrade pools, etc) were raised back in the beta but never addressed. They've built the core of a very solid game: the new bosses are interesting, the environments really help to sell the theme, and a lot of the little touches are already there. But it seems like the design philosophy was very much "one steps forward, two steps back". They don't honestly seem to know what kind of game it should be, and despite the day zero fixes and roadmap, this smacks of a developer that assumed the fanbase would love the game they designed without asking the audience what we actually wanted. Rogue Survivor needed to cook another year or so before being released, even into early access. I sincerely hope it gets good, but hope is not oft rewarded in video game development.

If I have to define this whole game I'd say "oops! all core spawn". Not a core spawn toned down for use in massive swarms; the same awful core spawn I had the displeasure to deal with in DRG. A enemy that moves fast, depending on weapon receives little damage, and now comes in larger numbers.  In DRG each class has these days a toolset guaranteed to work against them. Even if you don't, each class has a escape tool and ample space. Combined with their small numbers during their event it makes the core spawn managable. Here's completely different. Most of the weapons I have used so far deal little damage (7 to 20), combined with their now larger numbers and keeping their vertical leap makes it hard to kill them. It doesn't help that the caves are thighter and I have to keep running while attacked to find a good place to dig in. Oh, also, you either choose between your flare gun or a movement tool like the zip line or the plaster gun.  I understand the timer might not be all bad, because there's only one objective on each level; expenite, But I need either more time to do things orderly or have a nerfed enemy as a base enemy. If I do as much as a side quest or a toolbox quest, I lose minutes, If I do a health booster I lose minutes hacking. If I do a terminal repair, I lose minutes. So currently my only way to reach a decent time and not cross roads with the worms or the second swarm is to speedrun the level.  I'd say a better way would be to alter the core spawn behaviour based on how long the level has been going. Start to first breach the core spawn crawl and are easy to kill, very few in numbers. First breach they move normally and receive slightly less damage. Second they can now do their vertical leap all they want and receive less damage, now they come in numbers.

A lot of what made people fall in love with DRG is not present (yet?) in Rogue Core: Mission Control has been replaced with a boring robot, we're fighting rift-warping aliens instead of bugs, and the design generally leans a bit too far into the scifi direction than "we're old school miners". The hub area feels empty and way less cozy than the space rig. You need to play a minigame before you can chug a beer,  and - if i'm not mistaken - beers don't even give bonuses for the next mission any more.  Gameplay wise, the three biggest issues for me right now are: 1. The timer. Timed sections every now and then are a nice change of pace, but in Rogue Core the timer is always on, and it's _tight_. Apparently it's designed so you have to decide how many upgrades you want to pick up. This seems antithetical to developing your build, which to me is what roguelikes are all about: the build. 2. This upgrade system has many issues. I already talked about the implications of the timer. But also, upgrades are too scarce and provide mostly insignificant benefits. Some are really good, but you won't get them every run, which is kinda sad because - again - having a good build is fun. Getting only poopy ones is not. 3. Time waste. The gathering sequence at the beginning of each mission, needing to gather with your teammates for upgrading, waiting for others to make their upgrade pick, etc. I haven't measured it, but it feels like in a 40-minute run I'm spending like 7-8 minutes doing nothing. For a game that advertises itself as being "more combat focused" than DRG, that seems very odd.  Somebody on Reddit put it into a nutshell: In its current state and at a price tag of $30 and the level of developer competitiveness in the rogue genre, Rogue Core would drown with many of the other games released on Steam daily if it didn't have that DRG franchise reputation.

I like every thing else about this game but the players are the worst.

I expected to really enjoy a rogue-like take on Deep Rock Galactic, but the game in it's current state has serious issues.    Things I Don't Like:  - Upgrades pop up frequently, potentially mid-combat, and pause the game  - Upgrades don't significantly change gameplay. Most add a small buff to damage, ability uses etc.  - You must compete against your teammates for upgrades, which makes what should be a cooperative game feel adversarial. You must pick in a order the game determines too, so whoever goes last has almost no choice in what they get  - The upgrade selections are on a timer, so you are simultaneously interrupted to pick an upgrade, but also rushed to read all of them  - The upgrades which persist between runs are very underwhelming    My experience playing was having the game paused frequently to quickly read a lot of uninteresting options. It's especially baffling because the original Deep Rock seems to have systems that address all of these issues. Events granting overclocks in the original game didn't pause the game and didn't force teammates to compete for selections. The original game also had a huge number of interesting overclocks, ranging from safe to unstable, which drastically changed how a gun was used.    I feel like there is a lot of potential. One reason I haven't played DRG as frequently recently is I've learned most of the meta builds and unlocked everything, and this style of game would add a huge amount of replayablity to an already enjoyable core gameplay. I just don't think some core aspects of how the game works have been thought through well enough to recommend it.

Let me just say that I absolutely loved the first Deep Rock game. I tried and tried to like this game but I just don't. 13 hours in and I think I'm done trying.    First off, the power balance is way off and in the creatures favor. I am struggling to finish the most basic depth 1 missions without my group wiping. I get for depth 3 and 4 they should be challenging but 4 experienced players shouldn't be struggling to finish the very first mission in the game.     On top of that I have run into a lot of toxic players in random lobbies. They heart an upgrade and think they are entitled to it. If someone else takes it I have seen players grief the entire lobby by sabotaging it. I have had this happen on more than one occasion. I've seen people getting kicked right before the end of a long mission because of it. It's frustrating.

A deeply disappointing, arguably lazy endeavor. I could ramble about this game and all the ways it let me down for days but I will try to keep it brief.  The new weapons, while neat, are all quite underwhelming and rarely, if ever, feel good to use. Most of my playtime comes from going into missions hoping to discover a new weapon that actually felt nice to play with and got good results. However, it feels like for every single pro a weapon has, it has to have a con that overwhelms it. Does the weapon have little spread and do good damage? It has insane recoil, little magazine size, and a fire rate so slow that its damage output is kneecapped. Does it have a good fire rate and little recoil? It does next to no damage and has the spread of pocket sand. Of course, no weapon should just be perfect, but the cons always massively outweigh the benefits to weapons in a way that makes it seem as though the devs are deathly afraid of giving the players anything that might possibly work too well. The result is a weapon selection that has me rolling my eyes at every selection.   While we're rolling our eyes at selection, we have to talk about the expenite upgrades. Some of these are very good, actually, but the vast majority are deeply underwhelming. If you spend enough time looking at the total array of options, you'll probably start to see how certain upgrades would work well together. Unfortunately, because of the "negotiation" upgrade system (I'll talk about this after my next point), it's not often that you'll get these synergistic builds. Often times, your build at the end of a mission will be all over the place because the game just didn't want to give you enough perks relevant to one specific style (I'll get to this later).   While you're looking at expenite upgrades, you'll probably notice that there's quite a few tailored to melee damage. I have to say, melee certainly feels better than it did in DRG. That isn't saying much. You only have one melee option that isn't tied to the class system: your pickaxe. Its still very stiff, it still doesn't provide much feedback, but its faster and a little more responsive than it was in DRG. That's all it has going for it. I don't understand the odd focus on it if they aren't going to provide any sort variety in either weapons or perks, because all melee builds will end up looking to same in the astronomically unlikely situation the player gets access to the right perks. Every melee build wants vampire, every melee build wants stun, etc. etc. Despite the odd ratio of upgrades focused on melee, there just aren't enough options to make it interesting.   Its around that last point that certain people will say that, even if there were more upgrades for melee, you'd be less likely to get them thanks to the "negotiation" system. This is the second time I've put qoutes around the term because there simply isn't any negotiating going on. Every the team chooses to upgrade, they get a random assortment to choose from, but with the exception of the default upgrades, all of them can only be taken by one player per choice. You can select a heart icon next to a perk if it isn't your turn to choose, and this indicates your intention to take it when its your turn. Allegedly, there are people who disregard these hearts, and just take whatever they want. In roughly twenty hours, I haven't run into that. Any time someone has taken a hearted upgrade, its on accident. That doesn't mean the system works, it means the playerbase is largely willing to engage in it, and I would argue that's because most of the upgrades are so boring in the first place that there isn't any real reason to argue over them. Even if there was, there's simply no recourse for picking an item that was already hearted by another player, and no reason not to aside from the guilt of being a prick. If it were a true negotiation, there would be trades, compromises, discussion. As it is, nothing in negotiations ever really warrants a dispute, and obviously there's no option for trades or compromises.   To make matters worse, if you join late, you, of course, get no part in these negotiations, you are assigned whatever equipment is left over and you don't get any of the upgrades, often times this saddles you with gear that is undesirable by the already bargain bin tier selection. In other words, there is literally. Never. One. Single. Reason.  To join a game late. Its just asking for a bad time.   Of course, I can already hear people countering some of my arguments by saying that RC is a roguelike or lite or whatever the ♥♥♥♥ we're calling these things nowadays. To which I'd say, just because the choices are random doesn't mean they have to be bad. We've had systems in this genre to alleviate the frustration of bad RNG forever, and the road map says the devs are planning on implementing it. To that last point I'd say: why the ♥♥♥♥ isn't it already in the game. I'm not so cynical that I would call this game an asset flip, it has some new assets, but the vast majority are ports from DRG. Which makes me wonder: what the ♥♥♥♥ have they been doing these past couple years while they work on this game. How did it come out so undercooked, and where do they get the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ gall to charge 30 dollars for this underwhelming mess?  I would like to change my review. I would like to recommend this game. I would like for enough changes to be implemented that the game becomes worthy of peoples time and money. I don't see that happening.

It's fine on the surface but there are some severe issues that will only get worse over time if they are not addressed. For one the game does not scale difficulty with playercount, which gets aggravating very quickly once you go into some of the lower depths solo. This might be mitigated by playing coop but this game seriously has issues with the fun factor the larger your group gets.     Evidently this is a hero shooter now because unlike the main game, you can't take multiple characters of the same kind into a run. So already you have a chance of not being able to play as a character you actually want to play as right off the rip. Secondly the mid run upgrade system is the single worst implementation of roguelite elements I have ever seen in any of these games. No rerolls, no individualized options, you have a random set of starting guns and a random set of perks every level up. The icing of the ♥♥♥♥ cake is that your teammates select things one at a time. Overall it's just a bad idea built on the shaky ground of "promoting teamwork" which in my experience goes by smoothly right up until there's one legendary perk in the list and everyone in the group wants to take it. Same goes for the weapons, they've implemented some new ones that are really fun to use, but it's a random chance to even be able to get them and then a teammate could just swipe it before you get to take it.     If the coop aspects of this roguelite aren't addressed, this game is dead on arrival    PS: Launching the game and being forced to watch an unskippable video of some developer begging me to trust the plan after I already bought the game is a seriously bad first impression

As much as I love DRG, I'm disappointed with this game's release. Yes, many people's expectations weren't met. Yes, it's a completely different game set in the same universe we've come to love. Yes, it's still only alpha.  What attracted me to DRG? The opportunity to relax with teammates after a hard day. Each mission was an adventure, an exploration that didn't feel overwhelming or stressful, and it didn't feel necessary.  What's in the new game? Routine. Small, uninteresting locations, a growing threat, frantically jumping enemies, chaos. You can't even just drink a beer—you're forced to lift iron. There's no way to relax here.  Perhaps something will change in the future, we'll keep an eye on it. Unfortunately, I can't give it a neutral rating. For now, that's it. Rock and stone, brothers!

This game is symbolic of the problems when a good developer gets bought out. In this case by the Swedish company "Embracer Group" in 2021.  Since then our favorite, original game has remained fine because they would not dare touch the workhorse that generates the majority of their revenue. However, you can see a stark increase in DLC and spin-off game creation since that acquisition.  These games have been...generic to say the least. Their "deep" systems are not that deep at all as no thought went into them. They just add generic 10, 15, 20% modifiers or 2x ammunition stock for example.  I genuinely don't know what the goal of this game is. To strip away everything unique about the original game and be left with just combat? Combat that feels inherently worse and less rewarding than the original game in my opinion as well.   Their priority has switched to pleasing shareholders and driving profit. They need to appear to be making money. At quite literally every other cost.   I will keep a look out but I just see this being another cash grab to bleed a fanbase dry until their trust in this IP dies and they stop spending money. Only then will they kill the studio.   It just. Keeps. Happening.
